▸ 21.11 21.11 17
▸ 21.11.1 Acromegaly Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, acromegaly-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Cushing syndrome diabetes", "Thyrotoxicosis diabetes", "Phaeochromocytoma diabetes", "Glucagonoma diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Acromegaly diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise acromegaly as cause of secondary diabetes
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (OGTT GH suppression, IGF-1)
  • [ ] Manage diabetes in acromegaly
  • [ ] Apply definitive treatment (surgery, medical therapy)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGH/IGF-1 excess causing insulin resistance and diabetes
Prevalence50% impaired glucose tolerance; 25% diabetes
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance, ↓insulin signalling, ↑lipolysis

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGH/IGF-1 excess causing insulin resistance and diabetes
Prevalence50% impaired glucose tolerance; 25% diabetes
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance, ↓insulin signalling, ↑lipolysis

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
DiabetesOften mild-moderate; post-prandial hyperglycaemia
Acromegaly featuresCoarse features, macroglossia, sweating, prognathism, large hands/feet
CardiovascularHypertension, cardiomyopathy, premature atherosclerosis
RespiratorySleep apnoea (macroglossia, soft tissue hypertrophy)
MetabolicInsulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureDetail
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> ↓insulin receptor signalling, ↑lipolysis, ↓GLUT4
ReversibilityImproves with biochemical control (surgery/medical)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRoleInterpretation
IGF-1ScreeningAge-matched >ULN = positive
OGTT with GHGold standardGH not suppressed <1 ug/L (or <0.4 modern assays)
Random GHScreening>1 ug/L suggests acromegaly
MRI pituitaryLocalisationMicroadenoma vs macroadenoma
HbA1c/glucoseMonitor DMMonitor response to treatment

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRoleInterpretation
IGF-1ScreeningAge-matched >ULN = positive
OGTT with GHGold standardGH not suppressed <1 ug/L (or <0.4 modern assays)
Random GHScreening>1 ug/L suggests acromegaly
MRI pituitaryLocalisationMicroadenoma vs macroadenoma
HbA1c/glucoseMonitor DMMonitor response to treatment

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
GigantismEpiphyseal closure not yet fused
PseudoacromegalySevere insulin resistance + acanthosis; normal GH/IGF-1
Pituitary macroadenoma (non-functioning)No GH excess; mass effect only
Type 2DM + obesityNo acromegaly features; normal GH/IGF-1

7. Management

Diabetes Management (Pre-cure)

ApproachDetail
LifestyleWeight loss; exercise; low GI diet
Metformin1st line; improves insulin sensitivity
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RAIf CVD/CKD; weight loss benefit
SulfonylureasAvoid (weight gain)
InsulinIf severe hyperglycaemia

Definitive Treatment

AetiologyTreatment
Pituitary adenomaTranssphenoidal surgery (first-line)
Medical (if surgery contraindicated/recurrent)Somatostatin analogues (octreotide, lanreotide); Pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist); Dopamine agonists (cabergoline)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismGH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance (↓IRS-1, ↑lipolysis)
Prevalence25% DM; 50% IGT
DiagnosisOGTT: GH not suppressed <1 ug/L; IGF-1 >ULN
DM managementMetformin 1st line; improves with biochemical control
Definitive cureTranssphenoidal surgery -> often resolves DM

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of diabetes in acromegaly?GH/IGF-1 -> insulin resistance (↓insulin signalling, ↑lipolysis)
What is the diagnostic test for acromegaly?OGTT: GH not suppressed <1 ug/L; IGF-1 > age-matched ULN
Does diabetes resolve after acromegaly cure?Often improves/resolves with biochemical remission
What medical therapy for acromegaly?Somatostatin analogues (octreotide/lanreotide), pegvisomant, cabergoline
What is the GH suppression cutoff on OGTT?<1 ug/L (modern assays <0.4 ug/L)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Acromegaly DM = T1DM?NO - insulin resistance, not autoimmune
GH directly causes hyperglycaemia?GH -> insulin resistance -> compensatory hyperinsulinaemia -> beta-cell exhaustion
All acromegaly = diabetes?NO - 25% DM, 50% IGT; depends on duration/severity

Mnemonic: ACROMEGALY-DM

  • Acroghaly: GH/IGF-1 excess
  • Cause of DM: insulin resistance (GH -> ↓IRS-1, ↑lipolysis)
  • Resolution: surgery -> DM often resolves
  • OGTT: GH not suppressed <1 ug/L (gold standard)
  • Mediastinum: not relevant (pituitary)
  • EIGF-1: screening (age-matched ULN)
  • Growth hormone: pituitary adenoma
  • Adenoma: micro vs macro
  • Lanreotide/octreotide: somatostatin analogues
  • Yield: transsphenoidal surgery 1st line
  • Diabetes: 25% DM, 50% IGT
  • Medical: pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist)
  • Insulin resistance: GH -> ↓GLUT4, ↓insulin signalling
  • Acromegaly features: coarse features, macroglossia, sleep apnoea**

Topic Overview

Acromegaly diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Diabetes Management (Pre-cure)
Definitive Treatment
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.11.10 Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, ici-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Corticosteroid-induced diabetes", "Calcineurin inhibitor diabetes", "Antipsychotic-induced diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Immune checkpoint inhibitor diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise ICI-induced autoimmune diabetes (fulminant T1DM)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (autoantibodies, acute onset, DKA)
  • [ ] Manage acute presentation (DKA, insulin)
  • [ ] Apply long-term management

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence0.5-1.5% on anti-PD-1/PD-L1; higher with combination
OnsetMedian 6-12 weeks; can occur anytime
MechanismAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 -> loss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
PhenotypeFulminant T1DM; DKA at presentation common (60-80%)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence0.5-1.5% on anti-PD-1/PD-L1; higher with combination
OnsetMedian 6-12 weeks; can occur anytime
MechanismAnti-PD-1/PD-L1 -> loss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
PhenotypeFulminant T1DM; DKA at presentation common (60-80%)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureICI-DiabetesClassic T1DM
OnsetAcute (days-weeks)Subacute (weeks-months)
DKA at presentation60-80%25-30%
AutoantibodiesOften negative initiallyPositive (2+)
C-peptideVery low/undetectableLow
AgeOlder (median 60s)Bimodal (childhood/adolescence)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureICI-DiabetesClassic T1DM
OnsetAcute (days-weeks)Subacute (weeks-months)
DKA at presentation60-80%25-30%
AutoantibodiesOften negative initiallyPositive (2+)
C-peptideVery low/undetectableLow
AgeOlder (median 60s)Bimodal

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureICI-Diabetes
MechanismImmune checkpoint blockade -> loss of tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
AgentsAnti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab), Anti-PD-L1 (atezolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab)
OnsetWeeks-months after ICI initiation
DKACommon at presentation (60-80%)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
AutoantibodiesOften negative initially; may become positive
C-peptideVery low/undetectable
HbA1cOften normal at onset (acute)
KetonesElevated (DKA)
Pancreatic imagingDiffuse enlargement (autoimmune pancreatitis-like)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
AutoantibodiesOften negative initially; may become positive
C-peptideVery low/undetectable
HbA1cOften normal at onset (acute)
KetonesElevated (DKA)
Pancreatic imagingDiffuse enlargement (autoimmune pancreatitis-like)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Classic T1DMSlower onset; autoantibodies positive; younger age
Type 2DMNo DKA; autoantibodies negative
Acute pancreatitisAbdominal pain; amylase/lipase elevated; no autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer DMWeight loss; imaging mass; older

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Classic T1DMSlower onset; autoantibodies positive; younger age
Type 2DMNo DKA; autoantibodies negative
Acute pancreatitisAbdominal pain; amylase/lipase elevated; no autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer DMWeight loss; imaging mass; older

7. Management

Acute DKA

StepAction
1Standard DKA protocol (fluids, FRII, K+, monitor)
2Continue ICI controversy (generally hold; oncologist decision)
3High-dose steroids? (Not routinely; may worsen hyperglycaemia)

Long-term Management

AspectManagement
InsulinLifelong (fulminant beta-cell destruction)
RegimenBasal-bolus or CSII
ICI continuationMultidisciplinary decision (oncologist + endocrinologist)
SteroidsGenerally avoided (worsens hyperglycaemia)
MonitoringStandard T1DM follow-up

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Incidence0.5-1.5% on anti-PD-1/PD-L1
MechanismImmune checkpoint blockade -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
PresentationAcute; DKA in 60-80%; autoantibodies often negative initially
MechanismLoss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
ManagementStandard DKA protocol; lifelong insulin; ICI continuation debated
ICI agentsAnti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab, nivolumab); anti-PD-L1 (atezolizumab, durvalumab, avelumab)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the incidence of diabetes with immune checkpoint inhibitors?0.5-1.5% (higher with combination therapy)
What is the mechanism of ICI-induced diabetes?Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 -> loss of peripheral tolerance -> autoimmune beta-cell destruction
What is the typical presentation?Acute onset; DKA in 60-80%; autoantibodies often negative initially
How does it differ from classic T1DM?Older age, acute onset, DKA common, autoantibodies often negative initially
How do you manage ICI-induced diabetes?Standard DKA protocol; lifelong insulin; ICI continuation multidisciplinary decision

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
ICI diabetes = T1DM?Similar but: older age, acute onset, DKA common, autoantibodies often negative
Continue ICI after diabetes?Multidisciplinary decision; often held during DKA; may continue if cancer benefit > risk
Steroids for ICI diabetes?Generally avoided (worsens hyperglycaemia); different from ICI-colitis/hepatitis

Mnemonic: ICI-DIABETES-ICI

  • Imune checkpoint: PD-1/PD-L1 blockade
  • Causes: autoimmune beta-cell destruction (fulminant T1DM)
  • Immune: loss of peripheral tolerance
  • Diabetes: fulminant T1DM
  • Incidence: 0.5-1.5% (higher combo)
  • Anti-PD-1: pembrolizumab, nivolumab
  • Beta-cell: destroyed (autoimmune)
  • Early: 6-12 weeks median
  • Treatment: DKA protocol + insulin
  • Early DKA: 60-80% at presentation
  • Steroids: avoid (worsen hyperglycaemia)
  • Autoantibodies: often negative initially
  • C-peptide: very low
  • Insulin: lifelong
  • Negative Abs initially
  • Hold ICI? MDT decision
  • Immunotherapy: continued if benefit > risk
  • C-peptide: undetectable
  • Type 1: fulminant
  • Elderly: median age 60s
  • Shared decision: oncologist + endocrinologist**

Topic Overview

Immune checkpoint inhibitor diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Acute DKA
Long-term Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.11.11 Pancreatectomy And Cystic Fibrosis

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pancreatectomy-cf, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Chronic pancreatitis diabetes", "Pancreatic cancer diabetes", "Cystic fibrosis"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Pancreatectomy and cystic fibrosis

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage diabetes post-pancreatectomy (brittle diabetes)
  • [ ] Manage CF-related diabetes (CFRD)
  • [ ] Apply insulin and enzyme replacement strategies
  • [ ] Recognise unique features vs T1DM/T2DM

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage diabetes post-pancreatectomy (brittle diabetes)
  • [ ] Manage CF-related diabetes (CFRD)
  • [ ] Apply insulin and enzyme replacement strategies
  • [ ] Recognise unique features vs T1DM/T2DM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeaturePost-pancreatectomy DMCFRD
DefinitionDiabetes after pancreatectomy (total/partial)Diabetes in cystic fibrosis (CFTR mutation)
IncidenceTotal pancreatectomy: 100%; Partial: 10-30%20% adolescents; 50% adults with CF
MechanismSurgical loss of islets + exocrine tissueCFTR dysfunction -> thick mucus -> pancreatic destruction

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Post-pancreatectomy DM

FeatureTotalPartial
Diabetes100%10-30%
GlucagonAbsent (total)Low
Exocrine insuf.YesVariable
BrittleYes (no glucagon counter-regulation)Less

CFRD

FeatureDetail
AgeIncreases with age; 20% adolescents, 50% adults
ScreeningAnnual OGTT from age 10
DiagnosisOGTT (FBG>=7.0, 2h>=11.1) or HbA1c>=48 (controversial)
InsulinOnly treatment (no oral agents)
Exocrine insuf.~85% (pancreatic insufficiency)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePost-pancreatectomy DMCFRD
DefinitionPost-surgicalCFTR mutation
GlucagonAbsent (total)Low
Exocrine insuf.Yes~85%
BrittleYes (no glucagon)Variable
ScreeningPost-opAnnual OGTT from age 10

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

CFRD Classification

CategoryCriteria
CFRD without fasting hyperglycaemia2h OGTT >=11.1; FBG <7.0
CFRD with fasting hyperglycaemiaFBG >=7.0 or 2h OGTT >=11.1
Impaired glucose toleranceFBG <7.0; 2h OGTT 7.8-11.0
IndeterminateAcute illness; on steroids

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

CFRD Classification

CategoryCriteria
CFRD without fasting hyperglycaemia2h OGTT >=11.1; FBG <7.0
CFRD with fasting hyperglycaemiaFBG >=7.0 or 2h OGTT >=11.1
Impaired glucose toleranceFBG <7.0; 2h OGTT 7.8-11.0
IndeterminateAcute illness; on steroids

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestPost-pancreatectomyCFRD
OGTTNot usedGold standard (annual from age 10)
HbA1cUnreliable (post-op)Falsely low (anaemia)
C-peptideUndetectable (total)Low
Faecal elastaseLowLow

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoantibodies +; no CFTR mutation
T2DMObese; insulin resistance; no CFTR
Type 3c (other)Chronic pancreatitis, cancer

7. Management

Post-pancreatectomy DM

AspectTotalPartial
GlucagonAbsentLow
InsulinBrittle; CSII preferredStandard
Enzyme replacementEssential (enteric-coated)Variable
HypoglycaemiaSevere (no glucagon)Moderate

CFRD Management

PrincipleDetail
Insulin onlyNo oral agents
Basal-bolusStandard; CSII common
Excocrine enzymesEnteric-coated; dose by weight/meal
NutritionHigh calorie; high fat; unrestricted carbs
Annual screeningOGTT from age 10; HbA1c unreliable

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Total pancreatectomy100% DM; no glucagon = brittle; CSII essential
CFRDAnnual OGTT from age 10; insulin only; enzymes essential
GlucagonAbsent (total pancreatectomy); low (CFRD)
HypoglycaemiaSevere in total pancreatectomy (no glucagon)
CFRD screeningAnnual OGTT from age 10

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the screening for CFRD?Annual OGTT from age 10
How does CFRD differ from T1DM?CFTR mutation, exocrine insufficiency, insulin only, no oral agents
What is the glucagon status in total pancreatectomy?Absent (no alpha cells)
How do you manage hypoglycaemia in total pancreatectomy?IV dextrose (glucagon ineffective - no glucagon)
What is the screening for CFRD?Annual OGTT from age 10

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
CFRD = T2DM?NO - insulin only, exocrine insufficiency, CFTR mutation
HbA1c in CFRD?Falsely low (anaemia); use OGTT
Total pancreatectomy = T1DM?Similar but no glucagon = brittle; no autoantibodies

Mnemonic: PANCREATECTOMY-CFRD

  • Pancreatectomy: total = 100% DM
  • Absent glucagon: total pancreatectomy
  • No glucagon: hypoglycaemia severe
  • Cystic fibrosis: CFRD
  • Regular OGTT: annual from age 10
  • Enzyme replacement: enteric-coated
  • Absent glucagon = brittle DM
  • Total pancreatectomy = 100% DM
  • Exocrine insuf: enzymes essential
  • CFRD: insulin ONLY (no oral)
  • Type 3c: yes
  • Only insulin for CFRD
  • Malabsorption: enzymes + vitamins
  • Yes: insulin only for CFRD**

Topic Overview

Pancreatectomy and cystic fibrosis
1. Learning Objectives
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
Post-pancreatectomy DM
CFRD
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
CFRD Classification
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
CFRD Classification
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Post-pancreatectomy DM
CFRD Management

▸ 21.11.12 Pancreatic Cancer Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pancreatic-cancer-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Chronic pancreatitis diabetes", "Pancreatectomy and cystic fibrosis", "Drug-induced diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Pancreatic cancer diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise new-onset diabetes as presentation of pancreatic cancer
  • [ ] Differentiate pancreatic cancer diabetes from T2DM
  • [ ] Apply urgent imaging and referral
  • [ ] Manage diabetes in context of cancer treatment

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise new-onset diabetes as presentation of pancreatic cancer
  • [ ] Differentiate pancreatic cancer diabetes from T2DM
  • [ ] Apply urgent imaging and referral
  • [ ] Manage diabetes in context of cancer treatment

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionNew-onset diabetes as paraneoplastic manifestation of pancreatic adenocarcinoma
Incidence1% of new-onset DM >50y = pancreatic cancer; 25% of pancreatic cancer present with DM
TimingDiabetes precedes cancer diagnosis by 6-36 months (median 12)
MechanismTumour factors (adrenomedullin, SST, inflammatory cytokines) -> beta-cell dysfunction

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionNew-onset diabetes as paraneoplastic manifestation of pancreatic adenocarcinoma
Incidence1% of new-onset DM >50y = pancreatic cancer; 25% of pancreatic cancer present with DM
TimingDiabetes precedes cancer diagnosis by 6-36 months (median 12)
MechanismTumour factors (adrenomedullin, SST, inflammatory cytokines) -> beta-cell dysfunction

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePancreatic Cancer DMT2DM
Age at onset>50 years (peak 60-80)>40 years
Weight lossProminent, unintentionalOften stable/overweight
BMINormal/low; weight loss >5%Overweight/obese
Duration<2 years (rapid onset)Often >5 years
Family historyNegativeOften positive
AutoantibodiesNegativeNegative
C-peptideLow-normalNormal/high

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePancreatic Cancer DMT2DM
Age at onset>50 years (peak 60-80)>40 years
Weight lossProminent, unintentionalOften stable/overweight
BMINormal/low; weight loss >5%Overweight/obese
Duration<2 years (rapid onset)Often >5 years
Family historyNegativeOften positive
AutoantibodiesNegativeNegative
C-peptideLow-normalNormal/high

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Red Flags for Pancreatic Cancer

FeatureSignificance
New DM >50y1% have pancreatic cancer
Weight loss >5%Unintentional
New DM + weight loss8x risk
Duration <2 yearsSuggests secondary
No family historyUnlike T2DM
Elevated CA19-9If >37 U/mL

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Red Flags for Pancreatic Cancer

FeatureSignificance
New DM >50y1% have pancreatic cancer
Weight loss >5%Unintentional
New DM + weight loss8x risk
Duration <2 yearsSuggests secondary
No family historyUnlike T2DM
Elevated CA19-9If >37 U/mL

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
CT pancreas protocolGold standard (arterial/venous phase); mass, duct dilatation, mets
CA19-9Tumour marker (sensitivity 80%; specificity 90%); >37 U/mL
MRCPIf CT equivocal; ductal anatomy
EUS-FNATissue diagnosis if resectable/biopsy needed
HbA1c/glucoseMonitor diabetes
Exocrine functionFaecal elastase (often normal unless duct obstruction)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
CT pancreas protocolGold standard (arterial/venous phase); mass, duct dilatation, mets
CA19-9Tumour marker (sensitivity 80%; specificity 90%); >37 U/mL
MRCPIf CT equivocal; ductal anatomy
EUS-FNATissue diagnosis if resectable/biopsy needed
HbA1c/glucoseMonitor diabetes
Exocrine functionFaecal elastase (often normal unless duct obstruction)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMOverweight, gradual onset, FH+, no weight loss
Chronic pancreatitis DMExocrine insufficiency, calcification, alcohol history
Type 3c (other)Chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, post-pancreatectomy
Cushing/acromegalyHormone excess features

7. Management

Urgent Referral

ActionTimeline
CT pancreas protocolWithin 2 weeks (NICE)
CA19-9Baseline
MDT referralPancreatic cancer MDT

Diabetes Management

AspectDetail
InsulinPreferred (flexible, no hypoglycaemia if dosed correctly)
SteroidsIf on chemo; adjust insulin
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RACaution (weight loss, GI); consider if CVD/CKD
Surgery (Whipple)Diabetes may persist/resolve; insulin perioperative

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Red flagNew DM >50y + weight loss -> CT pancreas
Incidence1% of new DM >50y = pancreatic cancer
TimingDM precedes cancer by 6-36 months (median 12)
CA19-9>37 U/mL supportive
CT pancreasGold standard; protocol essential

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the significance of new-onset diabetes in a 60-year-old with weight loss?1% have pancreatic cancer; urgent CT pancreas required
What is the time window between diabetes and cancer diagnosis?6-36 months (median 12)
What is the key imaging?CT pancreas protocol (arterial/venous phase)
What CA19-9 level is concerning?>37 U/mL
How does pancreatic cancer diabetes differ from T2DM?Weight loss, rapid onset, no FH, normal/low BMI, age >50, negative Abs

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All new DM >50 = cancer?NO - 1% risk; but must exclude
CA19-9 = diagnostic?NO - not sensitive/specific enough alone; needs imaging
Diabetes resolves after Whipple?Often persists (beta-cell loss); may improve if insulin resistance from cancer factors

Mnemonic: PANCREAS-CANCER-DM

  • Pancreatic cancer: 1% of new DM >50y
  • Age: >50 years
  • New diabetes: <2 years duration
  • CA19-9: >37 U/mL
  • Rapid weight loss: >5% unintentional
  • Exclude: CT pancreas protocol (gold standard)
  • Age >50 + DM + weight loss = 8x risk
  • Seen: CT within 2 weeks (NICE)
  • Cancer: adenocarcinoma most common
  • Adenocarcinoma: 90%
  • New DM precedes: 6-36 months
  • CT protocol: arterial + venous phase
  • Exocrine: often preserved (faecal elastase normal)
  • Resectable: Whipple if early
  • Diabetes persists post-surgery usually**

2. Definition & Epidemiology

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

▸ 21.11.14 Phaeochromocytoma Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, phaeo-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Cushing syndrome diabetes", "Acromegaly diabetes", "Thyrotoxicosis diabetes", "Glucagonoma diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Phaeochromocytoma diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise phaeochromocytoma as cause of secondary diabetes
  • [ ] Explain mechanism (catecholamines -> glycogenolysis, ↓insulin)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic tests (plasma metanephrines, MIBG)
  • [ ] Manage hyperglycaemia in phaeochromocytoma

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionCatecholamine-secreting tumour causing secondary diabetes
Incidence50% impaired glucose tolerance; 35% diabetes
MechanismCatecholamines -> ↑glycogenolysis, ↑gluconeogenesis, ↓insulin secretion

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionCatecholamine-secreting tumour causing secondary diabetes
Incidence50% impaired glucose tolerance; 35% diabetes
MechanismCatecholamines -> ↑glycogenolysis, ↑gluconeogenesis, ↓insulin secretion

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
DiabetesOften mild-moderate; labile glucose
Catecholamine excessParoxysmal HTN, headache, sweating, palpitations, pallor
EpisodicSpell-like; triggered by stress, posture, drugs
GlucoseFluctuates with catecholamine surges

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
DiabetesOften mild-moderate; labile glucose
Catecholamine excessParoxysmal HTN, headache, sweating, palpitations, pallor
EpisodicSpell-like; triggered by stress, posture, drugs
GlucoseFluctuates with catecholamine surges

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureDetail
Mechanismα/β adrenergic -> ↑glycogenolysis, ↑gluconeogenesis, ↓insulin secretion
LabilityGlucose fluctuates with catecholamine surges

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
Plasma free metanephrinesGold standard (sensitivity 99%)
24h urinary metanephrinesConfirmatory
Clonidine suppression testIf metanephrines equivocal
CT/MRI abdomenLocalisation (adrenal vs extra-adrenal)
MIBG scintigraphyFunctional imaging; extra-adrenal
Genetic testingIf syndromic (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
Plasma free metanephrinesGold standard (sensitivity 99%)
24h urinary metanephrinesConfirmatory
Clonidine suppression testIf metanephrines equivocal
CT/MRI abdomenLocalisation (adrenal vs extra-adrenal)
MIBG scintigraphyFunctional imaging; extra-adrenal
Genetic testingIf syndromic (MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Essential HTNNo catecholamine excess; no glucose fluctuation
Cushing/AcromegalyDifferent hormone excess; specific features
Essential hypertensionNo catecholamine excess
Alcohol withdrawalHistory; similar spells but no tumour

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Essential HTNNo catecholamine excess; no glucose fluctuation
Cushing/AcromegalyDifferent hormone excess; specific features
Essential hypertensionNo catecholamine excess
Alcohol withdrawalHistory; similar spells but no tumour

7. Management

Acute Spell (Hypertensive Crisis + Hyperglycaemia)

StepAction
1Phentolamine IV (alpha-blocker) for BP
2Beta-blocker (ONLY after alpha-blockade) for tachycardia
3Insulin infusion if severe hyperglycaemia
4Fluids for volume expansion

Pre-operative (Definitive cure = surgery)

PhaseManagement
Alpha-blockadePhenoxybenzamine (irreversible) 10-30mg/day 10-14 days pre-op
Beta-blockadeAfter alpha (propranolol/atenolol) if tachycardia
Volume expansionHigh salt/fluids (prevents post-op hypotension)
GlucoseInsulin sliding scale; monitor labile glucose

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismCatecholamines -> ↑glycogenolysis, ↑gluconeogenesis, ↓insulin secretion
LabilityGlucose fluctuates with catecholamine surges
DiagnosisPlasma free metanephrines (gold standard 99% sens)
Pre-opAlpha-blockade 10-14 days -> beta-blockade -> volume expansion
SurgeryLaparoscopic adrenalectomy (curative)
GlucoseResolves post-resection; labile pre-op

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of hyperglycaemia in phaeochromocytoma?Catecholamines -> ↑glycogenolysis, ↑gluconeogenesis, ↓insulin secretion
What is the gold standard test for phaeochromocytoma?Plasma free metanephrines (99% sensitivity)
How does glucose behave in phaeochromocytoma?Labile - fluctuates with catecholamine surges
What is the pre-op preparation for phaeochromocytoma surgery?Alpha-blockade 10-14d -> beta-blockade after alpha -> volume expansion
Does diabetes resolve after resection?Yes - usually resolves; labile pre-op

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Phaeo DM = permanent?NO - resolves after tumour resection
Beta-blocker first?NO - MUST alpha-blockade FIRST (unopposed alpha -> hypertensive crisis)
All HTN = phaeo?NO - rule of 10s (10% extra-adrenal, 10% bilateral, 10% malignant, 10% familial)

Mnemonic: PHAEO-DM-SPELLS

  • Phaeo: catecholamine tumour
  • Hyperglycaemia: labile (spells)
  • Alpha/beta: glycogenolysis + gluconeogenesis + insulin down
  • Episodic: spells (HTN, sweating, palpitations)
  • Orthostatic: not typical
  • Diagnosis: plasma metanephrines (99% sens)
  • Metanephrines: plasma free (gold standard)
  • Spell: hypertension + hyperglycaemia together
  • Pre-op: alpha -> beta -> volume
  • Excision: laparoscopic adrenalectomy
  • Labile glucose: fluctuates with surges
  • Located: CT/MRI + MIBG
  • Syndrome: MEN2, VHL, NF1, SDHx**

Topic Overview

Phaeochromocytoma diabetes

2. Definition & Epidemiology

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
6. Differential Diagnosis

Acute Spell (Hypertensive Crisis + Hyperglycaemia)
Pre-operative (Definitive cure = surgery)

▸ 21.11.15 Protease Inhibitor Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, protease-inhibitor-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Corticosteroid-induced diabetes", "Antipsychotic-induced diabetes", "Calcineurin inhibitor diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Protease inhibitor diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise protease inhibitor-induced metabolic syndrome
  • [ ] Differentiate older vs newer protease inhibitors
  • [ ] Monitor for insulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, lipodystrophy
  • [ ] Apply management strategies

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence5-15% on PI-based ART develop diabetes
RiskOlder PIs (indinavir, lopinavir) > newer PIs (atazanavir, darunavir)
MechanismInsulin resistance, lipodystrophy, mitochondrial toxicity

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence5-15% on PI-based ART develop diabetes
RiskOlder PIs (indinavir, lopinavir) > newer PIs (atazanavir, darunavir)
MechanismInsulin resistance, lipodystrophy, mitochondrial toxicity

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureOlder PI (Indinavir, Lopinavir)Newer PI (Atazanavir, Darunavir)
Insulin resistanceHighModerate
LipodystrophySevere (lipoatrophy + lipohypertrophy)Mild
DyslipidaemiaSevere (↑TG, ↑LDL, ↓HDL)Moderate
Diabetes10-15%5-8%

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureOlder PI (Indinavir, Lopinavir)Newer PI (Atazanavir, Darunavir)
Insulin resistanceHighModerate
LipodystrophySevere (lipoatrophy + lipohypertrophy)Mild
DyslipidaemiaSevere (↑TG, ↑LDL, ↓HDL)Moderate
Diabetes10-15%5-8%

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

PI Generations & Metabolic Risk

GenerationAgentsMetabolic Risk
1st GenIndinavir, Saquinavir, NelfinavirHigh (IR, lipodystrophy, dyslipidaemia)
2nd GenLopinavir/r, Atazanavir/r, FosamprenavirModerate-High
3rd GenDarunavir/r, Tipranavir/rLower (darunavir best)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

PI Generations & Metabolic Risk

GenerationAgentsMetabolic Risk
1st GenIndinavir, Saquinavir, NelfinavirHigh (IR, lipodystrophy, dyslipidaemia)
2nd GenLopinavir/r, Atazanavir/r, FosamprenavirModerate-High
3rd GenDarunavir/r, Tipranavir/rLower (darunavir best)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
Glucose/HbA1cBaseline, 3 months, 6 months, annually
Lipid profileBaseline, 3 months, 6 months, annually
Weight/BMI/WCEvery visit
LFTBaseline, 3 months, annually
Renal function3-6 monthly

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMNo ART; obesity primary
NRTI lipodystrophyStavudine/ZDV -> lipoatrophy
Metabolic syndromeNo ART; central obesity

7. Management

ART Optimisation

StrategyDetail
Switch PITo newer PI (darunavir) or INSTI (DTG/BIC) if suppressed
Metabolic RxMetformin 1st line for IR; SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA if T2DM
LipidsStatin (atorvastatin ≤20mg with PI); fibrate if TG>5

Lipodystrophy Management

AspectManagement
LipoatrophyAvoid stavudine/ZDV; switch to TAF/ABC; tesamorelin if HIV
LipohypertrophyWeight loss; switch ART; surgical (liposuction)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Older PIIndinavir, lopinavir -> high IR, lipodystrophy, dyslipidaemia
Newer PIAtazanavir, darunavir -> lower metabolic risk
DarunavirBest metabolic profile among PIs
Statin + PIAtorvastatin ≤20mg; rosuvastatin 10mg; avoid simvastatin
Metformin1st line for IR

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Which protease inhibitor has least metabolic risk?Darunavir (best metabolic profile among PIs)
Which PI causes most lipodystrophy?Older PIs (indinavir, lopinavir, nelfinavir)
How do you manage PI-induced insulin resistance?Switch to newer PI (darunavir) or INSTI; metformin 1st line
What is the statin interaction with PIs?CYP3A4 inhibition -> ↑statin levels; atorvastatin ≤20mg, rosuvastatin 10mg
Which NRTI causes lipoatrophy?Stavudine, zidovudine (older)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All PIs = same metabolic risk?NO - older (indinavir, lopinavir) > newer (atazanavir, darunavir)
PI + statin = always contraindicated?NO - dose adjust statin; atorvastatin ≤20mg with boosted PI
Protease inhibitors cause direct beta-cell damage?No - mainly insulin resistance + lipodystrophy

Mnemonic: PI-METABOLIC-RISK

  • Protease inhibitors: older > newer metabolic risk
  • Indinavir: 1st gen, high IR + lipodystrophy
  • Lopinavir: 2nd gen, moderate-high
  • Atazanavir: 2nd gen, better than lopinavir
  • Darunavir: best metabolic profile (3rd gen)
  • Tiptrnavir: 3rd gen, similar to darunavir
  • Evolving: newer PIs better
  • Metabolic: IR, lipodystrophy, dyslipidaemia
  • Extreme: 1st gen (indinavir, nelfinavir, saquinavir)
  • TAF vs TDF: TAF > TDF weight gain
  • ATV/r: atazanavir/r better than LPV/r
  • Boosted PI: all affect statin (CYP3A4)
  • ATV: less dyslipidaemia than LPV
  • Best PI: darunavir (metabolic)
  • Combination: PI + NRTI = additive metabolic risk
  • Rosuvastatin: best with PI (≤10mg)
  • Avoid: simvastatin with PI
  • Viral load: suppressed before switch
  • INSTI: DTG/BIC better metabolic than PI
  • Switch: PI -> INSTI if metabolic issues
  • Know: darunavir = metabolic winner**

Topic Overview

Protease inhibitor diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
PI Generations & Metabolic Risk
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
PI Generations & Metabolic Risk
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
ART Optimisation
Lipodystrophy Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.11.16 Thiazide Diuretic And Beta Blocker Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, thiazide-beta-blocker-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Corticosteroid-induced diabetes", "Antipsychotic-induced diabetes", "Calcineurin inhibitor diabetes", "Immune checkpoint inhibitor diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Thiazide diuretic and beta-blocker diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise thiazide and beta-blocker induced hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Explain mechanisms (hypokalaemia, ↓insulin secretion, masked hypoglycaemia)
  • [ ] Apply risk mitigation strategies
  • [ ] Differentiate from T2DM and other drug-induced DM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Thiazides↑Diabetes risk 20-30% (dose-dependent)
Beta-blockers↑Risk 20-25%; masks hypoglycaemia
Mechanism (Thiazide)Hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin secretion; ↓insulin sensitivity
Mechanism (Beta-blocker)↓Insulin secretion; masks hypoglycaemia symptoms

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Thiazides↑Diabetes risk 20-30% (dose-dependent)
Beta-blockers↑Risk 20-25%; masks hypoglycaemia
Mechanism (Thiazide)Hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin secretion; ↓insulin sensitivity
Mechanism (Beta-blocker)↓Insulin secretion; masks hypoglycaemia symptoms

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureThiazide DMBeta-blocker DM
MechanismHypokalaemia -> ↓insulin secretion↓Insulin secretion; masks hypo
PatternFasting + post-prandialOften asymptomatic until severe
Dose relationshipDose-dependent (25-50mg > 12.5mg)Non-selective > cardio-selective
ReversibilityPartial on discontinuationPartial; improves

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureThiazide DMBeta-blocker DM
MechanismHypokalaemia -> ↓insulin secretion↓Insulin secretion; masks hypo
PatternFasting + post-prandialOften asymptomatic until severe
Dose relationshipDose-dependent (25-50mg > 12.5mg)Non-selective > cardio-selective
ReversibilityPartial on discontinuationPartial; improves

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Thiazide Risk by Dose

DoseDiabetes Risk
Low (12.5mg)Low-moderate
Standard (25mg)Moderate
High (50mg)High

Beta-blocker Selectivity

TypeDiabetes Risk
Non-selective (propranolol, nadolol)Highest
Cardio-selective (atenolol, metoprolol, bisoprolol)Lower
Vasodilating (carvedilol, nebivolol)Lowest (may improve IR)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
Glucose/HbA1cScreen before/after initiation; monitor
PotassiumCheck if on thiazides (hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin)
LipidsBeta-blockers may worsen TG/HDL

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
Glucose/HbA1cScreen before/after initiation; monitor
PotassiumCheck if on thiazides (hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin)
LipidsBeta-blockers may worsen TG/HDL

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMNo drug history; obesity/insulin resistance
Steroid diabetesKnown steroid use; post-prandial pattern
Antipsychotic DMWeight gain; specific agent history

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMNo drug history; obesity/insulin resistance
Steroid diabetesKnown steroid use; post-prandial pattern
Antipsychotic DMWeight gain; specific agent history

7. Management

Thiazide-Induced Hyperglycaemia

StrategyDetail
Dose reductionLow-dose (12.5-25mg) preferred
PotassiumSupplement if <4.0; K+-sparing combo
AlternativeACEi/ARB/CCB if glucose intolerant
MonitoringGlucose/K+ 4-6 weeks post-initiation

Beta-blocker Management

StrategyDetail
SelectivityPrefer cardio-selective (bisoprolol, nebivolol, carvedilol)
VasodilatingCarvedilol/nebivolol improve insulin sensitivity
MonitoringGlucose/HbA1c 3-monthly if high risk
HypoglycaemiaWarn: masks tremor/tachycardia; check glucose if unwell

Prevention & Mitigation

StrategyDetail
Low-dose thiazide12.5-25mg; avoid >25mg
Cardio-selective BBBisoprolol, metoprolol, nebivolol
Vasodilating BBCarvedilol, nebivolol (↑insulin sensitivity)
MonitorGlucose pre-treatment, 3/6/12mo, then annually
PotassiumMaintain >4.0 on thiazides

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ThiazideDose-dependent DM risk; hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin secretion; low-dose (12.5-25mg)
Beta-blockerNon-selective > cardio-selective; masks hypoglycaemia
Vasodilating BBCarvedilol, nebivolol -> improve insulin sensitivity
K+ monitoringEssential on thiazides (hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin)
Low-dose12.5-25mg HCTZ preferred

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do thiazides cause hyperglycaemia?Hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin secretion; ↓insulin sensitivity
Which beta-blockers have lowest diabetes risk?Vasodilating (carvedilol, nebivolol) -> improve insulin sensitivity
How do beta-blockers mask hypoglycaemia?Block adrenergic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia, sweating)
What is the thiazide dose relationship?Dose-dependent: 12.5mg low, 25mg moderate, 50mg high risk
What is the preferred beta-blocker in diabetes?Cardio-selective (bisoprolol, metoprolol) or vasodilating (carvedilol, nebivolol)
Does diabetes resolve after stopping thiazide?Partially improves; may persist if underlying IR

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All beta-blockers = same risk?NO - non-selective > cardio-selective > vasodilating (improves IR)
Thiazide DM = T2DM?Drug-induced; often reversible with dose reduction/stop
Beta-blocker in DM = contraindicated?NO - indicated for CAD/HF; choose cardio/vasodilating

Mnemonic: THIAZIDE-BETA-DM

  • Thiazide: hypokalaemia -> ↓insulin secretion
  • Hypokalaemia: ↓insulin release
  • Insulin sensitivity: ↓ with thiazides
  • Alternative: ACEi/ARB/CCB if glucose intolerant
  • Zinc: not relevant
  • Insulin: masked by beta-blockers
  • Depdose: low (12.5-25mg) preferred
  • Eff: dose-dependent DM risk
  • Beta-blocker: masks hypo (no tremor/tachy)
  • Effect: non-selective > selective > vasodilating
  • Type: non-selective worst; vasodilating best
  • Alpha/beta: carvedilol/nebivolol improve IR
  • Block: adrenergic symptoms
  • Linked: glycaemia + BP
  • Override: rarely contraindicated (CAD/HF)
  • Carvedilol: ↑insulin sensitivity
  • Know: monitor glucose/HbA1c 3-monthly on BB
  • Optimise: low-dose thiazide + cardio-selective BB
  • Kalium: check K+ on thiazides**

Topic Overview

Thiazide diuretic and beta-blocker diabetes

2. Definition & Epidemiology

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Beta-blocker Selectivity
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
6. Differential Diagnosis

▸ 21.11.17 Thyrotoxicosis Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, thyrotoxicosis-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Cushing syndrome diabetes", "Acromegaly diabetes", "Phaeochromocytoma diabetes", "Glucagonoma diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Thyrotoxicosis diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise thyrotoxicosis as cause of hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Explain mechanism (↑HGP, ↓insulin clearance)
  • [ ] Manage hyperglycaemia in thyrotoxicosis
  • [ ] Recognise resolution with euthyroidism

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionTransient hyperglycaemia due to thyroid hormone excess
MechanismThyroxine -> ↑HGP, ↓insulin clearance, ↑insulin degradation
Prevalence1-2% of thyrotoxicosis develop hyperglycaemia
ResolutionResolves with euthyroidism

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
HyperglycaemiaOften mild-moderate; fasting + post-prandial
Thyrotoxic signsWeight loss, tachycardia, tremor, heat intolerance, goitre
CardiacTachycardia, atrial fibrillation, high output HF
Glucose patternPost-prandial prominent; fasting may be normal

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureDetail
Mechanism↑HGP, ↓insulin clearance, ↑insulin degradation
ReversibilityResolves with euthyroidism
TypeUsually mild; rarely DKA (if underlying DM)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
TSHSuppressed (<0.01 mU/L)
Free T4/T3Elevated
Glucose/HbA1cMild elevation; HbA1c may be falsely low
TSH receptor antibodiesPositive (Graves)
Thyroid ultrasound/DopplerIf nodule

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
TSHSuppressed (<0.01 mU/L)
Free T4/T3Elevated
Glucose/HbA1cMild elevation; HbA1c may be falsely low
TSH receptor antibodiesPositive (Graves)
Thyroid ultrasound/DopplerIf nodule

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMNo thyrotoxic signs; persistent hyperglycaemia
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness; resolves
Steroid diabetesKnown steroid use; post-prandial pattern
Cushing/AcromegalySpecific features; cortisol/GH excess

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMNo thyrotoxic signs; persistent hyperglycaemia
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness; resolves
Steroid diabetesKnown steroid use; post-prandial pattern
Cushing/AcromegalySpecific features; cortisol/GH excess

7. Management

AspectManagement
ThyrotoxicosisTreat underlying (carbimazole, radioiodine, surgery)
Glucose controlOften resolves with euthyroidism; monitor
InsulinRarely needed; short-term if severe
MetforminIf persistent after euthyroidism
DKA riskRare (unless underlying DM); monitor if unwell

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Mechanism↑HGP, ↓insulin clearance, ↑insulin degradation
ResolutionResolves with euthyroidism
Glucose patternPost-prandial prominent; fasting often normal
HbA1cFalsely low (↑RBC turnover)
ManagementTreat thyrotoxicosis; glucose usually self-corrects

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How does thyrotoxicosis cause hyperglycaemia?↑HGP, ↓insulin clearance, ↑insulin degradation
Does thyrotoxicosis diabetes need specific treatment?Usually NO - resolves with euthyroidism; monitor glucose
What is the glucose pattern?Post-prandial prominent; fasting often normal
What happens to HbA1c in thyrotoxicosis?Falsely LOW (↑RBC turnover)
What is the management?Treat thyrotoxicosis; glucose monitoring; rarely insulin needed

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Thyrotoxicosis = permanent DM?NO - resolves with euthyroidism
HbA1c reliable?NO - falsely low (↑RBC turnover)
All thyrotoxicosis = DM?NO - 1-2% develop hyperglycaemia

Mnemonic: THYROTOXIC-DM

  • Thyroxine: ↑HGP, ↓insulin clearance
  • Hyperglycaemia: post-prandial prominent
  • Yes resolution: euthyroidism -> normal
  • ROID: thyroid hormone excess
  • Thyrotoxicosis: treat -> resolves
  • Overturns insulin: degradation ↑, clearance ↓
  • Xenobiotic: not a drug effect
  • Insulin: rarely needed
  • Clear: post-prandial > fasting
  • DM: transient
  • Mechanism: HGP ↑, insulin clearance ↓
  • Euthyroid -> normoglycaemia**

Topic Overview

Thyrotoxicosis diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis

▸ 21.11.2 Antipsychotic Induced Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, antipsychotic-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Corticosteroid-induced diabetes", "Calcineurin inhibitor diabetes", "Immune checkpoint inhibitor diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Antipsychotic-induced diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise antipsychotic-induced metabolic syndrome
  • [ ] Rank antipsychotics by diabetes risk
  • [ ] Apply monitoring and management protocols
  • [ ] Apply switching strategies

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence10-30% develop glucose dysregulation
Risk hierarchyOlanzapine ≈ Clozapine > Risperidone > Aripiprazole > Lurasidone > Ziprasidone
MechanismWeight gain, insulin resistance, direct beta-cell toxicity (clozapine/olanzapine)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence10-30% develop glucose dysregulation
Risk hierarchyOlanzapine ≈ Clozapine > Risperidone > Aripiprazole > Lurasidone > Ziprasidone
MechanismWeight gain, insulin resistance, direct beta-cell toxicity (clozapine/olanzapine)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
Weight gain5-15kg (clozapine/olanzapine highest)
Dyslipidaemia↑TG, ↓HDL, ↑LDL
GlucoseImpaired fasting glucose -> diabetes
Onset3-12 months (can be rapid with clozapine/olanzapine)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
Weight gain5-15kg (clozapine/olanzapine highest)
Dyslipidaemia↑TG, ↓HDL, ↑LDL
GlucoseImpaired fasting glucose -> diabetes
Onset3-12 months (can be rapid with clozapine/olanzapine)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Antipsychotic Diabetes Risk

RiskAgents
HighClozapine, Olanzapine
ModerateRisperidone, Quetiapine, Paliperidone
LowAripiprazole, Lurasidone, Ziprasidone, Amisulpride
LowestHaloperidol, Chlorpromazine (low dose)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
Weight/BMIBaseline, 4 weeks, 8 weeks, 12 weeks, quarterly
Fasting glucose/HbA1cBaseline, 12 weeks, annually
Lipid profileBaseline, 12 weeks, annually
ProlactinIf on risperidone/paliperidone

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
Weight/BMIBaseline, 4 weeks, 8 weeks, 12 weeks, quarterly
Fasting glucose/HbA1cBaseline, 12 weeks, annually
Lipid profileBaseline, 12 weeks, annually
ProlactinIf on risperidone/paliperidone

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMNo antipsychotic; obesity primary
Cushing syndromeCortisol excess; specific features
Steroid diabetesCorticosteroid use

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMNo antipsychotic; obesity primary
Cushing syndromeCortisol excess; specific features
Steroid diabetesCorticosteroid use

7. Management

Prevention & Monitoring

StrategyDetail
BaselineWeight, BMI, glucose, lipids, waist circumference
MonitoringWeight q4wk x12wk; glucose/lipids 12wk then annually
SwitchIf metabolic adverse effects; switch to lower risk agent
Dose reductionMinimum effective dose

Treatment of Established Diabetes

ApproachDetail
LifestyleDiet, exercise, weight management
Metformin1st line (weight neutral/loss; improves insulin sensitivity)
GLP-1 RA/SGLT2iWeight loss + glucose control
Switch antipsychoticHigh risk -> low risk (if psychiatric stable)

High-Risk Switching

FromTo
Clozapine/OlanzapineAripiprazole/Lurasidone/Ziprasidone
RisperidoneAripiprazole/Lurasidone

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Highest riskClozapine = Olanzapine > Risperidone > Aripiprazole/Lurasidone
MechanismWeight gain -> insulin resistance; direct beta-cell toxicity (clozapine/olanzapine)
MonitoringWeight q4wk x12wk; glucose/lipids 12wk, annually
First-line RxMetformin (weight neutral/loss); GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i if T2DM
Switch strategyHigh risk -> low risk if psychiatric stable

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Which antipsychotic has highest diabetes risk?Clozapine and Olanzapine (equal highest)
What is the mechanism?Weight gain -> insulin resistance; direct beta-cell toxicity (clozapine/olanzapine)
What monitoring is recommended?Weight q4wk x12wk; glucose/lipids 12wk then annually
First-line treatment for antipsychotic-induced diabetes?Metformin (weight neutral/loss); GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i if T2DM
When do you switch antipsychotics?Metabolic adverse effects; if psychiatric condition allows

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All antipsychotics = same risk?NO - Clozapine/Olanzapine >> Risperidone > Aripiprazole/Lurasidone/Ziprasidone
Aripiprazole = no risk?LOW risk but not zero; metabolic neutral
Weight gain = only mechanism?NO - direct beta-cell toxicity also (clozapine/olanzapine)

Mnemonic: ANTIPSYCHOTIC-DM-RISK

  • Antipsychotic: clozapine/olanzapine highest risk
  • Neuroleptic: weight gain -> insulin resistance
  • Tier 1: clozapine, olanzapine (highest)
  • Intermediate: risperidone, quetiapine, paliperidone
  • Poor metabolic: tier 1 > tier 2 > tier 3
  • Switching: high risk -> low risk if stable
  • Ype 3: aripiprazole, lurasidone, ziprasidone (low)
  • Clozapine: highest weight gain + DM risk
  • Hyperglycaemia: secondary to weight gain + direct beta-cell toxicity
  • Olanzapine = clozapine for DM risk
  • Treatment: metformin 1st line; GLP-1/SGLT2 if T2DM
  • Insulin resistance: weight gain primary driver
  • Cardio-metabolic: monitor lipids, glucose, weight
  • Monitoring: weight q4wk; glucose 12wk
  • Obesity: antipsychotic-induced
  • Tier risk: clozapine/olanzapine > risperidone > aripiprazole
  • Inert: lurasidone, ziprasidone lowest
  • Clozapine = olanzapine highest
  • Direct beta-cell toxicity: clozapine/olanzapine**

Topic Overview

Antipsychotic-induced diabetes

2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Antipsychotic Diabetes Risk
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis

7. Management
Prevention & Monitoring
Treatment of Established Diabetes
High-Risk Switching

▸ 21.11.3 Calcineurin Inhibitor Diabetes Tacrolimus Ciclosporin

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, calcineurin-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Corticosteroid-induced diabetes", "Antipsychotic-induced diabetes", "Immune checkpoint inhibitor diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Calcineurin inhibitor diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise calcineurin inhibitor-induced diabetes (post-transplant)
  • [ ] Differentiate tacrolimus vs ciclosporin risk
  • [ ] Apply monitoring and management post-transplant
  • [ ] Apply management strategies

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence10-30% post-transplant (NODAT)
TacrolimusHigher risk than ciclosporin (2-3x)
MechanismBeta-cell toxicity + insulin resistance
Risk factorsAge >40, obesity, HCV, family history, tacrolimus

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Incidence10-30% post-transplant (NODAT)
TacrolimusHigher risk than ciclosporin (2-3x)
MechanismBeta-cell toxicity + insulin resistance
Risk factorsAge >40, obesity, HCV, family history, tacrolimus

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTacrolimusCiclosporin
Diabetes riskHigher (2-3x)Lower
MechanismBeta-cell toxicity + IRMainly IR
Beta-cell toxicityYes (calcineurin in beta-cells)Less
Insulin resistanceYesYes

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTacrolimusCiclosporin
Diabetes riskHigher (2-3x)Lower
MechanismBeta-cell toxicity + IRMainly IR
Beta-cell toxicityYes (calcineurin in beta-cells)Less
Insulin resistanceYesYes

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureTacrolimusCiclosporin
NODAT incidence15-30%5-15%
MechanismBeta-cell toxicity + IRInsulin resistance
ReversibilityOften persistentMay improve with switch

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureTacrolimusCiclosporin
NODAT incidence15-30%5-15%
MechanismBeta-cell toxicity + IRInsulin resistance
ReversibilityOften persistentMay improve with switch

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
Fasting glucose/HbA1cWeekly x1mo, then monthly x3mo, then 3-monthly
Tacrolimus/ciclosporin levelPer protocol
HbA1c3-monthly (falsely low in CKD)
C-peptideLow in tacrolimus (beta-cell toxicity)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
Fasting glucose/HbA1cWeekly x1mo, then monthly x3mo, then 3-monthly
Tacrolimus/ciclosporin levelPer protocol
HbA1c3-monthly (falsely low in CKD)
C-peptideLow in tacrolimus (beta-cell toxicity)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Stress hyperglycaemiaPeri-operative; resolves
Steroid diabetesCorticosteroid use; post-prandial
Pre-existing DMKnown before transplant

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Stress hyperglycaemiaPeri-operative; resolves
Steroid diabetesCorticosteroid use; post-prandial
Pre-existing DMKnown before transplant

7. Management

Prevention

StrategyDetail
CNI choiceCiclosporin if high NODAT risk; minimise tacrolimus dose
Steroid minimisationEarly steroid withdrawal if possible
Weight managementPre-transplant weight loss
ScreeningHbA1c pre-transplant; annual post-transplant

Management of Established NODAT

ApproachDetail
CNI adjustmentReduce tacrolimus; switch to ciclosporin if high risk
MetforminIf eGFR >=30; 1st line
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RAIf eGFR permits; cardiorenal benefit
InsulinIf oral agents fail; flexible dosing
SulfonylureasAvoid (hypo risk with CNI)

CNI Dose Reduction/Switch

ScenarioAction
Tacrolimus high levelsReduce dose
Persistent NODATSwitch tacrolimus -> ciclosporin
Severe NODATConsider belatacept (CNIs-sparing)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
NODAT10-30% post-transplant; tacrolimus > ciclosporin (2-3x)
MechanismTacrolimus: beta-cell toxicity + IR; ciclosporin: mainly IR
Risk factorsTacrolimus, age>40, obesity, HCV, FH, steroids
MonitoringGlucose weekly x1mo, then monthly x3mo, then 3-monthly
ManagementMinimise tacrolimus; switch to ciclosporin; metformin/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
SwitchTacrolimus -> ciclosporin if NODAT persistent

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Which calcineurin inhibitor has higher NODAT risk?Tacrolimus (2-3x higher than ciclosporin)
What is the mechanism of tacrolimus-induced diabetes?Beta-cell toxicity (calcineurin in beta-cells) + insulin resistance
How does ciclosporin cause diabetes?Mainly insulin resistance; less beta-cell toxicity
How do you manage tacrolimus-induced NODAT?Reduce tacrolimus dose; switch to ciclosporin; start metformin/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Can NODAT be reversed by switching CNI?Ciclosporin may improve; tacrolimus beta-cell toxicity often persistent

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All NODAT = tacrolimus?Ciclosporin also causes but lower risk
NODAT = T2DM?Similar but CNI-specific mechanisms; may improve with CNI adjustment
All transplant patients get NODAT?10-30%; risk stratification essential

Mnemonic: CNI-NODAT-TACROLIMUS

  • Calcineurin: inhibitors (tacrolimus, ciclosporin)
  • NODAT: new-onset diabetes after transplant (10-30%)
  • Immunosuppression: necessary but diabetogenic
  • Tacrolimus: higher risk (2-3x ciclosporin)
  • Renal transplant: most common setting
  • Overweight: risk factor
  • Liver/heart/lung: also affected
  • Insulin resistance: ciclosporin main; tacrolimus = beta-cell tox + IR
  • Monitoring: glucose weekly x1mo, monthly x3mo
  • Units: tacrolimus levels guide dosing
  • Switch: tacrolimus -> ciclosporin if NODAT
  • Tacrolimus: beta-cell toxicity (calcineurin in beta-cells)
  • Age >40: risk factor
  • Ciclosporin: lower NODAT risk
  • Risk factors: tacrolimus, age, obesity, HCV, FH, steroids
  • Operational: minimise tacrolimus dose
  • Long-term: NODAT -> CVD risk
  • Insulin: if oral fail; flexible
  • Metformin: if eGFR>=30
  • Universal: screen all transplant
  • Steroid-free: reduces risk**

Topic Overview

Calcineurin inhibitor diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology

3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
4. Classification / Staging / Grading

5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
6. Differential Diagnosis

Prevention
Management of Established NODAT
CNI Dose Reduction/Switch

▸ 21.11.4 Chronic Pancreatitis Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, type3c, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Pancreatic cancer diabetes", "Pancreatectomy and cystic fibrosis", "Drug-induced diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Chronic pancreatitis diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Diagnose type 3c diabetes from chronic pancreatitis
  • [ ] Recognise exocrine insufficiency features
  • [ ] Apply management: pancreatic enzymes, insulin, nutritional support
  • [ ] Differentiate from T1DM, T2DM, other secondary DM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionDiabetes secondary to chronic pancreatitis with exocrine insufficiency
Incidence50% of CP develop diabetes at 20 years
MechanismPancreatic destruction -> loss of islets + exocrine tissue
AetiologyAlcohol (70%), smoking, genetic (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR), idiopathic

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionDiabetes secondary to chronic pancreatitis with exocrine insufficiency
Incidence50% of CP develop diabetes at 20 years
MechanismPancreatic destruction -> loss of islets + exocrine tissue
AetiologyAlcohol (70%), smoking, genetic (PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR), idiopathic

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureType 3cT1DMT2DM
Exocrine insufficiencyYes (steatorrhoea, weight loss)NoNo
AutoantibodiesNegativePositive (2+)Negative
C-peptideLowLow/absentNormal/high
GlucagonLow (alpha cell loss)LowNormal/high
HypoglycaemiaSevere/prolonged (↓glucagon)CommonVariable
Pancreatic imagingCalcification, atrophy, duct dilatationNormalNormal
Faecal elastase<200 mcg/g (often <100)NormalNormal

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureType 3cT1DMT2DM
Exocrine insufficiencyYes (steatorrhoea, weight loss)NoNo
AutoantibodiesNegativePositive (2+)Negative
C-peptideLowLow/absentNormal/high
GlucagonLow (alpha cell loss)LowNormal/high
HypoglycaemiaSevere/prolonged (↓glucagon)CommonVariable
Pancreatic imagingCalcification, atrophy, duct dilatationNormalNormal
Faecal elastase<200 mcg/g (often <100)NormalNormal

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

StageFeatures
EarlyRecurrent pancreatitis; normal glucose
DevelopingImpaired glucose tolerance; mild exocrine insufficiency
EstablishedDiabetes + exocrine insufficiency (faecal elastase <200)
AdvancedBrittle diabetes; severe exocrine insufficiency; malnutrition

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestType 3cT1DMT2DM
Faecal elastase<200 mcg/gNormalNormal
CT/MRI pancreasCalcification, atrophy, duct dilatationNormalNormal
AutoantibodiesNegativePositive (2+)Negative
C-peptideLowLow/absentNormal/high
GlucagonLowLowNormal/high
HbA1cOften elevatedElevatedElevated

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide, no exocrine insufficiency
T2DMObese, insulin resistance, negative Abs, normal exocrine
Pancreatic cancer DMNew-onset >50y, weight loss, imaging mass
Cystic fibrosis DMCFTR mutation; respiratory/GI symptoms

7. Management

Exocrine Replacement (Essential)

ComponentDetail
Pancreatic enzymesEnteric-coated microspheres; 25,000-50,000 U lipase/meal; with snacks
Dosing50,000 U lipase main meal; 25,000 U snack; titrate to symptoms
Acid suppressionPPI (improves enzyme efficacy)
MonitoringStool frequency/consistency; weight; fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)

Diabetes Management

AspectDetail
InsulinPreferred (flexible, physiologic); high doses often needed due to ↓glucagon
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RACaution (weight loss, GI effects); consider if CVD/CKD
SulfonylureasAvoid (hypo risk with erratic absorption)
MetforminIf eGFR allows; weight neutral
GlucagonIneffective (alpha cell loss); use IV dextrose for severe hypo

Brittle Diabetes Management

StrategyDetail
CSIIPreferred for brittle control; predictive low suspend
CGMEssential; predictive alerts
HypoglycaemiaHigh risk (↓glucagon, ↓counter-regulation); glucagon ineffective
GlucagonIneffective (alpha cell loss); use IV dextrose

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Faecal elastase<200 mcg/g = exocrine insufficiency (diagnostic)
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptideLow
GlucagonLow (alpha cell loss)
HypoglycaemiaSevere; glucagon ineffective; IV dextrose needed
ManagementPancreatic enzymes (enteric-coated) + insulin; SGLT2i/GLP-1 if CVD/CKD
ImagingCT: calcification, atrophy, duct dilatation

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the diagnostic test for exocrine insufficiency in type 3c diabetes?Faecal elastase <200 mcg/g
How does type 3c diabetes differ from T1DM?Negative autoantibodies, exocrine insufficiency, low glucagon
What is the management of diabetes in chronic pancreatitis?Pancreatic enzyme replacement + insulin; avoid sulfonylureas
Why is glucagon ineffective in type 3c diabetes?Alpha cell loss -> no glucagon reserve
What is the preferred insulin regimen?Basal-bolus or CSII; flexible for erratic absorption

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Type 3c = T2DM?NO - exocrine insufficiency, negative Abs, low glucagon
Metformin in type 3c?If eGFR allows; but insulin preferred for flexibility
Glucagon for hypo?INEFFECTIVE - alpha cell loss; use IV dextrose

Mnemonic: TYPE3C-PANCREAS

  • Type 3c: diabetes of exocrine pancreas
  • Yes exocrine insufficiency (faecal elastase <200)
  • Pancreatic enzymes: enteric-coated, 25-50k U/meal
  • Exocrine: steatorrhoea, weight loss, fat-soluble vit deficiency
  • 3c: secondary to pancreatitis
  • Calcification on CT
  • Pancreatic cancer: new DM >50y (different)
  • Alpha cell loss: low glucagon
  • Negative autoantibodies
  • Cystic fibrosis: separate (CFTR)
  • Response to insulin: high doses needed
  • Exocrine replacement: essential
  • Alcohol/smoking: aetiology
  • Steatorrhoea: hallmark**

Topic Overview

Chronic pancreatitis diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Exocrine Replacement (Essential)
Diabetes Management
Brittle Diabetes Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.11.5 Corticosteroid Induced Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, steroid-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Antipsychotic-induced diabetes", "Calcineurin inhibitor diabetes", "Phaeochromocytoma diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Corticosteroid-induced diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise steroid-induced hyperglycaemia pattern
  • [ ] Apply management: NPH insulin, basal-bolus, sliding scale
  • [ ] Adjust for steroid tapering
  • [ ] Monitor for complications

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionHyperglycaemia induced by glucocorticoid therapy
Incidence20-50% on steroids develop hyperglycaemia
MechanismCortisol -> ↑HGP, ↓insulin sensitivity, ↓β-cell function
PatternPost-prandial hyperglycaemia prominent (steroids given AM)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureSteroid DMT2DM
PatternPost-prandial >> fastingFasting + post-prandial
TimingPeaks 4-8h post-dose (AM steroids)Throughout day
Fasting glucoseOften normalElevated
Dose relationshipDirect (higher dose = worse hyperglycaemia)Chronic

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureSteroid DMT2DM
PatternPost-prandial >> fastingFasting + post-prandial
TimingPeaks 4-8h post-dose (AM steroids)Throughout day
Fasting glucoseOften normalElevated
Dose relationshipDirect (higher dose = worse hyperglycaemia)Chronic

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Steroid TypeRisk
High-dose IVVery high (pulse methylprednisolone)
Oral prednisolone >20mgHigh
Oral prednisolone 10-20mgModerate
Inhaled/topicalLow (systemic absorption variable)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Steroid TypeRisk
High-dose IVVery high (pulse methylprednisolone)
Oral prednisolone >20mgHigh
Oral prednisolone 10-20mgModerate
Inhaled/topicalLow (systemic absorption variable)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestPattern
SMBGPre-meal + 2h post-meal + bedtime
HbA1cFalsely low if new/rapid onset; rising if chronic
C-peptideNormal/high (insulin resistance)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestPattern
SMBGPre-meal + 2h post-meal + bedtime
HbA1cFalsely low if new/rapid onset; rising if chronic
C-peptideNormal/high (insulin resistance)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMChronic; fasting + post-prandial; no steroid history
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness; resolves; steroids not given
Cushing syndromeEndogenous; features; dexamethasone suppression

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMChronic; fasting + post-prandial; no steroid history
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness; resolves; steroids not given
Cushing syndromeEndogenous; features; dexamethasone suppression

7. Management

Insulin Regimens

Steroid DoseInsulin Strategy
Prednisolone >20mgBasal-bolus (NPH BD or glargine OD + rapid analogue pre-meal)
Prednisolone 10-20mgNPH insulin BD (0.2-0.3 U/kg/day)
Prednisolone <10mgMay not need insulin; monitor
IV methylprednisolone pulseIV insulin infusion (sliding scale)

NPH Insulin Protocol (Classic)

DoseTiming
2/3 total doseMorning (covers AM steroid peak)
1/3 total doseEvening

Steroid Tapering

PhaseInsulin Adjustment
WeaningReduce insulin proportionally to steroid reduction
Off steroidsStop insulin (unless pre-existing DM)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
PatternPost-prandial >> fasting (peaks 4-8h post-AM steroid)
NPH insulin2/3 AM, 1/3 PM (covers steroid peak)
Sliding scaleNOT recommended as sole therapy
TaperReduce insulin proportionally to steroid taper
HbA1cFalsely low if recent onset

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the characteristic glucose pattern in steroid diabetes?Post-prandial hyperglycaemia prominent; fasting often normal; peaks 4-8h post-AM steroid
Why is NPH insulin preferred?Peak action 4-8h matches steroid peak (AM dose); 2/3 AM, 1/3 PM
Is sliding scale insulin sufficient?NO - reactive, erratic, increases hypoglycaemia risk
How do you manage tapering steroids?Reduce insulin proportionally to steroid dose reduction; stop when steroids stopped
Why is fasting glucose often normal?Steroids given AM -> post-prandial peak; overnight clearance normal

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Steroid DM = permanent?Usually resolves when steroids stopped (unless pre-existing DM)
Sliding scale sufficient?NO - reactive, increases hypo risk; use basal/basal-bolus
All steroids = same risk?NO - IV pulse > oral > inhaled/topical; dose-dependent

Mnemonic: STEROID-HYPERGLYCAEMIA

  • Steroid: post-prandial hyperglycaemia
  • Timing: peaks 4-8h post-AM dose
  • Exogenous: cortisol excess -> insulin resistance
  • Resistant: insulin resistance
  • Oral prednisolone >20mg: high risk
  • Insulin: NPH 2/3 AM, 1/3 PM (covers peak)
  • Dose-dependent: higher dose = worse DM
  • Hyperglycaemia: post-prandial >> fasting
  • Yes taper: reduce insulin with steroid taper
  • Post-prandial: hallmark feature
  • Exogenous: cortisol excess
  • Resolves: off steroids -> DM resolves (usually)
  • Glucagon: not relevant
  • Low fasting: often normal
  • Yes NPH: 2/3 AM, 1/3 PM
  • Cushing: endogenous -> similar pattern
  • Alternating: not sliding scale
  • Exposure: dose/duration dependent
  • Alpha: not relevant
  • Monitor: pre-meal + 2h post-meal
  • Insulin: adjust with steroid dose
  • Adjust: proactively, not reactively
  • Methylpred IV pulse: IV insulin infusion
  • Inflammation: treated
  • Chronic: if long-term steroids -> consider pre-existing DM
  • Adrenal: suppression if long-term**

3. Clinical Features / Presentation
3. Clinical Features / Presentation

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
6. Differential Diagnosis

NPH Insulin Protocol (Classic)
Steroid Tapering

▸ 21.11.6 Cushing Syndrome Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, cushing-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Acromegaly diabetes", "Thyrotoxicosis diabetes", "Phaeochromocytoma diabetes", "Glucagonoma diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Cushing syndrome diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise Cushing syndrome as cause of secondary diabetes
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic tests (dexamethasone suppression, midnight cortisol)
  • [ ] Manage diabetes in Cushing syndrome
  • [ ] Apply definitive treatment (surgery, medical therapy)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionHypercortisolism causing insulin resistance and diabetes
Prevalence20-50% of Cushing syndrome develop diabetes
MechanismCortisol -> ↑HGP, ↓GLUT4, ↑lipolysis -> insulin resistance
TypeUsually T2DM-like (insulin resistance); can unmask latent DM

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeaturePresentation
DiabetesOften mild-moderate; post-prandial hyperglycaemia prominent
Cushing featuresMoon face, central obesity, striae, bruising, hypertension, proximal myopathy
HypokalaemiaCommon (cortisol mineralocorticoid effect)
MetabolicCentral obesity, dyslipidaemia, hypertension

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Diabetes in Cushing Syndrome

FeatureDetail
TypeInsulin resistance (T2DM-like)
SeverityCorrelates with cortisol levels
ReversibilityOften improves/resolves with eucortisolism

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Diabetes in Cushing Syndrome

FeatureDetail
TypeInsulin resistance (T2DM-like)
SeverityCorrelates with cortisol levels
ReversibilityOften improves/resolves with eucortisolism

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRoleInterpretation
1mg overnight dexamethasone suppressionScreeningCortisol >50 nmol/L = positive
48h low-dose dexamethasone (2mg/day)ConfirmationDay 3 9am cortisol >50 nmol/L = positive
Late-night salivary cortisolScreening>2x upper limit
24h urinary free cortisolConfirmation>3x upper limit (x2)
ACTHDifferentiateLow = adrenal; High = pituitary/ectopic
Imaging (MRI pituitary / CT adrenal)LocalisationMicroadenoma vs adenoma vs ectopic

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRoleInterpretation
1mg overnight dexamethasone suppressionScreeningCortisol >50 nmol/L = positive
48h low-dose dexamethasone (2mg/day)ConfirmationDay 3 9am cortisol >50 nmol/L = positive
Late-night salivary cortisolScreening>2x upper limit
24h urinary free cortisolConfirmation>3x upper limit (x2)
ACTHDifferentiateLow = adrenal; High = pituitary/ectopic
Imaging (MRI pituitary / CT adrenal)LocalisationMicroadenoma vs adenoma vs ectopic

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Pseudo-CushingAlcoholism, depression, obesity; mild hypercortisolism; dexamethasone suppression usually normal
Metabolic syndromeObesity, hypertension, dyslipidaemia; normal cortisol dynamics
Pseudo-Cushing (alcohol)Resolves with abstinence; history

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Pseudo-CushingAlcoholism, depression, obesity; mild hypercortisolism; dexamethasone suppression usually normal
Metabolic syndromeObesity, hypertension, dyslipidaemia; normal cortisol dynamics
Pseudo-Cushing (alcohol)Resolves with abstinence; history

7. Management

Diabetes Management

ApproachDetail
InsulinIf severe hyperglycaemia; flexible dosing
MetforminFirst-line oral; improves insulin sensitivity
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RAIf CVD/CKD; weight loss benefit
SulfonylureasAvoid (weight gain, hypoglycaemia)
SteroidsNot applicable (cortisol excess primary)

Definitive Treatment (Curative)

AetiologyTreatment
Pituitary adenomaTranssphenoidal surgery (first-line)
Adrenal adenomaAdrenalectomy (laparoscopic)
Ectopic ACTHTumour resection; bilateral adrenalectomy if unresectable
Medical (if surgery contraindicated)Ketoconazole, metyrapone, mifepristone, pasireotide

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismCortisol -> ↑HGP, ↓GLUT4, ↑lipolysis -> insulin resistance
Diabetes typeT2DM-like (insulin resistance)
Diagnosis1mg DST >50; UFC >3x ULN; midnight salivary cortisol
Treatment of DMMetformin 1st line; insulin if severe; improves with cure
Definitive cureSurgery (pituitary/adrenal/ectopic) -> often resolves DM

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of diabetes in Cushing syndrome?Cortisol -> ↑HGP, ↓GLUT4, ↑lipolysis -> insulin resistance
What is the screening test for Cushing syndrome?1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol >50 nmol/L = positive)
Does diabetes resolve after Cushing cure?Often improves/resolves with eucortisolism
What is pseudo-Cushing syndrome?Alcoholism, depression, obesity; mimics Cushing; DST usually normal
How do you treat diabetes in Cushing?Metformin 1st line; insulin if severe; definitive = surgery

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Cushing diabetes = T1DM?NO - insulin resistance (T2DM-like); C-peptide normal/high
All Cushing = diabetes?NO - 20-50% develop DM; depends on cortisol severity/duration
Post-prandial glucoseOften disproportionately elevated (cortisol post-prandial peak)

Mnemonic: CUSHING-DIABETES

  • Cushing: cortisol excess -> insulin resistance
  • Uncoupling: GLUT4 down -> glucose uptake down
  • Steroid: cortisol -> HGP up, lipolysis up
  • Hyperglycaemia: post-prandial prominent
  • Insulin resistance: T2DM-like
  • Negative feedback: lost (ACTH not suppressed)
  • Glucose test: DST 1mg >50 nmol/L
  • Dexamethasone suppression: diagnostic
  • Improves with cure: surgery -> eucortisolism
  • ACTH: pituitary vs adrenal vs ectopic
  • Bilateral adrenalectomy: last resort
  • Ectopic ACTH: lung, thymus tumours
  • Transsphenoidal: pituitary surgery
  • Exogenous steroids: iatrogenic Cushing -> similar DM**

Cushing syndrome diabetes

2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Diabetes in Cushing Syndrome
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Diabetes in Cushing Syndrome
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Diabetes Management
Definitive Treatment (Curative)
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
