▸ 21.11.9 Glucagonoma Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, glucagonoma-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Cushing syndrome diabetes", "Acromegaly diabetes", "Thyrotoxicosis diabetes", "Phaeochromocytoma diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Glucagonoma diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise glucagonoma as rare cause of secondary diabetes
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (plasma glucagon, imaging)
  • [ ] Recognise NME rash and associated features
  • [ ] Apply management (surgery, somatostatin analogues, insulin)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionAlpha-cell tumour secreting glucagon causing diabetes
Incidence1:20-50 million; ~1% of pancreatic NETs
MechanismGlucagon -> ↑HGP, ↓insulin effect -> diabetes
Median age50-60 years

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionAlpha-cell tumour secreting glucagon causing diabetes
Incidence1:20-50 million; ~1% of pancreatic NETs
MechanismGlucagon -> ↑HGP, ↓insulin effect -> diabetes
Median age50-60 years

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Classic Triad (NME)

FeatureDetail
Necrolytic Migratory ErythemaRed, blistering, migratory rash (perineum, groin, legs)
Diabetes MellitusMild-moderate; 90% have DM
Weight lossSignificant (catabolic)
DVT/Thrombosis10-15% (hypercoagulable)
AnaemiaNormochromic/normocytic
Stomatitis/glossitisAngular cheilitis, glossitis

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
NME rashRed, blistering, migratory (perineum, groin, legs)
Diabetes90% have DM
Weight lossSignificant (catabolic)
DVT10-15%
AnaemiaNormocytic
StomatitisAngular cheilitis, glossitis

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glucagonoma Syndrome Features

FeaturePrevalence
Diabetes90%
NME rash70%
Weight loss90%
DVT10-15%
Anaemia60%
HypoaminoacidaemiaYes

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Plasma glucagon>500-1000 pg/mL (normal <50-100)
CT/MRI pancreasMass in tail/body (60%)
Somatostatin receptor imagingOctreoscan (SSTR2+)
Amino acidsLow (hypoaminoacidaemia)
Zn/Cu/SeLow (due to rash)
OctreoscanSSTR2 positive

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
NME-like rashZn deficiency, biotin deficiency, pellagra
PhaeochromocytomaCatecholamine excess; HTN spells
PhaeochromocytomaParoxysmal HTN; metanephrines high
T2DM + rashNo glucagon excess; rash different

7. Management

Surgical (Curative if localised)

StageTreatment
LocalisedDistal pancreatectomy (+splenectomy if tail)
MetastaticDebulking; somatostatin analogues; systemic therapy

Medical Management

AgentRole
Somatostatin analoguesOctreotide/lanreotide -> ↓glucagon; improves rash, weight loss, DM
InsulinIf DM uncontrolled
MetforminIf insulin resistance component
AnticoagulationProphylactic (DVT risk)
Zinc supplementationImproves rash (hypoaminoacidaemia)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
TriadDM + NME rash + weight loss
Glucagon>500-1000 pg/mL (normal <50-100)
RashNME: migratory, blistering, perineum/groin/legs
Weight lossCatabolic (glucagon -> ↑catabolism)
DVT10-15% (hypercoagulable)
TreatmentSurgery (curative); somatostatin analogues ↓glucagon

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the classic triad of glucagonoma?DM + NME rash + weight loss
What is NME rash?Necrolytic Migratory Erythema: migratory, blistering, perineum/groin/legs
What is the diagnostic glucagon level?>500-1000 pg/mL (normal <50-100)
What is the treatment?Surgery (curative if localised); somatostatin analogues ↓glucagon
What amino acid abnormality?Hypoaminoacidaemia (glucagon -> ↑amino acid uptake in liver)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Glucagonoma = common?NO - extremely rare (1:20-50M)
Rash = diabetic dermopathy?NO - NME is different (migratory, blistering, perineum)
All DM + rash = glucagonoma?NO - rule out Zn/Pellagra/biotin deficiency

Mnemonic: GLUCAGONOMA-DIDMOAD

  • Glucagonoma: alpha-cell tumour
  • Level: glucagon >500-1000 pg/mL
  • UNME rash: Necrolytic Migratory Erythema (70%)
  • Catabolic: weight loss 90%
  • Amino acids: hypoaminoacidaemia
  • Glucagon: >500 pg/mL
  • Octreoscan: SSTR2+
  • NME: migratory, blistering, perineum
  • Octreotide: ↓glucagon, improves rash/DM
  • Metastases: 50% at dx
  • Acute DVT: 10-15%
  • Diabetes: 90%
  • Insulin: if needed
  • Operation: distal pancreatectomy (+splenectomy)
  • Autoimmune: no
  • Diagnosis: glucagon >500 + NME + DM**

Topic Overview

Glucagonoma diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
Classic Triad (NME)
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Glucagonoma Syndrome Features
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Surgical (Curative if localised)
Medical Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics

▸ 21.12.12 Diabetic Ketoacidosis Dka At Diagnosis

id: t1dm-dka-presentation

title: "Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) at Diagnosis"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • dka
  • clinical-presentation
  • new-onset
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Clinical presentation"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

DKA at diagnosis = first presentation of T1DM with diabetic ketoacidosis.

Prevalence: 25–40% of new T1DM in children; 15–25% in adults.

FCPS/MRCP relevance: High-yield acute presentation; exam questions on diagnostic criteria, severity grading, initial management, cerebral oedema prevention.

2. Diagnostic Criteria (JBS / ISPAD / NICE)

ParameterMildModerateSevere
Venous pH7.25–7.307.10–7.24<7.10
Bicarbonate (mmol/L)15–1810–14<10
Blood Ketones (β-OHB, mmol/L)>3.0>3.0>3.0
Glucose (mmol/L)>11>11>11
Anion Gap>12>16>20
ClinicalAlertDrowsy/confusedComa (GCS <12)

Diagnosis requires ALL three: Hyperglycaemia (>11 mmol/L) + Ketonaemia (>3 mmol/L) + Acidosis (pH <7.3 / bicarb <15).

3. Typical Presentation (New-Onset T1DM)

SymptomDurationPathophysiology
Polyuria / Polydipsia1–4 weeksOsmotic diuresis from hyperglycaemia
Weight loss2–8 weeksCatabolism (lipolysis, proteolysis) + glycosuria calories
Fatigue / LethargyDays–weeksInsulin deficiency → impaired glucose utilization
Abdominal painAcute (hours–days)Ketonaemia, gastric atony, dehydration
VomitingAcuteKetonaemia, gastric atony
Kussmaul breathingAcuteCompensatory respiratory alkalosis for metabolic acidosis
Acetone breathAcuteExhaled acetone from ketogenesis
Dehydration signsAcuteOsmotic diuresis + vomiting + poor intake
Altered consciousnessLate/severeCerebral dehydration, acidosis, hyperosmolality

Red flags for cerebral oedema (mainly children): Headache, bradycardia, hypertension, deteriorating GCS, pupillary asymmetry, respiratory arrest.

4. Differential Diagnosis at Presentation

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HHSGlucose >30 mmol/L, pH >7.3, bicarb >18, minimal ketones, older age, T2DM
Starvation ketosisGlucose normal/low, mild ketones (1–3 mmol/L), pH normal, no acidosis
Alcoholic ketoacidosisGlucose normal/low, high anion gap, history alcohol binge + malnutrition
SepsisFever, leukocytosis, lactate ↑, glucose variable
Acute abdomen (appendicitis, pancreatitis)Localized pain, no ketonuria/ketonaemia (or mild), glucose normal
Type 2 DM with stress hyperglycaemiaNo significant weight loss, older, overweight, ketones negative/trace

5. Initial Assessment (First 60 Minutes)

AssessmentAction
ABCDEAirway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability (GCS), Exposure
Vital signsHR, BP, RR, Temp, SpO2, GCS q15–30 min
Bedside glucoseCapillary + venous confirmation
Blood gas (venous/arterial)pH, pCO2, bicarb, lactate, electrolytes (K+, Na+, Cl-)
Blood ketones (β-OHB)Quantitative (preferred over urine)
Renal functionUrea, creatinine, eGFR
FBC, CRPInfection screen
ECGBaseline; monitor for K+ changes
Pregnancy testAll females of childbearing age

Calculate: Corrected Na+ = Measured Na+ + 0.016 × (Glucose – 5.5) — expect rise as glucose falls.

6. Initial Management Protocol (First 1–2 Hours)

StepActionDetails
1. Fluid Resuscitation0.9% NaCl10–20 mL/kg bolus (child: 10 mL/kg; adult: 1L) over 1h; reassess
2. Insulin InfusionFixed-rate IV insulin (FRIII)0.05–0.1 units/kg/h (child: 0.1 u/kg/h; adult: 0.05–0.1 u/kg/h)
3. PotassiumAdd to fluidsIf K+ <3.5 → hold insulin, replace K+ first; K+ 3.5–5.5 → 40 mmol/L in fluids; K+ >5.5 → no K+
4. BicarbonateRarely indicatedOnly if pH <6.9 or life-threatening hyperkalaemia; 1–2 mmol/kg over 1h
5. Monitoringq1–2hGlucose, ketones, VBG, electrolytes, fluid balance, GCS

Insulin: DO NOT BOLUS — start infusion only. Bolus increases cerebral oedema risk.

7. Cerebral Oedema — Prevention & Recognition

Risk FactorPrevention
Age <5 yearsStrict protocol adherence
Severe acidosis (pH <7.1)Avoid rapid glucose fall (>5 mmol/L/h)
High urea / severe dehydrationGradual rehydration (48h deficit replacement)
Bicarbonate useAvoid unless pH <6.9
Early insulin bolusNever bolus — infusion only

Treatment if suspected: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 20 min OR Hypertonic saline 3% 5 mL/kg over 30 min; intubation, hyperventilation, ICU transfer.

8. Transition to Subcutaneous Insulin

Criteria: Ketones <0.6 mmol/L, pH >7.3, eating/drinking tolerated, alert.

StepAction
1. Start basal insulin (glargine/degludec)Overlap with IV insulin 1–2h before stopping infusion
2. Start bolus insulin with mealsUse ICR/ISF estimates (TDD 0.5 u/kg)
3. Stop IV insulin30–60 min after first basal dose
4. EducationDAFNE referral, sick day rules, hypoglycaemia management

9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • DKA at diagnosis: 25–40% children, 15–25% adults — "new T1DM until proven otherwise"
  • Diagnostic triad: Hyperglycaemia + Ketonaemia + Acidosis
  • Venous pH sufficient — no need arterial unless respiratory compromise
  • β-OHB >3 mmol/L — diagnostic; urine ketones lag, semi-quantitative
  • FRIII rate: 0.1 u/kg/h (children), 0.05–0.1 u/kg/h (adults) — NO BOLUS
  • Fluid: 0.9% NaCl initially; switch to 0.45% NaCl + 5% dextrose when glucose <14 mmol/L
  • Potassium: ALWAYS check before insulin; replace if <3.5; add to fluids at 40 mmol/L
  • Cerebral oedema: Mainly children <5y; headache + bradycardia + hypertension = emergency
  • Transition: Basal insulin start → overlap 1–2h → stop IV insulin
  • Precipitants: Infection (UTI, pneumonia), missed insulin, new diagnosis, MI, stroke, drugs (SGLT2i, steroids)

10. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) / NG18 (DKA)
  2. JBDS 07 (2021) — DKA Management Adults
  3. ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines 2022 — DKA in Children
  4. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 14
  5. Dhatariya K et al. Diabet Med 2020 (JBDS guidelines)

11. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Clinical presentation/Classic symptomatic presentation]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Clinical presentation/LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults)]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) management protocol]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/DKA diagnosis criteria]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/DKA severity grading]]

3. Typical Presentation (New-Onset T1DM)

▸ 21.12.15 Flash Glucose Monitoring

id: t1dm-flash-glucose

title: "Flash Glucose Monitoring (isCGM / FreeStyle Libre)"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • glucose-monitoring
  • freestyle-libre
  • iscgm
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Glucose monitoring"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

Flash Glucose Monitoring (isCGM) = Intermittently scanned Continuous Glucose Monitoring. Sensor worn for 14 days; user scans with reader/phone to get glucose value + 8h trend graph.

Device: FreeStyle Libre (Libre 2, Libre 3).

FCPS/MRCP relevance: NICE NG17 recommends as option for T1DM; exam questions on technology, indications, limitations vs rtCGM.

2. Sensor Specifications

FeatureLibre 1Libre 2Libre 3
Wear duration14 days14 days14 days
Scan methodReader/Phone NFCReader/Phone NFC + Bluetooth alertsPhone only (Bluetooth)
AlarmsNoneOptional hypo/hyper alertsReal-time alerts
SizeStandardStandardWorld's smallest (2 pennies)
Warm-up1 hour1 hour1 hour
CalibrationFactory (none)Factory (none)Factory (none)
Accuracy (MARD)~11.4%~9.2%~7.9%

3. Indications (NICE NG17 / NHS England)

  • All T1DM adults — offer rtCGM first; isCGM if rtCGM not appropriate/preferred
  • T2DM on insulin (multiple daily injection) — if recurrent/severe hypoglycaemia, impaired awareness, or need for intensive monitoring
  • Pregnancy with T1DM — rtCGM preferred; isCGM if rtCGM unavailable
  • Children/YP with T1DM — rtCGM first line

NHS England criteria: HbA1c ≥58 mmol/mol (7.5%) OR disabling hypoglycaemia OR impaired awareness OR pregnancy OR need for intensive insulin management.

4. Key Metrics & Reports

MetricDefinitionTarget (T1DM)
Time in Range (TIR)3.9–10 mmol/L>70% (>16.8h/day)
Time Below Range (TBR)<3.9 mmol/L<4% (<1h/day)
Time Below Range L2<3.0 mmol/L<1% (<15 min/day)
Time Above Range (TAR)>10 mmol/L<25% (<6h/day)
Time Above Range L2>13.9 mmol/L<5% (<1.2h/day)
Glucose Variability (CV)SD/Mean × 100<36%
Glucose Management Indicator (GMI)Estimated HbA1c from CGMCorrelates with lab HbA1c
Daily ScansUser-initiated scans>8 scans/day (LibreLink)

LibreView reports: Ambulatory Glucose Profile (AGP), Daily Patterns, Time in Range, Sensor Usage, Glucose Variability.

5. isCGM vs rtCGM — Exam Differentiation

FeatureisCGM (Flash)rtCGM (Dexcom G6/G7, Guardian)
Data transmissionUser-initiated scanAutomatic (Bluetooth)
Real-time alarmsLibre 2/3 onlyYes (all)
Predictive alertsNoYes
CalibrationNoneG6: none; G7: none; Guardian: 2-4×/day
Sensor life14 daysG6: 10d; G7: 10d; Guardian: 7d
Warm-up1 hourG6: 2h; G7: 30m; Guardian: 2h
Acetaminophen interferenceNoG6: yes (falsely high); G7: minimal
Cost (NHS)LowerHigher

6. Limitations & Artefacts

IssueCauseManagement
Compression artefactLying on sensor → falsely lowReposition; confirm with SMBG if symptomatic
First 24h inaccuracySensor equilibrationUse SMBG for calibration decisions
Last 24h driftSensor degradationExpect wider variation
Temperature extremesSauna/hot tub/cold exposureAvoid; sensor range 10–45°C
MRI/CT/X-raySensor damageRemove before imaging
Ascorbic acid (high dose)Falsely elevatesAvoid megadose vit C

7. Clinical Application

  • Pattern review: Weekly AGP review → basal/bolus adjustments
  • Hypoglycaemia prevention: Trend arrows (↓, ↓↓) prompt pre-emptive carbs
  • Post-prandial review: 2h scan after meals → bolus adjustment
  • Exercise: Pre-exercise scan + trend → carb intake decision
  • Sick days: Frequent scans + ketone check if >13.9 mmol/L

Libre 2/3 alarms: Set hypo alert at 3.9 mmol/L (default); customizable.

8. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • MARD Libre 3: ~7.9% (best in class for isCGM)
  • Warm-up: 1 hour (vs 30 min–2h for rtCGM)
  • No calibration needed — factory calibrated
  • Compression lows: Classic trap — "patient wakes at 3am with 2.5 mmol/L on Libre but feels fine" → confirm with SMBG
  • NICE NG17: rtCGM first line for T1DM; isCGM if patient prefers or rtCGM contraindicated
  • Driving: Scan before driving (no alarm = must scan manually on Libre 1; Libre 2/3 have alarms)
  • Pregnancy: rtCGM preferred; isCGM acceptable if rtCGM not available

9. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) Type 1 diabetes in adults
  2. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 7
  3. FreeStyle Libre 2/3 IFU & clinical data
  4. Haak T et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2017 (IMPACT trial)
  5. NHS England Flash Glucose Monitoring National Arrangements

10. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Capillary blood glucose (SMBG)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Time in range (TIR) metrics]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Insulin pump therapy (CSII)]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/Hypoglycaemia classification (Level 1/2/3)]]

Topic Overview

2. Sensor Specifications
3. Indications (NICE NG17 / NHS England)
4. Key Metrics & Reports
5. isCGM vs rtCGM — Exam Differentiation
6. Limitations & Artefacts
7. Clinical Application
8. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls
9. Key References
10. Local Navigation
▸ 21.12.16 Gad65 Autoantibodies

id: t1dm-gad65-autoantibodies

title: "GAD65 Autoantibodies (Glutamic Acid Decarboxylase)"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • autoantibodies
  • gad65
  • lada
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Autoantibody testing"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

GAD65 autoantibodies = antibodies against glutamic acid decarboxylase 65 kDa isoform (GAD2).

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Most sensitive single autoantibody for T1DM; primary marker for LADA; exam questions on sensitivity, LADA diagnosis, neurological associations (stiff person syndrome).

2. Antigen Characteristics

PropertyDetail
Full nameGlutamic Acid Decarboxylase 65 (GAD2 / GAD65)
FunctionCatalyzes GABA synthesis from glutamate (neurotransmitter)
LocationPancreatic β-cells, brain (GABAergic neurons), adrenal medulla
Molecular weight65 kDa (vs GAD67 = 67 kDa, neuronal)
EpitopeConformational (middle + C-terminal domains)

3. Clinical Performance

MetricValueNotes
Sensitivity (new T1DM)70–80%Highest single Ab sensitivity
Sensitivity (LADA)70–80%Primary LADA marker
Specificity95–98%High; low false positive rate
Age distributionAll agesUnlike IA-2/IAA — not age-dependent
PersistenceYears to decadesOften persists lifelong
Prevalence in healthy1–2%Low; increases with age

4. Comparison with Other Autoantibodies

FeatureGAD65IA-2IAAZnT8
Sensitivity (T1DM)70–80%50–70%40–60% (kids)60–80%
Sensitivity (LADA)70–80%20–30%20–30%40–50%
Specificity95–98%>98%90–95%95–98%
Age preferenceAll agesChildrenYoung childrenAll ages
PersistenceLifelongDeclinesDeclinesPersists
Stiff person syndromeHigh titreNegativeNegativeNegative

5. LADA Diagnosis — GAD65 is Key

CriterionRole of GAD65
AgeTypically >30 years (often 30–50)
PresentationNon-ketotic, non-insulin requiring initially
AutoantibodiesGAD65 positive in 70–80%; IA-2/IAA/ZnT8 often negative
ProgressionInsulin requirement within 6 months to 6 years
HLA associationDR3-DQ2 / DR4-DQ8 (similar to T1DM)

NICE/ADA: Test GAD65 in all adults with new diabetes not typical for T2DM (lean, <50y, personal/family autoimmunity).

6. Neurological Associations

ConditionGAD65 TitreClinical Link
Stiff Person Syndrome (SPS)Very high (>100× diabetes titre)Anti-GAD65 inhibits GABA synthesis → hyperexcitability
Cerebellar ataxiaHighGABAergic cerebellar dysfunction
Limbic encephalitisHighOften paraneoplastic (thymoma, SCLC)
EpilepsyElevatedGABAergic inhibition loss

Exam pearl: High-titre GAD65 + neurological symptoms → think SPS / paraneoplastic — screen for thymoma, SCLC.

7. Assay Methodology

MethodPrincipleNotes
Radiobinding Assay (RBA)³⁵S-methionine labelled GAD65 + precipitationGold standard; conformational epitopes preserved
ELISARecombinant human GAD65 (baculovirus/HEK293) coated plateHigh throughput; may miss conformational epitopes
Luciferase Immunoprecipitation (LIPS)Luciferase-GAD65 fusion + luminescenceHigh sensitivity; wide range

Standardization: WHO International Standard (NIBSC 97/550); expressed in U/mL or WHO units/mL.

8. Interpretation Algorithm

New diabetes diagnosis
    ↓
GAD65 POSITIVE
    ├─ + IA-2 / ZnT8 / IAA → **T1DM confirmed** (multiple Abs)
    ├─ Adult, lean, non-ketotic, slow progression → **LADA** (GAD65 alone often sufficient)
    ├─ High titre + neuro symptoms → **Stiff Person Syndrome / Paraneoplastic** → screen malignancy
    └─ Elderly, overweight, typical T2DM → **False positive?** (1–2% healthy elderly); repeat + clinical correlation

GAD65 NEGATIVE
    ├─ IA-2/ZnT8/IAA positive → **T1DM still possible** (GAD65 neg in 20–30% T1DM)
    └─ All Abs negative → T2DM, monogenic, secondary diabetes

9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • GAD65 = most sensitive single Ab for T1DM (70–80%)
  • GAD65 = primary marker for LADA (70–80% positive; others often negative)
  • Persists lifelong — unlike IA-2/IAA which decline
  • All ages — not age-restricted like IA-2/IAA
  • High titre + neuro symptoms = Stiff Person Syndrome (not just diabetes)
  • Paraneoplastic: Thymoma, small cell lung cancer — screen if neuro signs
  • Not insulin-dependent — can test anytime (unlike IAA)
  • Assay: RBA gold standard; ELISA routine; conformational epitopes critical
  • False positive in elderly (1–2%) — always correlate clinically

10. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) — Type 1 diabetes diagnosis
  2. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 2 (Classification)
  3. Bonifacio E et al. Diabetes 2010 — Harmonization of autoantibody assays
  4. Hawa MI et al. Diabetologia 2013 — LADA definition (Immunology of Diabetes Society)
  5. McKeon A et al. Lancet Neurol 2013 — GAD65 neurological syndromes

11. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/IA-2 autoantibodies]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/Insulin autoantibodies (IAA)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/Zinc transporter 8 (ZnT8) autoantibodies]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Clinical presentation/LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults)]]
  • [[Monogenic and Atypical Diabetes/Neonatal diabetes]]

Topic Overview

2. Antigen Characteristics
3. Clinical Performance
4. Comparison with Other Autoantibodies
5. LADA Diagnosis — GAD65 is Key
6. Neurological Associations
7. Assay Methodology
8. Interpretation Algorithm
9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls
10. Key References
11. Local Navigation

▸ 21.12.20 Ia 2 Autoantibodies

id: t1dm-ia2-autoantibodies

title: "IA-2 Autoantibodies (Tyrosine Phosphatase)"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • autoantibodies
  • ia2
  • diagnosis
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Autoantibody testing"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

IA-2 autoantibodies = antibodies against insulinoma-associated antigen-2 (tyrosine phosphatase-like protein, ICA512).

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Part of T1DM autoantibody panel; exam questions on sensitivity, specificity, predictive value, distinction from GAD65.

2. Antigen Characteristics

PropertyDetail
Full nameInsulinoma-Associated Antigen 2 (IA-2) / ICA512 / PTPRN
LocationNeuroendocrine secretory granules (pancreatic β-cells, adrenal medulla, brain)
StructureTransmembrane receptor-like protein tyrosine phosphatase (inactive catalytic domain)
Molecular weight~105 kDa (intracellular domain used in assays)
FunctionDense core vesicle trafficking, insulin secretion modulation

3. Clinical Performance

MetricValueNotes
Sensitivity (new-onset T1DM)50–70%Lower than GAD65 (~70–80%)
Specificity>95%Very high — rare in healthy controls
Positive Predictive ValueHighEspecially with multiple antibodies
Age dependenceHigher in children/adolescentsLess common in adult-onset (LADA)
PersistenceDeclines over yearsOften becomes negative after 5–10 years

Key point: IA-2 is more specific but less sensitive than GAD65.

4. Comparison with Other Autoantibodies

FeatureGAD65IA-2IAAZnT8
Sensitivity (T1DM)70–80%50–70%40–60% (children)60–80%
Specificity95–98%>98%90–95%95–98%
LADA positivityHigh (70–80%)Low (20–30%)LowModerate (40–50%)
Age preferenceAll agesChildren > AdultsYoung childrenAll ages
PersistenceYears–decadesDeclinesDeclines (esp. if insulin started)Persists
Assay typeELISA/RBAELISA/RBARBA (insulin binding)ELISA

5. Predictive Value for T1DM

Antibody Profile5-Year Risk10-Year Risk
Single Ab (any)~10–15%~20–30%
IA-2 alone~20%~35%
GAD65 alone~15%~25%
Two Abs (any combo)~40–60%~60–80%
Three+ Abs>80%>90%
IA-2 + GAD65 + ZnT8>90%>95%

IA-2 positivity strongly predicts rapid progression to insulin requirement.

6. Clinical Utility

ScenarioRole of IA-2
New-onset diabetes (adult)Part of panel to confirm T1DM vs T2DM
LADA diagnosisLow sensitivity — GAD65 preferred for LADA
Paediatric T1DMHigh sensitivity in children; adds to multi-ab panel
Research / Prevention trialsEnrichment for high-risk (multiple Abs)
Post-transplant / MonogenicNegative IA-2 supports non-autoimmune aetiology
Recurrence post-pancreas transplantRising IA-2 may predict recurrence

7. Assay Methodology

MethodPrincipleAdvantage
Radiobinding Assay (RBA)Radiolabelled IA-2 + patient serum + precipitationGold standard; high sensitivity
ELISARecombinant IA-2 (intracellular domain) coated plateHigh throughput; automated; no radioactivity
Luciferase Immunoprecipitation (LIPS)Luciferase-tagged IA-2 + luminescenceHigh sensitivity; wide dynamic range

Standardization: WHO International Standard (NIBSC 05/210) for IA-2 autoantibodies; expressed in U/mL (units/mL).

8. Interpretation Algorithm

New diabetes diagnosis → autoantibody panel
    ↓
IA-2 POSITIVE
    ├─ + GAD65 + → **T1DM confirmed** (high specificity)
    ├─ + ZnT8 + → **T1DM confirmed** (high specificity)
    ├─ + IAA + → **T1DM confirmed** (if insulin-naïve)
    └─ IA-2 ONLY → **T1DM likely** (high specificity); check clinical context
        • Adult, lean, ketotic → T1DM
        • Adult, overweight, non-ketotic → consider LADA (check GAD65)

9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • IA-2 = ICA512 = tyrosine phosphatase — same antigen
  • Specificity >98% — highest among T1DM autoantibodies
  • Sensitivity 50–70% — lower than GAD65; misses some T1DM if used alone
  • Children > Adults — more common in paediatric T1DM
  • LADA: GAD65 is the marker; IA-2 low yield
  • Multiple antibodies (>2) = high predictive value (>80% 10-yr risk)
  • Declines over time — may become negative 5–10y post-diagnosis
  • Not insulin-dependent — unlike IAA, not affected by exogenous insulin
  • Assay: ELISA (routine), RBA (reference), LIPS (research)

10. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) — Diagnosis of T1DM
  2. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 2
  3. Bonifacio E et al. Diabetes 2010 — Antibody standardization
  4. Wenzlau JM et al. Nat Med 2007 — ZnT8 discovery
  5. ISPAD Clinical Practice Guidelines 2022 — Autoantibody testing

11. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/GAD65 autoantibodies]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/Insulin autoantibodies (IAA)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/Zinc transporter 8 (ZnT8) autoantibodies]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Clinical presentation/LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults)]]
  • [[Monogenic and Atypical Diabetes/Neonatal diabetes]]

2. Antigen Characteristics
3. Clinical Performance
4. Comparison with Other Autoantibodies
5. Predictive Value for T1DM
6. Clinical Utility
7. Assay Methodology
8. Interpretation Algorithm
9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls
10. Key References
11. Local Navigation
▸ 21.12.21 Iatrogenic Hypoglycaemia Unawareness

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, hypoglycaemia-unawareness, t1dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Hypoglycaemia unawareness = loss of autonomic warning symptoms; prevalence 20–25% T1DM; risk of severe hypo 6×; mechanisms: recurrent antecedent hypoglycaemia, autonomic neuropathy; management: avoid hypo 2–3 weeks (CGM, higher targets), structured education, CSII/HCL"

see_also: ["Severe hypoglycaemia", "Distal symmetric polyneuropathy", "Autonomic neuropathy", "Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Iatrogenic Hypoglycaemia Unawareness

[!info]
Hypoglycaemia unawareness (HU) = loss of autonomic warning symptoms (adrenergic: tremor, sweating, palpitations); prevalence 20–25% T1DM; severe hypoglycaemia risk 6×; caused by recurrent antecedent hypoglycaemia + autonomic neuropathy; reversible with 2–3 weeks hypo avoidance (CGM, higher targets, HCL).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define hypoglycaemia unawareness and diagnose clinically
  • [ ] Explain pathophysiology (HAAF, autonomic failure)
  • [ ] Identify risk factors and high-risk groups
  • [ ] Apply management: hypo avoidance, CGM, HCL, education
  • [ ] Understand reversibility timeline

2. Definitions

TermDefinition
Impaired Awareness of Hypoglycaemia (IAH)Diminished ability to perceive onset of hypoglycaemia (Gold score ≥4, Clarke score ≥4)
Hypoglycaemia Unawareness (HU)Complete loss of warning symptoms until neuroglycopenia (confusion, coma)
HAAF (Hypoglycaemia-Associated Autonomic Failure)Recurrent hypoglycaemia → blunted counter-regulatory response (↓ epinephrine, ↓ glucagon) → further hypoglycaemia risk

Gold Score (1-item): "Do you know when your hypos are starting?" 1=always aware → 7=never aware; ≥4 = IAH.

Clarke Score (8-item): Assesses frequency, symptoms, recognition; ≥4 = IAH.

3. Pathophysiology

MechanismDetails
Recurrent antecedent hypoglycaemia↑ Brain glucose transport → ↓ perception of falling glucose; ↓ sympathoadrenal response
Defective glucose counter-regulationLoss of glucagon response (intrinsic α-cell defect in T1DM); blunted epinephrine (HAAF)
Autonomic neuropathy↓ Adrenergic symptoms (tremor, palpitations, sweating) — not required for IAH
Cortisol/GHPreserved (late responders; not protective)
NeuroglycopeniaBrain glucose <1.5 mmol/L → cognitive dysfunction, confusion, seizures

Key: Glucagon response lost early in T1DM; epinephrine becomes critical → blunted in HAAF.

4. Risk Factors

StrongestContributing
Prior severe hypoglycaemiaDuration of diabetes (>10–15y)
Intensive therapy (HbA1c <48)Age (children, elderly)
Recurrent mild hypoglycaemiaAutonomic neuropathy
Tight glucose targetsSleep (nocturnal hypoglycaemia)
AlcoholExercise (delayed hypoglycaemia)
Cognitive impairment / dementiaPregnancy (tight targets)
Renal impairment (insulin clearance ↓)Medications (beta-blockers mask symptoms)

5. Clinical Consequences

OutcomeRisk Increase
Severe hypoglycaemia (requiring assistance)6-fold (IAH)
Nocturnal hypoglycaemia3–4x
Cognitive impairmentAcute (during hypo) + chronic (recurrent)
Cardiac arrhythmiasQT prolongation, ischaemia (sympathetic surge)
Driving accidentsImpaired reaction time, unawareness
Fear of hypoglycaemiaOvertreatment → hyperglycaemia; QoL ↓
Mortality"Dead in bed" syndrome (nocturnal, unexplained)

6. Management — Reversibility is Key

Step 1: Avoid Hypoglycaemia (2–3 Weeks)

StrategyTarget
Relax glucose targetsPre-meal 6–10 mmol/L; bedtime 7–10 mmol/L
CGM with alertsThreshold 4.0–4.5 mmol/L; predictive low alerts
Reduce insulin dosesBasal ↓ 10–20%; bolus ICR adjustment; correct stacking
Structured educationDAFNE refresher; hypoglycaemia avoidance skills
Carbohydrate consistencyRegular meals; bedtime snack if nocturnal risk

Step 2: Technology

TechnologyBenefit
rtCGM (Dexcom G6/G7, Libre 3)Real-time alerts; predictive alarms; ↓ severe hypo 50%
HCL (780G, Control-IQ, Omnipod 5, CamAPS)Automated basal suspend/predictive; ↑ TIR; ↓ TBR
Sensor-augmented pump (SAP)Threshold suspend (LGS/PLGS)

Step 3: Address Reversible Factors

FactorAction
AlcoholLimit; carb with drinks
ExerciseAdjust basal/bolus; carb before/during
Renal impairmentDose reduce insulin
MedicationsReview beta-blockers, sulfonylureas
GastroparesisProkinetics; small frequent meals

Step 4: If Persistent (Rare)

  • Pancreas transplant (simultaneous pancreas-kidney if ESRD)
  • Islet transplant (experimental)

7. Driving Regulations (UK DVLA / International)

ConditionRequirement
IAH (Gold ≥4)Must notify DVLA; licence may be refused/revoked
Severe hypoglycaemia (1 episode requiring assistance)Must notify DVLA; 3-month driving ban if awake; 12-month if asleep
HCL/rtCGM useMay support licence retention with medical review
Group 2 (HGV/PSV)Zero tolerance — any severe hypo = licence loss

8. Reversibility Evidence

StudyInterventionResult
Cranston et al. (1994)2 weeks strict hypo avoidanceEpinephrine response restored; awareness improved
DAFNE/HARPdocEducation + CBT + avoidanceSustained improvement in Gold score
CGM trials (DIAMOND, GOLD)rtCGM↓ TBR 50%; ↓ severe hypo; awareness improves
HCL trials780G, Control-IQ, Omnipod 5↓ TBR 40–50%; awareness recovery in subset

Timeline: 2–3 weeks avoidance → counter-regulatory recovery; 3–6 months sustained awareness if CGM/HCL continued.

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
IAH definitionGold score ≥4 or Clarke score ≥4
Prevalence T1DM20–25%
Severe hypo risk IAH6-fold increase
HAAF mechanismRecurrent hypo → blunted epinephrine → further hypo
Glucagon in T1DMLost early (intrinsic α-cell defect)
Reversibility2–3 weeks strict hypo avoidance restores awareness
Gold score1=always aware → 7=never aware; ≥4 = IAH
CGM in IAH↓ severe hypo 50%; real-time alerts
HCL in IAHAutomated suspend; ↑ TIR; ↓ TBR
Driving IAHMust notify DVLA; Group 2 = zero tolerance

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Acute complications|Acute complications]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Severe hypoglycaemia]], [[Distal symmetric polyneuropathy]], [[Autonomic neuropathy]], [[Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)]]

▸ 21.12.22 Insulin Analogues Rapid Long Acting Ultra Long

id: t1dm-insulin-analogues

title: "Insulin Analogues (Rapid, Long-Acting, Ultra-Long)"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • insulin-therapy
  • insulin-analogues
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Insulin therapy"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

Insulin analogues = recombinant human insulin with amino acid modifications altering pharmacokinetics.

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Core prescribing knowledge; exam questions on onset/peak/duration, conversion, pump compatibility, cost-effectiveness.

2. Classification & Pharmacokinetics

Rapid-Acting Analogues (Bolus / Prandial)

InsulinOnsetPeakDurationBrandNotes
Insulin lispro15 min30–90 min3–5 hHumalog, LiprologB28 Lys→Pro, B29 Pro→Lys
Insulin aspart10–20 min40–50 min3–5 hNovoRapid, FiaspB28 Pro→Asp
Insulin glulisine10–20 min30–90 min3–4 hApidraB3 Lys, B29 Glu
Faster aspart (Fiasp)5 min30–60 min3–5 hFiasp+ niacinamide (absorption enhancer)
Lyumjev (lispro-aabc)5 min30–60 min3–5 hLyumjev+ treprostinil + citrate

Exam tip: Fiasp & Lyumjev = ultra-rapid — can dose at meal start (even after first bite); standard rapid = 15–20 min before meal.

Long-Acting Analogues (Basal)

InsulinOnsetPeakDurationBrandNotes
Insulin glargine U1001–2 hNone (flat)20–24 hLantus, Abasaglar, SemgleeA21 Gly→Arg, B31/B32 Arg+Arg
Insulin detemir1–2 hMinimal16–24 hLevemirB29 Lys removed, C14 fatty acid (myristic) → albumin binding
Insulin glargine U3001–2 hNone (flatter)>24 h (30 h)Toujeo3× concentration → slower absorption
Insulin degludec U100/U2001 hNone (flattest)>42 hTresibaB29 Lys removed, C16 fatty acid (hexadecanedioic) → multi-hexamer chains

3. Key Pharmacokinetic Differences — Exam Focus

PropertyGlargine U100Glargine U300DetemirDegludec
Duration20–24 h>24 h (30 h)16–24 h>42 h
Variability (CV%)~20%~15%~15%~8% (lowest)
Dosing flexibility±1–2 h±3 h±2–3 h±8–24 h (any time of day)
Steady state2–3 days3–4 days2–3 days3–4 days
Hypoglycaemia (nocturnal)Reference↓ 20–30%↓ 10–20%↓ 30–40%
Weight gainReferenceSimilarLessSimilar
Injection volumeStandard1/3 volumeStandardU200 = 1/2 volume
Pump compatibleYes (off-label)NoYesNo (precipitation risk)

4. Concentrated Insulins — When to Use

InsulinConcentrationIndication
Glargine U300 (Toujeo)300 units/mL>80 units/day basal; volumes >1 mL; nocturnal hypo on U100
Degludec U200 (Tresiba)200 units/mL>80 units/day basal; injection volume reduction
Lispro U200 (Humalog)200 units/mLHigh bolus doses (>60 units/meal); pump users
Aspart U200 (NovoRapid)200 units/mLHigh bolus doses; pump users

Conversion: Unit-for-unit (1 unit U100 = 1 unit U200/U300); volume reduces 2–3×.

5. Basal-Bolus Regimen Construction

ComponentTypical Starting DoseAdjustment
Total Daily Dose (TDD)0.4–0.5 units/kg/day↓ if honeymoon, renal impairment, elderly
Basal proportion40–50% TDDTitrate to fasting 4–7 mmol/L
Bolus proportion50–60% TDDDivided by meals (ICR)
ICR (Insulin-to-Carb Ratio)500 / TDD (units per 10g)Adjust by 10–20% based on post-prandial
ISF (Insulin Sensitivity Factor)100 / TDD (mmol/L per unit)Adjust by 10–20% based on correction response

6. Switching Between Basal Insulins

SwitchConversionMonitoring
Glargine U100 → U3001:1 units; may need 10–20% ↑ after 1 weekCheck fasting at 3–7 days
Glargine U100 → Degludec1:1 units (or ↓10–20% if frequent hypoglycaemia)Titrate weekly; allow 3–4 days steady state
Detemir → Degludec1:1 units (detemir often bid → degludec once daily)Monitor for hypoglycaemia first week
NPH → Analogue↓ 20% total basal doseSignificant hypo risk reduction

7. Pump-Compatible Insulins

  • Preferred: Lispro, Aspart, Fiasp, Lyumjev (rapid analogues)
  • NOT recommended: Glargine (precipitation in tubing), Degludec (precipitation), Detemir (unpredictable absorption in pump)
  • Reservoir change: Every 48–72h (Fiasp/Lyumjev: 48h due to excipients)

8. Biosimilars & Cost

BiosimilarReferenceInterchangeableCost Saving
Abasaglar (glargine)LantusYes (NICE)~30%
Semglee (glargine)LantusYes (EMA/FDA)~30%
Truvelog (aspart)NovoRapidYes~30%
Sanofi glargine U300 (not biosimilar)ToujeoN/AN/A

NICE: Prescribe least costly equivalent; biosimilars preferred for new starts.

9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • Degludec duration >42h → allows flexible dosing time (unique among basals)
  • Degludec CV ~8% → lowest variability → least nocturnal hypoglycaemia
  • Glargine U300 → 1/3 injection volume; may need 10–20% higher dose than U100
  • Fiasp/Lyumjev → dose at meal start (not 15 min before); safer for unpredictable eating
  • Detemir → less weight gain (albumin binding limits mitogenic effect)
  • Pump: Only rapid analogues; change set q48–72h
  • Conversion: Always unit-for-unit for concentrated insulins; adjust dose clinically after
  • Biosimilars: Abasaglar/Semglee/Truvelog = interchangeable with reference

10. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) Type 1 diabetes in adults
  2. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 9
  3. Home PD et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2015 (Degludec SWITCH trials)
  4. Blevins TC et al. Diabetes Obes Metab 2015 (Glargine U300 EDITION trials)
  5. BNF / MIMS current pricing for biosimilars

11. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Basal-bolus regimen]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Carbohydrate counting and dose adjustment]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Insulin pump therapy (CSII)]]
  • [[Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/Hypoglycaemia classification (Level 1/2/3)]]

▸ 21.12.23 Insulin Autoantibodies Iaa

id: t1dm-iaa-autoantibodies

title: "Insulin Autoantibodies (IAA)"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • autoantibodies
  • iaa
  • insulin-autoantibodies
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Autoantibody testing"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

Insulin Autoantibodies (IAA) = antibodies against native human insulin.

Critical distinction: IAA = autoantibodies (pre-insulin therapy); IAA vs Insulin Antibodies (IA) = antibodies induced by exogenous insulin therapy.

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Only valid before insulin initiation; exam questions on timing, assay interference, paediatric sensitivity.

2. Key Concept: IAA vs Insulin Antibodies (IA)

FeatureIAA (Autoantibodies)IA (Insulin Antibodies)
OriginAutoimmune (pre-insulin)Induced by exogenous insulin
TimingPresent at diagnosis, pre-treatmentDevelop weeks–months after insulin start
Clinical significanceMarker of autoimmune β-cell destructionCause of insulin resistance, erratic absorption
Assay interferenceMust test before insulin exposureAlways positive on insulin therapy
DisappearanceDeclines post-diagnosisPersists while on insulin

Exam rule: IAA must be measured before first insulin dose (or within 2 weeks). After insulin therapy → measures IA, not IAA.

3. Clinical Performance

MetricValueNotes
Sensitivity (new T1DM)40–60% (children)Highest in young children (<5y: 60–70%)
Sensitivity (adult/LADA)20–30%Low — not useful for LADA
Specificity90–95%Lower than GAD65/IA-2/ZnT8
Age dependenceStrong inverse with age<5y: ~70%; 10–14y: ~50%; adult: <30%
PersistenceDeclines rapidly after insulin startHalf-life ~6 months post-insulin

4. Comparison with Other Autoantibodies

FeatureGAD65IA-2IAAZnT8
Sensitivity (children)70–80%50–70%60–70% (<5y)60–80%
Sensitivity (adult)70–80%30–50%20–30%50–60%
Specificity95–98%>98%90–95%95–98%
Pre-insulin requirementNoNoYES (critical)No
LADA utilityHighLowLowModerate
Assay typeELISA/RBAELISA/RBARBA (radiolabelled insulin)ELISA

5. Assay Methodology — Radiobinding Assay (RBA)

StepDetail
Tracer¹²⁵I-labelled human insulin (A14 Tyr or B25 Tyr)
IncubationPatient serum + tracer + excess unlabelled insulin (competition)
SeparationProtein A/G precipitation or PEG precipitation
ReadoutGamma counter; % bound = IAA level
UnitsnU/mL (nano-units/mL) or JDF units
StandardizationWHO Standard (NIBSC 83/500)

False positives: Proinsulin cross-reactivity (some assays); insulin therapy (exogenous insulin → IA, not IAA).

6. Clinical Utility & Indications

ScenarioIAA Role
Child <10y, new diabetesHigh yield — 60–70% positive; confirms T1DM
Adult new diabetesLow yield (<30%) — prefer GAD65 + IA-2 + ZnT8
LADA diagnosisNot recommended — low sensitivity
Pre-symptomatic screening (trial)Part of multiple-Ab panel (DPT-1, TrialNet)
Monogenic diabetes exclusionNegative panel supports monogenic (but not definitive)
Post-transplant recurrenceNot used (on insulin → IA positive)

7. Interpretation Algorithm

New diabetes, insulin-naïve
    ↓
Measure IAA (with GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8)
    ↓
IAA POSITIVE
    ├─ Child <10y + other Abs → **T1DM confirmed**
    ├─ Adult + GAD65/IA-2/ZnT8 + → **T1DM/LADA confirmed**
    └─ Isolated IAA + adult → **Low specificity**; consider assay artefact, repeat
IAA NEGATIVE
    ├─ Child + GAD65/IA-2/ZnT8 + → T1DM (IAA absent in ~40%)
    ├─ Adult + GAD65 + → LADA likely
    └─ All Abs negative → Consider T2DM, monogenic, secondary

8. Special Considerations

SituationImpact on IAA
Exogenous insulin (any)Invalidates IAA → measures insulin antibodies (IA)
Porcine/bovine insulin (historical)Higher immunogenicity → more IA
Insulin analogues (lispro, aspart, glargine)Lower immunogenicity but still induce IA
High proinsulin (insulinoma)Cross-reactivity in some assays → false positive
PregnancyTransient IAA possible; unclear significance

9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • IAA = pre-insulin only — most tested fact: "Patient on insulin for 3 months, IAA positive" → this is insulin antibodies (IA), not IAA
  • Highest sensitivity in young children (<5 years) — paediatric preference
  • Low sensitivity in adults/LADA — don't use for LADA screen (use GAD65)
  • Assay = RBA (radiobinding) — uses radiolabelled insulin
  • Specificity 90–95% — lower than GAD65/IA-2/ZnT8
  • Declines after insulin start — half-life ~6 months
  • Multiple Abs: IAA + GAD65 + IA-2 = very high predictive value
  • Not affected by insulin type for IAA (but insulin therapy induces IA)

10. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) — Diagnosis of T1DM
  2. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 2
  3. Bonifacio E et al. Diabetes 2010 — Antibody standardization (IAA workshop)
  4. Vehik K et al. Diabetes Care 2011 — TEDDY study IAA incidence
  5. ISPAD Clinical Practice Guidelines 2022 — Autoantibody testing

11. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/GAD65 autoantibodies]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/IA-2 autoantibodies]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Autoantibody testing/Zinc transporter 8 (ZnT8) autoantibodies]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Clinical presentation/Classic symptomatic presentation]]

▸ 21.12.28 Time In Range Tir Metrics

id: t1dm-tir

title: "Time in Range (TIR) Metrics"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • glucose-monitoring
  • tir
  • cgm-metrics
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Glucose monitoring"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

Time in Range (TIR) = percentage of time glucose spends within target range (3.9–10 mmol/L) as measured by CGM.

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Emerging standard for glycaemic assessment; ATTD consensus; correlates with complications; exam questions on targets, interpretation, relationship to HbA1c.

2. Standard CGM Metrics (ATTD 2019 Consensus)

MetricRangeTarget (T1DM / T2DM)Clinical Significance
TIR3.9–10 mmol/L>70% / >70%Primary efficacy endpoint
TAR (Level 1)>10–13.9 mmol/L<25% / <25%Hyperglycaemia exposure
TAR (Level 2)>13.9 mmol/L<5% / <5%Severe hyperglycaemia
TBR (Level 1)3.0–3.9 mmol/L<4% / <4%Hypoglycaemia alert
TBR (Level 2)<3.0 mmol/L<1% / <1%Severe hypoglycaemia
CV (Coefficient of Variation)SD/Mean × 100<36% / <36%Glycaemic variability

Pregnancy targets (T1DM): TIR >70% (3.5–7.8 mmol/L), TBR <4% (<3.5 mmol/L), CV <36%

3. TIR ↔ HbA1c Relationship (Derived from DCCT/ADAG)

TIREstimated HbA1cCategory
>70%<53 mmol/mol (<7.0%)Target
60–70%53–58 mmol/mol (7.0–7.5%)Suboptimal
50–60%58–64 mmol/mol (7.5–8.0%)Inadequate
<50%>64 mmol/mol (>8.0%)Poor

Formula (approximate): HbA1c (mmol/mol) ≈ 77.6 – 0.46 × TIR(%)

GMI (Glucose Management Indicator): Calculated from mean CGM glucose; correlates with lab HbA1c.

4. Clinical Interpretation Framework

Step 1: Assess Hypoglycaemia (Safety First)

  • TBR Level 2 >1% → immediate action (basal reduction, education, CGM alarms)
  • TBR Level 1 >4% → review patterns (nocturnal, post-exercise, alcohol)

Step 2: Assess Hyperglycaemia (Efficacy)

  • TAR Level 2 >5% → intensify therapy (bolus, basal, lifestyle)
  • TAR Level 1 >25% → review carbohydrate counting, ICR, meal timing

Step 3: Assess Variability

  • CV >36% → unstable control → review injection technique, site rotation, insulin absorption, CGM artefacts

Step 4: Assess TIR

  • TIR <70% → comprehensive regimen review (basal testing, ICR, ISF, lifestyle)

5. AGP (Ambulatory Glucose Profile) Interpretation

AGP FeatureWhat to Look ForAction
Median lineCentral tendencyShift up/down = basal adjustment
25–75th percentile (IQR)Day-to-day variabilityWide = erratic lifestyle/insulin
5–95th percentileExtremesOutliers = specific events (missed bolus, exercise)
Time blocksOvernight, morning, afternoon, eveningTargeted adjustments per block

Overnight (00:00–06:00): Most stable; reflects basal insulin adequacy.

6. Data Sufficiency Requirements

RequirementMinimum
Sensor wear>70% of time (14-day period → >10 days)
Readings per day>70% of expected (288/day for 5-min → >200/day)
Report period14 days ideal; 10 days minimum

Insufficient data: Do not make major regimen changes; optimize wear first.

7. Special Populations — Target Adjustments

PopulationTIR TargetTBR TargetRange
Frail elderly / limited life expectancy>50%<1% (L2)5.6–11.1 mmol/L
High hypoglycaemia risk / impaired awareness>50%<1% (L2)5.6–11.1 mmol/L
Pregnancy (T1DM)>70%<4% (L1)3.5–7.8 mmol/L
Children <6 years>60%<3% (L1)3.9–10 mmol/L
Children 6–18 years>65%<3% (L1)3.9–10 mmol/L

8. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • TIR >70% = HbA1c ~53 mmol/mol (7.0%) — memorize this equivalence
  • Each 5% increase in TIR ≈ 5 mmol/mol (0.5%) HbA1c reduction
  • CV >36% = high variability → independently predicts complications
  • TIR vs HbA1c: TIR captures hypoglycaemia & variability; HbA1c does not
  • ATTD 2019 consensus: TIR primary outcome for clinical trials
  • GMI vs HbA1c: GMI calculated from mean CGM glucose; discordance → haemoglobinopathy, anaemia, CGM artefact
  • Data sufficiency: 14 days, >70% wear — standard exam question

9. Key References

  1. Battelino T et al. Diabetes Care 2019;42:1593–1603 (ATTD Consensus)
  2. Bergenstal RM et al. Diabetes Care 2018;41:2275–2280 (TIR-HbA1c)
  3. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 6 & 7
  4. NICE NG17 (2022) — CGM metrics
  5. Danne T et al. Diabetologia 2017;60:1306–1315 (Pregnancy targets)

10. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Flash glucose monitoring]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Capillary blood glucose (SMBG)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Carbohydrate counting and dose adjustment]]
  • [[Classification and Diagnosis of Diabetes Mellitus/HbA1c diagnosis]]

Topic Overview

3. TIR ↔ HbA1c Relationship (Derived from DCCT/ADAG)
4. Clinical Interpretation Framework
Step 1: Assess Hypoglycaemia (Safety First)
Step 2: Assess Hyperglycaemia (Efficacy)
Step 3: Assess Variability
Step 4: Assess TIR

6. Data Sufficiency Requirements

8. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

10. Local Navigation
▸ 21.12.29 Zinc Transporter 8 Znt8 Autoantibodies

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, znt8, autoantibodies, t1dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - ZnT8 = 4th major T1DM autoantibody (after GAD65, IA-2, IAA); Slc30A8 gene; 60–80% T1DM at dx; improves prediction when combined; ZnT8-R/W/Q variants; not routine in all centres"

see_also: ["Autoantibody testing", "Type 1 diabetes mellitus", "Clinical presentation", "Latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (LADA)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Zinc Transporter 8 (ZnT8) Autoantibodies

[!info]
ZnT8 = 4th major T1DM autoantibody (Slc30A8 gene; zinc transporter in β-cell secretory granules); 60–80% positive at diagnosis; added to GAD65, IA-2, IAA panel → ↑ sensitivity to ~98%; ZnT8-R/W/Q arginine/tryptophan/glutamine variants at position 325; not yet routine in all centres.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe ZnT8 structure, function, and gene
  • [ ] State sensitivity/specificity in T1DM vs other diabetes
  • [ ] Apply ZnT8 in combination autoantibody panels
  • [ ] Understand ZnT8-R/W/Q polymorphism and antibody binding
  • [ ] Identify clinical utility (prediction, LADA, trial stratification)

2. Biology

FeatureDetail
Full nameZinc Transporter 8 (ZnT8; SLC30A8)
Locationβ-cell secretory granule membrane
FunctionTransports Zn2+ into granules → insulin crystallization (Zn2+-insulin hexamers)
GeneSLC30A8 (chromosome 8q24)
Polymorphismrs13266634 (C/T) → Arg325Trp (R/W); also Gln (Q) variant
Antibody targetC-terminal domain (intracellular)

3. Diagnostic Performance

PopulationZnT8 SensitivitySpecificity
New-onset T1DM (children)60–80%>99%
New-onset T1DM (adults)50–70%>99%
LADA40–60%>99%
T2DM<2%>99%
Healthy controls<1%—

Combined panel (GAD65 + IA-2 + IAA + ZnT8): Sensitivity ~98% at diagnosis.

4. ZnT8 Variants & Antibody Binding

Variant (rs13266634)Amino Acid 325Antibody BindingPopulation Frequency
CCArginine (R)Strong (ZnT8-R Ab)~50% (European)
TTTryptophan (W)Strong (ZnT8-W Ab)~30%
CTBoth R + WBoth~20%
RareGlutamine (Q)ZnT8-Q Ab<1%

Clinical implication: Assays should detect both R and W variants (or use recombinant antigen covering both) to avoid false negatives.

5. Clinical Utility

Use CaseValue
Diagnosis (atypical presentation)Antibody-negative "T2DM" → ZnT8+ confirms autoimmune
LADAZnT8+ in GAD65+/IA-2- cases → confirms autoimmune
Prediction (relatives)Multiple antibodies (including ZnT8) → ↑ 5y risk
Clinical trialsStratification (e.g., teplizumab, immunotherapy)
Post-transplantRecurrence monitoring (ZnT8 may persist)

6. Comparison of T1DM Autoantibodies

AntibodyTargetSensitivity (New T1DM)SpecificityNotes
GAD65Glutamic acid decarboxylase70–80%98%Most common; persists years
IA-2 (IA-2β)Insulinoma-associated antigen-260–70%99%More specific for progression
IAAInsulin (autoantibody)40–60% (children)99%Only valid if insulin-naive; age-dependent
ZnT8Zinc transporter 860–80%>99%Newest; adds sensitivity
Combined (4)All four~98%>99%Gold standard panel

7. Practical Considerations

IssueGuidance
CostMore expensive than GAD/IA-2; not all centres offer routinely
AvailabilityReference labs; specialist centres; not universal
TimingBest at diagnosis; declines over years (like IA-2)
StandardisationImproving (IDSP workshops); R/W variant coverage essential
InterpretationPositive = autoimmune aetiology; negative ≠ excludes (sensitivity <100%)

8. ZnT8 in LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults)

ScenarioZnT8 Role
GAD65+ / IA-2-ZnT8+ confirms autoimmune; predicts faster insulin requirement
GAD65- / IA-2-ZnT8+ rare but identifies autoimmune subset
PredictionMultiple Abs (GAD+ZnT8) → insulin within 3–5y vs GAD alone 6–10y

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ZnT8 geneSLC30A8 (chromosome 8)
ZnT8 functionZn2+ transport into β-cell granules → insulin crystallization
Sensitivity at dx60–80% (children); 50–70% (adults)
Specificity>99%
Combined panelGAD65 + IA-2 + IAA + ZnT8 = ~98% sensitivity
Polymorphismrs13266634 → Arg325Trp (R/W); assay must detect both
Clinical useAtypical presentation, LADA, prediction, trial stratification
Not routineCost/availability limited; specialist centres
IAA vs ZnT8IAA = insulin autoantibody (pre-insulin); ZnT8 = transporter

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Autoantibody testing|Autoantibody testing]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Autoantibody testing]], [[Type 1 diabetes mellitus]], [[Clinical presentation]], [[Latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (LADA)]]

Topic Overview

Zinc Transporter 8 (ZnT8) Autoantibodies
1. Learning Objectives
2. Biology
3. Diagnostic Performance
4. ZnT8 Variants & Antibody Binding
5. Clinical Utility
6. Comparison of T1DM Autoantibodies
7. Practical Considerations
8. ZnT8 in LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults)
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.12.3 Basal Bolus Regimen

id: t1dm-basal-bolus

title: "Basal-Bolus Regimen"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • insulin-therapy
  • basal-bolus
  • multiple-daily-injections
  • mdi
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Insulin therapy"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

Basal-Bolus Regimen (MDI) = Multiple Daily Injections: once/twice-daily long-acting basal insulin + rapid-acting bolus insulin with each meal.

Gold standard for T1DM not on pump therapy.

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Core management; exam questions on dose calculation, titration, troubleshooting, conversion to/from pump.

2. Regimen Structure

ComponentInsulin TypeFrequencyTimingProportion of TDD
BasalGlargine U100/U300, Detemir, DegludecOnce daily (Degludec/U300) or BID (Detemir/Glargine U100 in some)Fixed time (e.g., 22:00)40–50%
Bolus (Breakfast)Lispro, Aspart, Fiasp, Lyumjev, GlulisineWith meal15 min before (std) / at meal (ultra-rapid)20–25%
Bolus (Lunch)SameWith meal15 min before / at meal15–20%
Bolus (Dinner)SameWith meal15 min before / at meal15–20%
CorrectionSameAs neededWith meals or separateVariable

Total Daily Dose (TDD): 0.4–0.5 units/kg/day (new onset: 0.2–0.3 u/kg during honeymoon).

3. Starting Dose Calculation (New Diagnosis / Conversion)

Step 1: Estimate TDD

  • Weight-based: 0.4–0.5 units/kg/day
  • From previous regimen: Sum all insulin units in 24h

Step 2: Split Basal : Bolus

ApproachBasal %Bolus %
Standard50%50%
Conservative (elderly, renal, hypoglycaemia risk)60%40%
Low-carb diet60–70%30–40%

Step 3: Distribute Bolus Across Meals

MethodCalculation
Equal splitTotal bolus ÷ 3 (or ÷ 4 if snack)
Carb-proportionalBased on typical carb intake per meal
DAFNE standardBreakfast 30%, Lunch 25%, Dinner 30%, Supper 15%

Step 4: Set ICR & ISF

  • ICR: 500 ÷ TDD (adjust per meal: breakfast tighter)
  • ISF: 100 ÷ TDD (target correction to 5.5–6.0 mmol/L)

4. Basal Insulin Titration Protocol

Target Fasting GlucoseAction
<4 mmol/L↓ Basal by 10–20% (or 2–4 units)
4–7 mmol/LNo change (target achieved)
7–10 mmol/L↑ Basal by 10% (or 2–4 units)
>10 mmol/L↑ Basal by 20% (or 4–6 units); check adherence, 3am glucose

Frequency: Titrate every 3–7 days; allow 3–4 days for glargine U300/degludec steady state.

3am Check: If fasting high but 3am low (<3.9) → Somogyi → ↓ basal; if 3am high → dawn/insufficient basal → ↑ basal.

5. Bolus Titration (Pattern Management)

PatternAdjustment
Post-breakfast >9 mmol/L consistently↑ Breakfast ICR (1:12 → 1:10)
Post-lunch >9 mmol/L consistently↑ Lunch ICR
Post-dinner >9 mmol/L consistently↑ Dinner ICR
Pre-lunch low <4 mmol/L↓ Breakfast ICR or ↓ breakfast carbs
Pre-dinner low <4 mmol/L↓ Lunch ICR
Bedtime low <4 mmol/L↓ Dinner ICR or ↓ bedtime basal
Pre-breakfast high, post-breakfast OK↑ Basal (not breakfast bolus)

Review frequency: Weekly initially; then monthly once stable.

6. Basal Insulin Choice — Decision Algorithm

NEW T1DM START
    ↓
Prefer DEGLUDEC (Tresiba) — lowest variability, flexible timing, least nocturnal hypo
    ↓
If cost/access issue → GLARGINE U100 (biosimilar Abasaglar/Semglee)
    ↓
If high dose (>80u) / volume issue → GLARGINE U300 (Toujeo) or DEGLUDEC U200
    ↓
If weight gain concern → DETEMIR (Levemir) — but BID often needed
    ↓
Avoid NPH — higher variability, peak → nocturnal hypo

7. Sick Day Rules (Basal-Bolus)

PrincipleAction
Never stop basal insulinContinue full basal dose (or ↑ 20% if ketotic)
Bolus for carbs eatenUse usual ICR; if not eating, give correction only
Check ketonesIf BG >13.9 mmol/L or symptomatic → blood ketones q4h
Hydration150–250 mL/hour sugar-free fluids
Seek helpKetones >1.5 mmol/L + unable to keep fluids / vomiting → A&E for IV fluids/insulin

Correction scale for illness (if ketones negative):

  • BG 11–17 mmol/L: 10% TDD as correction
  • BG 17–22 mmol/L: 15% TDD
  • BG >22 mmol/L: 20% TDD + seek medical review

8. Conversion: Basal-Bolus ↔ Pump (CSII)

DirectionCalculation
MDI → PumpTDD × 0.8 (20% reduction) → 50% basal rate, 50% bolus
Pump → MDITotal daily basal (from pump) → basal analogue; bolus doses → ICR/ISF
Basal rate → Glargine U10024h basal units × 1.0–1.1
Basal rate → Degludec24h basal units × 0.9–1.0

9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • TDD 0.4–0.5 u/kg/day — starting estimate for fresh T1DM
  • Basal 50% / Bolus 50% — standard split
  • Titrate basal to fasting 4–7 mmol/L — every 3–7 days
  • 3am check distinguishes Somogyi vs Dawn — critical concept
  • Degludec preferred first-line (NICE/ADA) — flexibility, low hypo, low variability
  • Sick days: NEVER stop basal — exam favorite
  • MDI→Pump: reduce TDD by 20% (pump more efficient)
  • Bioequivalence: Glargine biosimilars (Abasaglar/Semglee) = Lantus; switch unit-for-unit

10. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) Type 1 diabetes in adults
  2. DAFNE Structured Education Curriculum
  3. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 9
  4. Home PD et al. Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2017 (Degludec vs Glargine)
  5. Davies MJ et al. Diabetologia 2022 (ADA/EASD Consensus)

11. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Insulin analogues (rapid, long-acting, ultra-long)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Carbohydrate counting and dose adjustment]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Insulin pump therapy (CSII)]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) management protocol]]
  • [[Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes]]

Topic Overview

1. Definition & Exam Relevance
2. Regimen Structure
3. Starting Dose Calculation (New Diagnosis / Conversion)
Step 1: Estimate TDD
Step 2: Split Basal : Bolus
Step 3: Distribute Bolus Across Meals
Step 4: Set ICR & ISF
4. Basal Insulin Titration Protocol
5. Bolus Titration (Pattern Management)
6. Basal Insulin Choice — Decision Algorithm
7. Sick Day Rules (Basal-Bolus)
8. Conversion: Basal-Bolus ↔ Pump (CSII)
9. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls
10. Key References
11. Local Navigation
▸ 21.12.4 Capillary Blood Glucose Smbg

id: t1dm-smbg

title: "Capillary Blood Glucose (SMBG)"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • glucose-monitoring
  • smbg
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Glucose monitoring"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

Self-Monitoring of Blood Glucose (SMBG) = capillary blood glucose measured by patient using a glucometer.

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Core skill for T1DM management; exam questions on technique, frequency, targets, and interpretation of patterns.

2. Indications & Targets (ADA/NICE)

PopulationPre-prandialPost-prandial (2h)Bedtime
T1DM adults4–7 mmol/L5–9 mmol/L6–8 mmol/L
Pregnancy (T1DM)<5.3 mmol/L<7.8 mmol/L (1h) / <6.4 mmol/L (2h)5.3–6.7 mmol/L
Children/Adolescents4–7 mmol/L5–9 mmol/L6–8 mmol/L
Frail/elderly5–8 mmol/L<10 mmol/L7–10 mmol/L

Frequency: Minimum 4–6×/day (pre-meals, bedtime, ±3am); more during illness, exercise, driving, pregnancy.

3. Technique & Common Errors

StepKey Point
Hand washingWarm water, dry thoroughly — alcohol swabs alter reading
Lancet depthAdjust to skin thickness; use sides of fingertips (less pain)
Blood dropApply to strip immediately; don’t smear
CodingModern meters auto-code; verify if manual
StorageStrips in original vial, lid tight, room temp, expiry date

Common errors: Insufficient blood, expired strips, extreme temperatures, haematocrit interference (low Hct → falsely high; high Hct → falsely low), certain drugs (paracetamol, ascorbic acid on some meters).

4. Pattern Management (DAFNE Principles)

PatternInterpretationAction
Fasting highDawn phenomenon / waning insulin / SomogyiCheck 3am glucose → adjust basal
Pre-lunch highBreakfast bolus insufficient / carb undercountIncrease breakfast ICR
Post-prandial highICR too low / injection-meal gap too shortIncrease ICR or inject 15–20 min before meal
Pre-dinner highLunch bolus insufficientIncrease lunch ICR
Bedtime highDinner bolus insufficient / snack without insulinAdjust dinner ICR / cover snack
Nocturnal hypoBasal too high / missed snack / exercise effectReduce basal by 10–20%

5. SMBG vs CGM — Exam Comparison

FeatureSMBGCGM (rtCGM/isCGM)
Data points4–10/day288/day (5-min)
TrendsNoYes (arrows)
Hypo alertsNoYes (rtCGM)
Time in RangeEstimate onlyDirect calculation
CostLowHigher (but cost-effective in T1DM)
CalibrationNoneFactory calibrated (most); some need 2×/day SMBG

NICE NG17: Offer rtCGM to all T1DM adults; isCGM (Flash) if rtCGM not appropriate.

6. Special Situations

SituationSMBG Action
Driving (DVLA Group 1)Test ≤2h before journey; every 2h on long journeys; must be ≥5 mmol/L
Driving (Group 2)Test ≤1h before; every 2h; must be ≥5 mmol/L; annual consultant report
ExerciseTest before, during (if >30 min), after; target 7–10 mmol/L pre-exercise
IllnessTest 2–4 hourly; check ketones if >13.9 mmol/L or symptomatic
PregnancyTest fasting, pre-meal, 1h post-meal, bedtime; ±3am

7. Quality Assurance

  • ISO 15197:2013: 95% of results within ±15% of lab value (≥5.5 mmol/L) or ±0.83 mmol/L (<5.5 mmol/L)
  • Meter comparison: Lab venous plasma glucose vs meter capillary — expect ~1 mmol/L difference capillary > venous post-prandially
  • Patient education: DAFNE/BERTIE courses; annual review of technique

8. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • SMBG frequency in T1DM: "At least 4 times daily" is minimum; NICE says "4–10 times"
  • Driving threshold: 5 mmol/L — memorize for both Group 1 and 2
  • Dawn phenomenon vs Somogyi: 3am check distinguishes (high in dawn, low in Somogyi)
  • Paracetamol interference: Falsely elevates glucose on GDH-PQQ strips (older meters); modern GO-based strips unaffected
  • Haematocrit effect: Anaemia → falsely high; polycythaemia → falsely low

9. Key References

  1. NICE NG17 (2022) Type 1 diabetes in adults
  2. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 7: Diabetes Technology
  3. DAFNE curriculum & pattern management guide
  4. DVLA Assessing fitness to drive — Chapter 3: Diabetes
  5. ISO 15197:2013 In vitro diagnostic test systems

10. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Flash glucose monitoring]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Time in range (TIR) metrics]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Carbohydrate counting and dose adjustment]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/Hypoglycaemia classification (Level 1/2/3)]]

Topic Overview

1. Definition & Exam Relevance
2. Indications & Targets (ADA/NICE)
3. Technique & Common Errors
4. Pattern Management (DAFNE Principles)
5. SMBG vs CGM — Exam Comparison
6. Special Situations
7. Quality Assurance
8. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls
9. Key References
10. Local Navigation
▸ 21.12.5 Carbohydrate Counting And Dose Adjustment

id: t1dm-carb-counting

title: "Carbohydrate Counting and Dose Adjustment"

type: topic

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

tags:

  • diabetes
  • type-1-diabetes
  • insulin-therapy
  • carbohydrate-counting
  • icr
  • isf
  • dafne
  • fcps
  • mrcp

source: davidson-25-ch25

section: "Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus / Insulin therapy"

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

1. Definition & Exam Relevance

Carbohydrate Counting = matching rapid-acting insulin dose to carbohydrate intake using an Insulin-to-Carbohydrate Ratio (ICR).

FCPS/MRCP relevance: Core T1DM self-management skill (DAFNE); exam questions on ICR/ISF calculations, dose adjustment algorithms, troubleshooting.

2. Core Calculations

Insulin-to-Carbohydrate Ratio (ICR)

MethodFormulaExample
500 Rule500 ÷ TDD (units) = grams CHO per 1 unitTDD 40u → 500/40 = 12.5g/unit (1u per 12.5g)
300 Rule (some use)300 ÷ TDDTDD 40u → 7.5g/unit (more conservative)
Weight-based1 unit per (weight in kg × 0.5–1) g70kg → 1u per 35–70g

Starting ICR by meal (dawn phenomenon adjustments):

  • Breakfast: 1 unit per 8–10g (higher insulin resistance)
  • Lunch: 1 unit per 10–12g
  • Dinner: 1 unit per 12–15g

Insulin Sensitivity Factor (ISF) / Correction Factor

MethodFormulaExample
100 Rule100 ÷ TDD = mmol/L drop per 1 unitTDD 40u → 100/40 = 2.5 mmol/L per unit
1800 Rule (mg/dL)1800 ÷ TDD = mg/dL drop per unitTDD 40u → 45 mg/dL per unit

Target correction glucose: Usually 5.5–6.0 mmol/L (individualized).

3. Bolus Dose Calculation

Bolus Dose = (Carbohydrate grams ÷ ICR) + ((Current BG – Target BG) ÷ ISF)

Example: BG 11 mmol/L, target 5.5, meal 60g CHO, ICR 1:12, ISF 1:2.5

  • Carb bolus: 60 ÷ 12 = 5 units
  • Correction: (11 – 5.5) ÷ 2.5 = 2.2 units
  • Total = 7.2 units (round to 7 or 7.5 by pen/pump)

4. DAFNE Adjustment Algorithm (Pattern-Based)

PatternActionMagnitude
Post-prandial >9 mmol/L consistently↑ ICR (more insulin per carb)10–20% (e.g., 1:12 → 1:10)
Post-prandial <4 mmol/L consistently↓ ICR (less insulin per carb)10–20% (e.g., 1:12 → 1:15)
Pre-meal high >7 mmol/L↑ correction (↓ ISF) or check prior ICRISF: 1:2.5 → 1:2.0
Pre-meal low <4 mmol/L↓ correction (↑ ISF)ISF: 1:2.5 → 1:3.0
3am low <3.9 mmol/L↓ basal (10–20%)Basal: 20u → 16–18u
3am high >7 mmol/L↑ basal (10–20%)Basal: 20u → 22–24u

Rule: Change one parameter at a time; review after 3 days of consistent pattern.

5. Advanced Carbohydrate Counting

FactorAdjustment
Fat/ProteinHigh fat (>30g) or protein (>40g) → delayed glucose rise (3–5h); consider dual-wave bolus (pump) or split dose (pen: 60% now, 40% at 2h)
Glycaemic IndexLow GI → slower rise; may need delayed bolus or extended bolus
AlcoholInhibits gluconeogenesis → delayed hypoglycaemia (3–12h); reduce bedtime basal or snack
FiberSubtract fiber from total carbs (UK labels: "carbohydrate" = available CHO; US: subtract fiber)

6. Practical Tools & Resources

ToolUse
Carbs & Cals (book/app)Visual portion guides
MyFitnessPal / CarbsControlFood database
Nutrition labelsUK: "Carbohydrate" = available CHO per 100g/portion
Weighing scalesGold standard for accuracy
Hand portionFist = ~30g cooked rice/pasta; Palm = ~20g bread; Cupped hand = ~15g cereal

7. Special Situations

SituationICR/ISF Adjustment
Exercise (planned)↓ pre-exercise bolus by 25–50%; ↓ basal by 20–50% during/after
Exercise (unplanned)15–30g carbs per 30 min; no bolus reduction possible
Illness↑ ISF (resistance) → may need 1.5–2× usual correction; check ketones q4h
MenstruationLuteal phase: ↑ insulin needs 10–20% (progesterone effect)
PregnancyT1DM: ICR tightens progressively (1:8 → 1:6 → 1:4); ISF increases
Renal impairment (eGFR <30)↓ insulin clearance → ↓ TDD 25–50%; ↑ hypoglycaemia risk
Hepatic impairment↓ gluconeogenesis → ↑ hypoglycaemia risk; ↓ basal

8. FCPS/MRCP Exam Pearls

  • 500 Rule = ICR; 100 Rule = ISF — memorize both
  • Breakfast ICR most aggressive (dawn phenomenon); dinner most generous
  • Adjust ONE thing at a time — classic exam trap
  • Fat/protein effect: Pizza/burger → late hyperglycaemia (3–5h); needs split/extended bolus
  • Alcohol: Reactive hypoglycaemia next morning — reduce bedtime basal
  • Renal failure: Insulin half-life ↑ → dose reduction; ISF ↓ (more sensitive)
  • DAFNE: "Dose Adjustment For Normal Eating" — structured education program
  • Bolus calculator apps: Must be FDA/CE approved; patient still needs understanding

9. Key References

  1. DAFNE Curriculum & Educator Handbook
  2. NICE NG17 (2022) — Structured education
  3. Walsh J & Roberts R. Pumping Insulin (6th ed.) — ICR/ISF tables
  4. ADA Standards of Care 2024 — Section 9
  5. Diabetes UK Carbohydrate Counting Resources

10. Local Navigation

  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Basal-bolus regimen]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Insulin analogues (rapid, long-acting, ultra-long)]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Glucose monitoring/Time in range (TIR) metrics]]
  • [[Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus/Insulin therapy/Insulin pump therapy (CSII)]]
  • [[Diabetic Emergencies/Hypoglycaemia classification (Level 1/2/3)]]

Topic Overview

1. Definition & Exam Relevance
2. Core Calculations
Insulin-to-Carbohydrate Ratio (ICR)
Insulin Sensitivity Factor (ISF) / Correction Factor
3. Bolus Dose Calculation
4. DAFNE Adjustment Algorithm (Pattern-Based)
5. Advanced Carbohydrate Counting

10. Local Navigation
