▸ 21.13 21.13 19
▸ 21.13.1 Alpha Glucosidase Inhibitors

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, alpha-glucosidase-inhibitors, acarbose, miglitol, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Acarbose = reversible alpha-glucosidase inhibitor; delays carbohydrate absorption in small intestine; ↓ post-prandial glucose; no hypoglycaemia alone; GI side effects (flatulence, diarrhoea) common; dumping syndrome late hypoglycaemia; contraindicated IBD, obstruction; rarely used UK"

see_also: ["Meglitinides", "Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone)", "Oral glucose-lowering agents", "Dumping syndrome", "Post-surgery diabetes management"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Alpha-Glucosidase Inhibitors

[!info]
Acarbose (Glucobay) = reversible alpha-glucosidase inhibitor; delays complex carbohydrate digestion in small intestinal brush border; ↓ post-prandial glucose excursion; no hypoglycaemia as monotherapy; GI side effects (flatulence, diarrhoea, abdominal pain) limit use; rarely used in UK; contraindicated IBD, obstruction.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain mechanism of alpha-glucosidase inhibition
  • [ ] State indications, dosing, and titration
  • [ ] Manage gastrointestinal side effects
  • [ ] Identify contraindications and drug interactions
  • [ ] Position in T2DM treatment algorithm

2. Mechanism of Action

StepAction
1. TargetAlpha-glucosidase enzymes (sucrase, maltase, isomaltase) on small intestinal brush border
2. InhibitionReversible competitive inhibition → delays oligosaccharide/disaccharide breakdown
3. ResultDelayed glucose absorption → ↓ post-prandial glucose peak; ↑ distal carbohydrate delivery → colonic fermentation
4. Secondary↑ GLP-1, GIP (distal nutrient delivery); ↓ triglycerides (delayed fat absorption)

No effect on insulin secretion or sensitivity — works purely on carbohydrate digestion.

3. Pharmacokinetics

PropertyAcarbose
Bioavailability<2% (minimal systemic absorption)
OnsetWith first meal
Peak effect1–2 hours post-dose
DurationCovers meal + 2–3h
EliminationFaecal (>90%), renal (<2% as metabolites)
Renal impairmentNo dose adjustment (minimal absorption) — but avoid if eGFR <25 (accumulation)

4. Dosing & Titration

PhaseDose
Starting25mg TDS (with first bite of each main meal)
Titration↑ 25mg TDS every 4–8 weeks based on HbA1c/PP glucose
Target50mg TDS (standard); max 100mg TDS (200mg TDS in some countries)
AdministrationSwallow whole with first bite of meal (or chew)

Important: Must be taken with first bite — not before or after meal.

5. Efficacy

OutcomeEffect Size
HbA1c reduction−0.5 to −0.8% (monotherapy)
Post-prandial glucose↓ 2–3 mmol/L (major effect)
Fasting glucoseMinimal change
WeightNeutral or slight loss (−0.5 to −1kg)
Triglycerides↓ 10–15%
CVD outcomesNo dedicated CVOT; ACE trial (acarbose in IGT) ↓ progression to T2DM, ↓ CV events

6. Adverse Effects

Adverse EffectIncidenceManagement
Flatulence40–80% (H2, CO2, CH4 from colonic fermentation)Slow titration; low fibre initially; simethicone; usually improves 4–8w
Diarrhoea15–30%Hydration; loperamide if needed; reduce dose
Abdominal pain / distension10–20%Slow titration; smaller meals
HepatotoxicityRare (<1%) — elevated transaminasesMonitor LFTs baseline, 3m, 6m; stop if >3x ULN

Side effects dose-related — slow titration essential.

7. Contraindications & Cautions

ContraindicationReason
Inflammatory bowel diseaseExacerbates diarrhoea, ulceration
Intestinal obstruction / herniaRisk of obstruction (gas, distension)
Significant chronic intestinal diseaseMalabsorption, short bowel
Renal impairment eGFR <25Accumulation (minimal but theoretical)
Pregnancy / BreastfeedingSafety not established
ChildrenNot studied

8. Drug Interactions

InteractionEffect
Digoxin↓ Digoxin absorption → monitor levels
Other oral drugsMay delay absorption → separate by 1–2h if critical
Insulin / SulfonylureaNo hypoglycaemia alone — but additive if combined (reduce insulin/SU)
CholestyramineMay bind acarbose → separate administration

9. Special Use: Post-Bariatric Late Dumping (Reactive Hypoglycaemia)

IndicationLate dumping syndrome (reactive hypoglycaemia post-RYGB/Sleeve)
Dose50–100mg TDS with meals
MechanismDelays carbohydrate absorption → blunts insulin surge
AlternativeOctreotide (if acarbose fails)

10. Position in Treatment Algorithm (NICE NG28 / ADA)

LineRole
MonotherapyIf metformin contraindicated/intolerant + post-prandial hyperglycaemia dominant
Add-onRarely used; not in NICE pathway (limited evidence, GI side effects)
NICENot in main pathway; specialist use only
ADALow-cost option if post-prandial focus; not preferred

11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MechanismReversible alpha-glucosidase inhibitor (brush border) → delays carb absorption
Main effect↓ Post-prandial glucose (2–3mmol/L); minimal fasting effect
HbA1c reduction−0.5 to −0.8%
HypoglycaemiaNone as monotherapy
Main side effectFlatulence 40–80% (colonic fermentation); diarrhoea
TitrationStart 25mg TDS → 50mg TDS → max 100mg TDS over 4–8w
AdministrationWith first bite of meal
ContraindicationsIBD, obstruction, chronic intestinal disease
RenalNo adjustment (eGFR ≥25); avoid <25
Special useLate dumping syndrome post-bariatric (acarbose 50–100mg TDS)

12. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Oral glucose-lowering agents|Oral glucose-lowering agents]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Meglitinides]], [[Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone)]], [[Dumping syndrome]], [[Post-surgery diabetes management]]

Topic Overview

Alpha-Glucosidase Inhibitors
1. Learning Objectives
2. Mechanism of Action
3. Pharmacokinetics
4. Dosing & Titration
5. Efficacy
6. Adverse Effects
7. Contraindications & Cautions
8. Drug Interactions
9. Special Use: Post-Bariatric Late Dumping (Reactive Hypoglycaemia)
10. Position in Treatment Algorithm (NICE NG28 / ADA)
11. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
12. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.13.10 Concentrated Insulins U 200 U 300 U 500

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, concentrated-insulin, u200, u300, u500, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Concentrated insulins: U200 (degludec, lispro), U300 (glargine), U500 (human regular); for high insulin resistance (>1.5u/kg or >100u/day); reduced volume, improved absorption, less injection site pain; dedicated pens/syringes; DO NOT CONVERT units"

see_also: ["Basal insulin initiation", "Basal-bolus intensification", "Pre-mixed insulin", "Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes", "Insulin resistance"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Concentrated Insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500)

[!info]
Concentrated insulins = higher units/mL for high insulin resistance; U200: degludec (Tresiba 200), lispro (Humalog 200); U300: glargine (Toujeo); U500: human regular (Humulin R U500); do not convert units — prescribe and dispense in units; dedicated pens/syringes only.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify indications for concentrated insulins
  • [ ] Differentiate U200, U300, U500 products
  • [ ] Prescribe and dispense safely (unit-based, no conversion)
  • [ ] Educate patients on device use
  • [ ] Understand pharmacokinetic advantages

2. Products & Indications

ConcentrationProductTypeIndication
U200 (200 units/mL)Degludec (Tresiba 200)Ultra-long basalHigh basal requirement (>80u/day); flexible timing
Lispro (Humalog 200)RapidHigh bolus requirement; pump users
U300 (300 units/mL)Glargine (Toujeo)Long basalHigh basal requirement (>80u/day); lower hypo vs U100
U500 (500 units/mL)Human Regular (Humulin R U500)Short/intermediateExtreme insulin resistance (>200u/day); IV/subQ

3. Pharmacokinetic Advantages

FeatureU100 StandardConcentrated (U200/U300/U500)
Injection volumeHigh (e.g., 80u = 0.8mL)50–80% less volume (e.g., 80u U200 = 0.4mL)
AbsorptionVariable at high volumesMore consistent (smaller depot)
Subcutaneous depotLarge → erraticSmaller → predictable
Pain/discomfortMore at high volumesLess
PharmacokineticsStandardSimilar or flatter (U300 glargine flatter than U100)

4. Indications for Use

Patient ProfileThreshold
High basal insulin dose>80–100 units/day (U200/U300)
High bolus insulin dose>30–50 units/meal (U200 lispro)
Total daily dose>200 units/day (U500)
Insulin resistanceBMI >40, lipodystrophy, acanthosis nigricans, steroid-induced
Injection site issuesLipohypertrophy, pain, limited sites
Pump usersHigh reservoir volume needs (U200 lispro)

5. Prescribing & Dispensing — Critical Safety Rules

RuleDetail
Prescribe in UNITSNever convert (e.g., "80 units" not "0.8mL" or "40 units U100 equivalent")
Dedicated penEach concentration has unique pen device (Tresiba U200 pen ≠ Tresiba U100 pen)
No vial/syringe for U200/U300Pen only (fixed ratio dial); U500 has dedicated U500 syringe (green cap)
Pharmacy dispensingMust match exact product + concentration
Patient education"Dial your dose in units" — pen shows units, not mL

6. Product-Specific Details

Degludec U200 (Tresiba 200)

  • Same PK as U100 (duration >42h, flat)
  • Pen: 600 units (3mL × 200u/mL)
  • Dose range: 1–160 units (2-unit increments)
  • Advantage: Fewer pen changes; flexible timing

Insulin Lispro U200 (Humalog 200)

  • Same PK as U100 lispro (onset 15min, peak 1h, duration 4h)
  • Pen: 600 units (3mL × 200u/mL)
  • Use: High bolus needs; pump reservoir (less frequent changes)
  • Do NOT use U200 in pump unless pump calibrated for U200

Glargine U300 (Toujeo)

  • Flatter profile vs U100; lower nocturnal hypoglycaemia
  • Pen: 450 units (1.5mL × 300u/mL)
  • Dose range: 1–80 units (1-unit increments)
  • Onset slightly slower; full effect day 3–5

Human Regular U500 (Humulin R U500)

  • Onset 30min, peak 2–4h, duration 8–12h
  • Vial + dedicated U500 syringe (green cap, 0.5mL = 250u)
  • SubQ: 2–3x daily; IV: continuous infusion (ICU)
  • For extreme resistance (>200u/day)

7. Switching from U100 to Concentrated

PrincipleAction
Unit-for-unitSame number of units (80u U100 → 80u U200)
Basal switchSame total daily basal units; monitor fasting
Bolus switchSame ICR/ISF initially; monitor PP
Monitor closelyFirst 1–2 weeks; some need 10–20% dose reduction (better absorption)
Pen trainingMandatory — different dial mechanics

Do NOT: Convert based on volume (mL) or "equivalence" calculations.

8. Common Errors & Prevention

ErrorPrevention
Prescribing "mL" instead of unitsEPR template: "Prescribe in units only"
Dispensing U100 pen for U200 prescriptionPharmacy check: product name + concentration
Patient using U100 syringe for U500Dedicated U500 syringe (green cap) only
Pump filled with U200 without calibrationPump must support U200; recalibrate
Dose confusion at transitionsWritten plan; "units = units" education

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
U200 productsDegludec (Tresiba 200), Lispro (Humalog 200)
U300 productGlargine (Toujeo)
U500 productHuman Regular (Humulin R U500)
IndicationHigh insulin resistance; basal >80–100u/day
Volume reduction50–80% less injection volume
Prescribing ruleUnits = units (no conversion)
Dedicated devicesEach concentration = unique pen/syringe
U500 syringeGreen cap dedicated syringe
PK advantageSmaller depot → more consistent absorption
Toujeo vs LantusU300 flatter, lower nocturnal hypo

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Topic Overview

Concentrated Insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500

2. Products & Indications
3. Pharmacokinetic Advantages
4. Indications for Use
5. Prescribing & Dispensing — Critical Safety Rules
6. Product-Specific Details
Degludec U200 (Tresiba 200)
Insulin Lispro U200 (Humalog 200)
Glargine U300 (Toujeo)

▸ 21.13.18 Medical Nutrition Therapy

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, medical-nutrition-therapy, mnt, diet, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - MNT = cornerstone T2DM management; 500kcal deficit → 0.5kg/wk weight loss; carb 40–45% (low GI, 130g/min), protein 15–20%, fat <35% (<10% sat); Mediterranean/DASH patterns; dietitian referral; weight loss 5–15% → remission possible"

see_also: ["Physical activity and exercise", "Weight management and remission", "Type 2 diabetes remission", "Lifestyle intervention", "Metabolic surgery"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT)

[!info]
MNT = cornerstone of T2DM management; 500kcal/day deficit → 0.5kg/week weight loss; carb 40–45% (low GI, ≥130g/day), protein 15–20%, fat <35% (<10% saturated); Mediterranean / DASH patterns; dietitian referral; 5–15% weight loss → remission possible.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define macronutrient distribution for T2DM
  • [ ] Apply caloric restriction for weight loss
  • [ ] Recommend dietary patterns (Mediterranean, DASH, low carb)
  • [ ] Address special populations (CKD, elderly, pregnancy)
  • [ ] Understand remission nutrition (very low calorie diets)

2. Macronutrient Distribution (ADA / EASD / NICE)

NutrientTargetRationale
Carbohydrate40–45% total calories (≥130g/day minimum)Glucose control; brain fuel; fibre source
Protein15–20% (1.0–1.5g/kg ideal BW)Satiety; muscle preservation; glycaemic neutral
Fat<35% (<10% saturated)CVD risk reduction; calorie density

Carbohydrate Quality > Quantity:

  • Low GI/GL (oats, legumes, whole grains, vegetables)
  • Fibre ≥30g/day (soluble: oats, psyllium, legumes)
  • Avoid: SSBs, refined grains, added sugars, ultra-processed

3. Caloric Restriction & Weight Loss

TargetStrategy
Weight loss5–15% (5% = metabolic benefit; 15% = remission)
Caloric deficit500–750 kcal/day below TDEE
Rate0.5–1kg/week
Protein during weight loss1.2–1.5g/kg (preserve lean mass)
Physical activityCombined with diet (see Physical Activity note)

DiRECT Trial: 800kcal/day formula diet → 46% remission at 1y (15kg loss); 70% remission if >15kg loss.

4. Dietary Patterns — Evidence-Based

PatternKey FeaturesDiabetes Evidence
MediterraneanEVOO, nuts, fish, vegetables, legumes, whole grains, winePREDIMED: ↓ T2DM incidence 30–40%; ↓ CVD
DASHFruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, whole grains, low sodium↓ BP; ↓ insulin resistance; ↓ T2DM risk
Plant-based / VegetarianLegumes, nuts, whole grains, minimal animal↓ HbA1c; ↓ weight; ↓ CVD risk
Low Carb (<130g/day)<26% carb; higher protein/fat↓ HbA1c short-term; weight loss; LDL may rise; long-term safety unclear
Very Low Calorie (VLCD)800kcal/day (formula)DiRECT: 46% remission; requires medical supervision

NICE/ADA: Individualised — no single "diabetic diet"; Mediterranean/DASH first-line.

4. Special Considerations

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

StageProteinPotassiumPhosphateSodium
G3a–G3b0.8g/kg (avoid >1.0)Restrict if >5.5Restrict if elevated<2.3g/day
G4–G50.6–0.8g/kg + ketoanaloguesRestrictRestrict (binders)<2g/day

Elderly / Frail

  • Avoid malnutrition > strict glycaemic control
  • Protein ≥1.2g/kg (sarcopenia prevention)
  • Liberalised diet (quality of life)
  • Oral nutritional supplements if intake inadequate

Pregnancy (GDM / Pre-existing)

  • Carb 175g/day minimum (fetal brain)
  • 3 meals + 2–3 snacks (prevent ketosis)
  • Low GI, high fibre
  • Folic acid 5mg (pre-conception)

5. Practical MNT Delivery

StepAction
1. AssessmentDiet history (24h recall, food frequency), weight, HbA1c, lipids, renal
2. Goal SettingSMART goals (weight, HbA1c, dietary changes)
3. EducationCarb awareness, label reading, portion sizes, cooking methods
3. MonitoringWeight monthly; HbA1c 3–6m; dietitian review 3–6m
4. BehaviouralSelf-monitoring (food diary, apps), problem-solving, relapse prevention

Referral: Dietitian at diagnosis (NICE); annual review; more frequent if not at target.

6. Weight Loss & Remission — Nutrition

ProgrammeDietWeight LossRemission
DiRECT800kcal formula (12–20w) → food reintroduction15kg (mean)46% at 1y, 36% at 2y
DROPLET810kcal formula (GP-led)10kg27% at 1y
Low Carb Program<130g carb (digital)7–10kg25–30%
Mediterranean + ExerciseAd lib Mediterranean + 150min/wk4–6kg15–20%

Remission definition (DiRECT): HbA1c <48 mmol/mol off all glucose medications for ≥2 months.

7. Common Myths vs Evidence

MythEvidence
"Avoid all carbs"Carbs essential; quality > quantity; <130g not needed for all
"Fruit is bad"Whole fruit ↓ T2DM risk; juice/dried fruit ↑ risk
"Special diabetic foods"No benefit; often high fat/calorie; normal food + portions
"High protein damages kidneys"Only if CKD G4–G5; normal kidneys tolerate 1.5–2g/kg
"Artificial sweeteners cause diabetes"No causal evidence; useful for SSB replacement

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MNT cornerstoneT2DM management foundation
Carb target40–45% calories (≥130g/day); low GI, high fibre
Weight loss target5–15% (5% = metabolic benefit; 15% = remission)
Caloric deficit500–750 kcal/day
Best dietary patternMediterranean (PREDIMED: ↓ T2DM 30–40%, ↓ CVD)
DiRECT remission800kcal formula → 46% at 1y (15kg loss)
Protein in CKDG3: 0.8g/kg; G4–5: 0.6–0.8g/kg + ketoanalogues
ElderlyPrevent malnutrition > strict control; protein ≥1.2g/kg
Carb minimum pregnancy175g/day
Meal pattern3 meals + 2–3 snacks (GDM)

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Lifestyle intervention|Lifestyle intervention]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Physical activity and exercise]], [[Weight management and remission]], [[Type 2 diabetes remission]], [[Metabolic surgery]]

Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT

2. Macronutrient Distribution (ADA / EASD / NICE)
3. Caloric Restriction & Weight Loss
4. Dietary Patterns — Evidence-Based
4. Special Considerations
Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
Elderly / Frail
Pregnancy (GDM / Pre-existing)

▸ 21.13.19 Meglitinides

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, meglitinides, repaglinide, nateglinide, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Meglitinides (repaglinide, nateglinide) = rapid-acting insulin secretagogues; pre-meal dosing; short duration; ↓ post-prandial glucose; hypoglycaemia risk; weight gain; renal adjustment; alternative to sulfonylurea if irregular meals; rarely used UK"

see_also: ["Sulfonylureas", "Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors", "Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone)", "Oral glucose-lowering agents", "Basal insulin initiation"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Meglitinides

[!info]
Meglitinides (Repaglinide, Nateglinide) = rapid-acting insulin secretagogues; pre-meal dosing (15min before); short duration (4–6h); ↓ post-prandial glucose; hypoglycaemia risk; weight gain; renal adjustment; alternative to sulfonylurea for irregular meal patterns; rarely used UK (cost, dosing frequency).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate meglitinides from sulfonylureas
  • [ ] State indications, dosing, and titration
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia risk
  • [ ] Apply renal/hepatic adjustments
  • [ ] Position in treatment algorithm

2. Agents

PropertyRepaglinide (Prandin)Nateglinide (Starlix)
ReceptorSUR1 (distinct from SU site on K-ATP channel)Same
Onset15–30 min10–20 min
Peak1 hour30–60 min
Duration4–6 hours3–4 hours
PotencyHigherLower
Meal flexibilityTake 15 min before each meal; omit if meal skippedSame

3. Mechanism of Action

StepAction
1. BindingBinds SUR1 subunit of K-ATP channel (distinct site from sulfonylureas)
2. Channel closure↓ K+ efflux → membrane depolarisation
3. Ca2+ influxVoltage-gated Ca2+ channels open
4. Insulin releaseRapid, short-lived insulin secretory burst
Key difference from SUShorter duration; glucose-dependent (less release at low glucose)

4. Dosing & Titration

Repaglinide

PhaseDose
Starting0.5mg TDS (pre-meal)
TitrationDouble dose every 1–2 weeks based on PP glucose
Max4mg TDS (16mg/day)
Renal adjustmentChild-Pugh A/B: 0.5mg TDS start, max 4mg TDS; Child-Pugh C: avoid

Nateglinide

PhaseDose
Starting60mg TDS (pre-meal)
Titration↑ to 120mg TDS if PP glucose not controlled
Max120mg TDS
Renal adjustmentNo adjustment (hepatic metabolism) — caution eGFR <30

Administration: 1–15 minutes before each main meal; omit if meal skipped.

5. Efficacy

OutcomeRepaglinideNateglinide
HbA1c reduction−1.0 to −1.5%−0.5 to −1.0%
PP glucose reduction−3 to −4 mmol/L−2 to −3 mmol/L
Fasting glucoseModest ↓Minimal
Weight gain1–3kg1–2kg
Hypoglycaemia riskLow–moderate (glucose-dependent)Low (lowest among secretagogues)

6. Adverse Effects

Adverse EffectIncidenceManagement
HypoglycaemiaLow–moderate (glucose-dependent; less than SU)✓ Omit dose if meal skipped; ✓ Educate on hypo symptoms
Weight gain1–3kgDietary counselling; consider GLP-1/SGLT2i if significant
GI (nausea, abdominal pain)<5%Take with food
Liver enzymesRare ↑ ALT/ASTMonitor baseline, 3m; stop if >3x ULN
Upper respiratory infection<5% (repaglinide)Symptomatic

7. Contraindications & Cautions

ConditionRepaglinideNateglinide
Type 1 DMContraindicatedContraindicated
DKAContraindicatedContraindicated
Severe hepatic impairmentContraindicatedCaution (hepatic metabolism)
Severe renal impairmentCaution (no dose adjustment)No adjustment
PregnancyCategory C (avoid)Category C (avoid)
GemfibrozilContraindicated (↑ repaglinide 8x)Caution
CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole, clarithromycin)Dose reduce / avoidCaution
CYP2C8 inhibitors (trimethoprim, montelukast)CautionContraindicated with gemfibrozil

8. Key Difference: Meglitinide vs Sulfonylurea

FeatureMeglitinideSulfonylurea
DosingPre-meal (TDS)Once/twice daily
Meal skippingOmit dose (no hypo)Hypo risk if dose taken without meal
DurationShort (3–6h)Long (12–24h)
HypoglycaemiaLower (glucose-dependent)Higher (prolonged)
Weight gainSimilarSimilar
Renal adjustmentRepaglinide: hepatic; Nateglinide: none neededMost need adjustment (except gliclazide)
CostHigherLow (generic)
Use caseIrregular meals, shift work, renal impairmentRegular meals, cost-sensitive

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MeglitinidesRepaglinide, Nateglinide — rapid-acting secretagogues
DosingPre-meal (15min before); omit if meal skipped
Repaglinide max4mg TDS (16mg/day)
Nateglinide max120mg TDS
Hypoglycaemia riskLower than SU (glucose-dependent, short duration)
Repaglinide hepaticContraindicated severe hepatic impairment
Repaglinide + gemfibrozilContraindicated (↑ 8x AUC via CYP2C8/OATP1B1)
Nateglinide renalNo dose adjustment needed
IndicationIrregular meals, shift work, renal impairment (alternative to SU)
UK useRarely used (cost, TDS dosing, generic SU preferred)

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Sulfonylureas]], [[Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors]], [[Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone)]], [[Basal insulin initiation]]

Topic Overview

Meglitinides
1. Learning Objectives
2. Agents
3. Mechanism of Action
4. Dosing & Titration
Repaglinide
Nateglinide
5. Efficacy
6. Adverse Effects
7. Contraindications & Cautions
8. Key Difference: Meglitinide vs Sulfonylurea
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.13.2 Amylin Analogues Pramlintide

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, amylin, pramlintide, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Pramlintide = synthetic amylin analogue; co-secreted with insulin; slows gastric emptying, suppresses glucagon, promotes satiety; adjunct to insulin in T1DM/T2DM; ↓ post-prandial glucose, ↓ weight; nausea main SE; not commonly used UK"

see_also: ["Injectable non-insulin therapy", "GLP-1 receptor agonists", "Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes", "Incretin physiology"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Amylin Analogues (Pramlintide)

[!info]
Pramlintide = synthetic amylin analogue (amylin co-secreted with insulin by β-cells); adjunct to insulin in T1DM/T2DM; slows gastric emptying, suppresses post-prandial glucagon, promotes satiety; ↓ post-prandial glucose, ↓ weight; nausea main side effect; not licensed UK (US FDA only).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain amylin physiology and deficiency in diabetes
  • [ ] Describe pramlintide mechanism of action
  • [ ] State indications, dosing, and titration
  • [ ] Manage side effects (nausea, hypoglycaemia)
  • [ ] Position relative to GLP-1 RA

2. Amylin Physiology

FeatureDetail
Co-secreted withInsulin (1:10–1:100 molar ratio) from β-cells
Deficient inT1DM (absolute); T2DM (relative, impaired secretion)
ActionsSlows gastric emptying; suppresses post-prandial glucagon; promotes satiety (hypothalamic); inhibits digestive enzymes
Net effectReduces post-prandial glucose excursion without increasing hypoglycaemia risk (when insulin adjusted)

3. Pramlintide (Symlin) — Pharmacology

PropertyDetail
StructureSynthetic analogue; 3 proline substitutions (positions 25, 28, 29) → prevents amyloid fibrillation
RouteSubcutaneous (separate injection from insulin)
Half-life~50 minutes
Onset~20 minutes
Duration3–4 hours
ClearanceRenal (no dose adjustment mild/mod CKD; avoid severe)

4. Indications & Dosing

FDA Indications (US)

  • T1DM: Adjunct to mealtime insulin (failed optimal control)
  • T2DM: Adjunct to mealtime insulin (± sulfonylurea, metformin)

Dosing Protocol

PopulationStarting DoseTitrationTarget Dose
T1DM15 µg pre-meal↑ 15 µg q3–7d30–60 µg pre-meal
T2DM60 µg pre-meal↑ 60 µg q3–7d120 µg pre-meal

Insulin adjustment: Reduce mealtime insulin by 50% at pramlintide initiation → then titrate based on glucose.

5. Efficacy

OutcomeEffect
HbA1c−0.3 to −0.5% (vs insulin alone)
Post-prandial glucose↓ 2–3 mmol/L (major effect)
Weight−1.5 to −2.5 kg (satiety)
Insulin dose↓ 10–20% (due to reduced post-prandial excursion)
Fasting glucoseMinimal change

6. Adverse Effects

Adverse EffectIncidenceManagement
Nausea30–50% (dose-limiting)Slow titration; take with food; antiemetic if needed; usually transient (4–8w)
Vomiting10–15%Reduce dose; extend titration interval
Hypoglycaemia↑ if insulin not reducedMandatory 50% mealtime insulin reduction at start
AnorexiaCommonContributes to weight loss
Injection site reactionsMildRotate sites
Anti-drug antibodies~20%No clinical significance

7. Contraindications & Cautions

ContraindicationReason
GastroparesisWorsens delayed emptying
Hypoglycaemia unawarenessRisk of severe hypo with insulin reduction complexity
Severe renal impairment (eGFR <30)Limited data; avoid
PregnancyCategory C; no human data
PaediatricNot studied <18y

8. Pramlintide vs GLP-1 RA

FeaturePramlintideGLP-1 RA
Meal-timeYes (pre-meal)Daily/weekly (not meal-timed)
Weight loss−1.5 to −2.5 kg−3 to −15 kg (semaglutide 2.4mg)
HbA1c reduction−0.3 to −0.5%−1.0 to −2.0%
Nausea30–50% (high)15–25% (dose-dependent)
Hypoglycaemia riskLow (if insulin reduced)Low
Cost/availabilityUS only; not UK/EUGlobal; multiple agents
PositioningNiche (adjunct to insulin)Preferred for weight/HbA1c

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
PramlintideAmylin analogue (synthetic); 3 proline substitutions
Amylin physiologyCo-secreted with insulin; slows gastric emptying, suppresses glucagon, satiety
Deficient inT1DM (absolute), T2DM (relative)
Dosing T1DMStart 15µg pre-meal → 30–60µg
Dosing T2DMStart 60µg pre-meal → 120µg
Insulin reductionReduce mealtime insulin 50% at initiation
Main side effectNausea 30–50% (dose-limiting); slow titration
Weight loss−1.5 to −2.5 kg
HbA1c reduction−0.3 to −0.5%
vs GLP-1 RAGLP-1 RA preferred (better efficacy, less nausea, global availability)
UK statusNot licensed (US FDA only)

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

FDA Indications (US)
Dosing Protocol

modified: 2026-06-16

  • "[[Diabetes MOC]]"

Metabolic surgery

[!info]
Topic-Group hub for Metabolic surgery — covers 2 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus/Metabolic surgery/Bariatric surgery indications.md|Bariatric surgery indications]]
  • [[Post-surgery management]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
Indications (NICE/ADA)BMI ≥35 (or ≥30 with comorbidities) · T2DM <10 years · C-peptide >200 pmol/L · Failed medical therapy
ProceduresRYGB (gold standard), Sleeve gastrectomy (most common), OAGB, BPD/DS
Remission ratesRYGB: 80% at 1y, 50% at 10y · SG: 60% at 1y · BPD/DS: 95% at 1y
MechanismsWeight loss, ↓ghrelin, ↑GLP-1/PYY, bile acid changes, microbiome, ↓caloric absorption
Post-surgeryStop insulin/sulfonylurea immediate · Continue metformin · Monitor B12, iron, Ca, vit D · Hypoglycaemia risk (especially dumping)

Quick Links

  • Parent Heading: [[../Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus|Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]

  • Topic Template: [[../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
  • Drug Reference: [[../../Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing|Drugs]]

▸ 21.13.24 Physical Activity And Exercise

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, physical-activity, exercise, aerobic, resistance, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - 150min/week moderate aerobic + 2–3x/week resistance; post-meal walk ↓ PP glucose 1–2mmol/L; HIIT effective; avoid exercise if glucose >14 + ketones; adjust insulin/carbs; CGM-guided exercise; sedentary breaks every 30min"

see_also: ["Medical nutrition therapy", "Weight management and remission", "Lifestyle intervention", "Hypoglycaemia in hospital", "Frailty and individualised HbA1c targets"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Physical Activity and Exercise

[!info]
ADA/NICE/ESC: 150min/week moderate aerobic + 2–3x/week resistance; post-meal walk ↓ PP glucose 1–2mmol/L; HIIT time-efficient; exercise snippets (sedentary breaks) every 30min; adjust insulin/carbs; CGM-guided; avoid if glucose >14 + ketones.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Prescribe exercise per guideline recommendations (frequency, intensity, type)
  • [ ] Differentiate aerobic, resistance, HIIT, flexibility benefits
  • [ ] Manage glucose around exercise (insulin, carbs, CGM)
  • [ ] Apply special considerations (complications, elderly, pregnancy)
  • [ ] Address sedentary behaviour reduction

2. Guideline Recommendations (ADA 2024 / NICE / ESC)

ComponentPrescription
Aerobic≥150 min/week moderate (3–6 METs) OR 75 min/week vigorous (>6 METs)
Frequency≥3 days/week; no >2 consecutive days without
Resistance2–3 sessions/week (non-consecutive); 8–10 exercises, 1–3 sets, 8–15 reps
Flexibility/Balance2–3x/week (yoga, tai chi) — especially elderly/neuropathy
Sedentary breaksEvery 30min — stand, walk 2–3min (↓ post-prandial glucose)

Total: ~150min aerobic + 60min resistance + flexibility weekly.

3. Exercise Types & Glycaemic Effects

TypeExamplesGlycaemic EffectKey Notes
Moderate aerobicBrisk walk, cycling, swimming, elliptical↓ glucose during/after (↑ insulin sensitivity 24–48h)40–60% HRmax or RPE 12–13 (talk test)
Vigorous aerobicRunning, HIIT, fast cyclingRapid ↓ glucose (↑ GLUT4 translocation)Risk of delayed hypoglycaemia (6–12h post)
HIIT4×4min at 85–95% HRmax + 3min recoveryTime-efficient; ↑ VO2max, insulin sensitivityNot for unfit/comorbid without screening
ResistanceWeights, bands, bodyweight↑ muscle mass → ↑ basal metabolic rate; ↓ insulin resistanceMajor muscle groups; progressive overload
Flexibility/BalanceYoga, tai chi, stretchingIndirect (↓ falls, ↑ adherence)Essential for neuropathy/elderly

4. Glucose Management Around Exercise

Pre-Exercise Assessment

GlucoseAction
<4.0 mmol/LTreat hypo first; delay exercise
4.0–6.9Carb snack 10–20g (if on insulin/sulfonylurea)
7.0–10.0Safe to start (ideal)
10.1–13.9Safe; monitor if on insulin
14.0–16.6Check ketones; if negative → OK with caution
>16.7 + ketonesDO NOT EXERCISE — risk DKA

Insulin/Carb Adjustment

Exercise TypeInsulin AdjustmentCarb Intake
Aerobic 30–60min↓ Basal 20–50% (if pump: temp basal 50–80%); ↓ Bolus 25–50%15–30g/hr if >60min or glucose <7
HIIT / Resistance↓ Basal 10–20%; ↓ Bolus 25%Often not needed (glucose may rise initially)
Prolonged (>90min)↓ Basal 50–80%; consider carb + protein during30–60g/hr + electrolytes

CGM-guided: Trend arrows ↑ → reduce carb; ↓ → add carb; → → standard.

Post-Exercise

  • Monitor 6–24h (delayed hypoglycaemia risk, esp. after aerobic)
  • Reduce basal 10–20% overnight if evening exercise
  • Protein + carb recovery (e.g., milk, yogurt) if prolonged

5. Special Populations

PopulationModifications
T1DMCGM essential; detailed insulin/carb plan; ketone check if >14
Elderly / FrailChair-based, balance, resistance bands; HIIT contraindicated; supervision
Peripheral neuropathyNon-weight-bearing (cycling, swimming, chair); daily foot check; proper footwear
Autonomic neuropathyAvoid Valsalva; monitor HR/BP; supine exercises; gradual position changes
CKD / DialysisExercise on non-dialysis days; avoid vascular access arm; fluid balance
Pregnancy (GDM/pre-existing)Moderate only; avoid supine >16w; HR <140; no contact sports; 150min/wk
Cardiovascular diseaseCardiac rehab referral; stress test if high intensity; avoid ischaemic threshold

6. Sedentary Behaviour — Independent Risk Factor

RecommendationEvidence
Break sitting every 30min2–3min light walk → ↓ post-prandial glucose 20–30%
Standing desk↓ Sedentary time 1–2h/day
TV/computer breaksAd breaks = movement cues
Step target7,000–10,000 steps/day (dose-response for mortality)

Key: Sedentary time independent of exercise predicts mortality and T2DM progression.

7. Exercise Prescription Template (FITT-VP)

ComponentPrescription
FrequencyAerobic ≥3d/wk; Resistance 2–3d/wk; Flexibility 2–3d/wk
IntensityModerate (RPE 12–13, "talk test") → Vigorous as tolerated
TimeAerobic 30–60min/session; Resistance 20–30min
TypeAerobic + Resistance + Flexibility/Balance
Volume150min aerobic + 60min resistance weekly
ProgressionIncrease duration → frequency → intensity (gradual)

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Aerobic minimum150min/week moderate (≥3 days, no >2 days off)
Resistance2–3x/week, non-consecutive, major muscle groups
Sedentary breaksEvery 30min — 2–3min walk
Post-meal walk10–15min → ↓ PP glucose 1–2mmol/L
Pre-exercise glucose <4Treat hypo first
Pre-exercise glucose >14 + ketonesDo not exercise
T1DM on insulin↓ Bolus 25–50%; ↓ Basal 20–50% (temp basal); carb snack if <7
Delayed hypo6–12h post-aerobic; reduce overnight basal
NeuropathyNon-weight-bearing (cycling, swimming)
HIITTime-efficient; screen first; not for unfit/comorbid

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Lifestyle intervention|Lifestyle intervention]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Medical nutrition therapy]], [[Weight management and remission]], [[Frailty and individualised HbA1c targets]], [[Hypoglycaemia in hospital]]

Topic Overview

Physical Activity and Exercise
1. Learning Objectives
2. Guideline Recommendations (ADA 2024 / NICE / ESC)
3. Exercise Types & Glycaemic Effects
4. Glucose Management Around Exercise
Pre-Exercise Assessment
Insulin/Carb Adjustment
Post-Exercise
5. Special Populations
6. Sedentary Behaviour — Independent Risk Factor
7. Exercise Prescription Template (FITT-VP)
8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.13.26 Pre Mixed Insulin

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pre-mixed-insulin, biphasic, initiation, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Pre-mixed insulin = fixed ratio rapid + intermediate (30/70, 25/75, 50/50); BD dosing (breakfast/dinner); for regular meals, unable carb count; inferior PP control vs basal-bolus; higher hypo risk; initiate 10u BD; titrate weekly; stop sulfonylurea"

see_also: ["Basal insulin initiation", "Basal-bolus intensification", "Concentrated insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500)", "Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes", "Treatment algorithms/NICE NG28 pathway"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Pre-Mixed Insulin

[!info]
Pre-mixed insulin = fixed ratio of rapid-acting + intermediate-acting (NPH or protamine) in one pen; BD dosing (pre-breakfast, pre-dinner); for patients with regular meals, unable to carb count; inferior post-prandial control vs basal-bolus; higher hypoglycaemia risk; not first-line (NICE: only if basal-bolus not feasible).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify available pre-mixed insulin ratios and components
  • [ ] Select appropriate patients for pre-mixed therapy
  • [ ] Initiate and titrate pre-mixed insulin safely
  • [ ] Compare pre-mixed vs basal-bolus regimens
  • [ ] Recognise limitations and transition criteria

2. Available Pre-Mixed Insulins

ProductRatio (Rapid/Intermediate)Rapid ComponentIntermediate ComponentProfile
Novomix 3030/70Aspart (30%)Aspart protamine (70%)Peak ~2–4h; duration ~12–16h
Humalog Mix 2525/75Lispro (25%)Lispro protamine (75%)Peak ~1–3h; duration ~12–16h
Humalog Mix 5050/50Lispro (50%)Lispro protamine (50%)Higher rapid for larger meals
Humulin M330/70Human regular (30%)NPH (70%)Human insulin; more peak
Insuman Comb 25/7525/75Human regular (25%)NPH (75%)Human insulin

Most common: Novomix 30 (30/70) and Humalog Mix 25 (25/75).

3. Indications for Pre-Mixed Insulin (NICE NG28)

IndicationCriteria
Regular meal patternFixed mealtimes (breakfast, dinner ~12h apart); similar carb content
Unable/Unwilling to carb countCognitive impairment, literacy issues, no structured education access
Basal-bolus not feasibleInjection burden (4–5/day), needle phobia, poor dexterity
SimplificationElderly, care home, complex regimens failing
Cost-consciousSingle pen vs multiple; but analogues cost similar

NICE position: Only if basal-bolus not possible — not first-line.

4. Contraindications / Avoid

ScenarioReason
Variable meal timesFixed peak → hypoglycaemia if meal delayed
Variable carbohydrate intakeFixed ratio → mismatch
Shift workCircadian mismatch
High post-prandial excursionsFixed rapid % often insufficient
Hypoglycaemia unawarenessHigher nocturnal hypo risk (intermediate peak)
Renal impairmentNPH/protamine accumulation → prolonged effect

5. Initiation Protocol

Starting Dose

MethodCalculation
Weight-based0.3–0.5 units/kg/day total (split ~50/50 or 60/40 breakfast/dinner)
Fixed10–12 units BD (pre-breakfast, pre-dinner)
From basal insulinSame total daily units split BD (e.g., basal 30u → 15u + 15u)

Typical start: 10–12 units pre-breakfast + 10–12 units pre-dinner.

Stop Sulfonylurea

  • Taper over 1–2 weeks as pre-mixed initiated (hypoglycaemia risk)

Continue

  • Metformin (Yes)
  • SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA / DPP-4i (Yes)
  • Pioglitazone (Caution: weight gain + oedema)

6. Titration Algorithm

TargetPre-Breakfast GlucosePre-Dinner GlucoseAction
Fasting / Pre-lunch4–7 mmol/L—Adjust dinner dose
Pre-dinner—4–7 mmol/LAdjust breakfast dose
If <4.0AnyAny↓ 2–4 units relevant dose
If 4–7AnyAnyNo change
If 7–10AnyAny↑ 2–4 units relevant dose
If >10AnyAny↑ 4–6 units relevant dose

Frequency: Weekly until stable; then monthly.

Nocturnal hypoglycaemia → reduce dinner dose 10–20%; consider bedtime snack.

7. Pre-Mixed vs Basal-Bolus Comparison

FeaturePre-Mixed (BD)Basal-Bolus (QDS)
Injections/day24–5
Meal flexibilityLow (fixed times, carbs)High (per meal)
Carb countingNot neededEssential
Post-prandial controlModerate (fixed rapid %)Superior (dose per meal)
Hypoglycaemia riskHigher (nocturnal peak)Lower (basal flat, bolus matched)
Weight gainSimilarSimilar
Education neededLowHigh (DAFNE)
CostSingle penMultiple pens

8. Transition from Pre-Mixed to Basal-Bolus

Indications for Switch:

  • HbA1c > target despite titration
  • Recurrent hypoglycaemia (esp. nocturnal)
  • Variable lifestyle/meals
  • Patient motivated + able to carb count
  • Access to structured education (DAFNE)

Method:

  1. Stop pre-mixed
  2. Start basal insulin (glargine/detemir/degludec) at 50–70% of previous total daily dose
  3. Add rapid-acting bolus at meals per ICR
  4. Monitor closely for 1–2 weeks

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Pre-mixed ratios30/70 (Novomix 30), 25/75 (Humalog Mix 25), 50/50 (Humalog Mix 50)
DosingBD (pre-breakfast, pre-dinner)
Best forRegular meals, unable to carb count, elderly
Avoid ifVariable meals, shift work, hypoglycaemia unawareness
Post-prandial controlInferior to basal-bolus
Hypoglycaemia riskHigher (nocturnal intermediate peak)
NICE positionOnly if basal-bolus not feasible
TitrationWeekly; adjust breakfast dose by pre-dinner glucose, dinner dose by fasting
Switch to basal-bolusIf HbA1c not controlled, recurrent hypo, variable lifestyle
Key limitationFixed rapid:intermediate ratio → mismatch with meals

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes|Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Basal insulin initiation]], [[Basal-bolus intensification]], [[Concentrated insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500)]], [[Treatment algorithms/NICE NG28 pathway]]

Topic Overview

Pre-Mixed Insulin
1. Learning Objectives
2. Available Pre-Mixed Insulins
3. Indications for Pre-Mixed Insulin (NICE NG28)
4. Contraindications / Avoid
5. Initiation Protocol
Starting Dose
Stop Sulfonylurea
Continue
6. Titration Algorithm
7. Pre-Mixed vs Basal-Bolus Comparison
8. Transition from Pre-Mixed to Basal-Bolus
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.13.30 Thiazolidinediones Pioglitazone

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pioglitazone, tzd, thiazolidinediones, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Pioglitazone = PPAR-γ agonist; ↑ insulin sensitivity (adipose, muscle, liver); ↓ HbA1c 0.5–1.0%; weight gain 2–4kg, fluid retention, HF contraindicated, fracture risk (women), bladder cancer signal; renal safe; add-on if metformin/SU contraindicated; **avoid HF, active bladder cancer, fracture risk"

see_also: ["Meglitinides", "Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors", "Oral glucose-lowering agents", "Heart failure in diabetes", "Frailty and individualised HbA1c targets"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Thiazolidinediones (Pioglitazone)

[!info]
Pioglitazone = PPAR-γ agonist; ↑ insulin sensitivity in adipose, muscle, liver; ↓ HbA1c 0.5–1.0%; weight gain 2–4kg, fluid retention, HF contraindicated, fracture risk (women), bladder cancer signal; renal safe (no dose adjustment); add-on if metformin/SU contraindicated; avoid HF, active bladder cancer, high fracture risk.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain PPAR-γ mechanism of action
  • [ ] State indications, dosing, and monitoring
  • [ ] Manage weight gain, oedema, and fracture risk
  • [ ] Apply contraindications (HF, bladder cancer, fractures)
  • [ ] Position in treatment algorithm

2. Mechanism of Action

StepAction
1. TargetPPAR-γ (Peroxisome Proliferator-Activated Receptor gamma) — nuclear receptor
2. ActivationForms heterodimer with RXR → binds PPRE (PPAR Response Elements) on DNA
3. Gene transcription↑ Adiponectin, ↓ resistin, ↓ TNF-α, ↓ leptin; ↑ fatty acid oxidation, ↓ lipolysis
4. Tissue effectsAdipose: ↑ differentiation, ↑ lipid storage (subcutaneous > visceral); Muscle: ↑ GLUT4, ↑ insulin signalling; Liver: ↓ gluconeogenesis, ↓ lipid accumulation
5. Net effect↑ Insulin sensitivity (peripheral > hepatic); ↓ glucose, ↓ TG, ↑ HDL

3. Dosing & Pharmacokinetics

PropertyPioglitazone
Starting dose15mg OD
Titration↑ to 30mg OD after 4–8 weeks; max 45mg OD
Onset4–8 weeks (gene transcription)
Peak effect12–16 weeks
Half-life16–24h (active metabolites)
Renal impairmentNo dose adjustment (hepatic metabolism)
Hepatic impairmentContraindicated (Child-Pugh B/C)
AdministrationWith or without food

4. Efficacy

OutcomeEffect
HbA1c reduction−0.5 to −1.0% (monotherapy/add-on)
Fasting glucose↓ 2–3 mmol/L
Post-prandial glucose↓ 2–3 mmol/L
Triglycerides↓ 10–20%
HDL↑ 5–10%
LDL↑ 5–10% (large buoyant particles)
Weight change+2 to +4kg (adipose expansion + fluid)
DurabilityLongest of oral agents (ADOPT: durable to 4–5y)

5. Adverse Effects & Major Risks

RiskIncidenceMechanismManagement
Weight gain2–4kg (dose-related)Adipocyte differentiation + fluid retentionCounsel; monitor; consider GLP-1/SGLT2i if problematic
Fluid retention / Oedema3–5% (ankle, peripheral)↑ ENaC in collecting duct; ↑ vascular permeabilityContraindicated in HF; reduce dose; loop diuretic if mild
Heart failure↑ Hospitalisation 2–3xFluid retention + direct cardiac effectsABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION (NYHA I–IV)
Fracture risk↑ 2x (women > men; distal limbs)↓ Bone formation, ↑ bone resorption; PPAR-γ in osteoblastsAvoid in osteoporosis; DEXA monitor; calcium/vit D
Bladder cancerSignal in PROactive/Epic (HR ~1.2–1.5)Unclear; PPAR-γ in urotheliumAvoid if active/prior; counsel; urine cytology if haematuria
Macular oedemaRare (<0.1%)Fluid retentionDiscontinue if visual changes
AnaemiaMild (Hb ↓ 0.5–1g/dL)Haemodilution (fluid retention)Monitor; usually not clinically significant

6. Contraindications

AbsoluteRelative
NYHA Class I–IV Heart FailureOsteoporosis / high fracture risk
Active bladder cancerPrior bladder cancer (caution)
Hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C)Macular oedema
Pregnancy / BreastfeedingElderly women (fracture risk)
Type 1 DM / DKAMacular oedema history

Rosiglitazone: Withdrawn in EU/UK (CV risk); not available.

7. Monitoring Requirements

ParameterFrequency
HbA1c3–6 monthly
Weight & oedemaEach visit
BPEach visit
LFTsBaseline, 3m, 6m, then annually
HaemoglobinBaseline, annually (mild anaemia)
Bone healthDEXA if risk factors; calcium/vit D
Bladder symptomsAsk re haematuria annually; urine dipstick if symptoms

8. Position in Treatment Algorithm (NICE NG28 / ADA)

LineRole
Add-on to metforminIf SU contraindicated (hypo risk, renal impairment)
Add-on to SUIf metformin contraindicated (eGFR <30)
Triple therapyMetformin + SU + Pioglitazone (if GLP-1/SGLT2i not suitable)
Avoid inHF, bladder cancer, fracture risk, hepatic impairment
Preferred overSulfonylurea if hypo risk high (eGFR <30, elderly)
Avoid ifGLP-1 RA / SGLT2i available (superior CV/renal benefit)

9. Key Trials

TrialFinding
PROactive (2005)Pioglitazone ↓ macrovascular composite (MI, stroke, CV death) in T2DM + CVD; ↑ HF hospitalisation
ADOPT (2006)Pioglitazone most durable glycaemic control vs metformin/SU (4–5y); ↑ weight, oedema, fractures
IRIS (2016)Pioglitazone ↓ stroke/MI 24% in insulin resistance + prior stroke/TIA (non-diabetic)
Meta-analysesBladder cancer signal (HR 1.2–1.5); "Black box" warning in US

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
MechanismPPAR-γ agonist → ↑ insulin sensitivity (adipose, muscle, liver)
HbA1c reduction−0.5 to −1.0%
Weight gain+2 to +4kg (adipose + fluid)
Fluid retention3–5%; ankle oedema
HF contraindicationABSOLUTE — NYHA I–IV; ↑ HF hosp 2–3x
Fracture risk↑ 2x (women, distal limbs) — DEXA monitor
Bladder cancerSignal (HR 1.2–1.5); avoid active/prior
RenalNo dose adjustment (hepatic metabolism)
DurabilityLongest of oral agents (ADOPT: 4–5y)
NICE positionAdd-on if SU contraindicated; avoid HF/fracture/bladder cancer
RosiglitazoneWithdrawn EU/UK (CV risk)

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

▸ 21.13.32 Weight Management And Remission

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, weight-management, remission, vlcd, direct, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Weight loss 5–15% → metabolic benefit; remission definition: HbA1c <48 off meds ≥2mo; DiRECT: 800kcal formula 12–20w → 46% remission at 1y (15kg loss); 70% if >15kg loss; maintenance critical; bariatric surgery >75% remission"

see_also: ["Medical nutrition therapy", "Physical activity and exercise", "Type 2 diabetes remission", "Metabolic surgery/Bariatric surgery indications", "Lifestyle intervention"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Weight Management and Remission

[!info]
Weight loss 5–15% → metabolic benefit; Remission = HbA1c <48 mmol/mol off glucose meds ≥2 months; DiRECT: 800kcal formula 12–20w → 46% remission at 1y (15kg loss); 70% if >15kg loss; maintenance critical; bariatric surgery >75% remission.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define weight loss targets for metabolic benefit vs remission
  • [ ] Apply DiRECT/VLCD protocol for remission
  • [ ] Define remission criteria and monitoring
  • [ ] Manage weight maintenance post-loss
  • [ ] Position pharmacotherapy (GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i) and surgery

2. Weight Loss Thresholds & Benefits

Weight LossMetabolic Benefit
3–5%↓ HbA1c 3–5 mmol/mol; ↓ BP; ↑ insulin sensitivity
5–10%Clinically significant: ↓ HbA1c 5–10; ↓ CVD risk; ↓ medication need
10–15%Remission possible: ↓ HbA1c 10–20; ↓ CVD events
>15%High remission rate: 70–80% (DiRECT); ↓ mortality

3. Remission Definition (Consensus: ADA/EASD/Diabetes UK)

CriteriaDetail
HbA1c<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
Duration≥2 months sustained
MedicationOff ALL glucose-lowering medications
ExclusionsNot on steroids, not pregnant, not acute illness

Note: "Remission" not "cure" — relapse common if weight regained.

4. DiRECT Trial Protocol (Evidence Base for Remission)

Phase 1: Total Diet Replacement (12–20 weeks)

  • 800–850 kcal/day formula diet (soups, shakes, bars)
  • All meals replaced — no conventional food
  • Weekly support (dietitian/health coach)
  • Target: 15kg loss (or >15% body weight)
  • Stop glucose meds on day 1 (except metformin if CKD)
  • Stop antihypertensives if BP normalises (monitor)

Phase 2: Food Reintroduction (2–8 weeks)

  • Stepwise: vegetables → lean protein → whole grains → fruit/dairy
  • Portion-controlled meals (400–600kcal each)
  • Continue weekly support

Phase 3: Weight Maintenance (Long-term)

  • Monthly support (group or individual)
  • Self-monitoring: weight weekly, food diary, activity
  • Relapse protocol: 2kg regain → return to formula 1 week
  • Medication re-start if HbA1c ≥48

4. DiRECT Key Results

OutcomeResult
Mean weight loss15kg (intervention) vs 3kg (control)
Remission at 1y46% (intervention) vs 4% (control)
Remission if >15kg loss70–80%
Remission at 2y36% (intervention)
Medication cessation74% off all glucose meds at 1y
BP reductionMean −10/−5 mmHg; 50% off antihypertensives
Quality of lifeImproved (EQ-5D)
Cost-effectivenessDominant (cost-saving per QALY)

5. Other Remission Programmes

ProgrammeDietDeliveryRemission Rate
DiRECT800kcal formulaSpecialist46% at 1y
DROPLET810kcal formulaPrimary care (GP)27% at 1y
Low Carb Program<130g carb/dayDigital (app)25–30% at 1y
Virta HealthKetogenic (<30g)Digital + telemedicine54% at 1y (selected)
Mediterranean + ExerciseAd lib Med + 150min/wkGroup15–20%

6. Weight Maintenance — Critical for Sustained Remission

StrategyEvidence
Monthly support (group/individual)DiRECT: monthly → 36% remission at 2y
Self-weighingWeekly; 2kg regain trigger → action
High physical activity>300min/week (NWCR: successful maintainers)
Protein-rich breakfast↓ Hunger, ↓ evening intake
Continued food trackingApps, food diaries
Behavioural skillsProblem-solving, relapse prevention, stress management

Relapse: >50% regain some weight at 2–5y; early intervention prevents full relapse.

7. Pharmacotherapy for Weight Loss (Adjunct)

AgentDoseWeight LossRemission Data
Semaglutide 2.4mg (Wegovy)2.4mg weekly−15–17%STEP trials: remission in T2DM subset
Tirzepatide 15mg (Mounjaro)15mg weekly−20–22%SURMOUNT: high remission rates
Liraglutide 3mg (Saxenda)3mg daily−5–8%SCALE: modest remission
Orlistat120mg TDS−3–5%Low remission
Naltrexone/Bupropion32/360mg−5%Limited T2DM data

Positioning: After lifestyle failure (BMI ≥30 or ≥27 + comorbidity); GLP-1 RA preferred.

8. Bariatric Surgery — Highest Remission Rates

ProcedureRemission RateMechanism
RYGB (Roux-en-Y)80–85% at 1y; 50–60% at 5yRestriction + malabsorption + incretin (GLP-1)
Sleeve Gastrectomy70–80% at 1y; 40–50% at 5yRestriction + ghrelin ↓ + GLP-1 ↑
OAGB (One-anastomosis)80–85%Similar to RYGB

NICE criteria: BMI ≥35 + T2DM (or ≥30 + recent T2DM <10y); ADA: BMI ≥30 + T2DM.

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Remission definitionHbA1c <48 off meds ≥2 months
Weight loss for metabolic benefit5–10% clinically significant
Weight loss for remission>15kg (or >15% body weight)
DiRECT protocol800kcal formula 12–20w → food reintro → maintenance
DiRECT remission46% at 1y; 70–80% if >15kg loss
DiRECT maintenanceMonthly support; 2kg regain trigger
Semaglutide 2.4mg−15–17% weight; remission in subset
Tirzepatide 15mg−20–22% weight loss
Bariatric surgeryRYGB/Sleeve: 75–85% remission at 1y
Relapse preventionMonthly support; weekly weigh; >300min activity

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Lifestyle intervention|Lifestyle intervention]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Medical nutrition therapy]], [[Physical activity and exercise]], [[Type 2 diabetes remission]], [[Metabolic surgery/Bariatric surgery indications]]

Topic Overview

Weight Management and Remission
1. Learning Objectives
2. Weight Loss Thresholds & Benefits
3. Remission Definition (Consensus: ADA/EASD/Diabetes UK)
4. DiRECT Trial Protocol (Evidence Base for Remission)
Phase 1: Total Diet Replacement (12–20 weeks)
Phase 2: Food Reintroduction (2–8 weeks)
Phase 3: Weight Maintenance (Long-term)
4. DiRECT Key Results
5. Other Remission Programmes
6. Weight Maintenance — Critical for Sustained Remission
7. Pharmacotherapy for Weight Loss (Adjunct)
8. Bariatric Surgery — Highest Remission Rates
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.13.5 Basal Insulin Initiation

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, basal-insulin, initiation, titration, t2dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Basal insulin initiation: when (HbA1c >58 on dual/triple oral), which (glargine U100, detemir, degludec), dose (0.1–0.2 u/kg or 10u), titration (3–4u weekly to fasting target); NICE NG28 algorithm; avoid hypoglycaemia"

see_also: ["Basal-bolus intensification", "Concentrated insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500)", "Pre-mixed insulin", "Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes", "Treatment algorithms/NICE NG28 pathway"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Basal Insulin Initiation

[!info]
Basal insulin = first injectable for T2DM failing oral therapy; glargine U100 / detemir / degludec preferred; start 10 units or 0.1–0.2 u/kg; titrate 2–4 units weekly to fasting target (4–7 mmol/L); once daily (same time); combine with metformin ± SGLT2i/DPP-4i; stop sulfonylurea.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify criteria for basal insulin initiation
  • [ ] Choose appropriate basal insulin (glargine U100, detemir, degludec)
  • [ ] Calculate starting dose and titration algorithm
  • [ ] Apply NICE NG28 treatment pathway
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia risk and combination therapy

2. When to Initiate Basal Insulin (NICE NG28 / ADA)

TriggerCriteria
HbA1c above target>58 mmol/mol (7.5%) on dual/triple oral therapy (metformin + SU ± DPP-4i/SGLT2i/pioglitazone)
Symptomatic hyperglycaemiaOsmotic symptoms despite oral therapy
Contraindication to oralsCKD G4–5 (eGFR <30), recurrent hypoglycaemia on SU, intolerance
Specific populationsPregnancy (pre-existing T2DM), steroid-induced, post-transplant

Not first-line: Newly diagnosed T2DM (lifestyle + metformin first).

3. Basal Insulin Choice

InsulinDurationProfileAdvantagesDosing
Glargine U100 (Lantus, Biosimilars)24hFlat, peaklessMost evidence; cheap (biosimilars); once dailyOnce daily (any time, consistent)
Detemir (Levemir)16–24hFlat; slightly lower weight gainUnit-for-unit ~same as glargine; predictableOnce or twice daily
Degludec (Tresiba)>42hUltra-long, flatFlexible timing (±8h); lowest hypoglycaemia (esp. nocturnal); U100/U200Once daily (any time)
Glargine U300 (Toujeo)>24hMore concentrated, flatterLower hypoglycaemia vs U100; U300 penOnce daily
NPH (Isophane)12–16hPronounced peak (4–8h)Cheap; higher nocturnal hypo; twice dailyTwice daily

NICE/ADA preference: Glargine U100, detemir, or degludec (long-acting analogues) over NPH.

4. Starting Dose

MethodCalculation
Weight-based0.1–0.2 units/kg (e.g., 80kg → 8–16 units)
Fixed10 units (most guidelines)
High HbA1c (>86/10%)0.2–0.3 u/kg or 20 units

Practical: Start 10 units once daily (bedtime or morning) for most; adjust for weight/renal function.

5. Titration Algorithm (NICE / ADA Consensus)

Target

  • Fasting glucose 4–7 mmol/L (individualise: 5–8 if hypoglycaemia risk)

Titration Schedule

ApproachDose AdjustmentFrequency
Weekly (standard)Fasting mean (3 days): <4 → ↓ 2–4u; 4–7 → no change; 7–10 → ↑ 2–4u; >10 → ↑ 4–6uWeekly
Self-titration (patient-led)Treat-to-target: FBS <4 → ↓ 2u; 4–5 → no change; 5.1–7 → ↑ 1u; 7.1–10 → ↑ 2u; >10 → ↑ 4uEvery 3 days
Automated (connected pen/app)Algorithm-drivenPer protocol

Key: Nocturnal hypoglycaemia → reduce dose 10–20%; persistent fasting >10 → review adherence, diet, add bolus.

6. Combination Therapy with Basal Insulin

Oral AgentContinue?Adjustment
MetforminYes (first-line)Continue full dose
SGLT2iYesContinue (synergistic weight/glucose benefit)
GLP-1 RAYes (add-on)Reduce basal 10–20% if added
DPP-4iYesContinue
PioglitazoneIf neededCaution: weight gain, oedema, HF
SulfonylureaStop (hypoglycaemia risk)Taper over 2–4 weeks
MeglitinideStop—
AcarboseStop—

7. Monitoring & Safety

ParameterFrequency
Fasting glucoseDaily (self-monitored); review weekly
HbA1c3 months post-initiation; then 6-monthly
HypoglycaemiaReview at each visit; ask about nocturnal
WeightMonthly (basal insulin ~2–4kg gain)
Injection techniqueReview annually (site rotation, needle length)

8. Special Populations

PopulationAdjustments
Elderly / FrailStart 5–8u; target fasting 6–8; degludec preferred (flexible timing)
CKD G3–G4Start low (5–8u); detemir/glargine U100 OK; degludec caution (limited data); monitor
CKD G5 / DialysisInsulin clearance ↓ → reduce 30–50%; monitor closely
PregnancyDetemir/glargine U100 (Category B); tight targets; frequent titration
RamadanBasal at iftar; reduce 20–30%; monitor

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Initiation triggerHbA1c >58 on dual/triple oral therapy
Preferred basalGlargine U100, detemir, degludec (analogues > NPH)
Starting dose10 units or 0.1–0.2 u/kg
Titration2–4 units weekly to fasting 4–7 mmol/L
SulfonylureaStop when starting basal insulin
Metformin/SGLT2i/GLP-1Continue with basal
Degludec advantage>42h duration; flexible timing; lowest nocturnal hypo
Fasting target4–7 mmol/L (individualise)
Nocturnal hypoReduce basal 10–20%; consider degludec
Weight gain~2–4kg with basal insulin

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes|Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Basal-bolus intensification]], [[Concentrated insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500)]], [[Pre-mixed insulin]], [[Treatment algorithms/NICE NG28 pathway]]

Topic Overview

Basal Insulin Initiation
1. Learning Objectives
2. When to Initiate Basal Insulin (NICE NG28 / ADA)
3. Basal Insulin Choice
4. Starting Dose
5. Titration Algorithm (NICE / ADA Consensus)
Target
Titration Schedule
6. Combination Therapy with Basal Insulin
7. Monitoring & Safety
8. Special Populations
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.13.6 Basal Bolus Intensification

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, basal-bolus, intensification, t2dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Basal-bolus = add rapid-acting insulin to basal; when basal optimised but HbA1c >58; 1+2+1 rule (1 unit per 10g carb, 2 units correction per 2mmol/L); count carbs; pre-bolus 15min; avoid fixed-dose regimens; QDS vs BD premix"

see_also: ["Basal insulin initiation", "Pre-mixed insulin", "Concentrated insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500)", "Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes", "Carbohydrate counting"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Basal-Bolus Intensification

[!info]
Basal-bolus = basal insulin + rapid-acting bolus at meals; escalate when basal optimised (fbst 4–7) but HbA1c >58; carb counting essential; ICR ~1u/10g, ISF ~2mmol/L per 2u; pre-bolus 15min; QDS regimen; avoid fixed-dose regimens unless carb counting not possible.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify criteria for basal-bolus escalation
  • [ ] Calculate initial bolus doses (ICR, ISF)
  • [ ] Apply carbohydrate counting principles
  • [ ] Titrate bolus and basal components
  • [ ] Avoid common pitfalls (stacking, fixed doses)

2. Indications for Basal-Bolus

CriterionThreshold
Basal optimisedFasting glucose 4–7 mmol/L consistently
HbA1c above target>58 mmol/mol (7.5%) despite basal + orals
Post-prandial hyperglycaemia2h PP >8.5 mmol/L consistently
Variable eating patternIrregular meals, shift work, carb variability
High insulin requirementBasal >0.5u/kg or >50u/day
Steroid-induced hyperglycaemiaHigh post-prandial excursions

3. Rapid-Acting Insulin Options

InsulinOnsetPeakDurationNotes
Aspart (NovoRapid)10–20 min1–3h3–5hStandard
Lispro (Humalog)10–20 min1–2h3–5hStandard
Faster Aspart (Fiasp)5–10 min30–60 min3–5hNiacinamide + arginine; better PP control
Lispro-aabc (Lyumjev)5–10 min30–45 min3–5hCitrate + treprostinil
Human regular (Actrapid)30–60 min2–4h6–8hNot preferred (delayed peak)

Preferred: Aspart, Lispro, Fiasp/Lyumjev for tighter PP control.

4. Initial Bolus Dose Calculation

Carbohydrate Ratio (ICR — Insulin-to-Carb Ratio)

MethodFormula
Rule of 500ICR = 500 / TDD (total daily dose) → grams carb per 1 unit
Rule of 300 (some use)ICR = 300 / TDD → more aggressive
Weight-based1 unit per 10g carb (starting point)

Starting ICR: 1 unit per 10–15g carb (adjust based on 2h PP glucose).

Insulin Sensitivity Factor (ISF — Correction Factor)

MethodFormula
Rule of 100 (mmol/L)ISF = 100 / TDD → mmol/L drop per 1 unit
Rule of 1800 (mg/dL)ISF = 1800 / TDD → mg/dL drop per 1 unit

Starting ISF: 2 mmol/L drop per 2 units (1u per 1mmol/L approximate).

5. Practical Initiation Protocol

Step 1: Basal Optimisation (Already Done)

  • Basal dose stable, fasting 4–7 mmol/L

Step 2: Add First Bolus (Usually Largest Meal)

MealInitial Bolus
BreakfastOften largest; start here if PP highest
DinnerIf post-dinner PP highest

Dose: ICR 1u/10g carb; pre-bolus 15min.

Step 3: Add Second Bolus (After 1–2 Weeks)

  • If HbA1c still > target, add bolus at next problematic meal

Step 4: Full Basal-Bolus (QDS)

  • Pre-breakfast, pre-lunch, pre-dinner, bedtime basal
  • Bedtime snack if basal peak overnight

6. Titration Rules

ParameterAdjustment
Bolus (if 2h PP >8.5)↑ 1 unit per meal (or tighten ICR: 1u per 8g)
Bolus (if 2h PP <4.0)↓ 1 unit (or widen ICR: 1u per 12g)
Basal (if fasting >7)↑ 2–4u weekly
Basal (if fasting <4)↓ 2–4u weekly
Correction (if CBG >10)Add ISF dose (e.g., 2u per 2mmol/L >10)

Avoid stacking: Wait 3–4h between bolus corrections.

7. Pre-Mixed Insulin vs Basal-Bolus

ScenarioPre-Mixed (BD)Basal-Bolus (QDS)
Regular meals, fixed carbsAcceptableBetter flexibility
Variable meals/carbsPoor fitPreferred
Carb counting abilityNot neededEssential
Injection burden2/day4–5/day
Hypoglycaemia riskHigher (fixed ratios)Lower (dose per meal)
Post-prandial controlLimitedSuperior

NICE: Pre-mixed only if carb counting not feasible and regular meals.

8. Carbohydrate Counting — Essentials

SkillRequirement
Read labelsTotal carb per serving (not "sugars")
Weigh portionsDigital scales for accuracy
Apps/ResourcesCarbs & Cals, MyFitnessPal, DAFNE app
Mixed mealsEstimate protein/fat effect (delayed rise)
AlcoholCarbs in beer/cider; spirits zero; hypoglycaemia risk later

Education: DAFNE principles — refer for structured education if not competent.

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Basal-bolus indicationBasal optimised + HbA1c >58 + PP >8.5
ICR starting1 unit per 10g carb (Rule of 500: 500/TDD)
ISF starting2 mmol/L per 2 units (Rule of 100: 100/TDD)
Pre-bolus timing15–20 minutes before meal
Rapid insulin choiceFiasp/Lyumjev for best PP control
Pre-mixedOnly if carb counting not possible + regular meals
Avoid stackingWait 3–4h between corrections
TitrationBolus: adjust by 1u per meal; Basal: 2–4u weekly
Bedtime basalOnce daily (glargine/detemir/degludec)
EducationRefer for carb counting (DAFNE)

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes|Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Basal insulin initiation]], [[Pre-mixed insulin]], [[Concentrated insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500)]], [[Treatment algorithms/NICE NG28 pathway]]

Topic Overview

Basal-Bolus Intensification
1. Learning Objectives
2. Indications for Basal-Bolus
3. Rapid-Acting Insulin Options
4. Initial Bolus Dose Calculation
Carbohydrate Ratio (ICR — Insulin-to-Carb Ratio)
Insulin Sensitivity Factor (ISF — Correction Factor)
5. Practical Initiation Protocol
Step 1: Basal Optimisation (Already Done)
Step 2: Add First Bolus (Usually Largest Meal)
Step 3: Add Second Bolus (After 1–2 Weeks)
Step 4: Full Basal-Bolus (QDS)
6. Titration Rules
7. Pre-Mixed Insulin vs Basal-Bolus
8. Carbohydrate Counting — Essentials
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.13.8 Ckd And Hf Guided Therapy

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, ckd-hf-guided-therapy, sgl2i, glp-1, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA preferred in CKD/HF regardless of HbA1c; eGFR thresholds; SGLT2i eGFR ≥20 (dapa/empa); GLP-1 RA eGFR ≥15; finerenone eGFR ≥25 A2/A3; avoid SU, TZD, DPP-4i (saxa/alo) in HF; metformin if eGFR ≥30"

see_also: ["Cardiovascular outcome trial (CVOT) guided therapy", "NICE NG28 pathway", "DKD management (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, finerenone, GLP-1 RA)", "Heart failure in diabetes/HFrEF and HFpEF", "Diabetes in CKD and dialysis"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

CKD and HF Guided Therapy

[!info]
Modern T2DM algorithm = comorbidity-driven (not HbA1c-driven); SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA first if CKD/HF/CVD; SGLT2i eGFR ≥20; GLP-1 RA eGFR ≥15; finerenone eGFR ≥25 A2/A3; avoid SU, TZD, saxagliptin/alogliptin in HF; metformin if eGFR ≥30.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply comorbidity-first treatment algorithm (CKD/HF/CVD)
  • [ ] Select SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA by eGFR threshold
  • [ ] Identify agents to avoid in CKD/HF
  • [ ] Sequence therapy per KDIGO/ESC/ADA guidelines
  • [ ] Monitor renal/cardiac parameters

2. Comorbidity-First Algorithm (ADA 2024 / KDIGO 2024 / ESC 2023)

Step 1: Confirm T2DM Diagnosis + Assess Comorbidities

ComorbidityFirst-Line Add-On (Beyond Metformin)
ASCVD (MI, stroke, PAD)SGLT2i (dapa/empa) + GLP-1 RA (semaglutide/dulaglutide)
HF (HFrEF or HFpEF)SGLT2i (dapa/empa) first; GLP-1 RA if obese HFpEF
CKD (eGFR <60 or ACR ≥30)SGLT2i (dapa/empa) + GLP-1 RA + finerenone (if A2/A3, eGFR ≥25)
Obesity (BMI ≥30)GLP-1 RA (semaglutide/tirzepatide) or SGLT2i
High CV risk onlySGLT2i or GLP-1 RA (per trial evidence)

Metformin: Continue if eGFR ≥30; stop if <30.

3. Agent Selection by eGFR & Comorbidity

SGLT2 Inhibitors (CKD/HF Indications)

AgentHF IndicationCKD IndicationeGFR ThresholdDose
DapagliflozinHFrEF, HFpEFT2DM + CKD, Non-diabetic CKD≥2010mg OD
EmpagliflozinHFrEF, HFpEFT2DM + CKD, Non-diabetic CKD≥2010mg OD
CanagliflozinHFrEFT2DM + CKD≥30100mg OD

Mechanism beyond glucose: Haemodynamic, anti-inflammatory, anti-fibrotic.

GLP-1 Receptor Agonists (CKD/HF Indications)

AgentCV IndicationHFpEF (obese)eGFR ThresholdDose
Semaglutide SCMACE ↓ (SUSTAIN-6)STEP-HFpEF (2.4mg)≥15 (no adjust)0.5→1.0mg wk
DulaglutideMACE ↓ (REWIND)—≥15 (no adjust)0.75–1.5mg wk
LiraglutideMACE ↓ (LEADER)FIGHT (neutral)≥15 (no adjust)1.2–1.8mg daily

No dose adjustment needed down to eGFR 15.

Finerenone (CKD Specific)

IndicationeGFRACRDose
T2DM + DKD (A2/A3)≥25A2 (3–30) or A3 (>30)10mg OD (eGFR 25–60); 20mg OD (eGFR ≥60)

Monitor K+ at 4 weeks, then quarterly; hold if K+ >5.5.

4. Agents to AVOID in CKD/HF

AgentAvoid InReason
Sulfonylureas (except gliclazide)CKD G3b–G5, HFHypoglycaemia; no CV benefit
Thiazolidinediones (TZDs)HF (any class), fracture riskFluid retention → HF exacerbation
DPP-4i: Saxagliptin, AlogliptinHFSAVOR/EXAMINE: ↑ HF hospitalisation
DPP-4i: Sitagliptin, LinagliptinGenerally safeNeutral CV; linagliptin no renal adjust
GLP-1 RA—Safe in HF (FIGHT, LIVE neutral; STEP-HFpEF benefit)
SGLT2i—Preferred in CKD/HF
MetformineGFR <30Lactic acidosis risk
Insulin—Safe; dose reduce by eGFR

5. Treatment Sequencing Examples

Scenario A: T2DM + HFrEF + CKD G3a (eGFR 50, ACR 10)

  1. Metformin (continue, eGFR 50)
  2. SGLT2i (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin 10mg) — first add-on (HF + CKD)
  3. GLP-1 RA (semaglutide) if HbA1c not controlled
  4. Stop sulfonylurea if present

Scenario B: T2DM + HFpEF + Obesity (BMI 35) + CKD G2

  1. Metformin
  2. SGLT2i (dapa/empa) — HFpEF indication
  3. Semaglutide 2.4mg — HFpEF + obesity (STEP-HFpEF)
  4. Finerenone if ACR ≥30 and eGFR ≥25

Scenario C: T2DM + CKD G3b (eGFR 35, ACR 50) + No CVD/HF

  1. Metformin (max 1g BD)
  2. SGLT2i (dapa/empa — eGFR ≥20) + GLP-1 RA (semaglutide/dulaglutide)
  3. Finerenone (eGFR ≥25, A2/A3) — add after SGLT2i+GLP-1
  4. Avoid TZD, SU, saxagliptin

Scenario D: T2DM + No Comorbidities (BMI 28, eGFR 80)

  1. Metformin
  2. SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA (per patient preference, cost, weight)
  3. DPP-4i (linagliptin) if injectable decline

6. Monitoring by Comorbidity

ComorbidityKey MonitoringFrequency
CKDeGFR, UACR, K+3–6 monthly (G3+); annual (G1–2)
HFNT-proBNP, weight, symptoms, K+, eGFR3–6 monthly
CVDLDL, BP, HbA1c6–12 monthly
SGLT2ieGFR, K+, volume status1–2w post-initiation; then 3m
GLP-1 RAWeight, GI symptoms, HR3m
FinerenoneK+ at 4w, then quarterlyPer protocol

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Algorithm shiftComorbidity-first (not HbA1c-first)
SGLT2i in CKDeGFR ≥20 (dapa/empa); cana ≥30
SGLT2i in HFHFrEF + HFpEF — dapa/empa both
GLP-1 RA in CKDNo dose adjust down to eGFR 15
GLP-1 RA in HFSemaglutide 2.4mg for HFpEF + obesity (STEP-HFpEF)
FinerenoneeGFR ≥25 + A2/A3; monitor K+ at 4w
Avoid in HFTZDs (contraindicated), saxagliptin/alogliptin (↑ HF hosp)
Avoid in CKDSU (hypo), TZD (HF), metformin <30
Metformin eGFR≥30 (max 1g BD if 30–45); <30 stop
SequenceMetformin → SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA (if CKD/HF/CVD) → others

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Treatment algorithms|Treatment algorithms]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

CKD and HF Guided Therapy
1. Learning Objectives
2. Comorbidity-First Algorithm (ADA 2024 / KDIGO 2024 / ESC 2023)
Step 1: Confirm T2DM Diagnosis + Assess Comorbidities
3. Agent Selection by eGFR & Comorbidity
SGLT2 Inhibitors (CKD/HF Indications)
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists (CKD/HF Indications)
Finerenone (CKD Specific)
4. Agents to AVOID in CKD/HF
5. Treatment Sequencing Examples
Scenario A: T2DM + HFrEF + CKD G3a (eGFR 50, ACR 10)
Scenario B: T2DM + HFpEF + Obesity (BMI 35) + CKD G2
Scenario C: T2DM + CKD G3b (eGFR 35, ACR 50) + No CVD/HF
Scenario D: T2DM + No Comorbidities (BMI 28, eGFR 80)

Topic Overview

Clinical presentation and screening
Disease-Level Topics
High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)
Quick Links
Tags
▸ 21.2 Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care 13
▸ 21.2.1 Annual Review And Complication Screening
[!info]
NICE 9 Care Processes = core annual review; achieves QOF indicators; optimal diabetes care = all 9 completed; care plan agreed; structured education offered; complications screened.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] List all 9 NICE care processes
  • [ ] Perform systematic annual review
  • [ ] Interpret screening results and escalate
  • [ ] Apply QOF diabetes indicators
  • [ ] Create shared care plan

2. NICE 9 Care Processes (Essential Annual Checks)

#Care ProcessTarget / Action
1HbA1cIndividualised target (see Glycaemic targets); measure 3–6 monthly until stable, then 6-monthly
2Blood PressurePer guideline targets (NICE <140/90, <130/80 if CKD/albuminuria)
3Lipids (Total, HDL, LDL, non-HDL, TG)Non-HDL <2.6 (primary) / <2.2 (secondary); statin per risk
4Urine ACR (albumin:creatinine ratio)Screen for DKD; ACR <3 (A1); if A2/A3 → ACEi/ARB max, SGLT2i, finerenone
5eGFR (CKD-EPI)Monitor CKD stage; adjust meds (metformin, SGLT2i, insulin)
6BMIWeight management; if ≥25 → lifestyle referral; if ≥30 → consider GLP-1/SGLT2i
7Smoking StatusDocument; offer cessation (NRT, varenicline, behavioural)
8Foot ExaminationNeurovascular assessment (10g monofilament, vibration, pulses); classify risk (IWGDF 1–3)
9Retinal ScreeningDigital photography; grading (R0–R3, M0–M1); annual (or 2-yearly if R0 x2)

All 9 completed = high-quality care; NICE QS6 / QOF DM002–DM012.

3. Expanded Annual Review Checklist

DomainAssessmentsFrequency
Glycaemic ControlHbA1c, CGM metrics (TIR, TBR, GMI), hypoglycaemia historyHbA1c 6m; CGM each visit
CardiovascularBP, lipids, ECG (if symptoms), QRISK3/ASCVD, aspirin/statin reviewAnnual
RenalUACR, eGFR, KP 2023 risk (if DKD)Annual (6m if A2/A3 or eGFR<60)
FeetInspection, monofilament, vibration (128Hz), pulses (DP/PT), capillary refill, deformity, footwearAnnual (3–6m if high risk)
EyesRetinal photography (mydriatic); OCT if DMO suspectedAnnual (2y if R0 x2)
NeuropathySymptom questionnaire (MNSI), 10g monofilament, vibrationAnnual
PsychosocialPHQ-9/GAD-7 (depression/anxiety), PAID/DDS (diabetes distress), eating disorder screenAnnual
Sexual HealthErectile dysfunction (men), sexual dysfunction (women)Ask annually
VaccinationsInfluenza (annual), Pneumococcal (PPV23), COVID, Hep B (if not immune)Per schedule
Medication ReviewAdherence, side effects, interactions, deprescribing (elderly), sick day rulesEach visit
LifestyleDiet, activity, alcohol, sleep, smokingEach visit
Pre-conceptionFolic acid 5mg, HbA1c <48, medication review, complication screenIf planning pregnancy

4. Complication Screening Details

Foot Risk Classification (IWGDF / Scottish)

Risk LevelFindingsAction
1 (Low)Normal sensation, palpable pulses, no deformityAnnual screen; self-care education
2 (Moderate)Neuropathy OR absent pulses OR deformity OR skin changes3–6 monthly podiatry; footwear assessment; self-care + professional care
3 (High)Previous ulcer/amputation OR active Charcot OR critical ischaemia OR on dialysis1–3 monthly specialist MDT; urgent referral if active problem

Retinopathy Screening (ETDRS / English National Programme)

GradeDescriptionReferral
R0No retinopathyRoutine annual
R1Background NPDR (microaneurysms, dot/blot haemorrhages)Annual
R2Pre-proliferative (venous beading, IRMA, multiple haemorrhages)Ophthalmology (6–12 weeks)
R3Proliferative (new vessels, vitreous haemorrhage, preretinal haemorrhage)Urgent ophthalmology (2–4 weeks)
M0No maculopathyRoutine
M1Maculopathy (exudates within 1DD of fovea, retinal thickening)Ophthalmology (4–8 weeks)

DKD Screening (KDIGO)

TestFrequencyAction Threshold
UACRAnnual (6m if A2/A3)A2 (3–30): ACEi/ARB max, SGLT2i; A3 (>30): + finerenone, nephrology
eGFRAnnual (6m if <60)G3a (45–59): monitor; G3b (30–44): adjust meds; G4 (15–29): nephrology; G5 (<15): RRT planning

5. Care Planning — Shared Decision Making

NICE NG28: Agree individualised care plan at each review covering:

  • Glycaemic target (individualised)
  • Cardiovascular risk (QRISK3, statin, aspirin, BP target)
  • Complication screening results (traffic light system)
  • Medication changes agreed
  • Lifestyle goals (SMART)
  • Education referral (if not completed/need refresher)
  • Next review date
  • Patient copy provided

6. QOF Diabetes Indicators (2024/25)

IndicatorDescriptionPoints
DM002Register of patients ≥17y with diabetes5
DM003HbA1c ≤58 mmol/mol (7.5%)17
DM004HbA1c ≤48 mmol/mol (6.5%)10
DM005BP ≤140/9011
DM006Non-HDL <2.6 (or >40% reduction)6
DM007Statin prescribed (if CVD/QRISK3≥10%)5
DM008UACR checked3
DM009eGFR checked3
DM010Foot exam (neurovascular)3
DM011Retinal screen3
DM012Smoking status + cessation offer3

7. Audit & Quality Improvement

MetricTarget
All 9 care processes completed>80% (NICE QS6)
HbA1c ≤58 mmol/mol>60%
BP ≤140/90>70%
Statin in eligible>85%
Foot risk documented100%
Retinal screening uptake>80%
Structured education attendance>50% of eligible

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
9 Care ProcessesHbA1c, BP, Lipids, UACR, eGFR, BMI, Smoking, Foot exam, Retinal screen
All 9 completedQuality marker; NICE QS6
Foot riskIWGDF 1 (low) = annual; 2 (moderate) = 3–6m podiatry; 3 (high) = 1–3m MDT
Retinopathy gradesR0/R1 = annual; R2 = 6–12w referral; R3 = urgent 2–4w; M1 = 4–8w
UACR frequencyAnnual; 6m if A2/A3
eGFR frequencyAnnual; 6m if <60
QOF HbA1c targets≤58 (7.5%) for points; ≤48 (6.5%) additional points
Care planShared decision-making; individualised targets; copy to patient
PsychosocialScreen annually (PHQ-9, PAID/DDS)
VaccinationsFlu annual, PPV23, COVID, Hep B

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care|Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
