▸ 21.2.10 Retinopathy Screening

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, retinopathy-screening, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Diabetic retinopathy", "Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)", "Retinopathy treatment (laser, anti-VEGF, vitrectomy)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Retinopathy screening

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply UK National Screening Programme protocol
  • [ ] Interpret ETDRS grading and referral thresholds
  • [ ] Apply screening intervals by DR severity
  • [ ] Recognise pregnancy-specific screening

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
ProgrammeUK National Diabetic Eye Screening Programme (NDESP)
MethodDigital retinal photography (mydriatic, 45°, 2-field)
Coverage target>80% eligible population
IntervalAnnual (2-yearly if no DR)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - screening asymptomatic)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

ETDRS / UK NSC Grading Scale

GradeETDRS LevelDescriptionReferral / Interval
R010-12No DR2-yearly
R1 (Mild NPDR)20-35Microaneurysms onlyAnnual
R2 (Moderate NPDR)35-43Haemorrhages, exudates, cotton wool6-12 months
R3 (Severe NPDR)43-534:2:1 Rule: 4 quad haem, 2 quad VB, 1 IRMAUrgent (2-4 weeks)
R4 (PDR)61+NVD, NVE, VH, pre-retinal haemUrgent (1-2 weeks)
M1 (DMO)—Thickening ≤1DD from foveaUrgent (2-4 weeks)

4:2:1 Rule (Severe NPDR / R3)

CriterionThreshold
4Haemorrhages/microaneurysms in 4 quadrants
2Venous beading in 2 quadrants
1IRMA in 1 quadrant
Any ONE = Severe NPDR (R3)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRole
Digital photographyScreening (mydriatic, 45°, 2-field)
OCTDMO diagnosis (CST); not for DR grading
Fluorescein angiographyPre-laser; NVE/NVD confirmation
HbA1c, BP, lipidsSystemic risk factor documentation

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRole
Digital photographyScreening (mydriatic, 45°, 2-field)
OCTDMO diagnosis (CST); not for DR grading
Fluorescein angiographyPre-laser; NVE/NVD confirmation
HbA1c, BP, lipidsSystemic risk factor documentation

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Hypertensive retinopathyAV nicking, copper/silver wiring; no microaneurysms
Retinal vein occlusionSectoral haemorrhages, disc oedema; unilateral
Radiation retinopathyPost-RT; capillary non-perfusion; no microaneurysms
Ocular ischaemic syndromeCarotid stenosis; mid-peripheral haemorrhages, NVI/NVA

7. Management

Screening Protocol

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Diabetic Patient
↓
Age/Type/Duration
↓
Annual Digital Photography
Annual from 5y post-dx
↓
Grade: R0-R4, M0-M1
↓
Grade
↓
2-yearly screen
Annual screen
6-12 month screen
Urgent refer 2-4 weeks
Urgent refer 1-2 weeks
Urgent refer 2-4 weeks

Referral Pathways

GradeReferralTimeline
R3 (Severe NPDR)Ophthalmology (medical retina)2-4 weeks
R4 (PDR)Ophthalmology (vitreoretinal)1-2 weeks
M1 (DMO)Ophthalmology (medical retina)2-4 weeks
R0-R2Continue screening programmePer interval

Quality Assurance

ElementStandard
Image qualityAdequate focus, exposure, field (disc + macula)
Grader certificationAnnual accreditation; kappa >0.85
Arbitration2nd grader for discordant/ungradable
FailsafeTrack non-attenders; recall within 6 weeks

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Screening methodDigital photography, mydriatic, 45°, 2-field (macula + disc)
IntervalsNo DR (R0): 2-yearly; Mild NPDR (R1): annual; Mod NPDR (R2): 6-12mo; Severe NPDR (R3): urgent 2-4wk; PDR (R4): urgent 1-2wk
4:2:1 RuleSevere NPDR: 4 quad haem OR 2 quad venous beading OR 1 quad IRMA
M1 (DMO)Thickening ≤1DD from fovea → urgent referral 2-4 weeks
NVD vs NVENVD on disc (high risk); NVE >1DD from disc
High-risk PDRNVD ≥1/3 disc, NVE ≥1/2 disc, VH/pre-retinal haem
QualityCertified graders; arbitration; failsafe for non-attenders

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the 4:2:1 rule for severe NPDR?Haemorrhages in 4 quadrants OR Venous beading in 2 quadrants OR IRMA in 1 quadrant — any ONE = Severe NPDR (R3)
What are the screening intervals by DR grade?R0: 2-yearly; R1: annual; R2: 6-12 months; R3: urgent 2-4 weeks; R4: urgent 1-2 weeks; M1: urgent 2-4 weeks
What is the referral threshold for PDR?R4 (NVD, NVE, VH, pre-retinal haem) → Urgent referral within 1-2 weeks
What is M1 grading?Diabetic macular oedema — retinal thickening ≤1 disc diameter from fovea → urgent referral 2-4 weeks
How does NVD differ from NVE?NVD: new vessels on/within 1DD of disc (higher risk); NVE: new vessels >1DD from disc
What constitutes high-risk PDR?NVD ≥1/3 disc area, NVE ≥1/2 disc area, vitreous haemorrhage, pre-retinal haemorrhage

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
R2 vs R3?R2 = moderate (haemorrhages, exudates, cotton wool); R3 = severe (4:2:1 rule met)
M1 = any maculopathy?M1 = thickening ≤1DD from fovea (centre-involving); non-centre = not M1
Screening in pregnancy?Pre-conception, each trimester, postpartum 6mo — rapid progression risk

Mnemonic: RETINA-SCREEN

  • R0: No DR → 2-yearly
  • ETDRS: 10-12 none, 20-35 mild, 43-53 severe, 61+ PDR
  • Two-field: macula + disc (45°, mydriatic)
  • Intervals: R0=2yr, R1=1yr, R2=6-12mo, R3=2-4wk, R4=1-2wk
  • NVD on disc; NVE elsewhere
  • A 4:2:1 rule = Severe NPDR (R3)
  • Screening: annual (T2DM at Dx, T1DM >5y)
  • Centre-involving DMO = M1 → urgent
  • Referral: R3=2-4wk, R4=1-2wk, M1=2-4wk
  • Exudates/cotton wool = R2 (moderate)
  • Emergency: R4 (PDR) 1-2 weeks
  • Ensure quality: certified graders, arbitration, failsafe
  • No DR = 2-yearly**
▸ 21.2.11 Self Monitoring Of Blood Glucose Smbg Frequency

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, smbg-frequency, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["HbA1c targets (individualised)", "Time in range (TIR) targets", "Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) frequency

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply SMBG frequency guidelines per diabetes type and treatment
  • [ ] Identify when to increase monitoring (illness, driving, pregnancy)
  • [ ] Apply structured SMBG protocols (paired testing, pattern management)
  • [ ] Recognise limitations of SMBG vs CGM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionCapillary blood glucose testing by patient
StandardFingerstick glucose meter; ISO 15197 accuracy (±15% >100 mg/dL)
CGM alternativertCGM/isCGM; reduces SMBG need

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SMBG Frequency by Patient Group

GroupFrequencyTiming
T1DM / T2DM on insulin4-10x/dayPre-meals, bedtime, pre-driving, 3am (basal check), suspected hypo
T2DM on SU/GLP-1/SGLT2i1-2x/dayPre-breakfast, pre-dinner, alternating
T2DM on metformin/DPP-4i2-3x/weekPre-breakfast, pre-dinner (rotating)
T2DM diet-onlyIndividualisedPre-breakfast (if monitoring)
Pregnancy (T1/T2/GDM)7x/dayFasting, 1h post-meals x3, bedtime, 3am
High hypoglycaemia riskAdditional 3am check+ CGM recommended

Structured SMBG Protocols

ProtocolSchedulePurpose
Paired testingPre-meal + 2h post-mealAssess carb/insulin match
Pattern management7-point profile (fasting, 3 meals pre/post, bed)Pattern identification
Basal check3am glucoseAssess basal insulin adequacy

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
SMBGDaily decision-making; dose adjustment
CGMPattern recognition; nocturnal hypo detection
HbA1c3-month average; long-term control
CGM vs SMBGCGM reduces SMBG need; SMBG for calibration/confirmation

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Structured SMBG Protocol (Example: T1DM)

TimePurpose
FastingBasal insulin adequacy
Pre-breakfastBolus calculation
2h post-breakfastBreakfast bolus adequacy
Pre-lunchPre-meal correction
2h post-lunchLunch bolus adequacy
Pre-dinnerDinner bolus calculation
2h post-dinnerDinner bolus adequacy
BedtimeOvernight basal; hypo prevention
3am (if needed)Basal check

Situational Increases

SituationAdditional Checks
Illnessq2-4h; ketones if glucose >14
Pre-drivingMandatory (DVLA)
ExercisePre, during (if >30min), post
AlcoholBedtime + 3am
Pregnancy7x/day (fasting, 1h post-meals x3, bedtime, 3am)
RamadanPre-dawn, midday, pre-iftar, 2h post-iftar, bedtime

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
T1DM/Insulin T2DM4-10x/day (pre-meals, bedtime, 3am, pre-driving)
T2DM non-insulinIndividualised (2-3x/week rotating pre-meals)
Pregnancy7x/day (fasting, 1h post x3, bedtime, 3am)
Illnessq2-4h + ketones
DrivingPre-driving mandatory
CGMReduces SMBG; predictive alerts; TIR/TBR

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How often should a T1DM patient on basal-bolus test?4-10x/day (pre-meals, bedtime, pre-driving, 3am, suspected hypo)
What is the SMBG frequency for T2DM on metformin only?Individualised (e.g., 2-3x/week pre-breakfast/pre-dinner rotating)
What is the SMBG frequency in pregnancy?7x/day: fasting, 1h post-meals x3, bedtime, 3am
When is 3am glucose check indicated?Suspected nocturnal hypoglycaemia; basal insulin adjustment; pregnancy
What is paired testing?Pre-meal + 2h post-meal to assess bolus insulin adequacy

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
SMBG = CGM?NO - CGM continuous; SMBG intermittent; CGM reduces SMBG need
All T2DM = daily SMBG?NO - individualised; non-insulin often 2-3x/week
HbA1c replaces SMBG?NO - HbA1c = 3-month average; SMBG = daily decisions

Mnemonic: SMBG-FREQUENCY-RULES

  • SMBG: self-monitoring blood glucose
  • Must: T1DM/insulin = 4-10x/day
  • Basal-bolus: pre-meals, bed, 3am, drive
  • Glucose: pre-meal + 2h post = paired testing
  • Frequency: T2DM non-insulin = individualised (2-3x/wk)
  • Regular: pregnant = 7x/day
  • Exercise: pre/during/post
  • Quarterly: HbA1c vs SMBG
  • Unique: individualised per treatment
  • Evidence: NICE/ADA guidelines
  • Not all same: treatment-dependent
  • CGM alternative: reduces SMBG need
  • Yes: pre-driving mandatory
  • Frequency: illness = q2-4h + ketones
  • Risk: hypo risk = more frequent
  • Exception: CGM may replace some SMBG
  • Quality: ISO 15197 (±15% >100 mg/dL)
  • Under-use: under-testing = missed hypo/hyper
  • Education: technique, interpretation, action
  • Non-invasive: CGM trend
  • Calibration: some CGM need SMBG
  • Yes: structured = pattern management**

Topic Overview

Self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) frequency
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
SMBG Frequency by Patient Group
Structured SMBG Protocols
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Structured SMBG Protocol (Example: T1DM)
Situational Increases
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.2.12 Time In Range Tir Targets

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, tir-targets, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["HbA1c targets (individualised)", "Self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) frequency", "Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Time in range (TIR) targets

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply ATTD consensus CGM targets (TIR, TBR, TAR, GMI, CV)
  • [ ] Interpret AGP report for clinical decision-making
  • [ ] Apply CGM metrics in clinical practice (insulin adjustment, lifestyle)
  • [ ] Recognise limitations of CGM metrics vs HbA1c

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
TIRTime in Range (70-180 mg/dL / 3.9-10.0 mmol/L)
TBRTime Below Range (<70 mg/dL / <3.9 mmol/L)
TARTime Above Range (>180 mg/dL / >10.0 mmol/L)
GMIGlucose Management Indicator (estimates HbA1c from CGM mean)
CVCoefficient of Variation (glucose variability)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

ATTD Consensus Targets (2019/2023)

MetricT1DM TargetT2DM TargetPregnancy
TIR (70-180 mg/dL)>70%>70%>70% (63-140 mg/dL)
TBR <70 mg/dL<4%<4%<4%
TBR <54 mg/dL<1%<1%<1%
TAR >180 mg/dL<25%<25%<25%
TAR >250 mg/dL<5%<5%—
CV<36%<36%<36%
GMI≈ HbA1c≈ HbA1c≈ HbA1c

TIR Categories (Clinical)

TIRCategoryAction
<50%Very poorImmediate intensification
50-70%SuboptimalIntensify therapy
>70%TargetMaintain
>85%ExcellentConsider deintensify if hypo risk

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
CGM (rtCGM/isCGM)Primary TIR measurement (14-day wear minimum)
AGP ReportStandardised visualisation (median, IQR, TIR, TBR, TAR, GMI, CV)
HbA1cComplementary; GMI ≈ HbA1c

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

CGM-Guided Therapy

AGP PatternAction
High TARIncrease basal/bolus; review carb counting
High TBRReduce basal/bolus; adjust timing; carb before bed
High CVReview meal regularity; carb consistency; bolus timing
Wide IQRReview carb estimation; bolus timing; snack patterns

Pregnancy-Specific

MetricTarget (T1DM)Target (T2DM)
TIR (63-140 mg/dL)>70%>70%
TBR <63 mg/dL<4%<4%
TAR >140 mg/dL<25%<25%

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
TIR target>70% (70-180 mg/dL / 3.9-10.0 mmol/L)
TBR <70<4%
TBR <54<1%
CV<36%
GMI≈ HbA1c from CGM mean glucose
PregnancyTIR 63-140 mg/dL >70%

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the ATTD TIR targets for T1DM?TIR >70% (70-180 mg/dL); TBR <4% (<70), <1% (<54); TAR <25% (>180); CV <36%
What is GMI?Glucose Management Indicator - estimates HbA1c from CGM mean glucose
What is the relationship between TIR and HbA1c?TIR 70% ≈ HbA1c 7.0% (53 mmol/mol); each 10% TIR ≈ 0.5% HbA1c
When is CV >36%?High glucose variability; associated with hypoglycaemia risk and complications

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
TIR replaces HbA1c?NO - complementary; HbA1c = 3-month average; TIR = daily variability
TIR same in pregnancy?NO - pregnancy range 63-140 mg/dL; tighter
GMI = HbA1c?Approximate; GMI from CGM mean; HbA1c from RBC glycation

Mnemonic: CGM-TIR-TARGET

  • CGM: rtCGM (Dexcom) vs isCGM (Libre)
  • Glucose: 70-180 mg/dL (3.9-10 mmol/L) = TIR range
  • Metrics: TIR>70%, TBR<4%/1%, TAR<25%, CV<36%
  • Trend arrows: ↑↑+20%, ↑+10%, →0%, ↓-10%, ↓↓-20%+carbs
  • Interpret: AGP report (median, IQR, daily profiles)
  • Real-time alerts: rtCGM only (Dexcom); isCGM scan (Libre)
  • GMI: estimates HbA1c from mean glucose
  • Evidence: ATTD consensus targets
  • TIR >70% = target
  • ATTD: consensus group
  • Range: 70-180 mg/dL (3.9-10 mmol/L)
  • GMI ≈ HbA1c
  • Evidence: TIR 10% = 0.5% HbA1c
  • Target: >70% TIR, <4% TBR, <1% TBR54
  • AGP: standardised visualisation
  • Range: TIR/TBR/TAR/CV/GMI
  • Evidence: ATTD 2019/2023
  • TIR vs HbA1c: complementary, not replacement**

Topic Overview

Time in range (TIR) targets
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
ATTD Consensus Targets (2019/2023)
TIR Categories (Clinical)
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
CGM-Guided Therapy
Pregnancy-Specific
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.2.13 X Pert Type 2

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, desmond-xpert, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["DAFNE (type 1)", "Diabetes education and structured education", "Self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) frequency"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

DESMOND/X-PERT (type 2)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe DESMOND and X-PERT programme structure
  • [ ] State eligibility and referral criteria
  • [ ] Apply outcomes evidence (HbA1c, weight, QoL)
  • [ ] Differentiate DESMOND vs X-PERT format

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DESMONDDiabetes Education and Self-Management for Ongoing and Newly Diagnosed
X-PERTeXpert Patient Education Research Training
TargetNewly diagnosed T2DM (DESMOND); T2DM any duration (X-PERT)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - education programmes)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

DESMOND

ComponentDetail
Format1-day group (6-12 participants) or 2x half-days
CurriculumSelf-management, lifestyle, medication understanding, goal setting, complications
EducatorsTrained lay educators + healthcare professionals
EvidenceHbA1c ↓0.5-1.0%; weight ↓2-3kg; self-efficacy ↑

X-PERT

ComponentDetail
Format6-week (2.5hr/week) group programme
CurriculumLifestyle, medications, monitoring, complications, psychological wellbeing
EducatorsTrained X-PERT educators (dietitians/nurses)
EvidenceHbA1c ↓0.3-0.5%; weight ↓3-4kg; sustained at 14 months

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Programme Comparison

FeatureDESMONDX-PERT
Duration1 day (or 2x half-day)6 weeks (2.5hr/week)
Group size6-1210-15
FocusSelf-management, goal-settingLifestyle, meds, monitoring, complications
EvidenceHbA1c ↓0.5-1.0%HbA1c ↓0.3-0.5%

NICE Recommendations

RecommendationDetail
Offer at diagnosisAll newly diagnosed T2DM
Annual reviewOffer refresher if not attended within 12 months
Quality criteriaTrained educators; structured curriculum; group (6-12); written materials; audit

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DESMOND1-day group; newly diagnosed T2DM; self-management, lifestyle
X-PERT6-week group; T2DM any duration; lifestyle, medications, monitoring
OutcomesHbA1c ↓0.3-1.0%; weight ↓2-4kg; self-efficacy ↑
NICEOffer at diagnosis; annual review; quality-assured programmes
EducatorsTrained lay + healthcare professionals

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is DESMOND?Diabetes Education and Self-Management for Ongoing and Newly Diagnosed; 1-day group for newly diagnosed T2DM
What is X-PERT?6-week structured education programme for T2DM (any duration)
How do DESMOND and X-PERT differ?DESMOND: 1-day, newly diagnosed; X-PERT: 6-week, any duration T2DM
What are the outcomes of structured education?HbA1c ↓0.3-1.0%; weight ↓2-4kg; self-efficacy ↑; QoL ↑
What are NICE quality criteria for structured education?Trained educators; structured curriculum; group 6-12; written materials; audit

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DESMOND = X-PERT?NO - DESMOND: 1-day, newly diagnosed; X-PERT: 6-week, any T2DM
Structured education = optional?NO - NICE: offer at diagnosis, then annually
Weight loss = primary outcome?Secondary; primary = self-management skills, HbA1c

Mnemonic: DESMOND-XPERT-T2DM

  • DESMOND: Diabetes Education Self-Management Ongoing Newly Diagnosed
  • Editor's: 1-day group (newly diagnosed)
  • Self-management: lifestyle, meds, goals
  • Modules: lifestyle, meds, monitoring, complications, goals
  • Once: at diagnosis, then annual
  • NICE: offered at diagnosis
  • Duration: 1 day (or 2 half-days)
  • X-PERT: 6-week (2.5hr/week) group
  • Programme: T2DM any duration
  • Expert: trained educators
  • Results: HbA1c↓0.3-1.0%, weight↓2-4kg
  • T2DM: any duration
  • Modules: lifestyle, meds, monitor, complications, psychology
  • Evidence: HbA1c↓0.3-0.5%, weight↓3-4kg
  • Refresher: annual if not attended
  • Quality: trained educators, group 6-12, audit
  • Annual: offer if not attended
  • Participants: 10-15
  • Trainer: trained lay + HCP
  • Self-efficacy: improved

Topic Overview

DESMOND/X-PERT (type 2

2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
DESMOND
X-PERT
5. Diagnosis & Investigations

7. Management
Programme Comparison
NICE Recommendations

▸ 21.2.2 Cv Risk Assessment

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, cv-risk-assessment, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Diabetes as cardiovascular risk equivalent", "ASCVD risk calculators (QRISK3, ASCVD Pooled Cohort)", "Statin therapy in diabetes (intensity, targets)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

CV risk assessment

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply QRISK3 and ASCVD Pooled Cohort risk calculators
  • [ ] Determine statin and aspirin indications
  • [ ] Apply BP and lipid targets
  • [ ] Apply SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for CV risk reduction

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
CV RiskDiabetes = 2-4x CVD risk
Leading cause deathCVD (50-60% of diabetes deaths)
EquivalentT2DM age >40 = CHD risk equivalent (historical)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - risk assessment)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Risk Calculators

CalculatorRegionIncludes Diabetes
QRISK3UKYes (type, duration, HbA1c, eGFR)
ASCVD Pooled CohortUSBinary (yes/no)
SCORE2 / SCORE2-DPEuropeDuration, complications

QRISK3 Variables

VariableIncluded
DiabetesType, duration, HbA1c
RenaleGFR, CKD
MedicationsSteroids, antipsychotics
Atrial fibrillationYes
MigraineYes
SLE/RAYes
DeprivationTownsend score

Statin Intensity Guidelines

GuidelinePopulationStatinTarget LDL
NICE (UK)T2DM age 40-84Atorvastatin 20mg (primary); 80mg (secondary)<1.8mmol/L or ↓50%
ADA/ACC (US)DM age 40-75High-intensity atorvastatin 40-80mg<1.8mmol/L or ↓50%
ADA/ACC (US)DM age >75Moderate-intensity<1.8mmol/L or ↓30-50%
ESC/EAS (EU)DM (very high risk)High-intensity<1.4mmol/L or ↓50%
ESC/EAS (EU)DM + ASCVDHigh-intensity + ezetimibe<1.0mmol/L or ↓50%

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
Lipid profileBaseline, 3 months post-statin, then 6-12 monthly
HbA1c3-monthly until target, then 6-monthly
BPEvery visit
UACR/eGFRAnnual

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
Lipid profileBaseline, 3 months post-statin, then 6-12 monthly
HbA1c3-monthly until target, then 6-monthly
BPEvery visit
UACR/eGFRAnnual

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

CV Risk Assessment Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Diabetes Patient
↓
Established ASCVD?
↓
High-intensity statin + aspirin 75mg
10-year Risk >=10% (QRISK3/ASCVD)?
↓
High-intensity statin; consider aspirin (ADA)
Lifestyle; consider moderate statin
↓
LDL target <1.8mmol/L or ↓50%
↓
LDL target met?
↓
Add ezetimibe 10mg
Continue + monitor 6-12mo

Aspirin Primary Prevention

GuidelineRecommendation
ADA 2024Age 50-70, ASCVD risk ≥10%, low bleeding risk
ESC 2021Not recommended (net harm)
NICE NG128Do not offer
ASCEND TrialDM no CVD: ↓vascular events 12% but ↑major bleeding 29% → net neutral

BP Targets

GuidelineTarget
ADA/ESC<130/80
NICE<140/90 clinic (<135/85 ABPM); <130/80 if CKD/albuminuria
KDIGOCKD/Albuminuria: <130/80

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Risk calculatorsQRISK3 (UK, includes DM details); ASCVD Pooled Cohort (US, binary DM)
StatinAge 40-75: high-intensity atorvastatin 40-80mg; target LDL <1.8mmol/L
Aspirin primaryADA: age 50-70, risk≥10%; ESC/NICE: not routinely
BP targetsADA/ESC: <130/80; NICE: <130/80 if CKD/albuminuria
LDL targetsPrimary: <1.8mmol/L; Secondary: <1.4mmol/L; Extreme: <1.0mmol/L

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What CVD risk calculator is used in the UK?QRISK3 – includes age, sex, ethnicity, smoking, diabetes (type, duration, HbA1c), CKD, eGFR, steroids, AF, BP, cholesterol, BMI, FH, deprivation
What statin intensity for T2DM age 40-75 without ASCVD?High-intensity atorvastatin 40-80mg (ADA/ACC); NICE: atorvastatin 20mg
When is aspirin recommended for primary prevention in diabetes?ADA: age 50-70, 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%, no bleeding risk; ESC/NICE: not routinely
What is the LDL target for primary prevention in diabetes?<1.8mmol/L or ≥50% reduction from baseline
What is the QRISK3 threshold for statin in UK?QRISK3 ≥10% 10-year CVD risk

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All diabetics need high-intensity statin?Age >75 or frail: moderate intensity; primary prevention age 40-75 = high-intensity
Aspirin for all diabetics?NO - primary prevention: selective (ADA) or not recommended (ESC/NICE); secondary: ALWAYS
LDL <1.8 for everyone?Secondary prevention: <1.4; extreme risk: <1.0 (ESC)

Mnemonic: CVD-RISK-DM-CALC

  • CVD risk: 2-4x in DM
  • Vascular death: 50-60% of DM deaths
  • Diabetes: risk enhancer in all calculators
  • RISK3 (UK): includes DM type, duration, HbA1c, eGFR
  • ISCVD Pooled (US): binary DM only
  • SCORE2 (EU): duration + complications
  • KDIGO: CKD specific
  • Calculator choice: by geography
  • Aspirin: primary prevention selective (ADA) or none (ESC/NICE)
  • LDL targets: primary <1.8; secondary <1.4
  • Calculator: don't mix (different populations)
  • Undersestimate: young T1DM (10yr low, lifetime high)
  • Lipoproteins: apoB, non-HDL if high TG
  • Age: young T1DM low 10yr risk
  • Thresholds: UK ≥10% (QRISK3); US ≥7.5% (ASCVD)
  • Overestimation: well-controlled T2DM
  • Refinement: CAC score, Lp(a) if intermediate risk**
▸ 21.2.2 Glycaemic Targets
[!info]
No single target — individualise by diabetes type, duration, age, frailty, complications, hypoglycaemia risk, pregnancy; NICE/ADA/ESC broadly aligned: T1DM <48 mmol/mol (6.5%); T2DM <48–58; pregnancy <48; elderly <53–75 by frailty.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State HbA1c targets by guideline and population
  • [ ] Apply individualisation principles (hypo risk, life expectancy, complications)
  • [ ] Use CGM metrics (TIR, TBR, TAR, GMI, CV) as primary targets
  • [ ] Differentiate fasting, pre-prandial, post-prandial targets
  • [ ] Apply pregnancy and inpatient targets

2. HbA1c Targets by Guideline

NICE NG28 / NG17

PopulationTargetNotes
T1DM (adults)<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)If achievable without problematic hypoglycaemia
T2DM (adults)<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)If achievable without hypoglycaemia; relax to <58 (7.5%) if high hypo risk, frailty, limited life expectancy
T2DM (new diagnosis)<48 mmol/molIntensive early control → legacy effect (UKPDS)
Pregnancy (pre-existing)<48 mmol/molPre-conception and throughout
Children T1DM<48 mmol/molIf achievable without severe hypo

ADA 2024 Standards of Care

PopulationTargetNotes
Most non-pregnant adults<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)Individualise
If achievable safely<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)Especially T1DM, new T2DM
High hypo risk / limited life expectancy / advanced complications<58–64 mmol/mol (7.5–8.0%)Relaxed
Pregnancy (pre-existing)<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)<42 (6.0%) if achievable without hypo
GDMNot applicable (use glucose targets)

ESC/EASD 2023

PopulationTarget
T1DM<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
T2DM<53 mmol/mol (7.0%); <48 if safe
Elderly/frailIndividualised by frailty (see below)
Pregnancy<48 (6.5%)

3. Individualisation Framework (ADA/ESC/NICE Consensus)

FactorTighten Target (<48 / <6.5%)Relax Target (>58 / >7.5%)
Diabetes durationShort (<5y T2DM; new T1DM)Long (>10–20y)
AgeYoung (<40–50)Elderly (>65–70)
FrailtyFit (CFS 1–3)Frail (CFS 5–7)
Hypoglycaemia riskNo severe hypo, good awarenessRecurrent Level 2/3, unawareness
ComplicationsNone / earlyAdvanced CVD, CKD G4–5, proliferative retinopathy
Life expectancy>10–15 years<5–10 years
Patient preferenceMotivated, engagedOverwhelmed, competing priorities
ComorbiditiesFewMany

HbA1c <42 (6.0%): Not routinely recommended — ↑ mortality signal in ACCORD, ↑ hypoglycaemia.

4. Frailty-Based Targets (Elderly) — See "Frailty and Individualised HbA1c Targets"

Frailty Category (CFS)HbA1c Target
Fit (1–3)<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
Vulnerable (4)<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)
Frail (5–7)<64 mmol/mol (8.0%)
Severely frail / End-of-life (8–9)<75 mmol/mol (9.0%) — symptom control only

5. CGM-Based Targets (ATTD / ADA / EASD Consensus) — Primary for Insulin Users

MetricTarget (General)Target (Pregnancy)Target (High Risk/Frail)
TIR (3.9–10.0 mmol/L / 70–180 mg/dL)>70%>70% (3.5–7.8)>50%
TBR Level 1 (3.0–3.9 / 54–69)<4%<4%<1%
TBR Level 2 (<3.0 / <54)<1%<1%<0.5%
TAR Level 1 (10.1–13.9 / 181–250)<25%<25%<50%
TAR Level 2 (>13.9 / >250)<5%<5%<10%
CV (Coefficient of Variation)<36%<36%<40%
GMI (Glucose Management Indicator)<53 (7.0%)<48 (6.5%)Individualised

TIR >70% ≈ HbA1c <53 (7.0%); each 5% TIR ≈ 0.5% HbA1c.

6. SMBG / Capillary Targets (Non-CGM)

PopulationFasting / Pre-Prandial1-Hour Post-Prandial2-Hour Post-PrandialBedtime
T1DM (NICE)4–7 mmol/L<9 mmol/L<8.5 mmol/L (2h)5–9 mmol/L
T2DM (NICE)4–7 mmol/L—<8.5 mmol/L—
Pregnancy (NICE)<5.3<7.8<6.4—
Pregnancy (ADA)<5.3 (95)<7.8 (140)<6.7 (120)—
Elderly/Frail5–8<11<106–10

7. Special Situations

SituationTarget
Pre-conception (T1/T2)HbA1c <48 (6.5%); <42 (6.0%) if safe
Pregnancy (T1/T2)Fasting <5.3; 1h <7.8; 2h <6.4; HbA1c <48 (NICE) / <42 if safe (ADA)
GDMSame as pregnancy targets
Inpatient (non-critical)Pre-meal 5–10; random <12; avoid <4
Inpatient (critical/ICU)6–10 mmol/L (NICE-SUGAR: intensive <6 ↑ mortality)
Post-transplantIndividualised; steroid hyperglycaemia common
Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes<53 (7.0%) if stable

8. The "Legacy Effect" — Early Intensive Control

TrialPopulationInterventionLong-Term Benefit
UKPDS (1998/2008)New T2DMHbA1c ~7.0% vs ~7.9%10y post-trial: ↓ MI 15%, ↓ death 13%, ↓ microvascular 24%
DCCT/EDIC (1993/2005)T1DMHbA1c ~7.0% vs ~9.0%30y follow-up: ↓ CVD 30%, ↓ retinopathy/nephropathy progression

Implication: Early intensive control in new T2DM and T1DM has lifelong benefits — "metabolic memory."

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
NICE T1DM<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
NICE T2DM<48 if safe; relax to <58 (7.5%) if hypo risk/frailty
ADA most adults<53 mmol/mol (7.0%); <48 if safe
Pregnancy HbA1c<48 (6.5%); ADA <42 (6.0%) if safe
Elderly frailty targetsFit <53; Vulnerable <58; Frail <64; End-of-life <75
CGM TIR target>70% (3.9–10.0); pregnancy 3.5–7.8 >70%
CGM TBR Level 2<1% (<3.0 mmol/L / 54 mg/dL)
CGM CV target<36%
Legacy effectUKPDS/DCCT: early intensive → lifelong CVD/microvascular benefit
Inpatient critical6–10 mmol/L (NICE-SUGAR: <6 ↑ mortality)
IndividualisationNo one-size-fits-all; balance benefit vs harm

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. HbA1c Targets by Guideline
NICE NG28 / NG17
ADA 2024 Standards of Care
ESC/EASD 2023
3. Individualisation Framework (ADA/ESC/NICE Consensus)
4. Frailty-Based Targets (Elderly) — See "Frailty and Individualised HbA1c Ta...
5. CGM-Based Targets (ATTD / ADA / EASD Consensus) — Primary for Insulin Users
6. SMBG / Capillary Targets (Non-CGM)
7. Special Situations
8. The "Legacy Effect" — Early Intensive Control
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.2.3 Dafne Type 1

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, dafne, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["DESMOND/X-PERT (type 2)", "Diabetes education and structured education", "Insulin therapy"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

DAFNE (type 1)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe DAFNE programme structure and content
  • [ ] Apply DAFNE eligibility criteria
  • [ ] State DAFNE outcomes (HbA1c, severe hypoglycaemia, QoL)
  • [ ] Recognise DAFNE-HART for pump users

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Full nameDose Adjustment For Normal Eating
TargetType 1 diabetes on basal-bolus insulin (MDI or CSII)
Format5-day group course (or 5x1-day weekly); 6-8 participants
EvidenceHbA1c ↓0.5-1.0%; severe hypo ↓; QoL ↑

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - education programme)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

DAFNE Curriculum (5 Days)

DayContent
Day 1Principles of carb counting; insulin:carb ratios; correction doses
Day 2Insulin sensitivity factor (ISF); blood glucose targets; pattern management
Day 3Exercise; alcohol; sick day rules; hypoglycaemia management
Day 4Eating out; travel; pregnancy; complications; pump basics
Day 5Review; personal action plan; goal setting; follow-up

DAFNE-HART (Pump Users)

AdditionContent
Pump skillsBasal rates, bolus wizard, temporary basal, suspend on low
AdvancedDual wave/square bolus; activity settings; CGM integration

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Eligibility (NICE TA)

CriteriaRequirement
DiagnosisT1DM ≥6 months
Insulin regimenBasal-bolus (MDI) or CSII
HbA1c>58 mmol/mol (7.5%) on MDI OR severe hypoglycaemia
MotivationWilling to self-manage; attend 5-day course

Outcomes (Evidence)

OutcomeDAFNE Effect
HbA1c↓0.5-1.0% (5-11 mmol/mol)
Severe hypoglycaemia↓50-70%
Quality of lifeSignificant improvement
WeightNeutral
Diabetes distressSignificant reduction

Quality Assurance

ElementStandard
EducatorsTrained DAFNE educators (dietitians/nurses)
Group size6-8 participants
CurriculumStandardised national curriculum
AuditMandatory annual outcome audit

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ProgrammeDose Adjustment For Normal Eating; 5-day group course
TargetT1DM on basal-bolus (MDI/CSII); HbA1c>58 or severe hypo
Core skillsCarb counting, ICR, ISF, correction doses, sick days
OutcomesHbA1c ↓0.5-1.0%; severe hypo ↓50-70%; QoL ↑
DAFNE-HARTDAFNE + pump skills for CSII users
QualityTrained educators; standardised curriculum; audit

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is DAFNE?Dose Adjustment For Normal Eating; 5-day structured education for T1DM on basal-bolus
Who is eligible for DAFNE?T1DM ≥6 months on basal-bolus (MDI/CSII) with HbA1c>58mmol/mol or severe hypoglycaemia
What are the core skills taught in DAFNE?Carbohydrate counting, insulin:carb ratio (ICR), insulin sensitivity factor (ISF), correction doses, sick day rules
What are the outcomes of DAFNE?HbA1c ↓0.5-1.0%; severe hypoglycaemia ↓50-70%; QoL improvement
What is DAFNE-HART?DAFNE plus pump skills (basal rates, bolus wizard, temporary basal, suspend on low)
What are the NICE criteria for DAFNE referral?T1DM, HbA1c>58mmol/mol on MDI or severe hypoglycaemia; willing/able to attend

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DAFNE = pump training?NO - DAFNE = MDI skills; DAFNE-HART = pump skills add-on
DAFNE = all T1DM?NO - only if HbA1c>58 or severe hypo; motivated
DAFNE = weight loss?NO - weight neutral; focus on glycaemic control

Mnemonic: DAFNE-EDUCATION

  • DAFNE: Dose Adjustment For Normal Eating
  • Adjustment: insulin for carbs (ICR/ISF)
  • Five days: structured group education
  • NICE criteria: HbA1c>58 or severe hypo on MDI/CSII
  • Education: carb counting, ICR, ISF, sick days
  • Evidence: HbA1c↓0.5-1.0%, hypo↓50-70%, QoL↑
  • DAFNE-HART: pump add-on (basal rates, bolus wizard)
  • UK standard: NICE TA approved
  • Cohort: 6-8 patients, 5 days
  • Audit: mandatory outcome audit
  • Type 1 only: not for T2DM
  • Insulin: MDI or CSII
  • Outcomes: HbA1c↓, hypo↓, QoL↑
  • Nat. curriculum: standardised**

Topic Overview

DAFNE (type 1
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
DAFNE Curriculum (5 Days)
DAFNE-HART (Pump Users)
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Eligibility (NICE TA)
Outcomes (Evidence)
Quality Assurance
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.2.3 Psychosocial Aspects
[!info]
Psychosocial comorbidity is the rule, not exception — Depression 2–3×, anxiety 20%, diabetes distress 30–45%, eating disorders ↑; all worsen glycaemic control and outcomes.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State prevalence of depression, anxiety, diabetes distress, eating disorders in diabetes
  • [ ] Distinguish diabetes distress from clinical depression
  • [ ] Apply screening tools (PHQ-9, GAD-7, DDS, PAID, SCOFF)
  • [ ] Explain bidirectional relationship with glycaemic control
  • [ ] Describe management principles and referral pathways

2. Prevalence (Meta-Analyses)

ConditionT1DMT2DMGeneral PopulationRelative Risk
Major Depressive Disorder12–18%10–15%5–7%2–3×
Depressive symptoms (subthreshold)25–30%20–25%10–15%2×
Anxiety disorders15–20%14–18%7–10%2×
Diabetes distress30–45%30–40%N/A—
Eating disorders2–4× (esp. women)↑ binge eating1–3%2–4×

3. Diabetes Distress vs Depression — Critical Distinction

FeatureDiabetes DistressMajor Depression
DefinitionEmotional burden of self-management demandsPervasive low mood, anhedonia, neurovegetative symptoms
ScopeDiabetes-specificGlobal
Prevalence30–45% (much higher)10–18%
DurationFluctuates with diabetes demands≥2 weeks persistent
Treatment responseDiabetes education, peer support, CBT-DMAntidepressants + CBT
Screening toolDDS-17 / PAID / PHQ-9PHQ-9

Key: Diabetes distress responds to diabetes-specific interventions (structured education, CGM, peer support), not antidepressants.

4. Screening Tools (NICE/ADA Recommended)

ToolTargetThresholdTime
PHQ-9Depression≥10 (moderate); ≥15 (severe)2 min
GAD-7Anxiety≥102 min
DDS-17 (Diabetes Distress Scale)Diabetes distressMean ≥2 (moderate); ≥3 (high)3 min
PAID (Problem Areas in Diabetes)Diabetes distress≥40 (moderate)2 min
WHO-5Well-being≤50 (poor)1 min
SCOFF / ESPEating disorders≥2 / ≥11 min

When to screen: At diagnosis, annually, when complications arise, if poor engagement, HbA1c not improving despite treatment.

5. Bidirectional Relationship with Glycaemic Control

Depression/Distress
       ↓
Poor self-care (missed doses, diet, monitoring, appointments)
       ↓
Hyperglycaemia → Complications → More distress
       ↑
Complications / hypoglycaemia fear → More distress

Evidence:

  • Depression → 1.5–2× higher HbA1c
  • Depression → 1.5–2× higher complication rates (retinopathy, CVD, mortality)
  • Treating depression → modest HbA1c improvement (~0.3–0.5%)

6. Eating Disorders in Diabetes

DisorderT1DM PrevalenceT2DM PrevalenceKey Features
Diabulimia (insulin omission for weight loss)Highest risk (women 15–30y)RareHbA1c very high, recurrent DKA, normal/low BMI
Binge eating (BED)5–10%15–25%Weight gain, guilt, no compensatory behaviour
Bulimia nervosa↑ vs general↑Binge + purge (vomiting, laxatives, insulin omission)
Anorexia nervosaRare in established T1DMRareSevere insulin restriction

Screening: SCOFF questionnaire; ask about insulin omission, weight/shape concerns.

7. Hypoglycaemia Fear / Avoidance

BehaviourConsequence
Overtreatment (running high to avoid hypos)HbA1c ↑, long-term complications
Reduced activity/exerciseFitness loss, weight gain, IR
Sleep disruption (nocturnal checking)Fatigue, distress
Avoidance of driving/travelSocial isolation

Management: CGM with alarms, structured education (DAFNE), psychological support.

8. Management Principles

Stepped Care Model (NICE/ADA)

StepInterventionFor
1. UniversalStructured education (DAFNE/DESMOND), peer support, diabetes distress screening (DDS/PAID)All
2. Mild distress/low moodGuided self-help (CBT-based apps, SilverCloud), diabetes-focused CBT (CBT-DM), DSN supportDDS 2–3, PHQ-9 <10
3. Moderate depression/anxietyHigh-intensity CBT (IAPT/psychology), antidepressant (SSRI 1st line), consider diabetes psychologyPHQ-9 10–19, GAD-7 ≥10
4. Severe/complexPsychiatry referral, combined psychotherapy + medication, crisis planningPHQ-9 ≥20, suicide risk, eating disorder, treatment-resistant

Antidepressant choice in diabetes:

DrugGlycaemic EffectWeightNotes
SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram, fluoxetine)Neutral/slight ↓ HbA1cNeutral/slight ↓1st line
SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine)NeutralNeutralAlso for neuropathic pain
Mirtazapine↑ HbA1c↑ WeightAvoid if obese
TCAs↑ HbA1c↑↑ WeightAnticholinergic; avoid
BupropionNeutral/↓↓ WeightLower seizure threshold (hypoglycaemia risk)

9. Special Populations

PopulationSpecific Issues
Young adults (18–30)Transition gap; high distress; eating disorders; "diabetes burnout"
Older adultsDepression often missed (attributed to aging); polypharmacy; cognitive decline; isolation
Pregnancy (GDM/pre-existing)Anxiety high; perinatal depression screen (EPDS); body image
Ethnic minoritiesCultural stigma; language barriers; different illness beliefs; lower access to psychology
Learning disabilitiesDiagnostic overshadowing; adapted tools needed

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Depression in diabetes2–3× general population
Diabetes distress30–45% prevalence; NOT depression — responds to education/peer support
Screening toolsPHQ-9 (depression), GAD-7 (anxiety), DDS-17/PAID (diabetes distress)
DiabulimiaInsulin omission for weight loss — T1DM women; recurrent DKA, high HbA1c
CBT-DMDiabetes-focused CBT — effective for distress AND HbA1c
Antidepressant 1st lineSSRI (sertraline/citalopram) — neutral weight/glycaemia
AvoidMirtazapine (weight↑), TCAs (weight↑, anticholinergic)
Hypoglycaemia fearCauses high HbA1c, reduced activity, social isolation — CGM helps
Eating disorder screenSCOFF / ESP — ask about insulin omission

11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Prevalence (Meta-Analyses)
3. Diabetes Distress vs Depression — Critical Distinction
4. Screening Tools (NICE/ADA Recommended)
5. Bidirectional Relationship with Glycaemic Control
6. Eating Disorders in Diabetes
7. Hypoglycaemia Fear / Avoidance
8. Management Principles
Stepped Care Model (NICE/ADA)
9. Special Populations
10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

▸ 21.2.4 Diabetes Education And Structured Education

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, diabetes-education, structured-education, dafne, desmond, xpert, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - DAFNE (T1DM), DESMOND/X-PERT (T2DM); NICE: offer at diagnosis; structured education = core; outcomes: HbA1c -5 to -10 mmol/mol; QALY gain; digital alternatives"

see_also: ["Annual review and complication screening", "Glycaemic targets", "Psychosocial aspects", "Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Diabetes Education and Structured Education Programmes

[!info]
Structured education = NICE quality standard — offer at diagnosis (T1DM: DAFNE; T2DM: DESMOND/X-PERT); evidence: HbA1c −5 to −10 mmol/mol, ↑ knowledge, ↑ QoL, cost-effective; digital options emerging.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Name and differentiate major structured education programmes
  • [ ] State NICE criteria for offering education
  • [ ] Summarise evidence for clinical outcomes
  • [ ] Apply referral criteria and timing
  • [ ] Understand digital/remote alternatives

2. Major Programmes

ProgrammeTypeFormatDurationKey Features
DAFNE (Dose Adjustment For Normal Eating)T1DMGroup (6–8), 5 days (or 5× weekly)Carb counting + insulin dose adjustment; skills-based; QISMET accredited
DAFNE 1-day/OnlineT1DMShorter/digitalRefresher/remote access
DESMOND (Diabetes Education and Self-Management for Ongoing and Newly Diagnosed)T2DMGroup, 1 day (6h) or 2× half-dayLifestyle, beliefs, self-management; QISMET accredited
X-PERT (eXtend, Practical, Education, Research, Training)T2DMGroup, 6× 2.5h weeklyProgressive pathway; insulin module available
X-PERT InsulinT2DM on insulin6 sessionsCarb counting, dose adjustment
BERTIET1DM (pump/MDI)4 daysSimilar to DAFNE, pump focus
Digital (MyDiabetes, DAFNE Online, Oviva, Liva)T1/T2App/web, 12 weeks + coachingRemote, scalable; NICE-approved some

3. NICE Guidance (NG17, NG28, QS6)

RecommendationDetails
Offer at diagnosisT1DM: DAFNE (or equivalent) at diagnosis; T2DM: DESMOND/X-PERT at diagnosis
Annual reviewAssess need for refresher/education at each annual review
QISMET accreditationOnly QISMET-accredited programmes meet NICE standard
Cultural adaptationMust be available in appropriate languages/culturally adapted
Digital optionsCan supplement but not replace face-to-face initially
Carer/familyInclude carer/family if patient wishes

4. Evidence Base

OutcomeDAFNE (T1DM)DESMOND/X-PERT (T2DM)
HbA1c−5 to −10 mmol/mol (0.5–1.0%) at 6–12m−3 to −6 mmol/mol at 12m
Severe hypoglycaemia↓ 50–60%Not primary outcome
Diabetes knowledge↑↑↑↑
Quality of life↑ (distress ↓)↑
WeightNeutral↓ 1–3 kg (X-PERT > DESMOND)
Cost-effectivenessCost-saving (DAFNE: £1,500/QALY)Cost-effective (<£20,000/QALY)
Sustained benefitUp to 5 years (with refreshers)12–24 months (booster needed)

Key trials: DAFNE RCT (2002), DESMOND RCT (2008), X-PERT RCT (2007), 5-year DAFNE follow-up.

5. Core Curriculum Components (QISMET)

DomainDAFNE (T1DM)DESMOND/X-PERT (T2DM)
PathophysiologyInsulin physiology, glucose regulationInsulin resistance, beta-cell decline
MonitoringCapillary/CGM, pattern managementSMBG/CGM, when to test
CarbohydrateCarb counting (CP/10g), label readingCarb awareness, portions, GI
Insulin/DoseICR, ISF, correction, basal testingWhen to start, titration basics
HypoglycaemiaRecognition, treatment, preventionRecognition, treatment
Illness/Sick daysSick day rules, ketone testingSick day rules
Physical activityExercise management, carb adjustmentExercise prescription
ComplicationsScreening, preventionScreening, prevention
PsychologicalDiabetes distress, burnoutBeliefs, health behaviour change
LifestyleAlcohol, travel, drivingWeight, diet, smoking, alcohol

6. Referral Criteria & Timing

Patient GroupTimingProgramme
Newly diagnosed T1DMWithin 6–12 months (after stabilisation)DAFNE
Newly diagnosed T2DMWithin 12 monthsDESMOND / X-PERT
T1DM not previously attendedAny timeDAFNE
T2DM intensifying (insulin start)At insulin initiationX-PERT Insulin / DAFNE principles
Pump startPre-pumpBERTIE / DAFNE pump module
Recurrent hypoglycaemiaUrgentDAFNE refresher / specialist
Diabetes distress/burnoutIdentified at reviewPsychosocial + education

7. Digital & Remote Education

PlatformTargetEvidence
DAFNE OnlineT1DMNon-inferior to face-to-face (DAFNE Online RCT)
OvivaT2DM↓ HbA1c, weight loss (NHS DPP)
Liva HealthcareT2DM↓ HbA1c, cost-effective
MyDiabetes (NHS)T1/T2Structured content, tracking
Low Carb ProgramT2DM/pre-diabetesRemission outcomes (Virta/Diabetes.co.uk)

NICE position: Digital can supplement or replace if face-to-face not accessible; must be QISMET/ORCHA accredited.

8. Barriers & Solutions

BarrierSolution
Non-attendance (30–50%)Flexible timing, digital, reminders, transport, childcare
Language/cultureTranslated materials, bilingual educators, community venues
Learning disabilitiesAdapted programmes (e.g., OK Diabetes)
Work commitmentsEvening/weekend, digital, employer engagement
DeprivationCommunity-based, link workers, incentivise

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
DAFNET1DM — carb counting + insulin dose adjustment (ICR, ISF); 5-day group
DESMONDT2DM — lifestyle, beliefs, self-management; 1-day group
X-PERTT2DM — 6 weekly sessions; progressive; insulin module
NICE offer at diagnosisT1DM: DAFNE; T2DM: DESMOND/X-PERT
QISMETQuality standard accreditation — required for NICE compliance
HbA1c benefit DAFNE−5 to −10 mmol/mol; ↓ severe hypo 50–60%
HbA1c benefit DESMOND−3 to −6 mmol/mol
Cost-effectivenessDAFNE cost-saving; DESMOND/X-PERT <£20k/QALY
Digital optionsDAFNE Online, Oviva, Liva — supplement or replace if accessible
RefresherAnnual review should assess need for top-up education

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care|Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Annual review and complication screening]], [[Glycaemic targets]], [[Psychosocial aspects]], [[Diabetes Monitoring and Quality of Care]]

Topic Overview

Diabetes Education and Structured Education Programmes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Major Programmes
3. NICE Guidance (NG17, NG28, QS6)
4. Evidence Base
5. Core Curriculum Components (QISMET)
6. Referral Criteria & Timing
7. Digital & Remote Education
8. Barriers & Solutions
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.2.6 Hba1C Targets Individualised

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, hba1c-targets, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Time in range (TIR) targets", "Self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) frequency", "Annual review and complication screening"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

HbA1c targets (individualised)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply individualised HbA1c targets per ADA/EASD/NICE guidelines
  • [ ] Recognise when to tighten or relax targets
  • [ ] Counsel on HbA1c limitations (CKD, haemoglobinopathies)
  • [ ] Apply targets in special populations (elderly, pregnancy, CKD)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Standard target<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
IndividualisationBased on age, comorbidities, hypoglycaemia risk, life expectancy
MeasurementNGSP/DCCT standardised; NGSP = IFCC × 0.0915 + 2.15

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

HbA1c Targets by Guideline

GuidelineStandardTightLoose
ADA 2024<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)<48 (6.5%) if safe<58-64 (7.5-8.0%) if frail
EASD/ADA 2023<53<48 if achievable<58 (7.5%) if frail/elderly
NICE NG28<48 (6.5%)—<58-64 if frail/elderly

Targets by Patient Profile

PatientTargetRationale
Newly diagnosed, young, no CVD<48 (6.5%)Long life expectancy; prevent complications
Standard<53 (7.0%)Most adults
Elderly/frail/comorbid<58-64 (7.5-8.0%)Avoid hypoglycaemia; limited life expectancy
CVD/CKD<53 (7.0%)CVD benefit; avoid hypoglycaemia
Pregnancy<48 (6.5%) pre-conception; <43 (6.1%) in pregnancyFetal protection
CKD/ESRDIndividualised; HbA1c unreliableUse fructosamine/GA/CGM

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequencyNotes
HbA1c3-monthly until target; then 6-monthlyNGSP/DCCT standardised
FPG/SMBGPer regimenAdjunct to HbA1c
Fructosamine/GAIf HbA1c unreliable2-3 week average
CGM (TIR/GMI)If availableTIR >70% ≈ HbA1c <53

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Target Adjustment Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
New T2DM Diagnosis
↓
Age/Comorbidities?
↓
Target <48 (6.5%)
Target <53 (7.0%)
Target <58-64 (7.5-8.0%)

Special Situations

SituationTarget Adjustment
CKD G3b-G5HbA1c unreliable; use fructosamine/GA; target higher
ESRD/DialysisHbA1c unreliable; target <64; use GA/fructosamine
PregnancyPre-conception <48; <43 in 1st tri; <48 in 2nd/3rd
HaemoglobinopathyHbA1c unreliable; use fructosamine/GA/CGM
Elderly >80/frail<64 (8.0%) prioritise avoiding hypo

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Standard target<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
Tight target<48 (6.5%) if achievable without hypo, short duration, no CVD
Loose target<58-64 (7.5-8.0%) if elderly, frail, comorbidities, hypo risk
CKD/ESRDHbA1c unreliable; use fructosamine/GA; target relaxed
PregnancyPre-conception <48; pregnancy <43-48
Elderly/frail<58-64 to avoid hypoglycaemia

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the standard HbA1c target for most adults with T2DM?<53 mmol/mol (7.0%)
When do you target <48 mmol/mol (6.5%)?Newly diagnosed, young, no CVD, long life expectancy, no hypoglycaemia risk
When do you relax targets to <58-64 (7.5-8.0%)?Elderly, frail, comorbidities, hypoglycaemia unawareness, limited life expectancy
Why is HbA1c unreliable in CKD/ESRD?Falsely low due to ↓RBC lifespan, ESA use; use fructosamine/GA
What is the pre-conception HbA1c target?<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
What is the HbA1c target in pregnancy?<48 (6.5%) generally; <43 (6.1%) if achievable without hypo

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
One target for all?NO - must individualise based on age, comorbidities, hypo risk
Lower = better?NO - tight control in elderly increases mortality (ACCORD)
HbA1c always reliable?NO - CKD, haemoglobinopathy, ESA, pregnancy, recent transfusion

Mnemonic: HBA1C-TARGETS

  • HbA1c standard: <53 (7.0%)
  • Better control: <48 (6.5%) if safe
  • Adjust: individualise by age, comorbidities, hypo risk
  • 1 strict: <48 (newly diagnosed, young, no CVD)
  • Comorbidities: relax for CVD, CKD, elderly, frail
  • Target loose: <58-64 (7.5-8.0%) if frail/hypo risk
  • ADA: <53 standard; <48 tight; <58 loose
  • Relax: elderly, hypo, limited LE, CKD
  • Goal: individualise
  • Elderly: <64 if frail/limited LE
  • Tight: <48 if safe
  • Special: pregnancy <48; CKD higher; haemoglobinopathy use GA
  • ADA/EASD: individualise
  • Refine: 3-monthly until target
  • Glycaemic: variability matters (TIR)
  • Evidence: ACCORD (tight = harm in elderly)
  • Targets: multiple tiers
  • Explain: individualised
  • Together: shared decision-making**
▸ 21.2.7 Nephropathy Screening Acr Egfr

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, nephropathy-screening, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Diabetic kidney disease staging (KDIGO)", "Albuminuria categories (A1/A2/A3)", "GFR categories (G1-G5)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Nephropathy screening

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply annual nephropathy screening protocol (ACR and eGFR)
  • [ ] Interpret KDIGO staging and heat map
  • [ ] Apply proteinuria reduction targets
  • [ ] Recognise non-diabetic kidney disease red flags

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Screening targetAll diabetes patients annually
MethodSpot urine ACR + eGFR (CKD-EPI)
GoalEarly DKD detection; slow progression

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - screening asymptomatic)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

KDIGO 2022 Heat Map (GFR × Albuminuria)

GFR CategoryeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)A1 (<3)A2 (3-30)A3 (>30)
G1≥90🟢 Low🟡 Mod🟠 High
G260-89🟢 Low🟡 Mod🟠 High
G3a45-59🟡 Mod🟠 High🔴 Very High
G3b30-44🟠 High🔴 Very High🔴 Very High
G415-29🔴 Very High🔴 Very High🔴 Very High
G5<15🔴 Very High🔴 Very High🔴 Very High

Albuminuria Categories (ACR)

CategoryACR (mg/mmol)mg/gTerminology
A1<3<30Normal/mildly increased
A23-3030-300Moderately increased (microalbuminuria)
A3>30>300Severely increased (macroalbuminuria)

GFR Categories

CategoryeGFRDescription
G1≥90Normal/high
G260-89Mildly decreased
G3a45-59Mild-moderate
G3b30-44Moderate-severe
G415-29Severely decreased
G5<15Kidney failure

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Screening Protocol

TestMethodFrequencyAction Threshold
ACRSpot urine (early morning)AnnualA2: start ACEi/ARB; A3: max ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i
eGFRCKD-EPI 2021AnnualG3b+: nephrology referral
Urine microscopyIf ACR >3As neededRBC casts = non-diabetic

Confirmation Protocol

StepAction
1Abnormal ACR (≥3 mg/mmol)
2Repeat 2/3 times over 3-6 months
3Confirm persistent ACR ≥3 = DKD

6. Differential Diagnosis

Red FlagSignificance
Rapid eGFR decline>5 mL/min/1.73m²/year
Active sedimentRBC >10/µL, RBC casts
No retinopathyEspecially if DM >10y + A3
Systemic symptomsFever, rash, arthralgia, haemoptysis
Low BMI at diagnosisType 2 DM usually overweight

6. Differential Diagnosis

Red FlagSignificance
Rapid eGFR decline>5 mL/min/1.73m²/year
Active sedimentRBC >10/µL, RBC casts
No retinopathyEspecially if DM >10y + A3
Systemic symptomsFever, rash, arthralgia, haemoptysis
Low BMI at diagnosisType 2 DM usually overweight

7. Management

Proteinuria Reduction Targets

TimepointTargetAction if Not Met
3-6 months↓ACR ≥30%Optimise ACEi/ARB; add SGLT2i
12 monthsACR <30 mg/mmol (A2) or <3 mg/mmol (A1)Add finerenone (if A3 on ACEi+SGLT2i)
OngoingSustained reductionMonitor 3-12mo by KDIGO category

Monitoring Intervals by KDIGO Category

Risk (Colour)ACR FrequencyeGFR FrequencyReview
Green (Low)AnnualAnnualPrimary care
Yellow (Moderate)6-monthly6-monthlyPrimary care ± specialist
Orange (High)3-6 monthly3-6 monthlyNephrology ± MDT
Red (Very High)1-3 monthly1-3 monthlyNephrology + MDT

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ScreeningAnnual spot urine ACR + eGFR (CKD-EPI)
ACR categoriesA1 <3; A2 3-30 (micro); A3 >30 (macro) mg/mmol
GFR categoriesG1-G5 (≥90, 60-89, 45-59, 30-44, 15-29, <15)
Heat mapG3aA2=moderate; G3bA3=very high risk
ACEi/ARBMax tolerated; target ACR <3 or ↓30%
SGLT2i1st line add-on (eGFR≥20 dapa/empa; ≥30 cana)
FinerenoneeGFR≥25, K+≤4.8; ACR≥30 on ACEi+SGLT2i
Proteinuria target↓ACR ≥30% at 3-6mo; ideal <3 mg/mmol

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the albuminuria categories (ACR)?A1: <3; A2: 3-30 (micro); A3: >30 (macro) mg/mmol
What are the GFR categories?G1 ≥90; G2 60-89; G3a 45-59; G3b 30-44; G4 15-29; G5 <15 mL/min/1.73m²
How do you confirm microalbuminuria?2/3 early morning spot urine ACR ≥3 mg/mmol in 3-6 months
What is the proteinuria reduction target?↓ACR ≥30% at 3-6 months; ultimate target <3 mg/mmol
When do you refer to nephrology?G4-5 (eGFR<30), rapid decline >5mL/min/yr, active sediment, A3 without retinopathy
What is the KDIGO heat map?GFR G1-5 × Albuminuria A1-3; colour codes risk (Green/Yellow/Orange/Red)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Single ACR diagnostic?NO - biological variability high; need 2/3 samples in 3-6mo
ACR vs PCR?ACR = albumin-specific (preferred for DKD); PCR = total protein
HbA1c in CKD?Falsely LOW (↓RBC lifespan); use fructosamine/GA

Mnemonic: NEPHROPATHY-SCREEN

  • Nephropathy: annual ACR + eGFR
  • Exclusion: active sediment, rapid decline, no retinopathy
  • Proteinuria: ACR ≥3 = microalbuminuria
  • Heat map: G1-5 × A1-3 (colour codes)
  • Reduction target: ↓ACR ≥30% at 3-6mo
  • Renal: eGFR CKD-EPI; G1-5
  • Only spot urine ACR (not PCR for DKD)
  • Persistent: 2/3 in 3-6mo confirms
  • ACEi/ARB: max dose; ACR↓30%
  • Target: ACR <3 or ↓30%
  • Yearly: annual screen all DM
  • SGLT2i: eGFR≥20 (dapa/empa)
  • CKD heat map: G3aA2=mod, G3bA3=very high
  • Refusal: nephrology G4-5, rapid decline
  • Exclude non-diabetic: active sediment, no retinopathy
  • Experts: nephrology MDT
  • Nephrology refer: G4-5, rapid decline, active sediment
  • Two of three: confirm ACR
  • Rapid decline: >5 mL/min/yr**

Topic Overview

Nephropathy screening
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
KDIGO 2022 Heat Map (GFR × Albuminuria)
Albuminuria Categories (ACR)
GFR Categories
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
Screening Protocol
Confirmation Protocol
6. Differential Diagnosis
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Proteinuria Reduction Targets
Monitoring Intervals by KDIGO Category
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.2.8 Neuropathy And Foot Screening

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, neuropathy-foot-screening, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Diabetic neuropathy", "Diabetic foot disease", "Foot risk classification (IWGDF/Scottish)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Neuropathy and foot screening

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Perform annual diabetic neuropathy and foot screening
  • [ ] Apply 10g monofilament technique correctly
  • [ ] Use 128Hz tuning fork and vibration perception test
  • [ ] Apply IWGDF/Scottish foot risk classification

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Screening targetAll diabetes patients annually
Tools10g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament, 128Hz tuning fork, ANNS questionnaire
GoalIdentify LOPS (loss of protective sensation) → refer podiatry/foot clinic

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Screening targetAll diabetes patients annually
Tools10g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament, 128Hz tuning fork, ANNS questionnaire
GoalIdentify LOPS (loss of protective sensation) → refer podiatry/foot clinic

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A — screening asymptomatic)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Screening Results Classification

CategoryMonofilamentVibrationANNSAction
NormalFeel all 6 sites>10s at hallux<3Annual rescreen
Possible DSPNMiss 1–2 sites5–10s3–6Repeat 6 months; education
LOPS (High risk)Miss ≥3 sites<5s≥7Refer podiatry/foot clinic; IWGDF risk 2–3

10g Monofilament Technique

StepDetail
Sites (6 per foot)Hallux pulp, 1st met head, 3rd met head, 5th met head, medial heel, lateral heel
ApplicationPerpendicular to skin; buckle (10g force); hold 1–2s
ResponsePatient says "yes" when felt; eyes closed
PassFeel at all 6 sites
FailCannot feel at ≥1 site

128Hz Tuning Fork

ParameterDetail
PlacementBony prominence: hallux IP joint (dorsal), medial malleolus
ActivationStrike against hand/knee; do not strike hard surface
TimingPatient indicates when vibration stops; examiner feels residual
Normal>10 seconds at hallux IP joint
Abnormal≤10 seconds

ANNS (Annual Neuropathy Symptom Score)

QuestionYes = 1
1. Numbness/tingling in feet?
2. Burning pain in feet?
3. Reduced sensation to touch?
4. Weakness in legs/feet?
Score ≥3Likely DSPN

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

ToolRole
MonofilamentPrimary screen (LOPS detection)
Tuning forkConfirmatory (large fibre function)
ANNSSymptom quantification
NCSNot routine; if atypical/mononeuropathy/radiculoplexus

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A — screening tool)

7. Management

Screening Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Annual Diabetic Review
↓
10g Monofilament (6 sites/foot)
↓
Feel all 6?
↓
128Hz Tuning Fork (hallux IP)
LOPS = Missed ≥1 site
↓
>10s?
ANNS Questionnaire
↓
Normal — Annual rescreen
Possible DSPN — Repeat 6mo
ANNS ≥3?
↓
Education + Repeat 6mo
LOPS / High risk
Possible DSPN — Repeat 6mo
↓
Refer Podiatry / Foot Clinic
↓
IWGDF Risk 2-3

Referral Pathways

FindingIWGDF RiskReferral
Normal0Annual rescreen
Possible DSPN (miss 1–2, vibration 5–10s, ANNS 3–6)1Podiatry; repeat 6 months
LOPS (miss ≥3, vibration <5s, ANNS ≥7)2–3MDT Foot Clinic

Patient Education (At Each Screen)

TopicKey Points
Daily foot inspectionCheck soles, between toes; mirror if needed
FootwearWide toe box, cushioned sole, no seams; avoid barefoot
Skin careMoisturise (not between toes); no corn plasters
Nail careStraight across; podiatry if difficult
Red flagsNew lesion, colour change, swelling, heat, pain → urgent review

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Annual screenAll DM (T1DM from Dx, T2DM from Dx)
Tools10g monofilament (6 sites), 128Hz tuning fork, ANNS
MonofilamentHallux, 1st/3rd/5th met heads, medial/lateral heel; buckle = 10g
Tuning fork128Hz on hallux IP >10s normal; ≤10s abnormal
ANNS4 questions; score ≥3 = likely DSPN
LOPSMiss ≥3 monofilament sites OR vibration <5s OR ANNS ≥7 → refer podiatry
IWGDF risk0: normal; 1: LOPS; 2: LOPS+PAD/deformity; 3: hx ulcer/amp

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do you perform 10g monofilament testing?Apply perpendicular to 6 sites/foot (hallux, 1st/3rd/5th met heads, medial/lateral heel); buckle to 10g; hold 1–2s; patient eyes closed says "yes" when felt
What are the 6 sites for monofilament testing?Hallux pulp, 1st metatarsal head, 3rd metatarsal head, 5th metatarsal head, medial heel, lateral heel
What is the normal vibration perception time with 128Hz tuning fork?>10 seconds at hallux interphalangeal joint
What is the ANNS and how is it scored?4 questions (numbness, burning, reduced touch, weakness); score ≥3 = likely DSPN
What constitutes LOPS (loss of protective sensation)?Cannot feel monofilament at ≥3 sites OR vibration <5s OR ANNS ≥7
What is the referral pathway for abnormal screening?Normal → annual; Possible DSPN (miss 1–2, vibration 5–10s, ANNS 3–6) → repeat 6mo; LOPS → refer podiatry/MDT foot clinic

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Monofilament at 1 site only?NO — must test 6 sites/foot; single site misses patchy LOPS
Tuning fork on soft tissue?NO — on bony prominence (hallux IP, medial malleolus)
ANNS = diagnosis?NO — screening tool; clinical exam + NCS for diagnosis

Mnemonic: SCREEN-FEET

  • Screening: annual all DM
  • Cmonofilament: 10g, 6 sites/foot (hallux, 1/3/5 met, med/lat heel)
  • Response: patient eyes closed, says "yes"
  • Examiner: buckle to 10g, hold 1-2s
  • Evaluation: feel all 6 = pass; miss ≥1 = LOPS risk
  • Normal: feel all 6, vibration >10s, ANNS <3
  • Fork: 128Hz on hallux IP >10s normal
  • Evaluate vibration: ≤10s abnormal
  • EANNS: 4 questions, ≥3 = likely DSPN
  • Triage: normal=annual; possible=6mo; LOPS=refer podiatry
  • Summary: monofilament primary, fork confirmatory, ANNS symptomatic
▸ 21.3 Diabetes Technology and Digital Health 15
▸ 21.3.1 Automated Insulin Delivery Aid Systems
[!info]
AID spectrum: HCL (basal only) → Advanced HCL (auto-correction) → Full closed-loop (basal + bolus) → Bihormonal (insulin + glucagon) → Multi-hormone (amylin, GLP-1).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify AID systems by automation level
  • [ ] Explain limitations of HCL (meal announcement burden)
  • [ ] Describe bihormonal systems and glucagon stability challenges
  • [ ] Understand role of faster insulins (faster aspart, Lyumjev, FiAsp)
  • [ ] Identify emerging multi-hormone approaches

2. AID Automation Levels

LevelNameBasalMeal BolusCorrection BolusStatus
0SMBG/MDIManualManualManualStandard
1CSII + CGMManualManualManualOpen loop
2PLGS / LGSSuspend on lowManualManualPredictive suspend
3HCL (Hybrid)AutomatedManualManual/semi-autoCurrent standard
4AHCL (Advanced HCL)AutomatedManualAutomated780G, Control-IQ, Omnipod 5
5Full Closed-LoopAutomatedAutomatedAutomatedExperimental
6BihormonalAutomated (insulin + glucagon)AutomatedAutomatedExperimental
7Multi-hormoneInsulin + glucagon + amylin/GLP-1AutomatedAutomatedPreclinical

3. Advanced HCL (Current Commercial) — Auto-Correction

SystemAuto-CorrectionMeal DetectionAlgorithm
Medtronic 780GYes (when glucose > target + rising)Yes (rises >2 mg/dL/min post-meal)PID
Tandem Control-IQYes (predictive >180 mg/dL at 30 min)No (user bolus)MPC
Omnipod 5Yes (SmartAdjust, predicted >target)NoMPC
CamAPS FXYes (adaptive, predicted)Yes (boost feature)MPC

Impact: AHCL adds ~5–8% TIR over HCL; reduces HbA1c additional 0.1–0.2%.

4. Full Closed-Loop — The Meal Challenge

Why not yet commercial?

  1. Insulin pharmacokinetics too slow: Even rapid analogues peak 60–90 min; meal glucose peaks 30–60 min
  2. Carb estimation error: ±20–30% typical → incorrect bolus
  3. No "feedforward" signal: No reliable meal detection (ghrelin, gastric motility sensors experimental)
  4. Safety: Unannounced meal + full auto-bolus = hypoglycaemia risk

Solutions in development:

  • Faster insulins (faster aspart, Lyumjev): Peak 30–40 min, offset 10–15 min earlier
  • Peritoneal insulin delivery: IP route → portal vein → hepatic first pass (faster)
  • Meal detection sensors: Wearable gastric electrography, acoustic, impedance
  • AI carb estimation: Photo-based (food recognition), voice logging

5. Bihormonal Systems (Insulin + Glucagon)

SystemConfigurationStatusKey Challenge
Beta Bionics iLetDual-chamber pump (insulin + glucagon)FDA approved 2023 (insulin-only); bihormonal trialsGlucagon stability (aqueous: 24–48h); catheter occlusion
Oregon Health (Dual-hormone)ResearchProven ↓ TBR furtherGlucagon cost, stability, dual catheter
DIY bihormonalExperimentalVery limitedNot practical

Glucagon formulations in development:

  • Dasiglucagon (Zealand Pharma): Stable aqueous, room temp — Phase 3
  • Glucagon analog (Xeris): Stable liquid — approved for rescue; pump stability studied
  • Lyophilised + dual-chamber cartridge: Reconstitute at use (adds complexity)

6. Faster Insulins — Enabling Full Closed-Loop

InsulinBrandOnsetPeakDurationAdvantage for AID
Faster aspartFiasp5–10 min40–60 min3–5hNiacinamide + arginine → faster absorption
Insulin lispro-aabcLyumjev5–10 min30–45 min3–5hCitrate + treprostinil → vasodilation
Ultra-rapid lisproURLi (experimental)<5 min~30 min2–3hEven faster

Evidence in AID: Faster aspart in HCL → ↓ post-prandial glucose, ↓ meal announcement time, ↑ TIR 3–5%.

7. Alternative Delivery Routes

RouteSystemAdvantage for AID
Subcutaneous (current)All commercialStandard; slow PK
Intraperitoneal (IP)Medtronic IP pump (research)Portal delivery → hepatic first pass; faster glucose lowering; physiological
InhaledAfrezza (Technosphere)Ultra-rapid (peak 12–15 min); meal bolus only; cough risk
Intradermal / MicroneedleExperimentalFaster absorption; less variability

8. Artificial Pancreas Pipeline (2024–2028)

TimelineMilestone
2024–2025AHCL standard (780G, Control-IQ, Omnipod 5, CamAPS)
2025–2026Faster insulin integration (Fiasp/Lyumjev in AID)
2026–2027Bihormonal approval (iLet dual-hormone)
2027–2028Full closed-loop (with faster insulin + meal detection)
2028+Multi-hormone (amylin/pramlintide + GLP-1 co-delivery)

9. Clinical Decision Framework

PatientCurrent Best AID Option
T1DM, wants tubed pump, best automationMedtronic 780G (auto-correction + meal detection)
T1DM, wants predictive algorithm, Dexcom userTandem Control-IQ or Omnipod 5
T1DM, wants tubeless, phone controlOmnipod 5
T1DM, wants flexibility, Android userCamAPS FX + Ypsomed/Dana
T1DM, tech-savvy, DIY acceptableLoop / AndroidAPS
T1DM, recurrent severe hypos despite HCLConsider bihormonal trial or IP insulin

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
HCL vs AHCLHCL = basal only; AHCL = basal + auto-correction bolus
Full closed-loop barrierInsulin too slow (peak 60–90 min vs meal 30–60 min)
Faster insulinsFiasp / Lyumjev — onset 5–10 min, peak 30–45 min
BihormonalInsulin + glucagon; glucagon stability main challenge (24–48h aqueous)
iLet (Beta Bionics)Dual-chamber pump; FDA approved insulin-only 2023; bihormonal in trials
DasiglucagonStable aqueous glucagon (Zealand) — Phase 3 for pump use
IP insulinIntraperitoneal → portal → hepatic first pass; physiological; research
AID levels0=SMBG → 3=HCL → 4=AHCL → 5=Full CL → 6=Bihormonal
CamAPS FX uniqueApp-based, works on multiple pumps, adaptive MPC
DIY systemsLoop, AndroidAPS, OpenAPS — support safety, document off-label

11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Insulin pump therapy (CSII)|Insulin pump therapy (CSII)]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Hybrid closed-loop (670G, 780G, CamAPS, Loop)]], [[Pump types (tethered, patch, closed-loop)]], [[Insulin analogues (rapid, long-acting, ultra-long)]], [[CGM-guided insulin adjustment]]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. AID Automation Levels
3. Advanced HCL (Current Commercial) — Auto-Correction
4. Full Closed-Loop — The Meal Challenge
5. Bihormonal Systems (Insulin + Glucagon)
6. Faster Insulins — Enabling Full Closed-Loop
7. Alternative Delivery Routes
8. Artificial Pancreas Pipeline (2024–2028)
9. Clinical Decision Framework
10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

▸ 21.3.1 Da Qing Study

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, da-qing, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP)", "Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study", "Metformin for prevention"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Da Qing study

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State Da Qing study design and key findings
  • [ ] Compare diet, exercise, and combined interventions
  • [ ] Apply 20-year follow-up outcomes (CVD/mortality benefit)
  • [ ] Contrast with DPP and Finnish DPS

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DesignCluster RCT (33 clinics); 577 IGT participants
LocationDa Qing, China
Duration6 years active intervention; 20-year follow-up
PopulationIGT (WHO 1985), age 25-57

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Intervention Arms

ArmIntervention6-year DM Reduction
DietCalorie restriction, vegetable ↑, alcohol ↓31%
Exercise≥30 min/day moderate46%
Diet + ExerciseCombined42%
ControlUsual careReference

20-Year Follow-up Outcomes

OutcomeIntervention vs Control
Diabetes incidence43% reduction (combined)
CVD events41% reduction (combined)
CVD mortality32% reduction (combined)
All-cause mortality29% reduction (combined)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Intervention Details

ArmComponents
DietCalorie restriction (1200-1500 kcal); vegetable ↑; sugar/alcohol ↓
Exercise≥30 min/day moderate (walking, Tai Chi); leisure time activity
CombinedDiet + exercise

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DesignCluster RCT (China); 577 IGT; 6y intervention, 20y follow-up
ArmsDiet (31%), Exercise (46%), Diet+Exercise (42%)
20y outcomesCombined: DM 43%↓, CVD events 41%↓, CVD death 32%↓, all-cause death 29%↓
Exercise aloneMost effective single intervention (46%)
Legacy effectBenefits persist 14y post-intervention

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What was the Da Qing study design?Cluster RCT, 577 IGT, 6y intervention, 20y follow-up
What were the 3 intervention arms?Diet, Exercise, Diet+Exercise
Which single intervention was most effective?Exercise alone (46% reduction)
What were the 20-year CVD/mortality benefits?CVD events 41%↓, CVD death 32%↓, all-cause death 29%↓

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Diet + Exercise = additive?NO - combined 42% vs exercise 46% (not additive)
Da Qing = DPP?Different populations (China vs US); different interventions
Diet alone effective?31% reduction; less than exercise (46%)

Mnemonic: DA-QING-20YEAR

  • Da Qing: China, IGT, 577 participants
  • Arms: Diet, Exercise, Diet+Exercise
  • Qing: 6-year intervention, 20-year follow-up
  • Intervention Exercise: 46% (best single)
  • Nutrition: 31% reduction
  • Group combined: 42%
  • 20-year: CVD 41%↓, CVD death 32%↓, death 29%↓
  • 0verall: Legacy effect persists
  • Years follow-up: 14 years post
  • Exercise best: 46% > 42% > 31%
  • Additive: NOT (exercise alone > combined)
  • Results: legacy effect proven
  • Year 20: CVD/mortality benefit**

Topic Overview

Da Qing study
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Intervention Arms
20-Year Follow-up Outcomes
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Intervention Details

▸ 21.3.10 Teplizumab Stage 2 Delay

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, t1-prevention, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Type 2 diabetes prevention", "Type 2 diabetes remission", "Immunotherapy trials"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Type 1 diabetes prevention

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain teplizumab mechanism and trial data
  • [ ] Identify eligible candidates (Stage 2 T1DM)
  • [ ] Apply immunotherapy trial landscape
  • [ ] Apply screening strategies

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
TargetStage 2 T1DM (2+ autoantibodies + dysglycaemia)
TeplizumabAnti-CD3 monoclonal antibody; FDA approved 2022
EfficacyDelays Stage 3 by median 2 years (48.4 vs 24.4 months)
EligibilityAge ≥8; Stage 2 T1DM; not pregnant

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - prevention)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

TN-10 Trial (Teplizumab)

ParameterTeplizumabPlacebo
Median time to Stage 348.4 months24.4 months
HR (95% CI)0.48 (0.28-0.82)Reference
Adverse eventsLymphopenia (transient), rash, CRSSimilar

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
AutoantibodiesGAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA (2+ for Stage 2)
OGTT/FPGDysglycaemia (Stage 2)
HLA typingDR3/DR4 (risk stratification)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A - prevention)

7. Management

Teplizumab Protocol

AspectDetail
IndicationStage 2 T1DM, age ≥8
DosingWeight-based IV daily x 14 days
MonitoringLymphocytes, liver function, CRS
ContraindicationsActive infection, recent live vaccine, pregnancy

Screening Programs

ProgramTarget
TrialNetRelatives of T1DM (15x risk)
Fr1da (Germany)General population children
ASK (US)General population children

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
TeplizumabAnti-CD3 mAb; delays Stage 3 by ~2 years
IndicationStage 2 T1DM (2+ Abs + dysglycaemia), age ≥8
Eligibility2+ autoantibodies + dysglycaemia (IFG/IGT)
ScreeningTrialNet (relatives); Fr1da (general population)
TrialTN-10: HR 0.48 (delays diagnosis ~2 years)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is teplizumab?Anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody; FDA approved for Stage 2 T1DM prevention
Who is eligible for teplizumab?Age ≥8, Stage 2 T1DM (2+ autoantibodies + dysglycaemia)
What did TN-10 trial show?Delays Stage 3 by median 2 years (HR 0.48)
What are the adverse effects?Lymphopenia (transient), rash, cytokine release syndrome
What screening programmes exist?TrialNet (relatives), Fr1da (general population Germany), ASK (US children)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Teplizumab = cure?NO - delays onset; not prevention
All Stage 2 get teplizumab?Only if age ≥8 and no contraindications
Teplizumab for Stage 3?NO - for Stage 2 (pre-symptomatic) only

Mnemonic: TEPLIZUMAB-T1DM-PREVENT

  • Teplizumab: anti-CD3 mAb
  • Eligibility: Stage 2 T1DM, age ≥8
  • Prevention: delays Stage 3 by ~2 years
  • Lymphopenia: transient adverse effect
  • Immunotherapy: anti-CD3 mAb
  • Zero cure: delays, not prevents
  • Unique: only approved prevention
  • Mechanism: modulates T-cells (partial agonism)
  • Age: ≥8 years
  • Biomarkers: 2+ Abs + dysglycaemia (IFG/IGT)
  • TrialNet: relatives screening
  • No cure: delays onset
  • One course: 14-day IV
  • Phase 3: TN-10 positive
  • Risk: lymphopenia, rash, CRS
  • Eligibility: Stage 2 (2+ Abs + IFG/IGT)
  • Vaccine: no live vaccines during
  • Efficacy: HR 0.48 (delay ~2y)
  • Not for Stage 3
  • T1DM prevention: only approved
  • Only prevention therapy
  • First approved T1DM prevention
  • Delays: median 2 years (48 vs 24 mo)
  • Autoantibodies: 2+ required
  • Immunomodulation: partial T-cell agonism
  • Cytokine release: adverse effect
  • Vaccine: avoid live vaccines during
  • Endpoints: Stage 3 onset
  • No insulin: teplizumab prevents need temporarily
  • TrialNet: at-risk relatives
  • Indication: Stage 2, age≥8
  • One-time: 14 days IV
  • Never cures: only delays
  • Screening: Fr1da, ASK, TrialNet**

Topic Overview

Type 1 diabetes prevention
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
TN-10 Trial (Teplizumab)
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Teplizumab Protocol
Screening Programs
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
