Type 2 diabetes remission

[!info]
Topic-Group hub for Type 2 diabetes remission — covers 4 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Diabetes Prevention and Remission/Type 2 diabetes remission/DiRECT trial.md|DiRECT trial]]
  • [[Low-calorie diet]]
  • [[Remission criteria]]
  • [[Maintenance]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
DiRECT TrialLow-calorie diet (825-853 kcal/d) 12-20wk → food reintroduction → maintenance · 46% remission at 1y, 36% at 2y, 23% at 5y
Remission CriteriaHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%) off ALL glucose-lowering medications for ≥3 months (ADA/Diabetes UK consensus 2021)
Low-Calorie DietFormula meal replacements (soups/shakes) · ~15kg mean weight loss → remission · Structured support essential
PredictorsShorter duration (<6y), lower baseline HbA1c, greater weight loss, C-peptide preserved
MaintenanceContinued weight maintenance, regular physical activity, monthly support, self-monitoring · Relapse if weight regain

  • Parent Heading: [[../Diabetes Prevention and Remission|Diabetes Prevention and Remission]]

▸ 21.3.2 Diabetes Prevention Program Dpp

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, t2dm-prevention, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Da Qing study", "Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study", "Metformin for prevention"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State DPP design, population, and key findings
  • [ ] Compare lifestyle vs metformin vs placebo
  • [ ] Apply DPP risk score for prevention eligibility
  • [ ] Apply DPP findings to clinical practice

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DesignRCT; 3234 participants; 2.8 years average follow-up
PopulationIGT (FPG 5.3-6.9, 2h 7.8-11.0), BMI ≥24, age ≥25
ArmsLifestyle vs Metformin 850mg BD vs Placebo

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DesignRCT; 3234 participants; 2.8 years average follow-up
PopulationIGT (FPG 5.3-6.9, 2h 7.8-11.0), BMI ≥24, age ≥25
ArmsLifestyle vs Metformin 850mg BD vs Placebo

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

DPP Results

ArmDiabetes Incidence ReductionNNT (3 years)
Lifestyle58%7
Metformin31%14
PlaceboReference-

Key Subgroup Findings

SubgroupLifestyle ReductionMetformin Reduction
Age ≥6071%15% (NS)
BMI ≥3553%53%
BMI <3562%15% (NS)
FPG 6.1-6.958%31%

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Lifestyle Intervention (DPP Protocol)

ComponentTarget
Weight loss≥7% body weight (16-session curriculum)
Physical activity≥150 min/week moderate intensity
DietLow-fat (<25% kcal), calorie restriction
BehaviouralSelf-monitoring, stimulus control, problem-solving

Metformin Protocol

AspectDetail
Dose850mg BD
Adherence72% at study end
GI side effects25% (vs 15% placebo)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Lifestyle58% reduction; 7% weight loss; 150min/week exercise
Metformin31% reduction; less effective than lifestyle
Age ≥60Lifestyle 71% (best), Metformin minimal
BMI ≥35Metformin effective (53%)
NNTLifestyle 7, Metformin 14 (3 years)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What was the diabetes incidence reduction in DPP lifestyle arm?58%
What was the metformin reduction?31%
What was the lifestyle intervention?≥7% weight loss, ≥150min/week exercise, low-fat diet
In which subgroup was metformin most effective?BMI ≥35 (53% reduction)
What was the NNT for lifestyle intervention?7 (over 3 years)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Metformin = lifestyle?NO - lifestyle 58%, metformin 31%
Metformin in elderly?Less effective in age ≥60
DPP = all prediabetes?IGT only (not IFG alone)

Mnemonic: DPP-LIFESTYLE-METFORMIN

  • DPP: Diabetes Prevention Program
  • Participants: 3234, IGT, BMI≥24, age≥25
  • Placebo vs Metformin vs Lifestyle
  • Lifestyle: 58% reduction (NNT 7)
  • Intervention: ≥7% weight loss, 150min/week exercise
  • Fat: <25% kcal
  • Effective: 58% > 31%
  • Subgroup: age≥60 lifestyle 71%
  • Those BMI≥35: metformin 53%
  • Years: 2.8 average follow-up
  • Metformin: 31% reduction (NNT 14)
  • Elderly: lifestyle > metformin in >60
  • Target: ≥7% weight loss
  • First line: lifestyle
  • Outcomes: CVD/mortality benefit (follow-up)
  • Reduction: 58% lifestyle, 31% metformin
  • Metformin: less than lifestyle
  • IGT only (not IFG alone)
  • NNT: lifestyle 7, metformin 14

Topic Overview

Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
DPP Results
Key Subgroup Findings
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Lifestyle Intervention (DPP Protocol)
Metformin Protocol
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.3.2 Hybrid Closed Loop 670G 780G Camaps Loop
[!info]
HCL = Automated basal insulin + user meal boluses — "Hybrid" because meals still need manual bolus; algorithms: PID (Medtronic) vs MPC (Tandem/Omnipod/CamAPS); all ↑ TIR 10–15%, ↓ TBR 50%.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Compare commercial HCL systems: algorithms, CGM, pump, targets
  • [ ] Explain PID vs MPC control theory
  • [ ] State key trial evidence (FLAIR, PIVOTAL, etc.)
  • [ ] Apply NICE/ADA criteria for HCL access
  • [ ] Troubleshoot common HCL issues

2. Commercial HCL Systems (UK/Global)

SystemPumpCGMAlgorithmTarget GlucoseKey Features
Medtronic 670G670GGuardian 3PID120 mg/dL (6.7) fixedFirst FDA HCL; auto-mode only; no Bluetooth
Medtronic 780G780GGuardian 3/4PID (SmartGuard)100, 110, 120 mg/dL adjustableAuto-correction bolus; meal detection; Bluetooth; remote monitoring
Tandem t:slim X2 + Control-IQt:slim X2Dexcom G6/G7MPC110–160 mg/dL (sleep 110)Predictive 30 min; sleep/exercise modes; auto-correction; remote
Omnipod 5Omnipod 5 (patch)Dexcom G6/G7MPC (SmartAdjust)110–150 mg/dL adjustableTubeless; phone controller; no separate PDM
CamAPS FX (app)Ypsomed / DANA-i / Dana RSDexcom G6/G7 / Libre 3MPC100–150 mg/dL adjustableApp-based; runs on Android; open to multiple pumps
Loop (DIY)Medtronic 600/700 series, Omnipod Eros/DASHDexcom G6 / LibreMPC (open-source)User-definedDIY; not regulated; Nightscout integration

3. Algorithm Comparison: PID vs MPC

AspectPID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)MPC (Model Predictive Control)
Used byMedtronic 670G/780GTandem Control-IQ, Omnipod 5, CamAPS FX, Loop
PrincipleReacts to current error (P), past error (I), rate of change (D)Predicts future glucose (30–60 min) using physiological model; optimises delivery
Meal handling780G: meal detection → auto-correctionPre-meal bolus still needed; predicts post-prandial rise
Hypoglycaemia preventionDerivative term → suspends on rapid fallPredictive suspend 30 min before predicted low
TuningGain parameters (Kp, Ki, Kd)Model parameters (insulin sensitivity, carb ratio, time constants)
AdaptabilityLimited (fixed gains)Adaptive (learns individual parameters over days)

4. Key Clinical Trials Evidence

TrialSystemPopulationTIR ChangeHbA1c ChangeTBR Change
FLAIR (2021)780GT1DM adults+14% (63→77%)−0.4%−50%
PIVOTAL (2023)780GT1DM adolescents+11%−0.3%−40%
Control-IQ RCT (2019)TandemT1DM 6–70y+11% (61→71%)−0.33%−52%
Omnipod 5 Pivotal (2021)Omnipod 5T1DM 6–70y+13%−0.4%−45%
CamAPS FX RCT (2021)CamAPST1DM 1–65y+10–15%−0.3 to −0.5%−50%
KidsAP (2022)CamAPSChildren 1–7y+18%−0.5%−60%

Consistent finding: All HCL systems achieve TIR >70% in majority; TBR <4%; HbA1c reduction 0.3–0.5%.

5. NICE Guidance (TA829, TA830, TA831, TA944 — 2023/2024)

SystemNICE TAIndication
Medtronic 780GTA829T1DM adults/children ≥7y; HbA1c ≥58 OR disabling hypos
Tandem Control-IQTA830T1DM adults/children ≥6y; HbA1c ≥58 OR disabling hypos
Omnipod 5TA831T1DM adults/children ≥2y; HbA1c ≥58 OR disabling hypos
CamAPS FX + Ypsomed/DanaTA944T1DM adults/children ≥1y; HbA1c ≥58 OR disabling hypos

Common criteria: T1DM; structured education; carb counting; CGM use; HbA1c ≥58 mmol/mol (7.5%) OR recurrent severe/unpredictable hypos.

6. Practical HCL Management

Settings to Optimise

ParameterRoleAdjustment
Target glucoseAlgorithm aims for thisLower target → more insulin, ↓ TAR, ↑ TBR risk
ICR / Carb ratioMeal bolus calculationTest with standard meals; adjust until 2-hr in range
ISF / Correction factorAuto-correction aggressivenessUsually algorithm-managed; verify
Max basal rateSafety ceilingSet 2–3× usual max
Active insulin timeIOB duration3–5h (matches insulin action)

Common Issues

IssueCauseFix
Persistent highs post-mealUnderestimated carbs, late bolus, ICR too weakAccurate carb count; pre-bolus 15–20 min; tighten ICR
Nocturnal hypoglycaemiaTarget too low, basal max too high, over-correctionRaise target; check basal max; reduce ISF
"Algorithm fighting"CGM noise, calibration issues, sensor compressionEnsure good CGM data; avoid compression sites
Exercise hypoglycaemiaAlgorithm doesn't know exercise plannedExercise mode (Tandem, Omnipod 5, CamAPS); temp target ↑
Dawn phenomenonAlgorithm may not anticipate780G: meal detection helps; others: check basal profile

7. DIY Systems (Loop, AndroidAPS, OpenAPS)

SystemStatusKey Points
LoopDIY (iPhone)MPC algorithm; RileyLink hardware; Nightscout; large community
AndroidAPSDIY (Android)OpenAPS port; works with Dana RS, Accu-Chek Insight, Ypsomed
OpenAPSDIY (Raspberry Pi/Intel Edison)Original DIY; "WeAreNotWaiting"

Clinician role: Support safety (settings review, IOB, max basal); document "off-label use"; don't discourage if patient competent.

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
HCL = Hybrid Closed-LoopAutomates basal only; user gives meal bolus
PID vs MPCPID = reactive (Medtronic); MPC = predictive (Tandem, Omnipod, CamAPS)
780G key featureAuto-correction bolus (unique); adjustable target (100/110/120)
Control-IQ key featurePredictive 30 min; sleep mode target 110; exercise mode
Omnipod 5Tubeless HCL; phone controller; SmartAdjust MPC
CamAPS FXApp-based HCL; works on multiple pumps (Ypsomed, Dana)
NICE criteria HCLT1DM; HbA1c ≥58 OR disabling hypos; education + carb counting
EvidenceAll systems: TIR +10–15%, TBR −50%, HbA1c −0.3–0.5%
Exercise modeRaises target (e.g., 140–160) → reduces insulin delivery
DIY systemsNot regulated; support safety, don't discourage competent users

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Insulin pump therapy (CSII)|Insulin pump therapy (CSII)]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Pump types (tethered, patch, closed-loop)]], [[Automated insulin delivery (AID) systems]], [[CGM-guided insulin adjustment]], [[Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)]]

2. Commercial HCL Systems (UK/Global)
3. Algorithm Comparison: PID vs MPC
4. Key Clinical Trials Evidence

Settings to Optimise
Common Issues

▸ 21.3.3 Direct Trial

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, direct-trial, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Low-calorie diet and weight loss", "Remission criteria (ADA, Diabetes UK)", "Maintenance of remission"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

DiRECT trial

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State DiRECT trial design, intervention, and key outcomes
  • [ ] Apply remission criteria and predictors
  • [ ] Contrast with other weight loss approaches
  • [ ] Apply clinical implications

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DesignCluster RCT; 306 participants; 24 general practices (Scotland/Tyneside)
PopulationT2DM <6 years, age 20-65, BMI 27-45, not on insulin
InterventionLow-calorie diet (825-853 kcal/day) 12-20 weeks -> food reintroduction -> maintenance
ControlBest practice usual care (NICE guidelines)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DesignCluster RCT; 306 participants; 24 general practices (Scotland/Tyneside)
PopulationT2DM <6 years, age 20-65, BMI 27-45, not on insulin
InterventionLow-calorie diet (825-853 kcal/day) 12-20 weeks -> food reintroduction -> maintenance
ControlBest practice usual care (NICE guidelines)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

DiRECT Outcomes

TimepointIntervention RemissionControl RemissionOdds Ratio
12 months46%4%19.7
24 months36%3%—
5 years23%——

Remission Definition

CriterionThreshold
HbA1c<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
MedicationsOff ALL glucose-lowering meds ≥3 months
SustainedAt 12 and 24 months

Weight Loss & Remission

Weight LossRemission Rate
0-5kg7%
5-10kg34%
10-15kg57%
≥15kg86%

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

DiRECT Intervention Phases

PhaseDurationDetails
1. Total Diet Replacement12-20 weeks825-853 kcal/day (soups/shakes); all meals replaced
2. Food Reintroduction2-8 weeksStepwise reintroduction; dietitian support
3. MaintenanceLong-termMonthly visits; weight monitoring; relapse management

Eligibility Criteria (DiRECT)

CriterionThreshold
T2DM duration<6 years
Age20-65 years
BMI27-45 kg/m2
InsulinNot on insulin
HbA1cNo upper limit (but <108 if on insulin)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Remission rate46% at 12mo; 36% at 24mo; 23% at 5y
DefinitionHbA1c <48mmol/mol off meds ≥3 months
Weight loss≥15kg -> 86% remission; ≥10kg -> 57%
DurationT2DM <6 years; BMI 27-45; not on insulin
InterventionLCD 825-853 kcal 12-20wk -> reintroduction -> maintenance

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the DiRECT trial remission rate at 12 months?46%
What is the remission definition?HbA1c <48mmol/mol off ALL glucose-lowering meds ≥3 months
What weight loss predicts remission?≥15kg -> 86%; ≥10kg -> 57%
What was the intervention?LCD 825-853 kcal/day x 12-20wk -> food reintroduction -> maintenance
Eligibility for DiRECT?T2DM <6y; age 20-65; BMI 27-45; not on insulin

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Remission = cure?NO - HbA1c <48 off meds ≥3mo; relapse possible
DiRECT = all T2DM?No - <6y duration, BMI 27-45, no insulin
LCD = starvation?825-853 kcal (formula products); supervised

Mnemonic: DIRECT-TRIAL-REMISSION

  • DiRECT: 46% remission at 12mo
  • Intervention: LCD 825-853 kcal
  • Remission: HbA1c<48 off meds ≥3mo
  • Eligibility: T2DM<6y, BMI 27-45, no insulin
  • Cluster RCT: 306 pts, 24 practices
  • Trial: 46% (12mo), 36% (24mo), 23% (5y)
  • Remission weight: ≥15kg=86%, ≥10kg=57%
  • Evidence: cluster RCT
  • Trial: 12-20wk LCD -> reintroduction -> maintenance
  • Relapse: common (weight regain)
  • Insulin: not allowed at baseline
  • Adherence: key predictor
  • Lifestyle: maintenance phase crucial
  • Weight loss: ≥15kg -> 86% remission**

▸ 21.3.4 Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, finnish-dps, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP)", "Da Qing study", "Metformin for prevention"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State Finnish DPS design and key findings
  • [ ] Compare lifestyle intervention components
  • [ ] Apply long-term outcomes (13-year follow-up)
  • [ ] Contrast with DPP and Da Qing study

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DesignRCT; 522 IGT participants
LocationFinland
DurationMean 4 years intervention; median 13-year follow-up
PopulationIGT (WHO 1999), BMI >25, age 40-65

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DesignRCT; 522 IGT participants
LocationFinland
DurationMean 4 years intervention; median 13-year follow-up
PopulationIGT (WHO 1999), BMI >25, age 40-65

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Intervention Details

ComponentRecommendation
Weight loss≥5% body weight
DietTotal fat <30% kcal; saturated fat <10%; fibre ≥15g/1000kcal
Exercise≥30 min/day moderate intensity
CounsellingIndividualised, 7 sessions year 1, quarterly thereafter

Key Results

OutcomeInterventionControlReduction
Cumulative DM incidence (4y)11%23%58%
13-year follow-up23%38%43%
Weight loss (1y)4.2kg0.9kg—

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Intervention Protocol

SessionFrequencyContent
Year 17 sessionsDiet, exercise, behaviour change
Years 2-4QuarterlyMaintenance, problem-solving
Post-interventionAnnualFollow-up

Goals (All Must Be Met for Max Benefit)

GoalTarget
Weight≥5% loss
Fat<30% total energy; saturated <10%
Fibre≥15g/1000kcal
Exercise≥30 min/day moderate

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Reduction58% at 4y; 43% at 13y
GoalsWeight ≥5%; fat <30%; fibre ≥15g/1000kcal; exercise ≥30min/day
ComparisonSimilar to DPP (58%); better long-term follow-up (13y)
Key difference vs DPPFinnish DPS: individual counselling; DPP: group + individual

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What was the diabetes reduction in Finnish DPS?58% at 4 years; 43% at 13 years
What were the 5 key goals?Weight ≥5%, fat <30%, saturated fat <10%, fibre ≥15g/1000kcal, exercise ≥30min/day
How does Finnish DPS compare to DPP?Similar 58% reduction; 13y follow-up shows sustained benefit
What was the weight loss at 1 year?4.2kg vs 0.9kg control

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Finnish DPS = DPP?Similar design/results; Finnish: individual counselling, 13y follow-up
Finnish DPS = Da Qing?Different populations/interventions
Finnish DPS lifestyle = DPP lifestyle?Similar intensive lifestyle; Finnish more individualised

Mnemonic: FINNISH-DPS-13YEAR

  • Finnish: 522 IGT, BMI>25, age 40-65
  • Intervention: individual counselling (7 sessions yr1)
  • Nutrition: fat<30%, sat<10%, fibre≥15g/1000kcal
  • Nordic: walking/exercise ≥30min/day
  • Intensive: 7 sessions yr1, quarterly yr2-4
  • Study: 58% reduction at 4y
  • H13-year: 43% reduction sustained
  • Diabetes: cumulative incidence 23% vs 38%
  • Prevention: 5 goals (weight, fat, fibre, exercise)
  • 1 year weight loss: 4.2kg vs 0.9kg
  • 3 year sustained: 3.5kg vs 0.8kg
  • Years follow-up: 13 (median)
  • Sustained: legacy effect proven**

▸ 21.3.5 Immunotherapy Trials

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, immunotherapy-trials, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Teplizumab (stage 2 delay)", "Type 2 diabetes prevention", "Type 2 diabetes remission"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Immunotherapy trials

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify key immunotherapy trials for T1DM prevention
  • [ ] Differentiate mechanism of action (teplizumab vs others)
  • [ ] Apply trial results to clinical context
  • [ ] Recognise current limitations

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GoalDelay or prevent Stage 3 T1DM in at-risk individuals
TargetStage 1-2 (2+ autoantibodies) or new-onset Stage 3
StatusMostly Phase 2/3 trials; teplizumab only approved

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GoalDelay or prevent Stage 3 T1DM in at-risk individuals
TargetStage 1-2 (2+ autoantibodies) or new-onset Stage 3
StatusMostly Phase 2/3 trials; teplizumab only approved

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Key Immunotherapy Trials

TrialAgentPopulationOutcome
TN-10 (Teplizumab)Anti-CD3Stage 2 (2+Ab + dysglycaemia)Delayed Stage 3 by ~2 years (HR 0.48)
AbataceptCTLA4-IgStage 1-2Modest C-peptide preservation
RituximabAnti-CD20New-onset (Stage 3)Preserved C-peptide at 1 year
VerapamilCalcium channel blockerNew-onsetPreserved beta-cell function
IL-2 (low dose)IL-2Stage 1-2↑Tregs, preserved C-peptide
ATG/G-CSFATG + G-CSFNew-onsetPreserved C-peptide at 2 years
AlefaceptAnti-CD2New-onsetPreserved C-peptide

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Key Immunotherapy Trials

TrialAgentPopulationOutcome
TN-10 (Teplizumab)Anti-CD3Stage 2 (2+Ab + dysglycaemia)Delayed Stage 3 by ~2 years (HR 0.48)
AbataceptCTLA4-IgStage 1-2Modest C-peptide preservation
RituximabAnti-CD20New-onset (Stage 3)Preserved C-peptide at 1 year
VerapamilCalcium channel blockerNew-onsetPreserved beta-cell function
IL-2 (low dose)IL-2Stage 1-2↑Tregs, preserved C-peptide
ATG/G-CSFATG + G-CSFNew-onsetPreserved C-peptide at 2 years
AlefaceptAnti-CD2New-onsetPreserved C-peptide

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Trial Eligibility (General)

CriterionDetail
Autoantibodies2+ (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)
Stage1 (normoglycaemia), 2 (dysglycaemia), or new-onset
AgeUsually 8-45 years
ExclusionActive infection, immunosuppression, pregnancy

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
TeplizumabOnly approved; delays Stage 3 by ~2 years
Abatacept/RituximabModest C-peptide preservation
Verapamil/IL-2/ATGExperimental; preserve beta-cell function
Stage 1/2Prevention target (teplizumab)
Stage 3 (new-onset)Preservation target (rituximab, abatacept, verapamil)
Current statusOnly teplizumab approved; rest trial phases

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What immunotherapies have shown benefit in T1DM?Teplizumab (approved), abatacept, rituximab, verapamil, IL-2, ATG/G-CSF
What is the difference between prevention and preservation trials?Prevention = Stage 1-2 (pre-symptomatic); Preservation = Stage 3 (new-onset)
What was the outcome of rituximab trial?Preserved C-peptide at 1 year in new-onset T1DM
What is the mechanism of teplizumab?Anti-CD3 mAb -> partial T-cell agonism -> modulates immune response
Are any immunotherapies approved for T1DM prevention?Teplizumab only (FDA 2022, Stage 2, age ≥8)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Immunotherapy = cure?NO - delay/preservation only; no cure yet
All trials = success?NO - many failed (oral insulin, nasal insulin, GAD-alum)
Teplizumab = only approved?YES - as of 2024, only FDA-approved prevention therapy

Mnemonic: IMMUNO-T1DM-TRIALS

  • Immunotherapy: T1DM prevention/preservation
  • Mechanism: modulate immune system (T-cells, B-cells, cytokines)
  • Multiple agents: teplizumab, abatacept, rituximab, verapamil, IL-2, ATG
  • Unique: teplizumab = only approved (FDA 2022)
  • New-onset: preservation trials (rituximab, abatacept, verapamil)
  • Only teplizumab approved for prevention (Stage 2)
  • Teplizumab: anti-CD3, delays Stage 3 ~2 years
  • Rituximab: anti-CD20, new-onset preservation
  • Interleukin-2: low dose -> Tregs
  • Abatacept: CTLA4-Ig, blocks T-cell activation
  • Verapamil: calcium channel blocker, new-onset
  • ATG/G-CSF: new-onset preservation
  • Limits: delay/preserve, not cure
  • Screening: relatives (TrialNet), general (Fr1da, ASK)
  • TrialNet: relatives of T1DM
  • Results: modest; delay 1-2 years
  • Immunotherapy: not for everyone
  • Adverse: lymphopenia, infections, CRS
  • Long-term: unknown durability
  • Stage 1/2: prevention; Stage 3: preservation**

▸ 21.3.6 Low Calorie Diet And Weight Loss

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, low-calorie-diet, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["DiRECT trial", "Remission criteria (ADA, Diabetes UK)", "Maintenance of remission"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Low-calorie diet and weight loss

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply very low-calorie diet (VLCD) and low-calorie diet (LCD) protocols
  • [ ] Identify suitable candidates for weight loss intervention
  • [ ] Manage weight loss phase, reintroduction, and maintenance
  • [ ] Monitor for complications and nutritional adequacy

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
VLCD<800 kcal/day; formula products; medical supervision required
LCD800-1200 kcal/day; formula or food-based
Weight loss1.5-2.5kg/week (VLCD); 0.5-1kg/week (LCD)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
VLCD<800 kcal/day; formula products; medical supervision required
LCD800-1200 kcal/day; formula or food-based
Weight loss1.5-2.5kg/week (VLCD); 0.5-1kg/week (LCD)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Diet Types

TypeCaloriesDurationSupervision
VLCD<800 kcal/day8-12 weeks (max 12-16)Medical required
LCD800-1200 kcal/day12-20 weeksDietitian/medical
Commercial800-1000 kcalVariableVariable

Formula Products

TypeExamplesNutrient Profile
ShakesOptifast, Cambridge, LighterLifeHigh protein, low carb, adequate fat, micronutrients
SoupsOptifast, LighterLifeSimilar to shakes
BarsVariousPortion-controlled

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestBaselineDuringPost
Weight/BMI✓WeeklyMonthly
Blood ketones✓Weekly (first 4w)As needed
Electrolytes✓2-4 weeklyMonthly
Uric acid✓MonthlyAs needed
LFT/Renal✓Monthly3-monthly
ECGIf risk factors——

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Protocol Phases

PhaseDurationCaloriesKey Actions
1. VLCD/LCD8-20 weeks800-850 kcalFormula products; 2-3L water; multivitamin
2. Reintroduction2-8 weeksStepwise ↑ (200kcal/week)Real food; dietitian-led
3. MaintenanceLifelongIndividualisedMonthly review; weight monitoring

Contraindications

ConditionReason
Pregnancy/lactationNutrient inadequacy
Severe psychiatricAnorexia, bulimia, depression
Recent MI/strokeMetabolic stress
PorphyriaTrigger risk
Uncontrolled goutUric acid elevation

Complication Management

ComplicationManagement
CholelithiasisUrsodeoxycholic acid 500mg BD (prophylaxis)
GoutAllopurinol if flare; hydration
HypotensionFluid/electrolyte replacement; reduce diuretics
Hair lossReassurance; adequate protein
Cold intoleranceReassurance; adequate clothing

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
VLCD<800 kcal; 8-12 weeks; medical supervision; formula products
Weight loss1.5-2.5kg/week; 15kg average in DiRECT
Remission predictor≥15kg loss -> 86% remission
CholelithiasisProphylaxis: ursodeoxycholic acid 500mg BD
ReintroductionStepwise (200kcal/week); dietitian-led
MaintenanceMonthly review; weight monitoring; relapse plan

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the difference between VLCD and LCD?VLCD <800 kcal (medical supervision); LCD 800-1200 kcal (dietitian supervision)
What is the weight loss on VLCD?1.5-2.5kg/week; ~15kg in 12 weeks
What is the remission rate with ≥15kg weight loss?86% (DiRECT)
What is the cholelithiasis risk on VLCD?High; prophylaxis with ursodeoxycholic acid 500mg BD
What is the reintroduction protocol?Stepwise increase 200kcal/week; dietitian-led; real food
When is VLCD contraindicated?Pregnancy, eating disorders, recent MI/stroke, porphyria

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
VLCD = starvation?NO - formulated to provide adequate protein/micronutrients
VLCD for everyone?NO - contraindications (pregnancy, eating disorders, recent MI)
LCD = VLCD?NO - LCD 800-1200 kcal; VLCD <800 kcal

Mnemonic: LCD-VLCD-WEIGHT-LOSS

  • LCD: 800-1200 kcal; VLCD: <800 kcal
  • Calories: VLCD <800; LCD 800-1200
  • Duration: VLCD 8-12wk; LCD 12-20wk
  • VLCD: formula products (shakes/soups)
  • Losing weight: 1.5-2.5kg/wk (VLCD)
  • Cholesterol: gallstones risk (ursodeoxycholic acid)
  • Adequate: protein, vitamins, minerals in formula
  • Liver: steatosis improves
  • Obesity: BMI 27-45 for VLCD
  • Reintroduction: stepwise 200kcal/week
  • Insulin sensitivity: improves
  • Efficacy: 15kg loss -> 86% remission
  • Weight loss: 15kg avg (DiRECT)
  • Eating disorder: contraindicated
  • Insulin: reduces/stop
  • Gout: uric acid up (allopurinol)
  • Hypotension: fluids, reduce diuretics
  • Type 2: remission possible
  • Long-term: maintenance crucial
  • Obesity: BMI 27-45
  • Supervision: medical (VLCD); dietitian (LCD)
  • Safety: monitoring (electrolytes, ketones, LFT)
  • Serious: cholelithiasis risk**

Topic Overview

Low-calorie diet and weight loss

2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Formula Products

Contraindications

▸ 21.3.7 Maintenance Of Remission

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, remission-maintenance, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["DiRECT trial", "Low-calorie diet and weight loss", "Remission criteria (ADA, Diabetes UK)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Maintenance of remission

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply maintenance strategies after achieving remission
  • [ ] Identify predictors of sustained remission vs relapse
  • [ ] Apply monitoring and early intervention for relapse
  • [ ] Counsel on long-term lifestyle sustainability

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Relapse rate~30-40% at 2 years; 60-70% at 5 years (DiRECT)
Key predictorWeight regain; <2kg regain = sustained remission
Relapse triggerWeight regain >2kg; medication reintroduction

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Relapse rate~30-40% at 2 years; 60-70% at 5 years (DiRECT)
Key predictorWeight regain; <2kg regain = sustained remission
Relapse triggerWeight regain >2kg; medication reintroduction

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Predictors of Sustained Remission

FactorAssociation
Weight maintenance<2kg regain from nadir
T2DM duration<3 years at remission
Baseline HbA1c<58 mmol/mol at remission
C-peptidePreserved (>600 pmol/L)
Age<50 years
Weight loss≥15kg at remission

Relapse Risk Factors

FactorRisk
Weight regain >2kg3x higher relapse
T2DM duration >6y2x higher relapse
Age >60Higher relapse
Baseline HbA1c >75Higher relapse

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
WeightMonthly (first year); quarterly thereafter
HbA1c3-6 monthly
Waist circumference6-monthly
CGM (TIR)If available; quarterly

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFrequency
WeightMonthly (first year); quarterly thereafter
HbA1c3-6 monthly
Waist circumference6-monthly
CGM (TIR)If available; quarterly

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Maintenance Protocol

ComponentStrategy
Weight monitoringWeekly self-weigh; monthly clinic visit year 1
DietMediterranean/low GI; 1500-1800 kcal; high protein
Exercise150-300 min/week moderate + resistance 2x/week
BehaviouralSelf-monitoring; problem-solving; relapse plan
MedicationNone (if remission); metformin if pre-diabetes

Relapse Early Intervention

TriggerAction
Weight regain >2kgIntensive lifestyle review; dietitian referral
HbA1c 42-47Intensify lifestyle; consider metformin if persistent
HbA1c ≥48 on 2 occasionsRestart glucose-lowering medication

Behavioural Strategies

StrategyEvidence
Self-monitoringDaily weight, food diary, activity tracker
Problem-solvingIdentify triggers (stress, social, emotional eating)
Relapse prevention planWritten plan; 3 "red flags" with actions
Social supportPeer groups, family involvement, digital apps

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Relapse rate30-40% at 2y; 60-70% at 5y (DiRECT)
Key predictorWeight maintenance (<2kg regain)
Relapse triggerWeight regain >2kg; HbA1c 42-47 -> intensify
Predictors of success<3y T2DM; <50y; ≥15kg loss; preserved C-peptide; HbA1c<58
MonitoringMonthly weight (yr1); HbA1c 3-6mo; relapse plan

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the relapse rate after T2DM remission?30-40% at 2 years; 60-70% at 5 years (DiRECT)
What is the key predictor of sustained remission?Weight maintenance (<2kg regain from nadir)
What weight regain triggers relapse?>2kg from nadir
What are predictors of sustained remission?T2DM <3y, age <50, ≥15kg loss, preserved C-peptide, HbA1c <58
What is the relapse intervention?Weight regain >2kg -> intensive lifestyle review; HbA1c 42-47 -> intensify

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Remission = permanent cure?NO - relapse common; ongoing monitoring required
Relapse = failure?NO - expected; early intervention restores remission
Medication during remission?None (by definition); if restart needed -> memo
Monitoring enough?Monthly weight + 3-6mo HbA1c minimum

Mnemonic: MAINTAIN-REMISSION

  • Maintain: weight <2kg regain
  • Annual: HbA1c 3-6mo; weight monthly
  • Individualised: diet, exercise, behaviour
  • Nutrition: Mediterranean/low GI; 1500-1800 kcal
  • Tracking: daily weight; food diary; activity
  • Avoid: weight regain >2kg = relapse trigger
  • Intensive: review if >2kg regain
  • No meds: true remission = off all meds
  • RELAX: relapse common (30-40% at 2y)
  • Lifestyle: 150-300min/week exercise
  • App: self-monitoring (weight, food, activity)
  • Plan: written relapse prevention (3 red flags)
  • Social: family/peer support
  • Early: intervene at HbA1c 42-47
  • Recover: restart meds if HbA1c≥48 x2
  • Visits: monthly yr1; quarterly thereafter
  • Sustain: 150-300min/week activity
  • Insulin: if relapse -> restart
  • Ongoing: lifelong monitoring
  • No cure: remission not cure**

Topic Overview

Maintenance of remission
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Predictors of Sustained Remission
Relapse Risk Factors
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Maintenance Protocol
Relapse Early Intervention
Behavioural Strategies

▸ 21.3.8 Metformin For Prevention

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, metformin-prevention, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP)", "Da Qing study", "Finnish Diabetes Prevention Study"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Metformin for prevention

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply DPP metformin prevention data
  • [ ] Identify populations where metformin is most effective
  • [ ] Apply ADA/USPSTF metformin prevention criteria
  • [ ] Contrast metformin vs lifestyle for prevention

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
EvidenceDPP metformin arm (31% reduction); ADA/USPSTF endorsement
IndicationPrediabetes (IGT/IFG/HbA1c 39-47) with high risk features
Efficacy31% reduction (DPP); less than lifestyle (58%)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
EvidenceDPP metformin arm (31% reduction); ADA/USPSTF endorsement
IndicationPrediabetes (IGT/IFG/HbA1c 39-47) with high risk features
Efficacy31% reduction (DPP); less than lifestyle (58%)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

ADA/USPSTF Criteria for Metformin Prevention

CriterionThreshold
BMI≥35 kg/m2 (or ≥23 Asian)
Age<60 years
PrediabetesIFG, IGT, or HbA1c 39-47 mmol/mol (5.7-6.4%)
HistoryGestational diabetes
Rising HbA1cDespite lifestyle intervention

Efficacy by Subgroup (DPP)

SubgroupMetformin Reduction
BMI ≥3553%
Age <6044%
Age ≥60Minimal (NS)
GDM historyEffective
Overall31%

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Prescribing Protocol

StepAction
1Confirm prediabetes (IFG/IGT/HbA1c 39-47)
2Assess criteria (BMI≥35, age<60, GDM history, rising HbA1c)
3Start metformin 500mg OD with food
4Titrate to 1000mg BD over 4 weeks (max 2000-2500mg)
5Monitor: HbA1c 6-monthly; eGFR annually; B12 annually

Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
HbA1c6-monthly
eGFRAnnually (contraindicated if <30)
B12Annually (deficiency risk)
WeightQuarterly

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Efficacy31% (DPP); lifestyle 58% (superior)
IndicationsBMI≥35, age<60, GDM history, rising HbA1c despite lifestyle
BMI≥3553% reduction (most effective subgroup)
Age≥60Minimal benefit
ContraindicatedeGFR<30; caution 30-44

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is metformin's role in diabetes prevention?31% reduction (DPP); 2nd line after lifestyle; ADA/USPSTF endorsed
In which subgroup is metformin most effective?BMI ≥35 (53% reduction)
What are the ADA criteria for metformin prevention?BMI≥35, age<60, GDM history, rising HbA1c despite lifestyle
How does metformin prevention compare to lifestyle?Lifestyle 58% > Metformin 31%; but metformin better than placebo
Is metformin effective in age ≥60?Minimal benefit in age ≥60

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Metformin = lifestyle?NO - lifestyle 58%, metformin 31%
Metformin for all prediabetes?NO - only high-risk (BMI≥35, age<60, GDM history)
Metformin replaces lifestyle?NO - lifestyle 1st line; metformin adjunct

Mnemonic: METFORMIN-PREVENT

  • Metformin: 31% reduction (DPP)
  • Efficacy: lifestyle 58% > metformin 31%
  • Target: BMI≥35, age<60, GDM history
  • Formula: 850mg BD (max 2000-2500mg)
  • Only high-risk: BMI≥35, age<60, GDM history
  • Reduction: 31% (vs 58% lifestyle)
  • Most effective: BMI≥35 (53%)
  • Ineffective: age≥60 (minimal)
  • No for: eGFR<30
  • Prevention: ADA/USPSTF endorsed
  • Renal: eGFR 30-44 half dose; <30 stop
  • Evidence: DPP primary
  • Versus lifestyle: 2nd line
  • Exclusion: eGFR<30
  • No cure: delays only
  • Target: HbA1c 39-47 (prediabetes)
  • Indication: prediabetes + high-risk features
  • Only if: BMI≥35/age<60/GDM/rising HbA1c
  • Not for everyone: high-risk only**

▸ 21.3.9 Remission Criteria Ada Diabetes Uk

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, remission-criteria, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["DiRECT trial", "Low-calorie diet and weight loss", "Maintenance of remission"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Remission criteria (ADA, Diabetes UK)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State ADA/Diabetes UK consensus remission criteria
  • [ ] Differentiate remission definitions (complete, partial, prolonged)
  • [ ] Apply criteria in clinical practice
  • [ ] Counsel patients on relapse vs remission

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
ConsensusADA/EASD/Diabetes UK/Diabetes UK (2021)
DefinitionHbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%) off ALL glucose-lowering medications ≥3 months
AlternativeFPG <7.0 mmol/L or eA1c <48 mmol/mol (if HbA1c unreliable)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
ConsensusADA/EASD/Diabetes UK/Diabetes UK (2021)
DefinitionHbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%) off ALL glucose-lowering medications ≥3 months
AlternativeFPG <7.0 mmol/L or eA1c <48 mmol/mol (if HbA1c unreliable)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Remission Categories (ADA/EASD 2021)

CategoryCriteria
Complete remissionHbA1c <48 mmol/mol off ALL glucose-lowering meds ≥3 months
Partial remissionFPG 5.6-6.9 mmol/mol off meds ≥3 months (pre-diabetes range)
Prolonged remissionComplete remission sustained ≥5 years

Ineligible for Remission Label

ScenarioReason
On any glucose-lowering medIncluding metformin
HbA1c ≥48 mmol/molDoes not meet threshold
<3 months off medsInsufficient duration
Bariatric surgery <3moWeight loss not stabilised

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestTiming
HbA1cAt 3 months off meds; then 3-6 monthly
FPGIf HbA1c unreliable (CKD, haemoglobinopathy)
CGMTIR >70% supportive but not diagnostic

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestTiming
HbA1cAt 3 months off meds; then 3-6 monthly
FPGIf HbA1c unreliable (CKD, haemoglobinopathy)
CGMTIR >70% supportive but not diagnostic

6. Differential Diagnosis

ScenarioDistinction
Partial remissionFPG 5.6-6.9 off meds ≥3mo (pre-diabetes range)
Good control on medsNot remission (on medication)
Transient normoglycaemia<3 months off meds

7. Management

Confirming Remission

StepAction
1Confirm off ALL glucose-lowering medications ≥3 months
2Measure HbA1c (or FPG if HbA1c unreliable)
3Confirm HbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
4Document remission in records
5Schedule 3-6 monthly monitoring

Relapse Definition

CriteriaAction
HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol on 2 occasionsRelapse confirmed; restart treatment
FPG ≥7.0 on 2 occasionsRelapse confirmed (if HbA1c unreliable)
Restart medicationIndividualised (lifestyle + metformin 1st line)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DefinitionHbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%) off ALL meds ≥3 months
CategoriesComplete (<48 off meds), Partial (FPG 5.6-6.9), Prolonged (≥5y)
ExclusionsOn any med, <3mo off meds, HbA1c ≥48
RelapseHbA1c ≥48 on 2 occasions -> restart treatment
Monitoring3-6 monthly HbA1c; counsel on weight maintenance

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the 2021 ADA/Diabetes UK remission definition?HbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%) off ALL glucose-lowering medications for ≥3 months
What are the remission categories?Complete (HbA1c<48 off meds ≥3mo), Partial (FPG 5.6-6.9 off meds ≥3mo), Prolonged (≥5y)
Can a patient be in remission if taking metformin?NO - must be off ALL glucose-lowering medications
What is the relapse threshold?HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol on 2 occasions (or FPG ≥7.0 if HbA1c unreliable)
Is HbA1c <48 on metformin remission?NO - must be off ALL glucose-lowering medications

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Remission = cure?NO - relapse possible; ongoing monitoring required
Partial remission = diabetes?NO - pre-diabetes range (FPG 5.6-6.9); better than diabetes
Metformin allowed in remission?NO - must be off ALL glucose-lowering meds
HbA1c unreliable?Use FPG or CGM TIR >70% as supportive

Mnemonic: REMISSION-CRITERIA-2021

  • Remission: HbA1c <48 off meds ≥3mo
  • EASD/ADA/DUK: 2021 consensus
  • Must be off: ALL glucose-lowering meds
  • Inclusive: metformin, SGLT2i, GLP-1, insulin, SU, DPP-4i
  • Sustained: ≥3 months
  • Standard: HbA1c <48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
  • Incomplete: FPG 5.6-6.9 (partial)
  • Off meds: completely
  • Not remission: on any med, <3mo, HbA1c≥48
  • Complete: HbA1c<48 off meds ≥3mo
  • Relapse: HbA1c≥48 x2 or FPG≥7.0 x2
  • Initiate: restart meds if relapse
  • Types: complete, partial, prolonged
  • Evidence: ADA/EASD/DUK 2021
  • Result: HbA1c<48, off meds, ≥3mo
  • Irreversible: NO (relapse possible)
  • Alonged: ≥5y prolonged remission
  • Code: HbA1c<48, no meds, ≥3mo
  • REMISSION = Remission: HbA1c<48, no meds, ≥3mo**

Topic Overview

Remission criteria (ADA, Diabetes UK
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Remission Categories (ADA/EASD 2021)
Ineligible for Remission Label
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Confirming Remission
Relapse Definition
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.4 Diabetes in Special Populations 15
▸ 21.4.1 Diabetes On Dialysis

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage glycaemic control in haemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis
  • [ ] Apply insulin dosing adjustments for dialysis
  • [ ] Use alternative glycaemic markers (GA, fructosamine, CGM)
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia risk in dialysis patients

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage glycaemic control in haemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis
  • [ ] Apply insulin dosing adjustments for dialysis
  • [ ] Use alternative glycaemic markers (GA, fructosamine, CGM)
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia risk in dialysis patients

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Prevalence20-30% dialysis patients have diabetes; leading cause of ESRD
MortalityDM on dialysis: 2x mortality vs non-DM; CVD main cause
HbA1cUnreliable in dialysis (falsely low); use GA/fructosamine/CGM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Prevalence20-30% dialysis patients have diabetes
MortalityDM on dialysis: 2x mortality vs non-DM; CVD main cause
HbA1cUnreliable in dialysis (falsely low); use GA/fructosamine/CGM

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureHaemodialysis (HD)Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)
Glucose variabilityHigh (pre/post dialysis swings)More stable; continuous glucose exposure
Hypoglycaemia riskHigh (post-dialysis); glucose-free dialysateLower; but glucose absorption from dialysate
Insulin requirementVariable; often ↓ post-HDOften higher (glucose absorption from dialysate)
HbA1c reliabilityUnreliable in bothUnreliable; GA/fructosamine essential

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureHaemodialysis (HD)Peritoneal Dialysis (PD)
Glucose variabilityHigh (pre/post dialysis swings)More stable; continuous glucose exposure
Hypoglycaemia riskHigh (post-dialysis); glucose-free dialysateLower; but glucose absorption from dialysate
Insulin requirementVariable; often ↓ post-HDOften higher (glucose absorption from dialysate)
HbA1c reliabilityUnreliable in bothUnreliable; GA/fructosamine essential

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glycaemic Monitoring in Dialysis

MarkerHDPDNotes
HbA1cUnreliableUnreliableFalsely low (↓RBC lifespan, ESA)
Glycated Albumin (GA)PreferredPreferred2-3 wk mean; GA <20% target
FructosamineAlternativeAlternative2-3 wk; <320 umol/L target
CGMEmergingEmergingReal-time; avoid hypo

Glycaemic Targets (Dialysis)

PopulationGA TargetFructosamine TargetPre-dialysis Glucose
HD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L
PD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glycaemic Monitoring in Dialysis

MarkerHDPDNotes
HbA1cUnreliableUnreliableFalsely low (↓RBC lifespan, ESA)
Glycated Albumin (GA)PreferredPreferred2-3 wk mean; GA <20% target
FructosamineAlternativeAlternative2-3 wk; <320 umol/L target
CGMEmergingEmergingReal-time; avoid hypo

Glycaemic Targets (Dialysis)

PopulationGA TargetFructosamine TargetPre-dialysis Glucose
HD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L
PD<20-21%<320 umol/L7-10 mmol/L

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Insulin Requirements by Modality

ModalityPatternInsulin Adjustment
HDPre-HD glucose high; post-HD rapid drop↓ dose post-HD; pre-HD correction
PDContinuous glucose absorption from dialysateHigher total daily dose; consistent
BothGlucose-free dialysate -> hypo riskMonitor pre/post; adjust basal

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HypoglycaemiaPost-HD (glucose-free dialysate); post-prandial (PD)
HyperglycaemiaPre-HD (catabolic); pre-dialysis (PD glucose absorption)
Glucose-free dialysate effectRapid glucose drop during HD

7. Management

Insulin Management on Dialysis

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Diabetes on Dialysis
↓
Modality?
↓
Insulin variable; ↓ post-HD
Insulin higher; glucose absorption from dialysate
↓
Pre-HD: correction if >10
Post-HD: ↓ basal 25-50%; monitor 1-2h post
Consistent higher dose; glucose absorption
↓
Monitor pre-HD, 1h post, bedtime
↓
GA/Fructosamine q3mo; CGM if available

Insulin Dosing Principles

PrincipleHDPD
Basal insulinDegludec/U-300 preferred (stable)Standard; consistent
Bolus insulinPre-meal; skip if pre-HD glucose <7Standard; match dialysate glucose
Dose reduction50-75% vs non-dialysis25-50%
U-100 vs U-300/U-200U-100 preferred (predictable)U-100 preferred

Hypoglycaemia Prevention

StrategyHDPD
Pre-dialysis glucoseTarget 7-10 mmol/LTarget 7-10 mmol/L
Post-dialysisMonitor 1-2h; ↓ basal if <5-
Dialysis day↓ basal 25-50% on HD daysConsistent
CGMReal-time; predictive alertsReal-time; predictive alerts
Glucose-free dialysateHypo risk -> monitor 1-2h post-

Medication Adjustments (Dialysis)

DrugHDPDNotes
SGLT2iContinue (dapa/empa)ContinueInitiate >=20; continue on dialysis
MetforminCONTRAINDICATED (eGFR<15)CONTRAINDICATEDStop
SUAvoid (hypo risk)AvoidGliclazide MR if essential
DPP-4iLinagliptin (no adjust)LinagliptinOthers dose reduce
GLP-1 RANo adjustNo adjustLimited data

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HbA1cUnreliable in dialysis; use GA <20% / fructosamine <320
InsulinReduce 50-75%; U-100 preferred; variable needs
HD vs PDHD: variable, post-HD hypo; PD: stable, ↑dose (dialysate glucose)
MonitoringGA <20% / Fructosamine <320; CGM emerging
Pre/post HDPre-HD high; post-HD rapid drop -> monitor 1-2h post
MedicationsSGLT2i continue; metformin STOP; SU avoid; linagliptin preferred DPP-4i

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Why is HbA1c unreliable in dialysis?↓RBC lifespan (uraemia, ESA, blood loss); carbamylation; falsely low
What alternative markers do you use in dialysis?Glycated Albumin (GA) <20%, Fructosamine <320 umol/L, CGM
How does insulin requirement differ between HD and PD?HD: variable, ↓post-HD; PD: stable, ↑dose (glucose absorption from dialysate)
What is the insulin dose reduction on dialysis?50-75% reduction vs non-dialysis
Why is hypoglycaemia common post-haemodialysis?Glucose-free dialysate -> rapid glucose drop; ↓hepatic glucose production
Which DPP-4i is preferred in dialysis?Linagliptin (no renal dose adjustment)
Can SGLT2i be used on dialysis?Yes - dapagliflozin/empagliflozin continue on dialysis (initiated >=20)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HbA1c useful in dialysis?NO - falsely low; use GA/Fructosamine/CGM
Insulin same HD vs PD?NO - HD variable/post-dialysis drop; PD stable higher dose
Metformin on dialysis?CONTRAINDICATED (eGFR <15)
SGLT2i in dialysis?Dapa/Empa: continue on dialysis if initiated >=20

Mnemonic: DIALYSIS-DM-INSULIN

  • Dialysis: HbA1c unreliable; use GA <20%, Fructosamine <320
  • Insulin: reduce 50-75% (U-100 preferred)
  • Alternatives: GA <20%, Fructosamine <320 umol/L, CGM
  • Low glucose post-HD: glucose-free dialysate -> hypo risk
  • You monitor: pre-HD, 1-2h post-HD, bedtime
  • SGLT2i: dapa/empa continue dialysis (if >=20)
  • Insulin dose: reduce 50-75% (U-100)
  • Stable PD: glucose absorption -> higher insulin needs
  • Dialysis types: HD variable; PD stable higher
  • Insulin U-100: preferred (predictable vs U-300/U-200)
  • Alternatives: GA <20%, Fructosamine <320, CGM
  • Low glucose post-HD: monitor 1-2h post
  • You use GA/Fructosamine: HbA1c unreliable
  • Stop metformin (eGFR<15); stop SU
  • Unique PD: dialysate glucose -> insulin needs
  • Stop SU: hypo risk; glic MR if essential**
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.4.10 Hypoglycaemia Risk In Elderly

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Quantify hypoglycaemia risk in elderly diabetes
  • [ ] Identify risk factors: renal impairment, polypharmacy, cognitive impairment
  • [ ] Apply preventive strategies: CGM, relaxed targets, simplified regimens
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia in cognitively impaired elderly

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Quantify hypoglycaemia risk in elderly diabetes
  • [ ] Identify risk factors: renal impairment, polypharmacy, cognitive impairment
  • [ ] Apply preventive strategies: CGM, relaxed targets, simplified regimens
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia in cognitively impaired elderly

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGlucose < 3.9 mmol/L (Level 1); < 3.0 mmol/L (Level 2); severe = requires assistance (Level 3)
IncidenceElderly T2DM: 2-3x higher than younger; insulin/SU = highest risk
Risk5x higher in frail vs fit; 2x in >75y vs <65y
MortalityHypoglycaemia = 2x CVD mortality; "dead in bed" in T1DM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGlucose < 3.9 mmol/L (Level 1); < 3.0 mmol/L (Level 2); severe = requires assistance (Level 3)
IncidenceElderly T2DM: 2-3x higher than younger; insulin/SU = highest risk
Risk5x higher in frail vs fit; 2x in >75y vs <65y
MortalityHypoglycaemia = 2x CVD mortality; "dead in bed" in T1DM

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureElderly Specifics
Atypical presentationConfusion, falls, agitation, drowsiness (not classic autonomic)
NeuroglycopenicPredominant: confusion, drowsiness, speech difficulty
Autonomic bluntedReduced sweating, tremor, palpitations
NocturnalCommon; "dead in bed" syndrome (T1DM)
AsymptomaticHypoglycaemia unawareness; cognitive impairment masks symptoms

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureElderly Specifics
Atypical presentationConfusion, falls, agitation, drowsiness (not classic autonomic)
NeuroglycopenicPredominant: confusion, drowsiness, speech difficulty
Autonomic bluntedReduced sweating, tremor, palpitations
NocturnalCommon; "dead in bed" syndrome (T1DM)
AsymptomaticHypoglycaemia unawareness; cognitive impairment masks symptoms

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Risk Factors in Elderly

CategoryRisk Factors
RenaleGFR < 45 (↓ insulin clearance); dialysis (glucose-free dialysate)
MedicationsInsulin, sulfonylureas, beta-blockers (mask symptoms), ACEi/ARB
CognitiveDementia, MCI (impaired recognition/reporting)
Physiological↓ counter-regulation (glucagon/epinephrine); ↓ renal gluconeogenesis
SocialLiving alone; erratic meals; carer-dependent; alcohol
ComorbiditiesCKD, HF, liver disease; malnutrition; polypharmacy

Severity Grading (ADA)

LevelGlucoseClinical
1 (Alert)< 3.9 mmol/LSelf-treat
2 (Clinical)< 3.0 mmol/LImpaired judgement; self-treat possible
3 (Severe)Any (requires help)Unconscious, seizure, needs 3rd party

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

ToolRole
CGMGold standard detection (nocturnal, asymptomatic); predictive alerts
SMBG4-6x/day if insulin/SU; pre-meal, bedtime, 3am
HbA1cFalsely LOW in CKD/ESRD (↓RBC lifespan); use fructosamine/GA
Renal functioneGFR; adjust insulin/SU doses
Cognitive screenMoCA/MoCA; identify impaired recognition

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
FallsHypoglycaemia = common cause; check glucose
Confusion/deliriumCheck glucose first (reversible cause)
Stroke/TIAFocal neurology; glucose may be high/low
SepsisFever, tachycardia; glucose variable
Medication toxicityDigoxin, opioids, benzodiazepines

7. Management

Prevention Strategies

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Elderly DM
↓
Assess hypo risk factors
↓
On SU/insulin?
↓
Review doses; STOP SU; reduce insulin
Review other meds (beta-blockers, ACEi)
↓
Simplify regimen: once-daily basal; stop SU
↓
Individualised HbA1c target per frailty
↓
CGM with predictive alerts
↓
Carer education: hypo recognition, glucagon
↓
Regular review: 3-monthly HbA1c; CGM review

Prevention Strategies by Domain

DomainIntervention
MedicationSTOP sulfonylureas; reduce insulin 20-50%; once-daily basal; stop bolus
TargetsRelax HbA1c per frailty (fit 53, mild 58, mod 64, sev 69 mmol/mol)
TechnologyCGM with predictive alerts (low glucose suspend if pump); SMBG 4x/day if no CGM
RenalInsulin dose ↓ 25-50% if eGFR < 45; stop SU if eGFR < 45
CognitiveCarer-administered insulin; CGM with remote monitoring; simplified regimen
SocialRegular meals; bedtime snack; alcohol moderation; glucagon available

Nocturnal Hypoglycaemia Prevention

StrategyDetail
Bedtime snack15-20g complex carb if bedtime glucose < 7 mmol/L
Basal insulinReduce evening dose; switch to degludec/U-300 (stable)
CGM alertsLow glucose suspend (pump); predictive alerts (CGM)
Bedtime glucoseTarget 7-10 mmol/L at bedtime

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Risk5x higher in frail; 2x in >75y; renal/polypharmacy/cognitive = multipliers
PresentationAtypical: confusion, falls, agitation (not tremor/sweat)
PreventionSTOP SU; reduce insulin; relax targets; CGM alerts; carer education
NocturnalBedtime glucose 7-10; basal reduction; CGM predictive alerts
RenalInsulin ↓ 25-50% if eGFR < 45; stop SU if eGFR < 45
CognitiveCarer-administered insulin; CGM remote monitoring; simplified regimen

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
Why is hypoglycaemia risk higher in elderly?Renal impairment (↓ insulin clearance), polypharmacy, cognitive impairment, blunted counter-regulation, erratic meals
How does hypoglycaemia present differently in elderly?Atypical: confusion, falls, agitation, drowsiness (not classic tremor/sweating); neuroglycopenic predominant
What medications increase hypoglycaemia risk in elderly?Sulfonylureas (highest), insulin, beta-blockers (mask symptoms), ACEi/ARB
How do you prevent nocturnal hypoglycaemia?Bedtime glucose 7-10; reduce evening basal; bedtime snack if <7; CGM predictive alerts
How do you adjust insulin in CKD?Reduce 25-50% if eGFR < 45; stop sulfonylureas if eGFR < 45; monitor glucose closely
What is hypoglycaemia unawareness in elderly?Blunted autonomic symptoms (autonomic neuropathy + cognitive impairment); sudden neuroglycopenia

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Elderly = same hypo symptoms?NO - autonomic blunted; neuroglycopenic (confusion, falls) predominant
HbA1c reliable in elderly CKD?NO - falsely LOW (↓ RBC lifespan, ESA); use fructosamine/GA
Glucagon in elderly?Works if glycogen present; ineffective in starvation/alcohol/liver failure

Mnemonic: HYPO-ELDERLY-RISK

  • Hypoglycaemia: 5x risk in frail; 2x in >75y
  • Yield: atypical presentation (confusion, falls, not tremor)
  • Polypharmacy: SU + insulin + beta-blockers = highest risk
  • Old age: blunted counter-regulation (glucagon/epinephrine down)
  • Elderly CKD: insulin clearance down; reduce dose 25-50%
  • Living alone: no one to recognize/help
  • Dementia: cognitive impairment = can't recognize/report
  • Elderly frail: Rockwood >=5 = 5x hypo risk
  • Renal: eGFR<45 -> insulin reduce 25-50%; stop SU
  • Low glucose at night: bedtime 7-10; CGM alerts; snack
  • Year: annual hypoglycaemia review
  • Renal: fructosamine/GA if HbA1c unreliable
  • Insulin: reduce 25-50% in CKD; stop bolus; simplify basal
  • Stop sulfonylureas FIRST (highest risk)
  • Know: glucagon ineffective if glycogen depleted (starvation/alcohol)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.4.11 Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Gdm

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply IADPSG (universal) and NICE (risk-based) GDM diagnostic criteria
  • [ ] State GDM glycaemic targets and monitoring frequency
  • [ ] Execute stepwise management: diet -> metformin -> insulin
  • [ ] Manage pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy (pre-conception, targets, teratogenicity)
  • [ ] Counsel on postpartum screening and lifetime T2DM risk

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionGlucose intolerance with onset or first recognition in pregnancy (excluding pre-existing diabetes)
Incidence2-14% pregnancies (varies by population, criteria); IADPSG criteria -> up diagnosis 2-3x vs older criteria
Risk FactorsBMI >=30, previous GDM, previous macrosomia (>4.5kg), family history T2DM, ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean, Middle Eastern), age >40, PCOS, corticosteroid use
PathophysiologyPlacental hormones (hPL, progesterone, cortisol, oestrogen) -> insulin resistance up 2-3x by 3rd trimester; pancreatic beta-cells fail to compensate
ComplicationsFetal: macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, hyperbilirubinaemia, RDS, stillbirth; Maternal: pre-eclampsia, C-section, future T2DM (50% at 10y), future GDM (recurrence 30-50%)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
AsymptomaticMostDetected on screening OGTT
Osmotic symptomsIf marked hyperglycaemiaPolyuria, polydipsia (often masked by pregnancy)
Recurrent infectionsCommonCandidiasis, UTIs
Macrosomia (clinical)Late pregnancySFH >dates, large for dates on USS
Red Flags: Pre-existing diabetes (known before pregnancy); fasting glucose >=7.0 at booking -> likely pre-existing DM not GDM.

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

GDM Diagnostic Criteria (24-28 weeks)

CriteriaApproach75g OGTT Cut-offs (mmol/L)Diagnosis

| IADPSG / WHO 2013 / ADA | Universal (all pregnant) | FBG >=5.1

1h >=10.0

2h >=8.5 | >=1 abnormal value = GDM |

NICE (UK)Risk-based (if risk factors)FBG >=5.6 OR 2h >=7.8Either abnormal = GDM
ADA (alternative)Universal or risk-basedSame as IADPSG>=1 abnormal = GDM
Carpenter-Coustan (older)Universal100g OGTT: FBG>=5.3, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.6, 3h>=7.8>=2 abnormal = GDM
IADPSG preferred internationally; NICE used in UK -- IADPSG diagnoses more women, down adverse outcomes.

Pre-existing Diabetes in Pregnancy

TypePre-conception TargetsPregnancy Targets
T1DMHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%)FBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L
T2DMHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%)Same as T1DM

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationTimingKey Details
Booking HbA1c/FPG<10 weeksExclude pre-existing DM (HbA1c >=48 or FPG >=7.0 = pre-existing)
75g OGTT24-28 weeksIADPSG: FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5 (1 abnormal = GDM); NICE: risk factors first
SMBGAfter diagnosisFBG + 1h post-prandial x4/day minimum; target FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4
HbA1cNot for monitoring in GDMUse in pre-existing DM (target <48mmol/mol); falsely low in 2nd/3rd tri
USS (fetal)28, 32, 36 weeksMacrosomia (EFW >90th centile), polyhydramnios, growth
Renal functionBaselineCKD affects metformin/insulin dosing

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Pre-existing diabetesKnown before pregnancy OR HbA1c >=48 / FPG >=7.0 at booking; requires pre-conception care
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, steroids; resolves; no prior dysglycaemia
Type 1 diabetes presenting in pregnancyKetosis, weight loss, young, lean; check autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2), C-peptide
MODY (GCK)Mild fasting hyperglycaemia, strong FH, non-progressive; genetic testing if suspected
Renal glycosuriaNormal glucose, glycosuria; benign

7. Management

GDM Stepwise Management

StepInterventionThreshold to Escalate
1. Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT)Individualised diet (1800-2000 kcal, 40-45% carb, 20% protein, 35% fat); 3 meals + 2-3 snacks; dietitian reviewFBG >5.5 OR 1h post-prandial >7.0 mmol/L on 2+ occasions in 1-2 weeks
2. Metformin500mg OD -> 500mg BD -> 1000mg BD (max 2g/day); crosses placenta but safe (MiG trial); stop at deliveryFBG >6.0 OR 1h >7.8 OR failure to achieve targets in 1-2 weeks
3. InsulinBasal-bolus (rapid analogue pre-meal + basal) or Basal only if fasting hyperglycaemia only; TDD 0.7 U/kg (higher in 3rd tri); stop at deliveryIf metformin fails or contraindicated (eGFR<30, hepatic, patient choice)

Glycaemic Targets (ADA/NICE/IADPSG aligned)

MeasureTarget
Fasting<5.3 mmol/L (95 mg/dL)
1-hour post-prandial<7.8 mmol/L (140 mg/dL)
2-hour post-prandial<6.4 mmol/L (115 mg/dL)
HbA1cNot recommended for monitoring in GDM

Pre-existing Diabetes in Pregnancy

PhaseKey Actions
Pre-conceptionHbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%); Folic acid 5mg OD (start >=3mo pre-conception -> 12wks); Stop teratogens: ACEi/ARB, statins, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, sulfonylureas; baseline retinal/renal/CV assessment
1st TrimesterIntensive monitoring (SMBG 7x/day); insulin dose down then up; screen for complications
2nd/3rd TrimesterInsulin requirements up 2-3x (placental insulin resistance); basal-bolus; tight targets; USS surveillance
Delivery38-39 weeks if well-controlled; 37-38 if complications; insulin infusion intrapartum (target 4-7 mmol/L)
PostpartumInsulin requirements drop dramatically; reduce to pre-pregnancy doses; breastfeeding -> adjust insulin/calories; OGTT 6-13 weeks postpartum

Medication Safety in Pregnancy

DrugSafetyNotes
InsulinSafe (does not cross placenta)Gold standard; human insulin/analogues
MetforminSafe (MiG trial)Crosses placenta but no teratogenicity; 1st line oral
GlibenclamideAlternativeCrosses placenta; up neonatal hypoglycaemia; 2nd line if metformin failed
SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA / DPP-4i / TZD / SU (except glibenclamide)ContraindicatedTeratogenicity / limited safety data

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
IADPSG GDMUniversal 75g OGTT 24-28wk; FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5; 1 abnormal = GDM
NICE GDMRisk factors (BMI>=30, prev GDM, macrosomia, FH, ethnicity, age>40) -> OGTT; FBG>=5.6 OR 2h>=7.8
GDM TargetsFBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L; SMBG 4x/day (FBG + 1h post-meal)
ManagementDiet -> Metformin (if FBG>5.5 or 1h>7.0) -> Insulin (basal-bolus/basal)
Pre-existing DMPre-conception HbA1c <48; Folic acid 5mg OD -> 12wks; Stop ACEi/ARB/statin/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/SU
IntrapartumInsulin IV infusion; target 4-7 mmol/L; reduce postpartum
PostpartumOGTT 6-13 weeks; annual HbA1c; lifetime T2DM risk 50%
Metformin in GDMMiG trial: safe, crosses placenta but no teratogenicity; 1st line oral; max 2g/day
Glibenclamide2nd line if metformin fails; crosses placenta; up neonatal hypoglycaemia
Drugs to STOPACEi/ARB, statins, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, sulfonylureas (except glibenclamide), TZDs

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the IADPSG diagnostic criteria for GDM?75g OGTT at 24-28 weeks: FBG >=5.1, 1h >=10.0, 2h >=8.5 mmol/L -- one or more abnormal values = GDM
How does NICE GDM screening differ from IADPSG?NICE: risk-based (BMI>=30, prev GDM, macrosomia, FH, ethnicity, age>40) -> OGTT if risk factors; thresholds: FBG>=5.6 OR 2h>=7.8; IADPSG: universal, lower thresholds
What are the glycaemic targets in GDM?FBG <5.3 mmol/L; 1h post-prandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2h post-prandial <6.4 mmol/L
What is the stepwise management of GDM?1) MNT + SMBG; 2) Metformin if FBG>5.5 or 1h>7.0 on 2+ occasions; 3) Insulin (basal-bolus or basal only) if metformin fails
What is the pre-conception care for pre-existing diabetes?HbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%); Folic acid 5mg OD (>=3mo pre-conception -> 12wks); Stop ACEi/ARB, statins, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, sulfonylureas; baseline retinal/renal/CV screen
Why is metformin preferred over glibenclamide in GDM?MiG trial: metformin safe, no teratogenicity; glibenclamide crosses placenta -> up neonatal hypoglycaemia; metformin 1st line
What happens to insulin requirements postpartum?Dramatic drop (placental insulin resistance removed); reduce to pre-pregnancy doses; monitor for hypoglycaemia; breastfeeding -> may need further reduction
When do you do postpartum OGTT after GDM?6-13 weeks postpartum (75g OGTT); annual HbA1c thereafter; lifetime T2DM risk ~50%

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
GDM = pre-existing diabetes?NO -- GDM = first detected in pregnancy; if HbA1c>=48 or FPG>=7.0 at booking -> pre-existing
HbA1c for GDM monitoring?NO -- falsely low in 2nd/3rd trimester (up RBC turnover); use SMBG
Metformin teratogenic?NO -- MiG trial: crosses placenta but no teratogenicity; 1st line oral
Insulin crosses placenta?NO -- large molecule; safe for fetus
Postpartum OGTT timing?6-13 weeks; not at 6 weeks (still placental hormones), not immediate

Mnemonic: GDM-IADPSG

  • GDM: hyperglycaemia 1st detected in pregnancy
  • Diagnosis: IADPSG universal 75g OGTT 24-28wk
  • Means: FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5 (1 abnormal = GDM)
  • IADPSG vs NICE: universal vs risk-based
  • ADA: same as IADPSG
  • Diet 1st (MNT), SMBG 4x/day
  • Post-prandial targets: FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4
  • Stepped: Diet -> Metformin (FBG>5.5/1h>7.0) -> Insulin
  • Glibenclamide 2nd line (metformin fail)
  • Pre-existing: HbA1c>=48 at booking -> pre-conception care
  • Reconception: HbA1c<48, Folic 5mg, STOP teratogens (ACEi/ARB/statin/SGLT2i/GLP-1/SU)
  • OGTT postpartum: 6-13wk
  • Screening postpartum: annual HbA1c, lifetime T2DM risk 50%
  • Gestational: resolves delivery but recurrence 30-50%

11. Mind Map

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus
Diagnosis
IADPSG: universal 75g OGTT
FBG>=5.1, 1h>=10.0, 2h>=8.5
NICE: risk-based
Targets
FBG <5.3
1h <7.8
2h <6.4
SMBG 4x/day
Management
Diet (MNT)
Metformin 1st line oral
Insulin (basal-bolus)
Pre-existing DM
Pre-conception HbA1c<48
Folic 5mg OD
Stop teratogens
Postpartum
OGTT 6-13wk
Annual HbA1c
T2DM risk 50%
Contraindicated
SGLT2i, GLP-1, DPP-4i
SU (except glibenclamide)
ACEi/ARB, statins

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionHyperglycaemia first detected in pregnancy (excl. pre-existing)
**Key Test"75g OGTT 24-28wk (IADPSG universal); SMBG 4x/day; not HbA1c
**Classification"GDM vs pre-existing (HbA1c>=48/FPG>=7.0 at booking)
**Acute Mgmt"Intrapartum: IV insulin infusion target 4-7 mmol/L
**Chronic Mgmt"Diet -> Metformin (FBG>5.5/1h>7.0) -> Insulin; Pre-existing: folic 5mg, stop teratogens
**Key Score"IADPSG: 1 abnormal = GDM; Targets FBG<5.3, 1h<7.8, 2h<6.4
**Complications"Macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypo, pre-eclampsia, C-section
**Prognosis"Resolves delivery; postpartum OGTT 6-13wk; T2DM risk 50% lifetime

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt

▸ 21.4.2 Cgm Metrics Tir Tbr Tar Gmi Cv

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, cgm, metrics, tir, tbr, tar, gmi, cv, rtCGM, isCGM, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - CGM metrics now standard of care; TIR targets replace HbA1c for daily decisions; GMI estimates HbA1c; CV measures variability"

see_also: ["Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM)", "CGM-guided insulin adjustment", "rtCGM vs isCGM", "Time in range (TIR) targets", "HbA1c targets (individualised)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

CGM Metrics (TIR, TBR, TAR, GMI, CV) — International Consensus

[!info]
Standardised CGM metrics (ATTD 2019, 2023) — TIR 70–180 mg/dL is primary outcome; TBR/TAR for safety; GMI estimates HbA1c; CV <36% = low variability.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define all standard CGM metrics and their targets
  • [ ] Apply TIR/TBR/TAR targets for T1DM, T2DM, pregnancy, elderly
  • [ ] Interpret GMI vs HbA1c concordance/discordance
  • [ ] Use CV and SD for glycaemic variability assessment
  • [ ] Apply Ambulatory Glucose Profile (AGP) visual analysis

2. Core CGM Metrics (ATTD Consensus 2019/2023)

MetricDefinitionTarget (Most Adults T1/T2)Target (Pregnancy)Target (Elderly/High Risk)
TIR (Time in Range)% time 70–180 mg/dL (3.9–10.0 mmol/L)>70% (>16.8 h/day)>70% (3.5–7.8 mmol/L)>50%
TAR (Time Above Range)% time >180 mg/dL (>10.0 mmol/L)<25% (<6 h/day)<25%<10% >250 mg/dL
TAR Level 2% time >250 mg/dL (>13.9 mmol/L)<5% (<1.2 h/day)<5%<5%
TBR (Time Below Range)% time <70 mg/dL (<3.9 mmol/L)<4% (<1 h/day)<4%<1%
TBR Level 2% time <54 mg/dL (<3.0 mmol/L)<1% (<15 min/day)<1%<1%

Minimum CGM wear for valid report: ≥70% data capture over 14 days (10 days minimum).

3. Derived Metrics

MetricFormulaTargetClinical Use
GMI (Glucose Management Indicator)3.31 + 0.02392 × mean sensor glucose (mg/dL)Matches HbA1c targetEstimates HbA1c from CGM; replaces "eHbA1c"
CV (Coefficient of Variation)(SD / Mean glucose) × 100%<36% (stable); 36–50% (moderate); >50% (high variability)Best single variability metric
SDStandard deviation of glucoseLess robust than CV
MAGEMean Amplitude of Glycaemic Excursions—Research; complex to calculate
LBGI/HBGILow/High Blood Glucose Indices—Risk quantification

4. TIR Targets by Population (ATTD 2023)

PopulationTIR (70–180)TBR (<70)TBR (<54)TAR (>180)TAR (>250)
T1DM / T2DM (most adults)>70%<4%<1%<25%<5%
Pregnancy T1DM>70% (3.5–7.8)<4%<1%<25%—
Pregnancy T2DM/GDM>70% (3.5–7.8)<4%<1%<25%—
Older / High Risk (frailty, CKD, CVD)>50%<1%<1%<10% (>250)<5%

Each 5% TIR increase ≈ 0.3–0.5% HbA1c reduction ≈ 15–20% microvascular risk reduction.

5. GMI vs HbA1c — Discordance

ScenarioGMI vs HbA1cCause
ConcordantWithin ±0.5% (5.5 mmol/mol)Normal RBC turnover
GMI > HbA1cGMI higherAnaemia, haemolysis, CKD, pregnancy, Hb variants, recent transfusion
HbA1c > GMIHbA1c higherIron deficiency, splenectomy, certain Hb variants

GMI formula: GMI (%) = 3.31 + 0.02392 × mean glucose (mg/dL)

  • Mean 154 mg/dL → GMI 7.0%
  • Mean 183 mg/dL → GMI 7.7%
  • Mean 212 mg/dL → GMI 8.4%

6. Coefficient of Variation (CV) — Glycaemic Variability

CVInterpretationAction
<36%Low variability — stableContinue current regimen
36–50%ModerateReview insulin timing, carb counting, basal rates
>50%High variabilityHigh hypo/hyper risk; consider technology (AID, CGM alerts)

Why CV over SD: CV normalises for mean glucose — two people with same SD but different means have different variability risk.

7. Ambulatory Glucose Profile (AGP) — Visual Analysis

Standardised 14-day AGP report (ATTD):

  1. Median line (50th percentile)
  2. Interquartile range (IQR) 25–75th percentile — "glucose corridor"
  3. 10–90th percentile — extremes
  4. Reference ranges (green 70–180, yellow >180, red <70)

Pattern recognition:

  • Dawn phenomenon: Rise 3–8 AM
  • Post-prandial spikes: After meals
  • Nocturnal hypoglycaemia: Flat line or dips 2–4 AM
  • Basal too high: Overnight gradual decline
  • Bolus mismatch: Sharp post-meal rise + late drop

8. rtCGM vs isCGM — Metric Availability

FeaturertCGM (Dexcom G6/G7, Guardian, FreeStyle Libre 3)isCGM (FreeStyle Libre 1/2)
Real-time alertsYes (predictive)No (Libre 2 has optional alarms)
Data completenessAutomatic (Bluetooth)Requires scan (≥8-hourly for 100%)
TIR validityHigh (≥70% auto)User-dependent (scanning frequency)
CalibrationNone (factory)None (factory)
GMI/CV accuracyHighHigh if scanned regularly

9. Clinical Application Workflow

1. Review AGP (14-day)
   ├─ Data completeness >70%?
   ├─ TIR >70%? → Continue
   ├─ TBR >4% or Level 2 >1%? → Reduce basal/bolus, review hypos
   ├─ TAR >25%? → Increase basal/bolus, carb counting, timing
   ├─ CV >36%? → Regular meals, pre-bolus, technology
   └─ GMI vs HbA1c discordant? → Check RBC indices

2. Shared decision-making with patient
3. Adjust 1 parameter at a time (basal → bolus → correction)
4. Re-review at 2–4 weeks

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
TIR target (most adults)>70% (70–180 mg/dL / 3.9–10.0 mmol/L)
TBR target<4% (<70 mg/dL); Level 2 <54 mg/dL <1%
TAR target<25% (>180 mg/dL); Level 2 >250 mg/dL <5%
Pregnancy TIR range3.5–7.8 mmol/L (63–140 mg/dL); target >70%
Elderly/frail TIR>50%; TBR <1% (<70); TAR <10% (>250)
GMI formula3.31 + 0.02392 × mean glucose (mg/dL)
CV target<36% = low variability
AGP minimum wear14 days, >70% data capture
Each 5% TIR ↑≈ 0.3–0.5% HbA1c ↓ ≈ 15–20% microvascular risk ↓
rtCGM vs isCGMrtCGM = real-time alerts; isCGM = scan-dependent

11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

Topic Overview

CGM Metrics (TIR, TBR, TAR, GMI, CV) — International Consensus
1. Learning Objectives
2. Core CGM Metrics (ATTD Consensus 2019/2023)
3. Derived Metrics
4. TIR Targets by Population (ATTD 2023)
5. GMI vs HbA1c — Discordance
6. Coefficient of Variation (CV) — Glycaemic Variability
7. Ambulatory Glucose Profile (AGP) — Visual Analysis
8. rtCGM vs isCGM — Metric Availability
