▸ 21.5.29 Type 2 Diabetes In Youth

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, t2dm-youth, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Type 1 diabetes in children", "Monogenic diabetes in youth", "Transition to adult care"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Type 2 diabetes in youth

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise rising T2DM in youth (obesity-driven)
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria and screening (ADA: BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 RF; age >= 10 or puberty)
  • [ ] Apply management: metformin 1st line; GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i if HbA1c > 75
  • [ ] Manage comorbidities (PCOS, NAFLD, sleep apnoea, hypertension)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
TrendRising with childhood obesity; 10-19y peak; T2DM > T1DM in some ethnic groups
Risk FactorsObesity (BMI >= 95th percentile), FH T2DM, ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean), acanthosis nigricans, PCOS, maternal GDM
ScreeningADA: age >= 10 or puberty + BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 risk factors

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureT2DM YouthT1DM Youth
ObesityUsually present (BMI >= 95th percentile)Absent/normal
Acanthosis nigricansCommon (insulin resistance marker)Rare
FH T2DMStrong (multiple relatives)Variable
AutoantibodiesNegativePositive (2+)
C-peptidePreserved/highLow/absent
DKA at dxRare25-30%

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Screening Criteria (ADA)

CriteriaThreshold
Age>= 10 years or onset of puberty
BMI>= 85th percentile
Risk Factors>= 2 of: FH T2DM, high-risk ethnicity, acanthosis nigricans, PCOS, maternal GDM, hypertension, dyslipidaemia

HbA1c Targets

AgeTarget
All<53 mmol/mol (7.0%) if achievable without hypo
If high risk<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestRole
HbA1c / FPG / OGTTDiagnosis (ADA criteria)
AutoantibodiesExclude T1DM (negative in T2DM)
C-peptidePreserved in T2DM
Lipids, LFT, UACRBaseline comorbidities

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide, DKA common
MODY<25y, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies, preserved C-peptide
Secondary DMSteroid, Cushing, acromegaly, etc.

7. Management

Stepwise Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
T2DM Youth
↓
Lifestyle + Metformin
↓
HbA1c > 75mmol/mol (9%)?
↓
Add GLP-1 RA (liraglutide 1.8mg) or SGLT2i (dapagliflozin 10mg)
Optimise metformin; lifestyle
↓
HbA1c target met?
Continue + monitor
↓
Add insulin; basal-bolus

Lifestyle Intervention

ComponentRecommendation
DietReduced calorie; low GI; dietitian-led
Exercise60min/day moderate-vigorous
Screen time<2 hours/day
Family involvementEssential; whole-family approach

Comorbidity Screening (Annual)

ConditionScreen
DyslipidaemiaFasting lipid profile
HypertensionBP at each visit
NAFLDALT, USS liver
PCOSIrregular menses, hyperandrogenism
Sleep apnoeaSnoring, daytime sleepiness
DepressionPHQ-9

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ScreeningAge >= 10 or puberty + BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 RF (ADA)
PresentationObesity, acanthosis nigricans, FH T2DM, negative Abs
1st lineLifestyle + Metformin
2nd lineGLP-1 RA (liraglutide) or SGLT2i (dapagliflozin) if HbA1c > 75
ComorbiditiesPCOS, NAFLD, HTN, dyslipidaemia, sleep apnoea
HbA1c target<53 (7.0%) if achievable without hypo

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the screening criteria for T2DM in youth?Age >= 10 or puberty + BMI >= 85th percentile + 2 risk factors (FH, ethnicity, acanthosis, PCOS, GDM, HTN, dyslipidaemia)
How does T2DM youth differ from T1DM?Obesity, acanthosis nigricans, negative Abs, preserved C-peptide, strong FH, no DKA
What is 1st line therapy for T2DM in youth?Lifestyle + Metformin
When do you add GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i in youth?HbA1c > 75mmol/mol (9%) on metformin
What comorbidities to screen for?PCOS, NAFLD, HTN, dyslipidaemia, sleep apnoea, depression

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
T2DM youth = T1DM?NO - obesity, acanthosis, negative Abs, preserved C-peptide
Metformin in children?Safe >10y; extend-release preferred for GI tolerance
Insulin in T2DM youth?If HbA1c > 75 on metformin + GLP-1/SGLT2i or symptomatic
Bariatric surgery in youth?Consider if BMI > 35 + comorbidity or BMI > 40; specialist centre

Mnemonic: T2DM-YOUTH-RISE

  • T2DM youth: rising with obesity
  • 2 screening: age >= 10 or puberty + BMI > 85th percentile + 2 RF
  • Diagnosis: obesity, acanthosis, FH, negative Abs
  • Metformin 1st line
  • Youth obesity: BMI > 95th percentile
  • Obesity: acanthosis nigricans marker
  • US screening: ADA age >= 10 or puberty + BMI > 85th percentile + 2RF
  • Type 2 vs 1: obesity, acanthosis, neg Abs, preserved C-peptide
  • HbA1c target: <53 (7.0%)
  • Rising trend: obesity epidemic
  • Insulin if HbA1c > 75 on metformin + GLP-1/SGLT2i
  • Screen comorbidities: PCOS, NAFLD, HTN, dyslipidaemia, apnoea
  • Ethnicity: South Asian, African-Caribbean higher risk

Local Navigation

  • **Parent Heading": [[../Diabetes in children and adolescents|Diabetes in children and adolescents]]
  • **Chapter Map": [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
  • **Chapter MOC": [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]
  • **Drug Reference": [[../../../Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing|Drugs]]
  • **Related": [[Type 1 diabetes in children], [[Monogenic diabetes in children], [[Transition to adult care]]

Tags

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #fcps #mrcp #full-fcps-mrcp-note

▸ 21.5.3 Dka Management Protocol

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Execute DKA management protocol per JBDS/ADA guidelines
  • [ ] Calculate fluid deficit and replacement rates
  • [ ] Manage fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII)
  • [ ] Replace potassium safely with cardiac monitoring
  • [ ] Monitor for and manage complications (cerebral oedema, hypoglycaemia, hypophosphataemia)
  • [ ] Transition to subcutaneous insulin

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GuidelinesJBDS (UK, 2023) / ADA (US, 2024) — broadly aligned
Core Principles1) Fluid resuscitation 2) Insulin infusion 3) K+ replacement 4) Bicarbonate only if pH<7.0 5) Monitoring 6) Precipitant treatment 7) Transition to SC insulin

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(See DKA diagnosis criteria — management applies after diagnosis)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

(See DKA severity grading — protocol adjusts by severity)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationTimingTarget/Action
Glucoseq1h↓3–5 mmol/L/hr; if <14 → add 5% dextrose
Blood ketonesq2–4h↓0.5 mmol/L/hr; target clearance <0.6
VBG (pH, HCO3, pCO2)q2–4hpH ↑, HCO3 ↑; severe: arterial line q1–2h
K+q1–2hMaintain 4.0–5.0; replace per level
Fluid balanceHourlyInput/output, CVP if severe
Neurology (GCS)q1–4h by severityCerebral oedema watch in children

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A — management of confirmed DKA)

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation

PhaseFluidRateDuration
1st hour0.9% NaCl1L60 min
Hours 2–30.9% NaCl500ml/hr2 hr
Hours 4–240.9% NaCl250ml/hrAdjust to replace deficit over 24–48h
If Na+ ↑ >5 mmol/LSwitch to 0.45% NaClPer protocolHypernatraemia prevention
If glucose <14 mmol/L5% Dextrose + 0.45%/0.9% NaClMatch insulin infusionPrevent hypoglycaemia
Total deficit: ~5–7L (100ml/kg). Replace 50% in 12h, rest over 24–48h.

Insulin Therapy

ParameterDetail
RegimenFixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII)
Dose0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid/Humulin S in 50ml 0.9% NaCl = 1U/ml)
Bolus?NO IV bolus — increases cerebral oedema risk
DurationUntil ketones <0.6 mmol/L AND pH >7.3 AND bicarbonate >18
Glucose <14Add 5% dextrose; DO NOT STOP INSULIN until ketone clearance
Transition to SCGive 1st SC dose (basal/bolus) → continue FRII 30–60min → stop FRII

Potassium Replacement

Serum K+ (mmol/L)K+ in Fluids (mmol/L)Notes
<3.060 (or 80 in severe)Hold insulin until K+ >3.3 — cardiac arrest risk
3.0–3.560Cardiac monitor; replace aggressively
3.5–5.540Standard replacement
>5.5Hold (0)Recheck q1h; usually falls with insulin
Target: 4.0–5.0 mmol/L. Total K+ requirement often 500–1000mmol over 24h.

Bicarbonate

IndicationDose
pH <7.0 (severe)100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
pH 7.0–7.3Do not give — no outcome benefit, paradoxical CNS acidosis risk

Monitoring & Complications

ComplicationPrevention / Management
Cerebral oedemaChildren <20y: avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr; mannitol 0.5–1g/kg if suspected; hyperventilation; ICU
HypokalaemiaK+ replacement per protocol; cardiac monitor; hold insulin if K+ <3.3
HypoglycaemiaAdd 5% dextrose when glucose <14; don't stop insulin until ketones clear
HypophosphataemiaIf <0.3 mmol/L: IV phosphate (risk of hypocalcaemia)
Hyperchloraemic acidosisFrom 0.9% NaCl; switch to 0.45% NaCl if Na+ rising rapidly
⬇ Clinical Pathway
DKA Confirmed
↓
Severity?
↓
Ward: FRII 0.1U/kg/hr + 0.9% NaCl protocol
HDU: FRII + closer monitoring
ICU: FRII + arterial line + CVP + inotropes if shock
↓
K+ replacement per level
↓
Glucose <14?
↓
Add 5% dextrose
Continue 0.9% NaCl
↓
Ketones <0.6 AND pH >7.3?
↓
1st SC insulin dose → wait 30-60min → stop FRII

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Fluids1L 0.9% NaCl/hr ×1, then 500ml/hr ×2, then 250ml/hr; total 5–7L over 24–48h
InsulinFRII 0.1U/kg/hr; NO bolus; continue till ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18
K+<3.0: 60–80mmol/L (hold insulin till >3.3); 3.0–3.5: 60; 3.5–5.5: 40; >5.5: hold
BicarbONLY pH <7.0: 100mmol/2h
DextroseStart 5% dextrose when glucose <14; DO NOT stop insulin
Transition1st SC dose → overlap FRII 30–60min → stop FRII
Cerebral oedemaChildren: mannitol 0.5–1g/kg; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop
PrecipitantTreat infection (antibiotics), stop SGLT2i, etc.

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the initial fluid regimen for DKA?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500ml/hr for 2h, then 250ml/hr; total deficit ~5–7L over 24–48h
What insulin regimen is used?Fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRII) 0.1 U/kg/hr (e.g., 50U Actrapid in 50ml 0.9% NaCl); NO IV bolus
How do you manage potassium in DKA?Replace in fluids: K+ <3.0 → 60–80mmol/L (hold insulin till K+>3.3); 3.0–3.5 → 60mmol/L; 3.5–5.5 → 40mmol/L; >5.5 → hold. Cardiac monitoring essential.
When do you add dextrose?When glucose <14 mmol/L; switch to 5% dextrose + 0.45%/0.9% NaCl; continue insulin until ketones clear
When do you give bicarbonate?ONLY if pH <7.0: 100mmol NaHCO3 in 400ml water + 20mmol KCl over 2h
How do you transition to subcutaneous insulin?Give 1st SC dose (basal + bolus if eating) → continue FRII for 30–60min → stop FRII
What are the complications of DKA treatment?Hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia (if dextrose not added), cerebral oedema (children), hyperchloraemic acidosis (0.9% NaCl), hypophosphataemia
What is the cereal oedema management?Mannitol 0.5–1g/kg IV stat; hyperventilation; ICU; avoid rapid osmolar drop >3 mOsm/kg/hr

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Stop insulin when glucose normal?NO — continue FRII until ketones <0.6, pH >7.3, HCO3 >18; glucose controlled with dextrose
Bicarbonate for all acidosis?NO — only pH <7.0; paradoxical CNS acidosis, hypokalaemia risk
Insulin bolus to start?NO — never bolus; FRII 0.1U/kg/hr only; bolus → cerebral oedema

Mnemonic: DKA-FLUIDS

  • Fluids: 1L → 500 → 250 ml/hr (0.9% NaCl)
  • Low glucose <14 → 5% dextrose
  • Units insulin: 0.1U/kg/hr FRII (NO bolus)
  • Insulin continued until ketones <0.6 + pH >7.3
  • Dextrose + insulin overlap
  • Stop FRII 30-60min after 1st SC dose
  • K+: <3.0→60-80, 3-3.5→60, 3.5-5.5→40, >5.5→hold
  • Bicarb: ONLY pH<7.0, 100mmol/2h
  • Cerebral oedema: kids, mannitol 0.5-1g/kg
  • Transition: SC dose → wait → stop FRII

Diabetes in children and adolescents

[!info]
Topic-Group hub for Diabetes in children and adolescents — covers 4 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Type 1 in children]]
  • [[Type 2 in youth]]
  • [[Diabetes in Special Populations/Diabetes in children and adolescents/Monogenic diabetes in children.md|Monogenic diabetes in children]]
  • [[Diabetes in Special Populations/Diabetes in children and adolescents/Transition to adult care.md|Transition to adult care]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
T1D in ChildrenPeak 10-14y · DKA at dx 25-30% · HbA1c target <53mmol/mol (7.0%) · Family-centred care, school plan
T2D in YouthRising with obesity · Age 10-19y, puberty, FH, acanthosis nigricans · Metformin 1st line · GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i if HbA1c >75
Monogenic in ChildrenNeonatal DM <6mo (KCNJ11/ABCC8) · MODY (GCK: no rx, HNF1A: SU responsive) · Genetic testing if probability >6%
TransitionStructured programme age 16-18 · Joint paediatric-adult clinics · DAFNE-Youth · Psychosocial support

Topic Overview

Diabetes in children and adolescents
Disease-Level Topics
High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

Tags
▸ 21.5.4 Hhs Diagnosis Criteria

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State HHS diagnostic criteria (glucose, osmolality, pH, ketones)
  • [ ] Differentiate HHS from DKA and mixed DKA-HHS
  • [ ] Execute HHS management protocol (fluids priority, low-dose insulin)
  • [ ] Monitor osmolality decline rate (<3 mOsm/kg/hr)
  • [ ] Recognise mortality predictors

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionHyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State: severe hyperglycaemia + hyperosmolality + minimal ketosis/acidosis
Diagnostic CriteriaGlucose >30 mmol/L (540 mg/dL) + Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg + pH >7.30 + Bicarbonate >18 mmol/L + Minimal ketonaemia
Calculated Osmolality2×Na + Glucose + Urea (mmol/L) — normal 285–295
Incidence<1% of diabetic admissions; 10x less common than DKA
Mortality10–20% (vs 1–5% DKA) — higher in elderly, comorbidities
Typical PatientElderly T2DM, nursing home, infection, steroid use, non-compliance

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Altered consciousnessUniversalConfusion, lethargy, coma (correlates with osmolality)
Severe dehydrationUniversal>10L deficit; dry mucosa, poor turgor, hypotension
Polyuria → oliguriaProgressivePre-renal AKI
Focal neurological signs25–50%Hemiparesis, seizures, nystagmus (resolve with treatment)
No Kussmaul breathingAbsentNo acidosis → no respiratory compensation
PrecipitantsInfection (pneumonia, UTI), MI, stroke, drugs (steroids, diuretics, antipsychotics), non-compliance

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureHHSDKAMixed DKA-HHS
Glucose>30 mmol/L>11.1 mmol/LVariable
Osmolality>320 mOsm/kg<320>320
pH>7.30<7.30<7.30
Bicarbonate>18<15<15
KetonesMinimal>3 mmol/L>3 mmol/L
Age>60y<60y (often)Variable
Mortality10–20%1–5%Intermediate

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
GlucoseConfirm hyperglycaemia>30 mmol/L
Calculated osmolalityConfirm hyperosmolality2Na + Glucose + Urea >320 mOsm/kg
VBGExclude acidosispH >7.3, HCO3 >18
Blood ketonesExclude significant ketosis<3 mmol/L (but may be mildly elevated)
U&E, CreatinineRenal function, Na+, K+AKI common; corrected Na+ if glucose high
Corrected Na+Account for hyperglycaemiaMeasured Na+ + 1.6×(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
ECGK+ effects, ischaemiaSilent MI common
Infection screenPrecipitantBlood/urine culture, CXR, CRP

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
DKAAcidosis (pH<7.3), ketosis (>3 mmol/L), lower glucose
Mixed DKA-HHSBoth acidosis AND hyperosmolality present
Severe dehydration aloneNormal glucose, normal osmolality
StrokeFocal signs without hyperglycaemia/osmolality
Septic encephalopathyInfection + confusion, but glucose/osmolality normal

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation (PRIORITY)

PhaseFluidRateKey Points
1st hour0.9% NaCl1LRapid restoration of perfusion
Hours 2–60.9% NaCl500–1000ml/hrAdjust to correct deficit; HHS deficit 8–12L (150–200ml/kg)
If corrected Na+ rising >100.45% NaClPer protocolPrevent rapid osmolar fall
If glucose <145% Dextrose + 0.45% NaClMatch insulinPrevent hypoglycaemia
Goal: Correct deficit over 24–48h; osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr

Insulin Therapy

ParameterHHS (vs DKA)
TimingDelay 1–2h after fluids started — fluids alone drop glucose
Dose0.05 U/kg/hr (HALF of DKA dose) — lower insulin sensitivity
Bolus?NO
TargetGlucose decline 2–4 mmol/L/hr; stop when glucose 10–15 mmol/L
PotassiumSame as DKA — replace per level

Monitoring

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Osmolality (calculated)HourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2–4 mmol/L/hr
Na+ (corrected)2-hourlyAvoid rapid correction
K+1–2 hourly4.0–5.0 mmol/L
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected
Fluid balanceHourlyPositive then neutral
⬇ Clinical Pathway
HHS Suspected
↓
Labs: Glucose, Osmolality, VBG, Ketones, U&E
↓
pH >7.3 AND Osmolality >320?
↓
HHS Diagnosis
DKA or Mixed
↓
0.9% NaCl 1L/hr ×1-2h
↓
Delay insulin 1-2h
↓
FRII 0.05U/kg/hr
↓
Monitor osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
↓
K+ management per DKA protocol
↓
Glucose 10-15 → switch to SC insulin

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Diagnostic triadGlucose >30 + Osmolality >320 + pH >7.3 + minimal ketones
HHS vs DKAHHS: older, T2DM, higher glucose/osmolality, NO acidosis, higher mortality (10–20%)
Fluids FIRST1L 0.9% NaCl/hr before insulin; deficit 8–12L
Insulin HALF dose0.05 U/kg/hr (vs 0.1 in DKA); delay 1–2h
Osmolality decline<3 mOsm/kg/hr — prevent cerebral oedema
Mortality predictorsAge >70, coma, hypotension, osmolality >350, comorbidities, delayed presentation
Neurological signsHemiparesis, seizures common — resolve with treatment

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the diagnostic criteria for HHS?Glucose >30 mmol/L, calculated osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, pH >7.30, bicarbonate >18 mmol/L, minimal ketonaemia
How does HHS differ from DKA?HHS: older, T2DM, glucose >30, osmolality >320, pH >7.3, NO significant ketosis, mortality 10–20%. DKA: younger, T1DM, glucose >11.1, pH <7.3, ketones >3, mortality 1–5%.
What is the initial fluid management in HHS?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500–1000ml/hr; total deficit 8–12L over 24–48h. FLUIDS BEFORE INSULIN.
What insulin dose in HHS?0.05 U/kg/hr (half DKA dose); start AFTER 1–2h of fluid resuscitation.
What is the target osmolality decline rate?<3 mOsm/kg/hr — rapid correction causes cerebral oedema.
How do you calculate osmolality?2×Na+ + Glucose + Urea (all in mmol/L). Corrected Na+ = Measured Na+ + 1.6×(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
What are mortality predictors in HHS?Age >70, coma/GCS<12, systolic BP <90, osmolality >350, significant comorbidities, delayed presentation >24h.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Insulin before fluids in HHS?NO — fluids alone drop glucose via dilution and improved renal perfusion; insulin delayed 1–2h
Same insulin dose as DKA?NO — half dose (0.05 vs 0.1 U/kg/hr); HHS more insulin sensitive
Cerebral oedema only in DKA?Also in HHS if osmolality corrected too fast — same <3 mOsm/kg/hr rule

Mnemonic: HHS-FLUIDS-FIRST

  • Hyperglycaemia >30 mmol/L
  • Hyperosmolality >320 mOsm/kg
  • Serum pH >7.3 (NO acidosis)
  • Fluids FIRST (1L/hr, then 500–1000)
  • Low insulin (0.05 U/kg/hr, delayed)
  • Urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr
  • Insulin after 1-2h fluids
  • Decline osmolality <3 mOsm/kg/hr
  • Stop insulin at glucose 10-15
  • Fatal if missed (10-20% mortality)
  • Infection screen/treat
  • Resolve neuro signs with treatment
  • Switch to SC when stable

Local Navigation

  • Parent Heading: [[Diabetic Emergencies/Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS)|Diabetic Emergencies/Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS)]]

  • Chapter MOC: [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]

  • **Related": [[]]

Diabetes in CKD and dialysis

[!info]
Topic-Group hub for Diabetes in CKD and dialysis — covers 3 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[HbA1c in CKD]]
  • [[Drug dosing in CKD]]
  • [[Diabetes in Special Populations/Diabetes in CKD and dialysis/Diabetes on dialysis.md|Diabetes on dialysis]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
HbA1c in CKDFalsely LOW in CKD/ESRD (↓RBC lifespan, ESA use) · Use fructosamine (2-3wk avg) or glycated albumin (3wk) · Target HbA1c may be misleading
Drug Dosing (eGFR)Metformin: ≥45 full, 30-45 half, <30 contraindicated · SGLT2i: initiate ≥45/30, continue ≥20/15 · GLP-1 RA: no adjustment · DPP-4i: adjust (linagliptin no adjust) · SU: avoid glibenclamide, gliclazide OK · Insulin: ↓25-50%
DialysisGlucose-free dialysate → hypoglycaemia risk · Insulin requirements variable (pre/post dialysis) · Monitor pre/post dialysis glucose · Peritoneal dialysis: glucose absorption from dialysate

Quick Links

  • Parent Heading: [[../Diabetes in Special Populations|Diabetes in Special Populations]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]
  • Chapter Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
  • Topic Template: [[../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
  • Drug Reference: [[../../Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing|Drugs]]

▸ 21.5.5 Hhs Management Protocol

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Execute HHS management protocol (fluids priority, low-dose insulin)
  • [ ] Monitor osmolality decline rate (<3 mOsm/kg/hr)
  • [ ] Manage electrolytes (K+, Na+) and fluid balance
  • [ ] Recognise and manage complications (cerebral oedema, rhabdomyolysis)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DiagnosisGlucose >30 mmol/L + Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg + pH >7.3 + Bicarbonate >18 + Minimal ketones
Mortality10-20% (vs 1-5% DKA) - higher in elderly, comorbidities
Key PrincipleFLUIDS FIRST - insulin delayed 1-2h after fluid resuscitation

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(See HHS diagnosis criteria)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

(See HHS diagnosis criteria)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationFrequencyKey Details
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2-4 mmol/L/hr
Calculated osmolalityHourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr (2Na+Glucose+Urea)
Corrected Na+2-hourlyMeasured Na+ + 1.6(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
K+1-2 hourly4.0-5.0 mmol/L target
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected

6. Differential Diagnosis

(See HHS vs DKA differentiation)

7. Management

Fluid Resuscitation (PRIORITY)

PhaseFluidRateKey Points
1st hour0.9% NaCl1LRapid restoration of perfusion
Hours 2-60.9% NaCl500-1000ml/hrAdjust to correct deficit; HHS deficit 8-12L (150-200ml/kg)
If corrected Na+ rising >100.45% NaClPer protocolPrevent rapid osmolar fall
If glucose <145% Dextrose + 0.45% NaClMatch insulinPrevent hypoglycaemia
Goal: Correct deficit over 24-48h; osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr

Insulin Therapy

ParameterHHS (vs DKA)
TimingDelay 1-2h after fluids started -- fluids alone drop glucose
Dose0.05 U/kg/hr (HALF of DKA dose) -- lower insulin sensitivity
Bolus?NO
TargetGlucose decline 2-4 mmol/L/hr; stop when glucose 10-15 mmol/L
PotassiumSame as DKA -- replace per level

Monitoring

ParameterFrequencyTarget
Osmolality (calculated)HourlyDecline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
GlucoseHourlyDecline 2-4 mmol/L/hr
Na+ (corrected)2-hourlyAvoid rapid correction
K+1-2 hourly4.0-5.0 mmol/L
Urine outputHourly>0.5 ml/kg/hr
Neurology (GCS)HourlyImprovement expected
Fluid balanceHourlyPositive then neutral
⬇ Clinical Pathway
HHS Suspected
↓
Labs: Glucose, Osmolality, VBG, Ketones, U&E
↓
pH >7.3 AND Osmolality >320?
↓
HHS Diagnosis
DKA or Mixed
↓
0.9% NaCl 1L/hr x1-2h
↓
Delay insulin 1-2h
↓
FRII 0.05U/kg/hr
↓
Monitor osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
↓
K+ management per DKA protocol
↓
Glucose 10-15 -> switch to SC insulin

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Fluids FIRST1L 0.9% NaCl/hr before insulin; deficit 8-12L over 24-48h
Insulin HALF dose0.05 U/kg/hr (vs 0.1 in DKA); delay 1-2h
Osmolality decline<3 mOsm/kg/hr -- prevent cerebral oedema
Mortality predictorsAge >70, coma, hypotension, osmolality >350, comorbidities, delayed presentation
Neurological signsHemiparesis, seizures common -- resolve with treatment

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the initial fluid management in HHS?0.9% NaCl 1L in 1st hour, then 500-1000ml/hr; total deficit 8-12L over 24-48h. FLUIDS BEFORE INSULIN.
What insulin dose in HHS?0.05 U/kg/hr (half DKA dose); start AFTER 1-2h of fluid resuscitation.
What is the target osmolality decline rate?<3 mOsm/kg/hr -- rapid correction causes cerebral oedema.
How do you calculate osmolality?2xNa+ + Glucose + Urea (all in mmol/L). Corrected Na+ = Measured Na+ + 1.6(Glucose-5.5)/5.5
What are mortality predictors in HHS?Age >70, coma/GCS<12, systolic BP <90, osmolality >350, significant comorbidities, delayed presentation >24h.
How does HHS differ from DKA management?Fluids priority (insulin delayed 1-2h); half insulin dose (0.05 vs 0.1 U/kg/hr); osmolality monitoring; higher mortality.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Insulin before fluids in HHS?NO - fluids alone drop glucose via dilution and improved renal perfusion; insulin delayed 1-2h
Same insulin dose as DKA?NO - half dose (0.05 vs 0.1 U/kg/hr); HHS more insulin sensitive
Cerebral oedema only in DKA?Also in HHS if osmolality corrected too fast -- same <3 mOsm/kg/hr rule

Mnemonic: HHS-FLUIDS-FIRST

  • Hyperglycaemia >30 mmol/L
  • Hyperosmolality >320 mOsm/kg
  • Serum pH >7.3 (NO acidosis)
  • Fluids FIRST (1L/hr, then 500-1000)
  • Low insulin (0.05 U/kg/hr, delayed)
  • Urine output >0.5 ml/kg/hr
  • Insulin after 1-2h fluids
  • Decline osmolality <3 mOsm/kg/hr
  • Stop insulin at glucose 10-15
  • Fatal if missed (10-20% mortality)
  • Infection screen/treat
  • Resolve neuro signs with treatment
  • Switch to SC when stable

11. Mind Map

HHS Management
Fluids
1L/hr x1-2h
500-1000ml/hr
Deficit 8-12L
Correct over 24-48h
Insulin
HALF dose: 0.05 U/kg/hr
Delayed 1-2h after fluids
Stop at glucose 10-15
Monitoring
Osmolality <3 mOsm/kg/hr decline
Glucose 2-4 mmol/L/hr decline
K+ 1-2 hourly
Neuro hourly
Mortality
10-20%
Age>70, coma, hypotension

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGlucose >30, Osmolality >320, pH >7.3, min ketones; fluid deficit 8-12L
**Key Test"Calculated osmolality; glucose hourly; corrected Na+
**Classification"HHS vs DKA: pH>7.3, no acidosis, osmolality>320
**Acute Mgmt"FLUIDS FIRST (1L/hr x1-2h); insulin 0.05U/kg/hr delayed 1-2h
**Chronic Mgmt"Switch to SC insulin at glucose 10-15; treat precipitant
**Key Score"Osmolality decline <3 mOsm/kg/hr
**Complications"Cerebral oedema, rhabdomyolysis, AKI, DVT, mortality 10-20%
**Prognosis"Resolves with fluids; neuro signs reversible; address precipitant

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.5.6 Glucagon Im In Auto Injector

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State glucagon indications, dosing, and routes
  • [ ] Choose appropriate formulation (IM, IN, auto-injector)
  • [ ] Recognise contraindications (starvation, alcohol, adrenal insufficiency)
  • [ ] Manage glucagon failure

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
IndicationSevere hypoglycaemia (unconscious/seizure/unable to swallow) when IV access unavailable
MechanismStimulates hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis -> increases blood glucose
RequirementRequires hepatic glycogen stores -- ineffective if depleted

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Glucagon Formulations

FormulationDoseRouteOnsetDurationKey Features
GlucaGen HypoKit1mg (adult), 0.5mg (<25kg)IM/SC10-15min60-90minReconstitute powder; standard UK
Baqsimi3mgIntranasal10-15min60-90minNeedle-free; device-based; single use
Gvoke HypoPen/Pre-filled syringe0.5mg (paed), 1mg (adult)SC (auto-injector/syringe)10-15min60-90minReady-to-use liquid; stable at room temp
Zegalogue (dasiglucagon)0.6mgSC (auto-injector/syringe)10min60-90minReady-to-use; stable; no reconstitution

Dosing by Age/Weight

PopulationIM/SC (GlucaGen)Intranasal (Baqsimi)Auto-injector (Gvoke)
Adult1mg3mg1mg
Child <25kg / <6-8y0.5mg3mg (age>=4)0.5mg

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRole
Blood glucoseConfirm hypo before/after; recheck 15min post-glucagon
KetonesIf DKA suspected -- glucagon contraindicated (requires insulin)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ScenarioGlucagon Effective?
Insulin/SU hypoglycaemiaYES (glycogen present)
Starvation/prolonged fastingNO - glycogen depleted
Alcohol-induced hypoglycaemiaNO - glycogen depleted, blocks gluconeogenesis
Adrenal insufficiencyNO - impaired counter-regulation
Post-bariatric hypoglycaemiaVariable (may have glycogen)

7. Management

Acute Administration Protocol

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Severe Hypoglycaemia
↓
IV Access?
↓
IV Dextrose 75-80ml 20% (or 150ml 10%) bolus -> 10% infusion
Conscious?
↓
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult) or Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
↓
Recheck glucose 15min
↓
Glucose >3.9?
↓
IV access? -> dextrose; or 2nd glucagon NOT recommended
Complex carb snack if meal >1h

Key Administration Points

StepAction
1Check glucose (if possible) -- but DON'T delay if clinical hypo
2Choose formulation: IM/SC (GlucaGen), IN (Baqsimi), Auto (Gvoke)
3Administer per device instructions
4Position recovery position (aspiration risk if vomiting)
5Recheck glucose at 15min
6If no response in 15min -> IV dextrose (do NOT repeat glucagon)
7Once glucose >3.9 -> complex carbs if next meal >1h

Post-Glucagon Care

ActionDetail
Complex carbsOnce conscious and glucose >3.9; snack if meal >1h away
MonitoringGlucose q15-30min for 1-2h
Investigate causeReview insulin/SU doses, meal timing, alcohol, exercise
EducationPatient/carer training on glucagon use; expiry dates

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
IndicationSevere hypo (unconscious/seizure/unable to swallow) + NO IV access
Dose adult1mg IM/SC (GlucaGen); 3mg IN (Baqsimi); 1mg auto-injector (Gvoke)
Dose child0.5mg <25kg/age<8y; Baqsimi 3mg (age>=4)
Onset/Duration10-15min onset; 60-90min duration
ContraindicationsStarvation, alcohol, adrenal insufficiency -- NO glycogen -> NO effect
FailureIf no response 15min -> IV dextrose; do NOT repeat glucagon (glycogen depleted)
Post-glucagonRecheck 15min; complex carbs if glucose>3.9; monitor 1-2h

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the dose of glucagon for severe hypoglycaemia in an adult?1mg IM/SC (GlucaGen); 3mg intranasal (Baqsimi); 1mg SC auto-injector (Gvoke)
What is the dose for a child <25kg?0.5mg IM/SC (GlucaGen); 3mg IN (Baqsimi, age>=4); 0.5mg auto-injector (Gvoke)
When is glucagon CONTRAINDICATED?Starvation/prolonged fasting, alcohol-induced hypoglycaemia, adrenal insufficiency -- NO hepatic glycogen stores
What if glucagon fails to raise glucose in 15 minutes?Give IV dextrose (75-80ml 20% or 150ml 10%); do NOT repeat glucagon
How long does glucagon effect last?60-90 minutes -- complex carbs needed if next meal >1h away
What are the available glucagon formulations?GlucaGen HypoKit (IM/SC, 1mg), Baqsimi (intranasal, 3mg), Gvoke (auto-injector/syringe, 0.5/1mg), Zegalogue (dasiglucagon, 0.6mg)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Glucagon works in starvation?NO -- requires hepatic glycogen; use IV dextrose instead
Repeat glucagon if no response?NO -- depletes glycogen; if no response 15min -> IV dextrose
Glucagon in DKA?NO -- DKA has hyperglycaemia; glucagon would worsen it; insulin needed

Mnemonic: GLUCAGON-1MG

  • Glucagon: 1mg IM/SC adult
  • Licenced: IM, IN, Auto-injector
  • Under 25kg: 0.5mg
  • Contraindicated: starvation, alcohol, adrenal insufficiency
  • Administration: IM/SC/IN/Auto; 10-15min onset
  • Glucose check: 15min post-dose
  • Again? NO repeat -- if fail -> IV dextrose
  • Once glucose>3.9: complex carbs if meal>1h
  • Nasal Baqsimi 3mg (age>=4)
  • 1mg adult / 0.5mg <25kg
  • Must have glycogen (fails in starvation/alcohol)
  • Gvoke auto-injector ready-to-use

11. Mind Map

Glucagon
Indications
Severe hypo
No IV access
Unconscious/seizure
Formulations
GlucaGen 1mg IM/SC
Baqsimi 3mg IN
Gvoke 1mg Auto
Zegalogue 0.6mg
Dosing
Adult: 1mg
<25kg: 0.5mg
Contraindicated
Starvation
Alcohol
Adrenal insufficiency
Failure
No response 15min
-> IV dextrose
NO repeat glucagon

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionHepatic glycogenolysis stimulator for severe hypoglycaemia without IV access
**Key Test"Glucose at 15min post-dose; must have glycogen stores
**Classification"IM/SC (GlucaGen), IN (Baqsimi), Auto (Gvoke/Zegalogue)
**Acute Mgmt"1mg IM/SC adult; 3mg IN; 0.5mg <25kg; recheck 15min
**Chronic Mgmt"Education on use; expiry dates; carer training
**Key Score"1mg adult / 0.5mg child; onset 10-15min; duration 60-90min
**Complications"Nausea, vomiting; inefficacy if glycogen depleted
**Prognosis"Effective if glycogen present; life-saving in severe hypo

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Definition & Epidemiology
Classification & Staging
Diagnosis & Investigations
Management (Acute)
Management (Chronic)
Complications
Viva Questions
DDx Distinctions
Mnemonics/Algorithms

  • **Chapter Map": [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Tags

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #fcps #mrcp #full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.5.7 3

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify hypoglycaemia (Level 1/2/3 per ADA/IHSG)
  • [ ] Manage hypoglycaemia in conscious and unconscious patients
  • [ ] Dose glucagon (IM, IN, auto-injector)
  • [ ] Recognise hypoglycaemia unawareness and manage
  • [ ] Prevent recurrent hypoglycaemia

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionPlasma glucose <3.9 mmol/L (70 mg/dL) — threshold for neuroendocrine response
Classification (ADA/IHSG 2017)Level 1: <3.9 mmol/L (alert value); Level 2: <3.0 mmol/L (clinically significant); Level 3: Severe (requires assistance)
IncidenceT1DM: 2–3 severe episodes/patient-year; T2DM on insulin: 1–2; T2DM on SU: 0.5–1
Mortality~4–10% of T1DM deaths; "dead in bed" syndrome (nocturnal hypo + arrhythmia)
Risk FactorsInsulin/SU use, CKD, elderly, hypoglycaemia unawareness, tight control, alcohol, exercise, erratic meals

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

LevelGlucoseSymptomsManagement
Level 1 (Alert)<3.9 mmol/LAutonomic: sweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger, anxietyOral carbs 15–20g; recheck 15min
Level 2 (Clinical)<3.0 mmol/LNeuroglycopenic: confusion, drowsiness, speech difficulty, ataxia, visual disturbanceOral carbs if able; glucagon if not
Level 3 (Severe)Any (requires help)Unconscious, seizure, coma — needs 3rd party assistanceGlucagon IM/IN + IV dextrose; emergency services

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SystemCategoriesKey Features
ADA/IHSGLevel 1: <3.9 mmol/LAutonomic symptoms; self-treat
Level 2: <3.0 mmol/LNeuroglycopenic; impaired judgement
Level 3: SevereRequires assistance; unconscious/seizure
Counter-regulationNormalGlucagon ↑, epinephrine ↑, cortisol/GH ↑
Impaired (T1DM)Glucagon response lost first, then epinephrine blunted → unawareness
UnawarenessAbsent autonomic symptomsSudden neuroglycopenia; risk of severe hypo 6x higher

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
Capillary/venous glucoseConfirm hypo<3.9 = Level 1; <3.0 = Level 2
CGMDetect nocturnal/unrecognisedTIR/TBR metrics; alerts for <3.9
Whipple's triadDiagnosticSymptoms + low glucose + relief with glucose
Insulin/C-peptideFactitious hypoglycaemiaExogenous insulin → high insulin, low C-peptide; insulinoma → high both

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Factitious hypoglycaemiaSurreptitious insulin/SU use; high insulin, low C-peptide; surreptitious access
InsulinomaFasting hypoglycaemia; high insulin + high C-peptide + high proinsulin; CT/MRI pancreas
Adrenal insufficiencyHypoglycaemia + hypotension + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia; cortisol low
Post-bariatric hypoglycaemiaLate dumping (1–3h post-meal); nesidioblastosis; GLP-1 mediated
Non-islet cell tumour hypoglycaemia (NICTH)Large tumours; IGF-2 mediated; low insulin, low C-peptide, low IGF-1

7. Management

Acute Management

ScenarioInterventionDose / Details
Conscious, able to swallowOral carbohydrate15–20g fast-acting carbs (glucose tablets, 150ml juice, 5–6 jelly babies); recheck 15min; repeat if <3.9
Conscious, unable/unwilling to swallowGlucagon1mg IM/SC (adult); 0.5mg <25kg child; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi); Auto-injector 0.5mg/1mg (Gvoke)
Unconscious / seizureIV dextrose + Glucagon75–80ml 20% dextrose (or 150ml 10%) IV bolus; then 10% dextrose infusion; Glucagon 1mg IM if no IV access
Hospital insulin errorSame as above + root cause analysisInsulin passport; double-check protocol; CGM in hospital

Glucagon Details

FormulationDoseRouteOnsetDuration
GlucaGen HypoKit1mg (adult), 0.5mg (<25kg)IM/SC10–15min60–90min
Baqsimi3mgIntranasal10–15min60–90min
Gvoke (auto-injector)0.5mg/1mgSC10–15min60–90min
Zegalogue (dasiglucagon)0.6mgSC/auto-injector10minReady-to-use, stable at room temp

Prevention of Recurrent Hypoglycaemia

StrategyDetails
Hypoglycaemia unawareness reversalAvoid <3.9 for 2–3 weeks → restores autonomic symptoms; higher targets (HbA1c 58–64), CGM alerts, CSII
Medication adjustmentReduce/stop SU; switch to GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i/DPP-4i; insulin dose reduction; basal-bolus → CSII/AID
EducationCarb counting, sick day rules, alcohol + food, exercise adjustments
TechnologyCGM with predictive alerts; AID systems (reduce hypo by 30–50%)
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Hypoglycaemia Suspected
↓
Glucose <3.9?
↓
Not hypoglycaemia
Conscious & able to swallow?
↓
15-20g fast carbs → recheck 15min
IV access?
↓
Glucose >3.9 at 15min?
75-80ml 20% dextrose IV → 10% dextrose infusion
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC or Nasal 3mg
↓
Complex carb snack if next meal >1h
Monitor glucose q15-30min

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ClassificationLevel 1: <3.9; Level 2: <3.0; Level 3: severe (needs help)
Conscious treatment15–20g fast carbs → recheck 15min → repeat if needed
Unconscious/no IVGlucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg
Unconscious + IV75–80ml 20% dextrose IV bolus → 10% dextrose infusion
Glucagon typesIM/SC (GlucaGen), IN (Baqsimi), Auto-injector (Gvoke), Dasiglucagon (Zegalogue)
Unawareness reversalAvoid <3.9 for 2–3 weeks → restores glucagon/epinephrine response
Hospital preventionInsulin passport, double-check, CGM, reduce sliding scale

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do you classify hypoglycaemia?Level 1: <3.9 mmol/L (alert); Level 2: <3.0 mmol/L (clinically significant); Level 3: Severe — requires assistance (unconscious/seizure)
Management of conscious hypoglycaemia?15–20g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, 150ml juice, jelly babies) → recheck 15min → repeat if <3.9 → complex carb snack if meal >1h away
Management of unconscious hypoglycaemia?IV access: 75–80ml 20% dextrose (or 150ml 10%) IV bolus → 10% dextrose infusion. No IV: Glucagon 1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; or Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi)
Glucagon dose and routes?1mg IM/SC (adult), 0.5mg <25kg; Nasal 3mg (Baqsimi); Auto-injector 0.5/1mg (Gvoke); Dasiglucagon 0.6mg SC (Zegalogue)
What is hypoglycaemia unawareness?Absent autonomic symptoms due to blunted epinephrine response (glucagon already lost in T1DM); sudden neuroglycopenia; 6x risk severe hypo
How do you reverse hypoglycaemia unawareness?Strict avoidance of glucose <3.9 mmol/L for 2–3 weeks → restores autonomic symptoms; use higher targets, CGM alerts, CSII/AID
What is the "dead in bed" syndrome?Nocturnal hypoglycaemia → cardiac arrhythmia (QT prolongation) → sudden death in young T1DM; prevention: CGM nocturnal alerts, avoid tight control at bedtime

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Glucagon in starvation/alcoholINEFFECTIVE — requires hepatic glycogen stores; use IV dextrose
Glucagon repeat doseNOT recommended — depletes glycogen; if no response in 15min → IV dextrose
Level 3 definition"Requires assistance" — NOT defined by specific glucose value

Mnemonic: HYPO-15

  • Hypoglycaemia: <3.9 mmol/L
  • You check glucose
  • Patient conscious? → 15–20g carbs
  • Oral carbs: glucose tabs, juice, jelly babies
  • 15min recheck
  • 5 → if still low, repeat 15g
  • Unconscious: No IV → Glucagon 1mg IM/IN; IV → 20% dextrose 75ml
  • Unawareness: avoid <3.9 ×2-3 weeks → restores symptoms
  • Glucagon: needs glycogen (fails in starvation/alcohol)

  • Parent Heading: [[Diabetic Emergencies/Severe hypoglycaemia|Diabetic Emergencies/Severe hypoglycaemia]]

Diabetes in HIV

[!info]
Topic-Group hub for Diabetes in HIV — covers 2 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[ART metabolic syndrome]]
  • [[Diabetes in Special Populations/Diabetes in HIV/Insulin resistance in HIV.md|Insulin resistance in HIV]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
ART Metabolic SyndromeProtease inhibitors (lopinavir, atazanavir) → insulin resistance, dyslipidaemia, lipodystrophy · Integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir, bictegravir) → weight gain, ↑diabetes risk
Insulin ResistanceChronic inflammation, mitochondrial toxicity, visceral adiposity, direct β-cell toxicity (older NRTIs)
ScreeningADA: Annual FPG/HbA1c in HIV on ART · Higher risk with PI, age >40, BMI >25, FH, HCV co-infection
ManagementPrefer integrase inhibitors (less metabolic) · Metformin 1st line · SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for CV/renal benefit · Monitor weight, lipids, glucose

  • Parent Heading: [[../Diabetes in Special Populations|Diabetes in Special Populations]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #topic-group-hub #fcps #mrcp

Topic Overview

Diabetes in HIV
Disease-Level Topics
High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)
Quick Links
Tags
▸ 21.5.8 Insulin Error
[!info]
Inpatient hypoglycaemia = 10–20% of diabetes admissions; insulin errors = leading preventable cause (wrong dose, wrong insulin, wrong timing, sliding scale misuse); protocolised regimens + capillary glucose monitoring + staff education reduce events.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define inpatient hypoglycaemia thresholds
  • [ ] Identify common insulin prescribing/administration errors
  • [ ] Apply prevention strategies (protocols, prescriptions, monitoring)
  • [ ] Manage inpatient hypoglycaemia per severity
  • [ ] Report and review incidents (learning culture)

2. Definitions (ADA / NICE / JBDS)

CategoryCapillary / Venous GlucoseClinical Context
Level 1 (Alert)3.9–3.0 mmol/L (70–54 mg/dL)Asymptomatic or mild symptoms; intervention needed
Level 2 (Clinically Significant)<3.0 mmol/L (<54 mg/dL)Neuroglycopenic symptoms; urgent treatment
Level 3 (Severe)<2.0 mmol/L (<40 mg/dL) OR requires assistanceAltered consciousness, seizure, coma; medical emergency

Inpatient target: Avoid <4.0 mmol/L; treat at 3.9 mmol/L.

3. Common Insulin Errors in Hospital

Error TypeExamplesPrevention
Wrong dose10 units instead of 1; 40 units instead of 4; decimal pointUnit-specific syringes; double-check; no "U" abbreviation (write "units")
Wrong insulinRapid given instead of basal; basal instead of rapid; concentrated (U200/U300) ≠ U100Colour-coded labels; tall-man lettering (HumUlin vs HumAlog); separate storage
Wrong timingRapid insulin given without meal; basal given at wrong time; pre-meal given post-mealLink insulin to meals on chart; pre-meal = 15–20 min before; meal tray verification
Sliding scale misuseSliding scale AS ONLY REGIMEN (no basal); excessive correction doses; no meal bolusBasal-bolus mandatory; sliding scale supplemental only; max correction dose cap
Omitted doseBasal insulin held for "low glucose" (e.g., 5.5) without reviewNever omit basal without senior review; reduce by 20–50% instead
Duplicate doseNurse + patient both give; shift handover failureElectronic MAR; double-sign for insulin; patient self-administration policy
Renal adjustment failureFull insulin dose in eGFR <30 → prolonged hypoglycaemiaDose reduce per eGFR; pharmacy review
Concentrated insulin errorU200/U300/U500 given as U100 volumeSeparate prescribing; dedicated syringes; volume check

4. High-Risk Clinical Scenarios

ScenarioSpecific Risks
NPO / Fasting (pre-op, procedures)Baseline insulin still needed; reduce basal 20–50%; dextrose infusion if prolonged
Renal impairment (AKI, CKD G4–5)Insulin clearance ↓ → reduce dose 30–50%; monitor q4–6h
Corticosteroids (dexamethasone)Hyperglycaemia day 1–2 → ↑ insulin; then hypoglycaemia as steroid wears off
Enteral/Parenteral NutritionFeed interruption → hypoglycaemia; insulin matched to feed rate
Elderly / Frail / DementiaErratic intake, polypharmacy, renal impairment, autonomic neuropathy
Post-bariatric surgeryAltered anatomy, rapid gastric emptying, dumping → unpredictable glucose
Critical illness (ICU)Stress hyperglycaemia → insulin infusion; target 6–10 mmol/L (NICE-SUGAR)

5. Prevention Strategies (JBDS / NICE)

Prescribing

  • Basal-bolus regimen for all insulin-requiring inpatients (sliding scale supplemental only)
  • Pre-printed / electronic order sets with dose calculators
  • No "U" abbreviation — write "units"
  • Concentrated insulins (U200, U300, U500) — separate prescribing pathway
  • Renal dosing guidelines on chart (eGFR-based)

Administration

  • Double-check by two nurses for insulin (or barcode scanning)
  • Meal-coordinated rapid insulin (pre-meal 15–20 min)
  • Capillary glucose before every insulin dose (unless continuous infusion)
  • Patient self-administration if competent (assessed, documented)

Monitoring

  • Capillary glucose pre-meals, bedtime, 3am (if on insulin)
  • Target 5–10 mmol/L (individualised; tighter in critical care)
  • Hypo alert at 3.9 — inform doctor, review insulin
  • Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) emerging for inpatients

Systems

  • Hypoglycaemia protocol on every ward (IV dextrose, glucagon, escalation)
  • Mandatory reporting of Level 2/3 events (Datix/Safety system)
  • Monthly audit of hypoglycaemia rates, insulin errors, harm
  • Staff education (induction + annual) — insulin safety, device use

6. Inpatient Hypoglycaemia Treatment

SeverityConscious?Treatment
Level 1 (3.0–3.9)Yes15–20g fast-acting carb (glucose gel, juice, 4–5 dextrose tabs); recheck 15 min
Level 2 (<3.0)Yes20g fast-acting carb + complex carb; recheck 15 min; review insulin
Level 3 (<2.0 or needs help)No / ConfusedIV 20% dextrose 100mL (or 10% 200mL); IM glucagon 1mg if no IV access; recheck 10 min
Seizure/ComaNoIV 20% dextrose 100mL + glucagon 1mg IM; protect airway; senior review; stop insulin infusion

Post-treatment: Observe 30–60 min; recheck glucose; identify cause; adjust regimen.

7. Reporting & Learning

StepAction
ImmediateTreat patient; ensure safety; document on chart
Within 24hReport via incident system (Datix); classify harm (none/low/moderate/severe/death)
Within 72hRoot cause analysis for Level 3 or recurrent Level 2
MonthlyWard/unit report: rates, themes, actions
QuarterlyHospital-wide review; share learning; update protocols

Just culture — focus on system failures, not individual blame.

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Inpatient hypo definitionLevel 1: 3.0–3.9; Level 2: <3.0; Level 3: <2.0 or needs help
Most common insulin errorWrong dose (especially 10x), wrong insulin, wrong timing
Sliding scaleSupplemental only — never sole regimen
Never omit basalReduce 20–50% if concerned; never hold without review
NPO patientBasal insulin still needed (reduce 20–50%); dextrose if prolonged
Renal impairmentReduce insulin 30–50% (eGFR <30); monitor q4–6h
Concentrated insulinU200/U300/U500 — separate prescribing, dedicated syringes
Level 3 treatmentIV 20% dextrose 100mL + glucagon 1mg IM
ReportingAll Level 2/3 mandatory; root cause for Level 3
Target inpatient glucose5–10 mmol/L (avoid <4.0)

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Severe hypoglycaemia|Severe hypoglycaemia]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Severe hypoglycaemia]], [[Insulin therapy in type 2 diabetes]], [[Diabetes in the elderly]], [[Diabetes in CKD and dialysis]]

3. Common Insulin Errors in Hospital
4. High-Risk Clinical Scenarios

Prescribing
Administration
Monitoring
Systems

Diabetes in pregnancy

[!info]
Topic-Group hub for Diabetes in pregnancy — covers 5 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[Gestational diabetes (GDM)]]
  • [[Pre-existing diabetes]]
  • [[GDM screening]]
  • [[GDM management]]
  • [[Postpartum follow-up]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
GDM ScreeningIADPSG: Universal 75g OGTT 24-28wk (FBG≥5.1, 1h≥10.0, 2h≥8.5) · NICE: Risk factors → OGTT · ADA: Universal or risk-based
GDM TargetsFBG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4 mmol/L · HbA1c not recommended for monitoring
GDM ManagementDiet 1st → Metformin (if FBG >5.5 or 1h >7.0) → Insulin · Glyburide alternative
Pre-existing DMPre-conception HbA1c <48mmol/mol (6.5%) · Folic acid 5mg OD to 12wk · Stop ACEi/ARB/statin/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
PostpartumOGTT 6-13 weeks · Annual HbA1c · Lifetime T2DM risk 50%

▸ 21.6 Microvascular Complications 20
▸ 21.6.10 Diabetic Nephropathy Vs Other Proteinuric Diseases

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Differentiate DKD from other causes of proteinuria in diabetes
  • [ ] Recognise red flags for non-diabetic kidney disease
  • [ ] Select appropriate investigations (immunology, complement, biopsy)
  • [ ] Apply biopsy indications in diabetes

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Clinical dilemma~30% of proteinuric diabetes have non-DKD component
Biopsy yieldNon-diabetic pathology in 20–30% of biopsied diabetics with proteinuria
Key principleNot all proteinuria in diabetes = DKD

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Red Flag for Non-DKDSignificance
Rapid eGFR decline>5 mL/min/1.73m²/year
Active urinary sedimentRBC >10/µL, RBC casts, WBC casts
No diabetic retinopathyEspecially if DM >10y or A3 proteinuria
Systemic symptomsFever, rash, arthralgia, haemoptysis, neuropathy
Low BMI at diagnosisType 2 DM usually overweight
Family historyHereditary kidney disease (Alport, ADPKD, Fabry)
Short DM duration<5y with heavy proteinuria

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Common Non-DKD Proteinuric Diseases in Diabetes

DiseaseTypical ACRKey FeaturesInvestigations
Hypertensive nephrosclerosisA1–A2Long-standing HTN; no retinopathy; bland sedimentClinical; exclude DKD
Chronic GN (IgA, membranous, FSGS)A2–A3Haematuria, RBC casts; may have low complementAutoantibodies, complement, biopsy
AmyloidosisA3 (nephrotic)Nephrotic syndrome; systemic (cardiac, neuropathy, macroglossia)Congo red +ve; FLC, SAP scan
Multiple myelomaA3 (Bence Jones)CRAB features; Bence Jones proteinuriaUrine protein electrophoresis; FLC, bone marrow
Diabetic AINVariableDrug-induced (NSAIDs, PPI, antibiotics); eosinophiliaDrug history; biopsy
Obstructive uropathyVariableHydronephrosis on US; post-void residualUS bladder/kidneys
Alport syndromeA2→A3FH haematuria/renal failure; hearing loss, ocularGenetic (COL4A5); biopsy
Fabry diseaseA2→A3Angiokeratomas, acroparaesthesia, cornea verticillataα-galactosidase A; genetic (GLA)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Stepwise Workup for Atypical Proteinuria

StepInvestigationPurpose
1Urine microscopyRBC casts = glomerulonephritis; WBC casts = AIN/pyelonephritis
2AutoantibodiesANA, dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM, C3, C4
3Serum FLC + electrophoresisMyeloma, amyloidosis (AL)
4Complement C3/C4Low C3 = post-strep, MPGN, lupus; Low C4 = cryoglobulinaemia
5Viral serologyHBV, HCV, HIV (membranous, MPGN)
6Renal ultrasoundSize, obstruction, cysts (ADPKD)
7Renal biopsyIf diagnosis unclear or immunosuppression considered

Biopsy Indications in Diabetes

IndicationCriteria
Rapid declineeGFR drop >5 mL/min/yr
Active sedimentRBC >10/µL, RBC/WBC casts
No retinopathyDM >10y with A3 proteinuria
Systemic featuresFever, rash, arthralgia, haemoptysis
Short durationDM <5y with heavy proteinuria
Immunosuppression consideredBefore starting steroids/rituximab/etc.

6. Differential Diagnosis Table

FeatureDKDNon-DKD Proteinuria
RetinopathyUsually present (if DM>10y)Often absent
Proteinuria onsetGradual (years)May be acute/subacute
HaematuriaRareCommon (RBC casts = GN)
eGFR declineSlow (2–4 mL/min/yr)Rapid (>5 mL/min/yr)
Systemic symptomsAbsentMay be present
Biopsy (if done)Kimmelstiel-Wilson, GBM thickeningImmune deposits, amyloid, myeloma casts

7. Management

ScenarioAction
Strong DKD featuresContinue DKD management (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, etc.)
Red flags presentComplete workup (above); consider biopsy
Biopsy-proven non-DKDTreat specific disease (immunosuppression for GN, chemo for myeloma, etc.)
Mixed DKD + non-DKDTreat both; DKD management + disease-specific

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Red flagsRapid eGFR decline, active sediment, no retinopathy, systemic symptoms, FH
Urine microscopyRBC casts = GN; WBC casts = AIN/pyelonephritis
Biopsy indicationsRapid decline, active sediment, no retinopathy, systemic features, short DM duration
Common non-DKDHypertensive nephrosclerosis, GN (IgA, membranous), amyloidosis, myeloma
Not all proteinuria in DM = DKD~20–30% have non-diabetic component

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the red flags suggesting non-diabetic kidney disease in a diabetic with proteinuria?Rapid eGFR decline (>5 mL/min/yr), active urinary sediment (RBC casts), absent retinopathy (esp if DM>10y), systemic symptoms, family history, short DM duration with heavy proteinuria
When do you biopsy a diabetic with proteinuria?Rapid decline >5mL/min/yr, active sediment, absent retinopathy with heavy proteinuria, systemic features suggesting systemic disease, DM duration <5y with A3 proteinuria
What does RBC casts in urine indicate?Glomerulonephritis — not DKD (DKD typically has bland sediment)
How does hypertensive nephrosclerosis differ from DKD?Long-standing HTN, bland sediment, no retinopathy, usually A1–A2 proteinuria
What investigation confirms amyloidosis?Congo red staining (apple-green birefringence) on biopsy; serum free light chains, SAP scan

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All proteinuria in DM = DKD?NO — 20–30% have non-diabetic component; always check red flags
No retinopathy = no DKD?If DM >10y and no retinopathy with heavy proteinuria → think non-DKD
Biopsy always needed?NO — only if red flags or immunosuppression considered

Mnemonic: DDx-PROTEINURIA-DM

  • DKD red flags: rapid eGFR decline, active sediment, no retinopathy
  • DM duration: <5y + heavy proteinuria = biopsy
  • x (exclude): non-DKD causes
  • Proteinuria: not all in DM = DKD
  • RBC casts = GN (not DKD)
  • Ophthalmoscopy: no retinopathy + DM>10y = red flag
  • Tubular: WBC casts = AIN/pyelonephritis
  • Electrophoresis: myeloma, amyloidosis
  • Immunology: ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, C3, C4
  • Nephrotic syndrome: consider amyloid, myeloma, membranous
  • Ultrasound: size, cysts, obstruction
  • Renal biopsy: if red flags or immunosuppression considered
  • IgA nephropathy: common GN in DM
  • Amyloidosis: Congo red, FLC
  • Myeloma: Bence Jones, CRAB
  • Diabetic AIN: drugs (NSAIDs, PPI, abx)
  • No retinopathy + proteinuria = think non-DKD

  • Parent Heading: [[Microvascular Complications/Diabetic nephropathy|Microvascular Complications/Diabetic nephropathy]]

  • **Chapter MOC": [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]

  • **Related": [[]]

Tags

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #fcps #mrcp #full-fcps-mrcp-note

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Common Non-DKD Proteinuric Diseases in Diabetes
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
Stepwise Workup for Atypical Proteinuria
Biopsy Indications in Diabetes
6. Differential Diagnosis Table
7. Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics

Tags
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.6.11 Diabetic Nephropathy
[!info]
DKD = leading cause of ESRD (40–50% of incident dialysis); KDIGO G+A staging; natural history: hyperfiltration → normoalbuminuria → microalbuminuria (A2) → macroalbuminuria (A3) → declining GFR → ESRD; SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, finerenone = disease-modifying beyond ACEi/ARB.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe natural history and pathophysiology of DKD
  • [ ] Stage using KDIGO G (GFR) + A (Albuminuria) categories
  • [ ] Screen and monitor (UACR, eGFR)
  • [ ] Apply disease-modifying therapy (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, finerenone)
  • [ ] Differentiate DKD from non-diabetic kidney disease

2. Natural History (Mogensen Model - T1DM; Similar T2DM)

StageGFRAlbuminuriaClinicalDuration
1. Hyperfiltration↑↑ (>120–150)Normal (A1)AsymptomaticDiagnosis – 5y
2. Silent / NormoalbuminuricNormal/HighA1 (<30 mg/g)Asymptomatic5–15y
3. Incipient / MicroalbuminuriaNormalA2 (30–300 mg/g)Earliest detectable10–20y
4. Overt / Macroalbuminuria↓ DecliningA3 (>300 mg/g)Hypertension, oedema15–25y
5. ESRD<15 (G5)VariableUraemia, need RRT20–30y

T2DM: More heterogeneous; normoalbuminuric DKD common (30–50% of DKD); may have retinopathy absent.

3. Pathophysiology

MechanismKey Mediators
HaemodynamicHyperfiltration → glomerular hypertension → afferent/efferent arteriolar dysregulation
MetabolicAGE formation, PKC activation, polyol pathway, hexosamine pathway → oxidative stress
InflammatoryNF-κB, TGF-β, MCP-1, TNF-α → macrophage infiltration, fibrosis
RAAS↑ Ang II → efferent constriction → glomerular hypertension; direct profibrotic
SGLT2-mediatedProximal tubular glucose reabsorption → ↑ distal NaCl delivery → macula densa → afferent dilatation

4. KDIGO Staging (G + A) — Risk Matrix

GFR Categories (G)

GeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)
G1≥90
G260–89
G3a45–59
G3b30–44
G415–29
G5<15

Albuminuria Categories (A)

AACR (mg/mmol)ACR (mg/g)Term
A1<3<30Normal/mildly increased
A23–3030–300Moderately increased (micro)
A3>30>300Severely increased (macro)

Risk Matrix (Heat Map)

A1 (Green)A2 (Yellow)A3 (Orange/Red)
G1, G2LowModerateHigh
G3aModerateHighVery High
G3bHighVery HighVery High
G4, G5Very HighVery HighVery High

5. Screening & Monitoring

TestFrequencyThreshold for Action
UACR (first morning urine)Annual (T1DM from 5y post-dx; T2DM from dx)A2: ACEi/ARB max, SGLT2i; A3: + finerenone, nephrology
eGFR (CKD-EPI)Annual (6m if <60)G3b: adjust meds; G4: nephrology; G5: RRT planning
BPEvery visit<130/80 (KDIGO <120/80)
HbA1c3–6 monthlyIndividualised target

UACR > eGFR for early detection — A2 precedes GFR decline.

6. Disease-Modifying Therapy — The "Big Four"

Drug ClassAgentsKey TrialsIndication in DKD
ACEi / ARBRamipril, losartan, etc.RENAAL, IDNT, MICRO-HOPEFirst-line; max tolerated dose; ↓ intraglomerular pressure
SGLT2iDapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozinCREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEYeGFR ≥20 (dapa/empa); ↓ progression 30–40%; non-diabetic CKD too
GLP-1 RASemaglutide, dulaglutide, liraglutideFLOW, AWARD-7, LEADEReGFR ≥15; ↓ albuminuria, ↓ eGFR decline, CV benefit
FinerenoneFinerenone 10–20mgFIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKDeGFR ≥25, A2/A3; non-steroidal MRA; monitor K+

Additive benefit — each class works via distinct pathways; combination is standard.

7. Management by KDIGO Stage

StageManagement
G1–2, A1Optimise glucose/BP/lipids; annual screen; lifestyle
G1–2, A2ACEi/ARB max dose + SGLT2i (if eGFR ≥20); BP <130/80; GLP-1 RA if T2DM
G1–2, A3Above + finerenone (if eGFR ≥25); nephrology referral
G3a, A2/A3Above + adjust metformin (max 1g BD); avoid NSAIDs; monitor K+/eGFR 3–6m
G3b, A2/A3Above + reduce/stop metformin if <45; SGLT2i still OK (dapa/empa ≥20); nephrology co-care
G4, any ANephrology lead; stop metformin; adjust all renally-cleared drugs; RRT planning; avoid contrast
G5RRT (HD/PD/transplant); palliative option

8. Non-Diabetic Kidney Disease in Diabetes — When to Suspect

ClueSuggests
No retinopathy (especially T1DM)Non-diabetic (e.g., hypertensive nephrosclerosis, GN)
Rapid GFR decline (>5 mL/min/yr)Non-diabetic / superimposed
Active urine sediment (RBC casts, dysmorphic RBC)Glomerulonephritis
Heavy proteinuria (>3g/day) early in courseNephrotic syndrome (MN, FSGS)
Short diabetes duration (<5y T1DM, <10y T2DM) with A3Unlikely DKD
Systemic symptoms (rash, arthralgia, haemoptysis)Vasculitis, SLE, amyloidosis

Renal biopsy if diagnosis uncertain — especially if immunosuppression considered.

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
DKD leading cause ESRD40–50% incident dialysis
Natural history stagesHyperfiltration → microalbuminuria (A2) → macroalbuminuria (A3) → ↓GFR → ESRD
Normoalbuminuric DKD30–50% T2DM; screen with eGFR too
ScreeningUACR + eGFR annual (T1DM from 5y; T2DM from dx)
KDIGO G+A6 GFR categories (G1–5) + 3 Albuminuria (A1–3) = risk matrix
First-lineACEi/ARB max dose (unless K+ >5.5, bilateral RAS)
SGLT2i in DKDeGFR ≥20 (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin); CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY
FinerenoneeGFR ≥25, A2/A3; monitor K+ at 4w
GLP-1 RAeGFR ≥15; FLOW trial renal benefit
No retinopathy + DKDThink non-diabetic kidney disease → biopsy

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Microvascular Complications|Microvascular Complications]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Natural History (Mogensen Model - T1DM; Similar T2DM)
3. Pathophysiology
4. KDIGO Staging (G + A) — Risk Matrix
GFR Categories (G)
Albuminuria Categories (A)
Risk Matrix (Heat Map)
5. Screening & Monitoring
6. Disease-Modifying Therapy — The "Big Four"
7. Management by KDIGO Stage
8. Non-Diabetic Kidney Disease in Diabetes — When to Suspect
9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.6.13 Autonomic Neuropathy Cardiovascular Gi Genitourina

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify autonomic neuropathy: cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, sudomotor
  • [ ] Diagnose cardiovascular autonomic neuropathy (CAN): orthostatic hypotension, HRV, silent MI
  • [ ] Manage gastroparesis: prokinetics, dietary, gastric emptying study
  • [ ] Manage erectile dysfunction / neurogenic bladder
  • [ ] Apply screening tests: Valsalva, tilt table, HRV, gastric emptying

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionAutonomic nerve fibre damage from chronic hyperglycaemia
Prevalence~20–40% diabetes; CAN 20%, gastroparesis 5–12%, ED 30–50% men
Risk FactorsHbA1c, duration, age, DSPN, microvascular complications

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Cardiovascular (CAN)

FeaturePresentation
Orthostatic hypotension↓SBP ≥20 mmHg or ↓DBP ≥10 mmHg within 3min standing; syncope, falls
Resting tachycardia>100 bpm (early); fixed heart rate (late)
Silent myocardial ischaemiaNo angina despite ischaemia (↓afferent cardiac nerves); ↑sudden cardiac death
Exercise intoleranceBlunted HR response; ↓exercise capacity
HRV↓Time/frequency domain measures

Gastrointestinal

FeaturePresentation
GastroparesisNausea, vomiting (undigested food), early satiety, bloating, erratic glucose, weight loss
Diarrhoea/ConstipationAutonomic dysregulation; nocturnal diarrhoea common
Oesophageal dysmotilityDysphagia, reflux

Genitourinary

FeaturePresentation
Erectile dysfunction30–50% men; autonomic + vascular + psychological
Retrograde ejaculationBladder neck incompetence; infertility
Neurogenic bladderAtonic (retention, overflow) or spastic (frequency, urgency, incontinence); recurrent UTIs

Sudomotor

FeaturePresentation
AnhidrosisDistal (feet/legs); ↓thermoregulation
Gustatory sweatingFacial/neck sweating with eating (Frey's syndrome variant)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

CAN Staging (Ewing / CAN Subscore)

StageCriteria
EarlyAbnormal HRV (1+ test)
DefiniteOrthostatic hypotension + abnormal HRV
SevereOrthostatic hypotension + resting tachycardia + silent MI + HRV severely reduced

Gastroparesis Grading (GCSI)

GradeSymptoms
MildNausea, early satiety
ModerateVomiting 1–3x/week, weight loss <5%
SevereDaily vomiting, weight loss >5%, hospitalisation

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

Cardiovascular Autonomic Tests (Ewing Battery)

TestNormalAbnormal
HRV (deep breathing)E:I ratio >1.5≤1.5
Valsalva ratio>1.5≤1.2
30:15 ratio>1.04≤1.0
Orthostatic BP↓SBP <10↓SBP ≥20
Sustained handgripDBP rise >16≤10

Gastroparesis

InvestigationDetails
Gastric emptying scintigraphyGold standard: T½ solids >120min (or >40min liquids)
13C-octanoate breath testAlternative; non-radioactive
Wireless motility capsulepH, pressure, transit time

Genitourinary

InvestigationDetails
IIEF-5ED severity score
UrodynamicsNeurogenic bladder type
Post-void residual>150ml = retention

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Autonomic neuropathy (non-diabetic)Amyloid, autoimmune (paraneoplastic), HIV, alcohol, drugs
Medication effectsβ-blockers (mask tachycardia), diuretics (orthostasis), anticholinergics
Cardiac diseaseHeart failure, arrhythmias (independent of CAN)
GI obstructionMechanical vs gastroparesis (imaging)
Psychogenic EDMorning erections preserved; situational

7. Management

Cardiovascular Autonomic Neuropathy

InterventionDetails
Orthostatic hypotension↑Salt/fluid (2–3L/day), compression stockings, head-up tilt sleep, fludrocortisone 0.1–0.3mg OD, midodrine 2.5–10mg TDS
Silent MI preventionIntensive risk factor control; consider stress testing if high risk
Resting tachycardiaIvabradine if HR >70 on max β-blocker; avoid non-selective β-blockers

Gastroparesis

LineAgentDoseNotes
DietarySmall frequent meals (6–8/day), low fat/fibre, liquid calories—Foundation
1st line prokineticMetoclopramide10mg TDS (max 5 days continuous)D2 antagonist; EPS risk (limit 5d); avoid parkinsonism
Domperidone10mg TDSPeripheral D2; less CNS EPS; cardiac QT risk
Erythromycin250mg TDS (short-term 2–4wk)Motilin agonist; tachyphylaxis; QT
2nd linePrucalopride / TegaserodSpecialist5-HT4 agonists
RefractoryGES (gastric electrical stimulation), pyloroplasty, jejunostomySurgicalSpecialist centres

Erectile Dysfunction

LineIntervention
1stPDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil 50mg, tadalafil 10mg, vardenafil 10mg) — take 30–60min pre-activity
2ndVacuum erection device
3rdIntraurethral alprostadil (MUSE)
4thIntracavernosal alprostadil injection
5thPenile prosthesis

Neurogenic Bladder

TypeManagement
Atonic (retention)Clean intermittent catheterisation (CIC); bethanechol 10–25mg TDS
Spastic (urgency)Anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tolterodine); botulinum toxin A; sacral neuromodulation
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Autonomic Neuropathy
↓
System
↓
Orthostatic HTN: fludrocortisone/midodrine
Gastroparesis: diet → metoclopramide/domperidone/erythromycin
ED: PDE5i → vacuum → alprostadil → prosthesis
Anhidrosis: avoid heat; gustatory sweating: reassure
↓
Silent MI: risk factor control
Bladder: CIC (atonic) / anticholinergics (spastic)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
CANOrthostatic hypotension (↓SBP≥20), resting tachycardia, silent MI, HRV ↓
Orthostatic hypotension RxFludrocortisone 0.1–0.3mg OD, midodrine 2.5–10mg TDS, compression stockings
GastroparesisNausea, vomiting, erratic glucose; gastric emptying T½ >120min; metoclopramide/domperidone/erythromycin
EDPDE5i 1st line; vacuum → alprostadil → prosthesis
Neurogenic bladderCIC for retention; anticholinergics/botox for urgency
ScreeningAnnual: orthostatic BP, HRV (if available), symptom questionnaire

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is cardiovascular autonomic neuropathy (CAN)?Orthostatic hypotension (↓SBP≥20), resting tachycardia >100bpm, silent MI, reduced HRV, exercise intolerance
How do you diagnose orthostatic hypotension?↓SBP ≥20 mmHg or ↓DBP ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing
How do you manage orthostatic hypotension?Non-drug: ↑salt/fluid, compression stockings, head-up tilt; Drug: fludrocortisone 0.1–0.3mg OD, midodrine 2.5–10mg TDS
What is gastroparesis and how do you diagnose it?Delayed gastric emptying without obstruction; symptoms: nausea, vomiting, early satiety, erratic glucose; Gastric emptying scintigraphy: T½ solids >120min
What are the prokinetics for gastroparesis?Metoclopramide 10mg TDS (max 5d, EPS risk), Domperidone 10mg TDS (less EPS, QT risk), Erythromycin 250mg TDS (short-term, tachyphylaxis)
How do you manage erectile dysfunction in diabetes?1st: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil 50mg, tadalafil 10mg); 2nd: vacuum device; 3rd: intraurethral/intracavernosal alprostadil; 4th: penile prosthesis
What is a silent MI in diabetes?Myocardial ischaemia/infarction without typical angina due to cardiac autonomic denervation; ↑sudden cardiac death risk

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
CAN = all autonomic?NO — CAN = cardiovascular only; autonomic includes GI, GU, sudomotor
Metoclopramide long-term?NO — max 5 days continuous (EPS risk); use domperidone for chronic
PDE5i with nitrates?CONTRAINDICATED — severe hypotension

Mnemonic: AUTO-NOMIC

  • Autonomic: CV, GI, GU, Sudomotor
  • Upright: orthostatic hypotension (↓SBP≥20) → fludrocortisone/midodrine
  • Tachycardia: resting >100 (early CAN)
  • Orthostatic: ↓SBP≥20 → midodrine/fludrocortisone
  • Nausea/vomiting: gastroparesis (T½>120min)
  • Oesophageal: dysmotility
  • Medication: metoclopramide (5d max), domperidone, erythromycin
  • Impotence: PDE5i → vacuum → alprostadil → prosthesis
  • Catheterisation: CIC for atonic bladder
  • Neurogenic bladder: anticholinergics for urgency, CIC for retention

  • Parent Heading: [[Microvascular Complications/Diabetic neuropathy|Microvascular Complications/Diabetic neuropathy]]

  • **Chapter MOC": [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]
