#medicine #diabetes #davidson #fcps #mrcp #full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.6.6 Diabetic Foot Ulcer Dfu Classification Wagner Univ

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply Wagner classification (depth-based, grades 0-5)
  • [ ] Apply University of Texas classification (depth x infection x ischaemia)
  • [ ] Understand why UT classification predicts outcomes better
  • [ ] Communicate ulcer stage clearly to MDT

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PurposeStandardise DFU description for prognosis, communication, research
WagnerDepth only (0-5); simple but limited
University of TexasDepth (Grade 0-3) x Stage (A-D infection/ischaemia); better prognostic value
UsageWagner: historical/UK; UT: international/research

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - classification based on examination)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Wagner Ulcer Classification (1981)

GradeDescriptionDepth
0Pre-ulcer (callus, deformity, intact skin)None
1Superficial ulcer (skin/subcutaneous)<=Subcut
2Deep ulcer to tendon, capsule, or boneTendon/capsule/bone
3Deep + abscess or osteomyelitisBone + infection
4Forefoot gangreneGangrene (partial)
5Whole foot gangreneGangrene (complete)

University of Texas (UT) Classification (1996)

Grade (Depth)Stage A (Clean)Stage B (Infected)Stage C (Ischaemic)Stage D (Infected + Ischaemic)
0Pre-ulcer---
1SuperficialInfectedIschaemicInfected + Ischaemic
2Deep to tendon/capsuleInfectedIschaemicInfected + Ischaemic
3Deep to bone/jointInfectedIschaemicInfected + Ischaemic
Infection (Stage B/D): >=2 of: purulence, erythema >2cm, warmth, induration, pain, fever, leukocytosis
Ischaemia (Stage C/D): Absent pulses OR ABI <0.9 OR TBI <0.7

Comparison: Wagner vs UT

AspectWagnerUT
DimensionsDepth onlyDepth + Infection + Ischaemia
Grades0-5Grade 0-3 x Stage A-D (16 combos)
Prognostic valueLimitedSuperior (predicts healing/amp)
Clinical useSimple, historicalPreferred for MDT/research

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRole
Clinical examDepth (probe-to-bone), infection signs, pulses
Probe-to-bonePositive -> osteomyelitis likely (Wagner 3 / UT Grade 3)
ABI / TBIIschaemia assessment (Stage C/D)
X-ray / MRIOsteomyelitis depth (Wagner 2 vs 3 / UT Grade 3)
ESR / CRPInfection monitoring

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Feature
Neuropathic ulcerPlantar, painless, callus rim, warm, pulses present
NeuroischaemicMargins, painful, cool, absent pulses
IschaemicDistal/toes, painful, gangrene, absent pulses
Venous ulcerGaiter area, oedema, lipodermatosclerosis, pulses present

7. Management by Classification

ClassificationTypical Management
Wagner 0 / UT 0Preventive: callus debridement, offloading insoles
Wagner 1 / UT 1AOffloading (TCC), debridement, dressings
Wagner 1 / UT 1BOffloading + oral antibiotics (PEDIS 2)
Wagner 1 / UT 1COffloading + vascular assessment
Wagner 2 / UT 2A/2BOffloading + debridement +/- antibiotics
Wagner 2 / UT 2C/2DOffloading + vascular referral +/- antibiotics
Wagner 3 / UT 3A/3BAntibiotics (IV if PEDIS 3) + MRI for osteomyelitis
Wagner 3 / UT 3C/3DIV antibiotics + vascular referral + MRI
Wagner 4-5 / UT n/aUrgent vascular + surgical (amputation likely)

PEDIS Infection Grading (for UT Stage B/D)

GradeDefinitionTreatment
1UninfectedLocal care
2Mild (superficial, <2cm erythema)Oral antibiotics
3Moderate (deep, >2cm, abscess)IV antibiotics
4Severe (sepsis/SIRS)IV abx + ICU + urgent surgical
⬇ Clinical Pathway
DFU Identified
↓
Assess Depth
↓
Probe-to-bone?
↓
Wagner 3 / UT Grade 3
Depth to tendon/capsule?
↓
Assess Infection
Wagner 2 / UT Grade 2
Wagner 1 / UT Grade 1
↓
Purulence/erythema>2cm?
↓
UT Stage B/D + PEDIS 2-3
UT Stage A/C
↓
Antibiotics: oral (2) / IV (3)
Assess Ischaemia
↓
Absent pulses / ABI<0.9?
↓
UT Stage C/D -> Vascular referral
UT Stage A/B

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Wagner0: pre-ulcer; 1: superficial; 2: deep to tendon; 3: bone/abscess; 4: forefoot gangrene; 5: whole foot gangrene
UTGrade 0-3 (depth) x Stage A-D (A=clean, B=infected, C=ischaemic, D=both)
UT vs WagnerUT superior - incorporates infection & ischaemia; better prognostic value
PEDIS1: uninfected; 2: mild (oral abx); 3: moderate (IV abx); 4: severe (sepsis/surgery)
Probe-to-bonePositive -> Wagner 3 / UT Grade 3; osteomyelitis likely

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the Wagner classification for diabetic foot ulcers?0: pre-ulcer; 1: superficial; 2: deep to tendon/capsule; 3: bone/abscess; 4: forefoot gangrene; 5: whole foot gangrene
What is the University of Texas classification?Grade 0-3 (depth) x Stage A-D (A=clean, B=infected, C=ischaemic, D=both)
Why is UT preferred over Wagner?UT incorporates infection and ischaemia (Stage B/C/D); better prognostic value for healing/amputation
What defines infection in UT classification?>=2 of: purulence, erythema >2cm, warmth, induration, pain, fever, leukocytosis -> Stage B/D
What defines ischaemia in UT classification?Absent pedal pulses OR ABI <0.9 OR TBI <0.7 -> Stage C/D
What is the probe-to-bone test?Sterile blunt probe through ulcer to bone; positive PPV 89% for osteomyelitis (Wagner 3 / UT Grade 3)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Wagner 3 = osteomyelitis?Wagner 3 = deep + abscess/osteomyelitis; probe-to-bone confirms
UT Grade = Wagner Grade?No - UT Grade 3 = bone/joint (like Wagner 3); UT Grade 2 = tendon/capsule (Wagner 2); UT adds infection/ischaemia stages
PEDIS = UT Stage?PEDIS grades infection severity (1-4); UT Stage B/D = presence of infection; PEDIS guides antibiotic choice

Mnemonic: DFU-WAGNER-UT

  • DFU classification: Wagner (depth) vs UT (depth x infection x ischaemia)
  • First: Wagner 0-5 (simple, historical)
  • UT: Grade 0-3 x Stage A-D (superior, prognostic)
  • Wagner 0: pre-ulcer; 1: superficial; 2: tendon; 3: bone; 4: forefoot gangrene; 5: whole foot
  • Assess depth: probe-to-bone -> Wagner 3 / UT Grade 3
  • Grade UT: 0=pre, 1=superficial, 2=tendon, 3=bone
  • No infection = Stage A; Infected = Stage B; Ischaemic = Stage C; Both = Stage D
  • Evidence: UT better prognostic value
  • Result: MDT uses UT for communication
  • Ulcer depth: probe-to-bone = bone involvement
  • Tx: PEDIS 1-4 guides antibiotics (1=none, 2=oral, 3=IV, 4=sepsis/surgery)

  • **Chapter MOC": [[../../Diabetes MOC|Diabetes MOC]]

  • **Related": [[]]

Tags

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #fcps #mrcp #full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.6.7 Diabetic Foot Disease
[!info]
Diabetic foot disease: leading cause of non-traumatic lower limb amputation — IWGDF risk 0–3 drives screening/referral; Wagner 0–5 / UT Grade 0–3 × Stage A–D classify ulcers; TCC (total contact cast) = gold standard offloading; PEDIS grades infection; MDT reduces amputations 50–80%; Charcot (Eichenholtz 0–3) = hot swollen foot, preserved pulses, TCC immobilisation.

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Apply IWGDF/Scottish foot risk classification (0–3) for screening and referral
  • [ ] Classify DFU using Wagner (depth) and University of Texas (depth × infection × ischaemia)
  • [ ] Execute DFU management: offloading (TCC), debridement, infection (PEDIS), revascularisation
  • [ ] Diagnose Charcot neuroarthropathy (Eichenholtz 0–3) and differentiate from osteomyelitis
  • [ ] Coordinate MDT care (diabetologist, podiatrist, vascular, orthotics, microbiology, orthopaedics)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionFoot ulceration, infection, or destruction in diabetes from neuropathy, ischaemia, or both
EpidemiologyLifetime DFU risk 15–25%; 85% amputations preceded by DFU; mortality 5yr post-amputation ~50–70%
PathophysiologyNeuropathy (LOPS → unrecognised trauma) + Ischaemia (PAD → impaired healing) + Immunopathy (↑infection risk)
Risk FactorsPrior ulcer/amputation, LOPS, PAD, deformity, nephropathy, retinopathy, poor glycaemic control, smoking

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
Neuropathic ulcer50%Plantar metatarsal heads, toes; painless (LOPS); callus rim; warm foot, pulses present
Neuroischaemic ulcer30%Margins (heel, lateral border); painful; cool foot, pulses absent; delayed healing
Ischaemic ulcer10%Distal toes, heel; painful at rest; gangrene; absent pulses, dependent rubor
Charcot foot1–2%Hot, swollen, erythematous foot; preserved pulses; deformity (rocker-bottom); often unilateral
Infection50–60% DFUCellulitis, purulence, systemic signs; osteomyelitis if probe-to-bone +ve

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

IWGDF / Scottish Foot Risk Classification

RiskCriteriaScreening IntervalReferral
0 (Low)No LOPS, no PAD, no deformityAnnualNone
1 (Moderate)LOPS only6-monthlyPodiatry
2 (High)LOPS + PAD and/or deformity3–6 monthlyPodiatry + vascular if PAD
3 (Very High/Active)Prior ulcer or amputation1–3 monthlyMDT foot clinic
LOPS = Loss of Protective Sensation (cannot feel 10g monofilament at ≥1 site)

Wagner Ulcer Classification (Depth)

GradeDescription
0Pre-ulcer (callus, deformity, no break in skin)
1Superficial ulcer (skin/subcutaneous only)
2Deep ulcer to tendon, capsule, or bone
3Deep + abscess/osteomyelitis
4Forefoot gangrene
5Whole foot gangrene

University of Texas (UT) Classification (Depth × Infection × Ischaemia)

Grade (Depth)Stage A (Clean)Stage B (Infected)Stage C (Ischaemic)Stage D (Infected + Ischaemic)
0Pre-ulcer———
1SuperficialInfectedIschaemicInfected + Ischaemic
2Deep to tendon/capsuleInfectedIschaemicInfected + Ischaemic
3Deep to bone/jointInfectedIschaemicInfected + Ischaemic
UT preferred — incorporates infection and ischaemia; predicts healing/amputation better than Wagner alone.

PEDIS Infection Classification

GradeDefinitionTreatment
1UninfectedLocal wound care
2Mild (superficial, <2cm erythema)Oral antibiotics (flucloxacillin 500mg QDS, or co-amoxiclav)
3Moderate (deep, >2cm erythema, abscess, lymphangitis)IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam, or ceftriaxone + metronidazole)
4Severe (sepsis, SIRS)IV antibiotics + ICU + urgent surgical debridement

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
10g monofilamentLOPS screening6 sites/foot; annual (risk 0), 6-monthly (1), 3-monthly (2–3)
Vibration (128Hz)Neuropathy severity<10s at hallux IP = abnormal
ABI / TBIPAD screeningABI <0.9 diagnostic; TBI if ABI >1.4 (calcified vessels)
Probe-to-bone testOsteomyelitis screeningSterile probe to bone through ulcer; PPV 89% if positive
ESR / CRPInfection monitoringESR >40 suggestive; CRP trend
Plain X-rayOsteomyelitis / CharcotPeriosteal reaction, destruction, gas; Charcot: fragmentation, dislocation
MRIOsteomyelitis / CharcotGold standard for osteomyelitis (sens 90%, spec 85%); Charcot: marrow oedema, joint subluxation
Tc-99m / WBC scanOsteomyelitis if MRI contraindicatedAlternative to MRI

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Charcot vs OsteomyelitisCharcot: hot swollen foot, preserved pulses, bilateral rare, X-ray fragmentation/dislocation, no systemic signs; Osteomyelitis: ulcer + probe-to-bone, elevated ESR/CRP, MRI marrow oedema
Charcot vs CellulitisCharcot: bilateral rare, deformity, preserved pulses; Cellulitis: spreading erythema, warmth, systemic signs
Ischaemic vs Neuropathic ulcerIschaemic: painful, cool, absent pulses, dependent rubor; Neuropathic: painless, warm, callus rim, pulses present
Gout vs CharcotGout: acute monoarticular (1st MTP), exquisitely tender, crystals in aspirate; Charcot: midfoot, less tender, preserved pulses
DVT vs CharcotDVT: calf tenderness, +ve Wells, D-dimer, US; Charcot: foot deformity, preserved pulses, bilateral rare

7. Management

Offloading (Cornerstone)

MethodEfficacyIndications
TCC (Total Contact Cast)Gold standard — heals 80–90% neuropathic ulcersPlantar neuropathic ulcers; compliant patient
Removable Cast Walker (RCW)Good if worn 24/7If TCC contraindicated (infection, severe PAD)
Felted foam / insolesAdjunctMinor ulcers / prevention
Surgical offloadingTendon lengthening, metatarsal head resectionRecurrent ulcers despite conservative
TCC contraindications: Active infection (Grade 3–4), severe PAD (ABI<0.5), patient non-compliance, severe oedema

Infection Management (PEDIS)

GradeAntibioticsDuration
2 (Mild)Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS OR Co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS1–2 weeks
3 (Moderate)Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g TDS OR Ceftriaxone 2g OD + Metronidazole 500mg TDS2–4 weeks
4 (Severe)Same as Grade 3 + ICU support + urgent surgical debridement4–6 weeks

Revascularisation

IndicationApproach
Tissue loss (Wagner ≥3 / UT Stage C/D)Urgent vascular imaging (CT/MR angiography) → Endovascular 1st (angioplasty/stent) → Surgical bypass if unsuitable
Rest painSame pathway

Charcot Neuroarthropathy (Eichenholtz Staging)

StageFeaturesManagement
0 (Development)Hot, swollen, erythematous; normal X-ray; MRI: marrow oedemaTCC immobilisation (non-weightbearing 8–12wk → weightbearing in TCC 4–8wk)
1 (Fragmentation)Bone fragmentation, joint dislocation, debris; "rocker-bottom"TCC until quiescent
2 (Coalescence)Decreased heat/swelling; early fusionTransition to custom footwear/orthoses
3 (Reconstruction)Consolidated, stable deformityCustom footwear, accommmodative orthoses, surgery if unstable
Bisphosphonates: IV zoledronic acid 5mg / pamidronate 60mg — some evidence for ↓turnover; not standard

MDT Composition

MemberRole
DiabetologistGlycaemic control, medical optimisation
PodiatristDebridement, offloading, wound care
Vascular surgeonRevascularisation (endovascular/surgical)
OrthotistCustom footwear, insoles, TCC application
MicrobiologistAntibiotic stewardship, culture interpretation
Orthopaedic surgeonDebridement, amputation, Charcot reconstruction
Nurse specialistWound dressing, patient education, coordination
⬇ Clinical Pathway
New DFU
↓
Assess: IWGDF risk, Wagner/UT, PEDIS, ABI
↓
PEDIS Grade?
↓
Offloading (TCC) + debridement + dressings
TCC + oral abx + debridement
TCC + IV abx + debridement ± vascular
ICU + IV abx + URGENT surgical debridement ± amputation
↓
Healing at 4wk?
↓
Review: infection? ischaemia? offloading adherence?
Continue → preventive footwear
↓
MDT discussion: vascular imaging, MRI, surgical options
↓
Revascularisation if PAD
↓
Continue offloading + wound care

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
IWGDF Risk0: no LOPS/PAD; 1: LOPS; 2: LOPS+PAD/deformity; 3: hx ulcer/amputation
ScreeningAnnual (0), 6-monthly (1), 3-monthly (2–3); 10g monofilament + vibration + ABI
Wagner0: pre-ulcer; 1: superficial; 2: deep to tendon; 3: bone/abscess; 4: forefoot gangrene; 5: whole foot
UT ClassificationGrade 0–3 (depth) × Stage A–D (A=clean, B=infected, C=ischaemic, D=both)
PEDIS Infection1: none; 2: mild (oral abx); 3: moderate (IV abx); 4: severe (sepsis + surgical)
OffloadingTCC = gold standard (heals 80–90%); RCW if TCC contraindicated
CharcotEichenholtz 0–3; hot swollen foot + preserved pulses = Charcot until proven otherwise; TCC immobilisation
Probe-to-bonePositive → osteomyelitis likely (PPV 89%); MRI gold standard
MDTDiabetologist, podiatrist, vascular, orthotist, microbiology, orthopaedics → reduces amputations 50–80%
RevascularisationEndovascular 1st → surgical bypass if unsuitable; urgent if tissue loss

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the IWGDF foot risk classification?0: no LOPS/PAD; 1: LOPS; 2: LOPS+PAD/deformity; 3: prior ulcer/amputation — drives screening interval
How do you classify a diabetic foot ulcer?Wagner (depth 0–5) or University of Texas (Grade 0–3 depth × Stage A–D infection/ischaemia) — UT preferred
What is the gold standard offloading for neuropathic plantar ulcers?Total Contact Cast (TCC) — heals 80–90%; contraindicated in active infection, severe PAD, non-compliance
What is PEDIS infection classification?1: uninfected; 2: mild (oral abx); 3: moderate (IV abx); 4: severe (sepsis + urgent surgery)
How do you differentiate Charcot from osteomyelitis?Charcot: hot swollen foot, preserved pulses, bilateral rare, X-ray fragmentation/dislocation, no systemic sepsis; Osteomyelitis: ulcer + probe-to-bone +ve, elevated ESR/CRP, MRI marrow oedema
What is the probe-to-bone test?Sterile blunt probe through ulcer to bone; positive PPV 89% for osteomyelitis
When do you revascularise a diabetic foot?Tissue loss (Wagner ≥3 / UT Stage C/D) → urgent CT/MR angiography → endovascular 1st → surgical bypass
What is the MDT for diabetic foot?Diabetologist, podiatrist, vascular surgeon, orthotist, microbiologist, orthopaedic surgeon, nurse specialist — reduces amputations 50–80%
How is Charcot managed?TCC immobilisation (non-WB 8–12wk → WB in TCC 4–8wk) until quiescent (Eichenholtz stage 2); then custom footwear; bisphosphonates optional

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Wagner vs UTWagner = depth only; UT = depth + infection + ischaemia; UT predicts outcome better
Charcot = osteomyelitis?NO — Charcot has preserved pulses, hot swollen foot; osteomyelitis has ulcer + probe-to-bone + elevated inflammatory markers
TCC for all ulcers?NO — contraindicated in active infection (PEDIS 3–4), severe PAD (ABI<0.5), non-compliance
Bisphosphonates for Charcot?Not standard; some use IV zoledronic acid 5mg for ↓bone turnover; limited evidence

Mnemonic: FOOT-IWGDF

  • Foot risk: IWGDF 0-3 (0=none, 1=LOPS, 2=LOPS+PAD, 3=hx ulcer)
  • Offloading: TCC gold standard (80-90% heal)
  • Osteomyelitis: probe-to-bone +ve (PPV 89%), MRI gold standard
  • Treatment: PEDIS 1-4 (1=none, 2=oral abx, 3=IV abx, 4=sepsis/surgery)
  • Ischaemia: ABI <0.9; TBI if calcified; revascularise if tissue loss
  • Wagner 0-5: depth only; UT preferred (grade × stage)
  • Grade UT: 0-3 depth × A-D (A=clean, B=infected, C=ischaemic, D=both)
  • Debridement: sharp, regular, with offloading
  • Foot MDT: diabetologist, podiatry, vascular, orthotist, micro, ortho → amputations ↓50-80%

11. Mind Map

Diabetic Foot Disease
Risk Classification
IWGDF 0-3
Screening intervals
Ulcer Classification
Wagner 0-5 (depth)
UT Grade 0-3 × Stage A-D
Infection
PEDIS 1-4
Antibiotics per grade
Offloading
TCC (gold standard)
RCW, felted foam
Charcot
Eichenholtz 0-3
Hot swollen + pulses
TCC immobilisation
MDT
Diabetologist, podiatrist
Vascular, orthotist
Micro, ortho
Amputation ↓50-80%

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionFoot ulcer/infection/destruction from neuropathy + ischaemia + immunopathy; leading cause non-traumatic amputation
Key Test10g monofilament (LOPS), ABI (PAD), probe-to-bone (osteomyelitis), Wagner/UT, PEDIS
ClassificationIWGDF 0–3 risk; Wagner 0–5; UT Grade×Stage; PEDIS 1–4; Charcot Eichenholtz 0–3
Acute MgmtPEDIS 3–4: IV abx + surgery; Charcot: TCC; Infected ulcer: debridement + abx
Chronic MgmtOffloading (TCC), debridement, revascularisation if PAD, MDT follow-up, preventive footwear
Key ScoreIWGDF risk → screening interval; UT → healing prognosis
ComplicationsOsteomyelitis, amputation, sepsis, death (5yr post-amp 50–70%)
PrognosisMDT reduces amputations 50–80%; TCC heals 80–90% neuropathic ulcers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes

▸ 21.6.7 Euglycaemic Dka Sglt2 Inhibitor Associated

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, euglycaemic-dka, sgl2i, dka, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Euglycaemic DKA = DKA with glucose <14 mmol/L (often 8–13); SGLT2i-associated; precipitated by starvation, illness, surgery, alcohol, pregnancy; same management but earlier dextrose; stop SGLT2i; check ketones if unwell on SGLT2i"

see_also: ["Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)", "DKA complications (cerebral oedema, hypokalaemia)", "SGLT2 inhibitors", "Diabetes in pregnancy"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2 Inhibitor Associated)

[!info]
Euglycaemic DKA (euDKA) = DKA with blood glucose <14 mmol/L (typically 8–13 mmol/L); SGLT2i major risk factor; same acidosis/ketosis as typical DKA but delayed recognition; stop SGLT2i immediately; dextrose infusion earlier; same insulin/fluid/K+ management.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define euglycaemic DKA and diagnostic criteria
  • [ ] Identify SGLT2i as major precipitant
  • [ ] Recognise clinical scenarios triggering euDKA
  • [ ] Apply modified management (earlier dextrose, SGLT2i cessation)
  • [ ] Counsel patients on SGLT2i sick-day rules

2. Definition & Diagnostic Criteria

Euglycaemic DKA = Triad of:

  1. Blood glucose <14 mmol/L (often 8–13 mmol/L; rarely <8)
  2. Ketonaemia (β-hydroxybutyrate ≥3 mmol/L) OR ketonuria (+++)
  3. Metabolic acidosis: pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15 mmol/L, anion gap >10

Contrast with typical DKA: Glucose >14 mmol/L (usually >20)

3. SGLT2 Inhibitors — Mechanism of euDKA Risk

MechanismEffect
Glycosuria↓ Glucose reabsorption → ↓ glucose availability → ↓ insulin secretion
Glucagon ↑Low glucose + low insulin → ↑ glucagon → ↑ lipolysis + ketogenesis
Volume depletionOsmotic diuresis → ↑ catecholamines, cortisol → ↑ lipolysis
Renal ketone handlingSGLT2i may ↓ renal ketone clearance

Net effect: Insopenia + glucagon excess + volume depletion → accelerated ketogenesis despite "normal" glucose.

4. Precipitating Factors (Often Multifactorial)

CategoryTriggers
SGLT2i useAll agents (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozin, ertugliflozin)
Starvation / Low carbLow calorie, ketogenic diet, intermittent fasting, Ramadan
Acute illnessInfection, gastroenteritis, MI, stroke, pancreatitis
Surgery / ProceduresPre-op fasting + SGLT2i continued → high risk
Alcohol excessImpairs gluconeogenesis; starvation ketosis
PregnancyPhysiological ketogenesis + SGLT2i (contraindicated)
Insulin reductionPump failure, missed doses, dose reduction
Cocaine / stimulant useCatecholamine surge

Key: SGLT2i + any stressor = euDKA risk.

5. Clinical Presentation

FeatureTypical DKAEuglycaemic DKA
Glucose>14 (usually >20)<14 (8–13 typical)
SymptomsPolyuria, polydipsia, dehydrationNausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, malaise (less dehydration)
KetonesHighHigh (often higher relative to glucose)
pH/BicarbSevere acidosisSimilar severity
RecognitionEasier (high glucose prompts check)Delayed (glucose "reassuring")

Diagnostic pitfall: Glucose 9 mmol/L → "not DKA" → miss ketones/acidosis.

6. Diagnosis — High Index of Suspicion

Check ketones (blood β-hydroxybutyrate) in ANY unwell patient on SGLT2i, regardless of glucose.

TesteuDKA Finding
Blood glucose8–13 mmol/L
β-hydroxybutyrate≥3 mmol/L
Venous pH<7.3
Bicarbonate<15 mmol/L
Anion gap>10
HbA1cOften normal/near-normal (euDKA can occur at any HbA1c)

7. Management — Modifications from Typical DKA

StepTypical DKAEuglycaemic DKA
1. STOP SGLT2iN/AIMMEDIATELY (do not restart until resolved + review)
2. IV Fluids0.9% NaCl bolusesSame; volume depletion often prominent
3. InsulinFixed-rate IV insulin (0.1u/kg/h)Same rate (0.05–0.1u/kg/h)
4. DextroseStart when glucose <14START EARLIER (when glucose <11–12) — 5–10% dextrose
5. PotassiumProtocol-basedSame protocol (monitor 1–2h)
6. BicarbonateNot routineSame (pH <6.9)
7. MonitoringGlucose, ketones, pH, K+ q1–2hGlucose + ketones + pH + K+ q1–2h

Key difference: Dextrose infusion starts at higher glucose threshold to allow continued insulin for ketone suppression without hypoglycaemia.

8. Sickle Day Rules for SGLT2i Patients

SituationAction
Acute illness (fever, infection, vomiting, diarrhoea)STOP SGLT2i; check ketones; hydrate; seek medical review if ketones >0.6 or unwell
Pre-surgery/procedureSTOP SGLT2i 3 days before (elective); emergency → stop immediately
Fasting (religious, medical)STOP SGLT2i; discuss with diabetes team
Low carb/ketogenic dietAvoid on SGLT2i; if essential → stop SGLT2i
PregnancyContraindicated — stop immediately, switch to insulin

9. Outcomes & Prognosis

AspectData
MortalitySimilar to typical DKA (~1–2% in-hospital)
ICU admissionSimilar
Length of staySlightly longer (delayed diagnosis)
RecurrenceHigh if SGLT2i restarted without counselling
Long-termNo specific renal/cardiac sequelae beyond DKA itself

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
euDKA glucose threshold<14 mmol/L (typically 8–13)
SGLT2i euDKA mechanismGlycosuria → ↓ insulin + ↑ glucagon → ketogenesis
TriggersSGLT2i + starvation/illness/surgery/alcohol/pregnancy
DiagnosisCheck ketones in ANY unwell SGLT2i patient — regardless of glucose
Management keySTOP SGLT2i immediately; start dextrose earlier (glucose <11–12)
Insulin rateSame (0.05–0.1u/kg/h)
Sick day rulesStop SGLT2i if ill/fasting/surgery; check ketones
Pre-opStop SGLT2i 3 days before elective surgery
PregnancySGLT2i contraindicated
RecurrenceHigh if SGLT2i restarted without education

11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)|Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

Topic Overview

Euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2 Inhibitor Associated
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Diagnostic Criteria
3. SGLT2 Inhibitors — Mechanism of euDKA Risk
4. Precipitating Factors (Often Multifactorial)
5. Clinical Presentation
6. Diagnosis — High Index of Suspicion
7. Management — Modifications from Typical DKA
8. Sickle Day Rules for SGLT2i Patients
9. Outcomes & Prognosis
10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.6.8 Diabetic Kidney Disease Staging Kdigo
[!info]
DKD = leading cause of ESRD worldwide — KDIGO staging (GFR G1–5 × Albuminuria A1–3); ACEi/ARB max tolerated + SGLT2i (1st line, eGFR≥20) + finerenone (eGFR≥25) + GLP-1 RA; proteinuria reduction targets (↓30% or ACR<3); multidisciplinary at G4–5.

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Apply KDIGO 2022 staging (G1–5 × A1–3 heat map)
  • [ ] Diagnose DKD: persistent ACR ≥3 mg/mmol (≥30 mg/g) + eGFR <60 or DM duration >10y
  • [ ] Execute management: ACEi/ARB → SGLT2i → finerenone → GLP-1 RA
  • [ ] Set proteinuria reduction targets and monitoring intervals
  • [ ] Recognise non-diabetic kidney disease (red flags for referral)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionChronic kidney disease attributed to diabetes: persistent albuminuria (ACR ≥3 mg/mmol) and/or eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² in diabetes
Epidemiology~40% T1DM, ~30% T2DM develop DKD; leading cause of ESRD (40–50% of dialysis starts)
Risk FactorsHbA1c, hypertension, duration, smoking, dyslipidaemia, family history, ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean)
PathophysiologyHyperglycaemia → hyperfiltration → glomerular hypertension → mesangial expansion → basement membrane thickening → Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules → sclerosis

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

PresentationFrequencyKey Features
AsymptomaticEarly CKD (G1–3a)Detected on screening (ACR/eGFR)
OedemaNephrotic syndrome (A3)Periorbital, peripheral, ascites
HypertensionProgressiveOften difficult to control; volume overload
AnaemiaG3b+EPO deficiency; Hb <110g/L
Bone/mineral disorderG3b+↑PTH, ↓vit D, ↑phosphate, vascular calcification
Uraemic symptomsG5Nausea, pruritus, pericarditis, neuropathy
Red Flags for Non-Diabetic Kidney Disease: Rapid eGFR decline (>5 mL/min/yr), active urinary sediment (RBC/casts), haematuria, no retinopathy, family history of hereditary kidney disease, low BMI at diagnosis → refer nephrology.

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

KDIGO 2022 Heat Map (GFR × Albuminuria)

GFR CategoryeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)A1 (<3 mg/mmol)A2 (3–30 mg/mmol)A3 (>30 mg/mmol)
G1≥90Low risk (🟢)Moderate (🟡)High (🟠)
G260–89Low (🟢)Moderate (🟡)High (🟠)
G3a45–59Moderate (🟡)High (🟠)Very high (🔴)
G3b30–44High (🟠)Very high (🔴)Very high (🔴)
G415–29Very high (🔴)Very high (🔴)Very high (🔴)
G5<15Very high (🔴)Very high (🔴)Very high (🔴)
Colour codes: 🟢 Low → annual monitor; 🟡 Moderate → 6-monthly; 🟠 High → 3–6 monthly + nephrology; 🔴 Very high → nephrology + MDT

Albuminuria Categories (ACR)

CategoryACR (mg/mmol)ACR (mg/g)Terminology
A1<3<30Normal/mildly increased
A23–3030–300Moderately increased (microalbuminuria)
A3>30>300Severely increased (macroalbuminuria)

GFR Categories

CategoryeGFRDescription
G1≥90Normal/high
G260–89Mildly decreased
G3a45–59Mild–moderate
G3b30–44Moderate–severe
G415–29Severely decreased
G5<15Kidney failure

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
ACR (spot urine)Albuminuria quantificationPreferred over PCR; early morning sample; avoid exercise/UTI/fever; confirm with 2/3 positive in 3–6 months
eGFR (CKD-EPI)GFR estimationStandardised; if eGFR<60 + DM >10y + retinopathy → DKD likely
Urine microscopyExclude non-diabeticRBC >10/µL, casts → consider non-diabetic
HbA1cGlycaemic controlTarget individualised; falsely low in CKD/ESRD
Renal ultrasoundStructural assessmentSmall kidneys = chronic; normal/large = infiltrative/obstructive
Kidney biopsyIf non-diabetic suspectedRapid decline, active sediment, no retinopathy, systemic disease signs

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Hypertensive nephrosclerosisNo albuminuria (or mild), long-standing HTN, no retinopathy, benign urinary sediment
Chronic glomerulonephritisHaematuria, RBC casts, proteinuria; may have low complement
AmyloidosisNephrotic syndrome, low albumin, systemic features (cardiac, neuropathy); Congo red +ve
Multiple myelomaBence Jones proteinuria, anaemia, hypercalcaemia, lytic lesions; urine protein electrophoresis
Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney diseaseFamily history, large cystic kidneys, hepatic cysts; haematuria common
Obstructive uropathyHydronephrosis on US; post-void residual; recurrent UTIs

7. Management

Stepwise DKD Management (KDIGO 2022/ADA 2024)

StepInterventionTarget/Details
1. LifestyleLow salt (<5g/day), protein 0.8g/kg, weight control, smoking cessation, SGLT2i if indicatedBP <130/80
2. ACEi/ARBMax tolerated dose (e.g., ramipril 10mg, losartan 100mg)Target ACR <3 or ↓30%; monitor K+/Cr (rise ≤30% acceptable)
3. SGLT2i1st line add-on (empagliflozin 10mg, dapagliflozin 10mg, canagliflozin 100mg)Initiate eGFR ≥20 (dapa/empa) / ≥30 (cana); continue to dialysis
4. FinerenoneNon-steroidal MRA (10–20mg OD)eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8; add if ACR≥30 on ACEi/ARB+SGLT2i
5. GLP-1 RASemaglutide (FLOW), liraglutide (LEADER renal)CV/renal benefit; weight loss
6. BP control<130/80 (ADA/KDIGO); ACEi/ARB + CCB/thiazideAvoid dual RAS blockade
7. StatinAtorvastatin 20–40mg (SHARP)LDL <1.8mmol/L or ↓50%

Proteinuria Reduction Targets

TargetAction if Not Met
ACR reduction ≥30% at 3–6 monthsOptimise ACEi/ARB dose; add SGLT2i
ACR <3 mg/mmol (normoalbuminuria)Ideal but rarely achieved in established DKD
ACR <30 mg/mmol (A2)Acceptable if A3 → A2 transition

Monitoring Intervals by KDIGO Category

CategoryeGFRACRBPReview
Low (Green)AnnualAnnual6-monthlyPrimary care
Moderate (Yellow)6-monthly6-monthly3–6 monthlyPrimary care ± specialist
High/Very High (Orange/Red)3–6 monthly3–6 monthly1–3 monthlyNephrology + MDT
⬇ Clinical Pathway
DKD Diagnosed
↓
ACEi/ARB max tolerated
↓
ACR ≥30 or eGFR<60?
↓
Add SGLT2i (eGFR≥20)
Monitor 6-12 monthly
↓
ACR≥30 on ACEi+SGLT2i?
↓
Add Finerenone (eGFR≥25, K+≤4.8)
Monitor 3-6 monthly
↓
Add GLP-1 RA if CVD/obesity
↓
BP<130/80 + Statin + Lifestyle
↓
Progress to G4/G5?
↓
Nephrology MDT + Transplant/Dialysis prep
Continue monitoring

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
KDIGO StagingG1–5 (eGFR) × A1–3 (ACR); heat map for risk; G3aA2 = moderate, G3bA3 = very high
DiagnosisPersistent ACR ≥3 mg/mmol (2/3 in 3–6mo) + DM (≥10y or retinopathy)
ACEi/ARBMax tolerated dose; target ACR <3 or ↓30%; rise in Cr ≤30% acceptable; K+ ≤5.5
SGLT2i1st line add-on; eGFR ≥20 (dapa/empa) / ≥30 (cana); continue to dialysis; CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY
FinerenoneNon-steroidal MRA; eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8; FIDELIO/FIGARO; add if ACR≥30 on ACEi/ARB+SGLT2i
GLP-1 RASemaglutide (FLOW ✓), liraglutide (LEADER renal ✓); CV/renal benefit
BP Target<130/80; ACEi/ARB + CCB/thiazide; avoid dual RAS blockade
Proteinuria Target↓ACR ≥30% at 3–6mo; ultimate <3 mg/mmol
HbA1c in CKDFalsely LOW (↓RBC lifespan); use fructosamine/glycated albumin; target individualised
ReferralG4–5, rapid decline >5mL/min/yr, active sediment, ACR>70 without retinopathy

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the KDIGO staging for DKD?G1–5 (eGFR: ≥90, 60–89, 45–59, 30–44, 15–29, <15) × A1–3 (ACR: <3, 3–30, >30 mg/mmol); heat map for risk
How do you diagnose DKD?Persistent albuminuria (ACR ≥3 mg/mmol on 2/3 occasions in 3–6 months) + diabetes (≥10 years or retinopathy present)
What is the stepwise management of DKD?1) Lifestyle 2) ACEi/ARB max dose 3) SGLT2i (eGFR≥20) 4) Finerenone (eGFR≥25, K+≤4.8) 5) GLP-1 RA 6) BP<130/80 7) Statin
What are the eGFR thresholds for SGLT2i in CKD?Dapagliflozin/Empagliflozin: initiate ≥20, continue to dialysis; Canagliflozin: initiate ≥30
When do you add finerenone?ACR ≥30 mg/mmol on max ACEi/ARB + SGLT2i; eGFR ≥25, K+ ≤4.8; FIDELIO/FIGARO trials
What are the proteinuria reduction targets?↓ACR ≥30% at 3–6 months; target ACR <3 mg/mmol (ideal) or <30 mg/mmol (acceptable)
Why is HbA1c unreliable in CKD?Falsely LOW due to ↓RBC lifespan, ESA use; use fructosamine (2–3 week avg) or glycated albumin (3 week)
When do you refer DKD to nephrology?G4–5 (eGFR<30), rapid decline >5mL/min/yr, active urinary sediment, ACR>70 without retinopathy, refractory hypertension

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
ACEi + ARB together?NO — dual RAS blockade ↑hyperkalaemia, AKI, no added benefit (ONTARGET, VA NEPHRON-D)
SGLT2i in low eGFR?YES — initiate ≥20 (dapa/empa) / ≥30 (cana); continue to dialysis — renal benefit persists
Finerenone vs spironolactone?Finerenone: non-steroidal, selective MRA, less hyperkalaemia, no gynaecomastia; eGFR≥25, K+≤4.8
HbA1c in ESRD?Falsely low; use fructosamine/glycated albumin for monitoring

Mnemonic: DKD-ACE-SGLT-FIN

  • DKD: ACR ≥3 + DM (10y/retinopathy)
  • KDIGO: G1-5 × A1-3 heat map
  • Diagnose: 2/3 ACR ≥3 in 3-6mo
  • ACEi/ARB: max dose, ACR↓30%, Cr rise ≤30% OK
  • Continue SGLT2i to dialysis (eGFR≥20 dapa/empa)
  • Eliminate dual RAS blockade
  • SGLT2i: 1st add-on (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)
  • GFR ≥20 dapa/empa, ≥30 cana
  • Lower BP <130/80
  • Target ACR ↓30% or <3
  • Finerenone: eGFR≥25, K+≤4.8, on ACEi+SGLT2i (FIDELIO/FIGARO)
  • Insulin/GLP-1: GLP-1 RA renal benefit (FLOW, LEADER)
  • Nephrology refer: G4-5, rapid decline, active sediment

11. Mind Map

Diabetic Kidney Disease
Diagnosis
ACR ≥3 (2/3 in 3-6mo)
DM 10y or retinopathy
Staging (KDIGO)
G1-5 (eGFR)
A1-3 (ACR)
Heat map
Management
ACEi/ARB max
SGLT2i (≥20)
Finerenone (≥25, K+≤4.8)
GLP-1 RA
BP <130/80
Statin
Targets
ACR ↓30%
ACR <3 ideal
Monitoring
eGFR + ACR 3-12mo
By KDIGO category
Referral
G4-5
Rapid decline
Active sediment

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionDKD: persistent ACR ≥3 mg/mmol + DM (≥10y or retinopathy); leading cause ESRD
**Key Test"ACR (spot urine, early morning); eGFR (CKD-EPI); avoid PCR
**Classification"KDIGO G1-5 × A1-3; heat map: G3aA2=moderate, G3bA3=very high
**Acute Mgmt"AKI on CKD: hold ACEi/ARB/SGLT2i/diuretics; volume resuscitation
**Chronic Mgmt"ACEi/ARB max → SGLT2i (eGFR≥20) → Finerenone (eGFR≥25) → GLP-1 RA → BP<130/80
**Key Score"KDIGO heat map; ACR ↓30% at 3-6mo
**Complications"ESRD, CVD, anaemia, bone disease, hyperkalaemia
**Prognosis"G3aA2: 5yr ESRD risk ~1%; G3bA3: ~50%; SGLT2i/finerenone ↓progression 30-40%

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

▸ 21.7 Pathophysiology of Diabetes 16
▸ 21.7.1 Ascvd Risk Calculators Qrisk3 Ascvd Pooled Cohort

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, ascvd-risk, qrisk3, pooled-cohort, cv-risk-calculators, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - QRISK3 vs ASCVD Pooled Cohort; diabetes as risk enhancer; when to use which calculator; statin eligibility; communication of risk"

see_also: ["Diabetes as cardiovascular risk equivalent", "Statin therapy in diabetes (intensity, targets)", "Aspirin primary prevention", "BP targets in diabetes (NICE, ADA, ESC)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

ASCVD Risk Calculators (QRISK3, ASCVD Pooled Cohort)

[!info]
QRISK3 = UK primary care (includes deprivation, ethnicity, CKD, RA, SLE, medications); ASCVD Pooled Cohort = US/ACC/AHA (race, lipid-focused); both ↑ risk with diabetes but neither fully captures diabetes duration/complications.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Compare QRISK3 vs ASCVD Pooled Cohort equations
  • [ ] Identify variables unique to each calculator
  • [ ] Apply diabetes-specific risk enhancement
  • [ ] Interpret 10-year risk thresholds for statin/aspirin decisions
  • [ ] Communicate risk to patients effectively

2. QRISK3 (UK / NICE)

Variables (20+)

TraditionalQRISK3-Specific
Age, sex, smoking, SBP, TC, HDLTownsend deprivation score
Treated hypertensionEthnicity (detailed: Indian, Pakistani, Bangladeshi, etc.)
Diabetes (T1/T2 + duration)CKD stage (eGFR + ACR)
BMIRheumatoid arthritis
Family history premature CVDSystemic lupus erythematosus
Atrial fibrillation
Migraine
Corticosteroid use
Antipsychotic use
Severe mental illness
ED (erectile dysfunction)

Diabetes in QRISK3

  • T1DM and T2DM separate variables
  • Duration of diabetes included
  • HbA1c not directly in equation (but via diabetes variable)

Output

  • 10-year CVD risk % (CHD + stroke + TIA)
  • NICE CG181: ≥10% 10-year risk → offer statin (atorvastatin 20mg)

3. ASCVD Pooled Cohort Equations (ACC/AHA 2013)

Variables

VariableNotes
Age40–79 years
SexMale / Female
RaceWhite / Black / Other (coefficients differ)
Total cholesterolmg/dL
HDL cholesterolmg/dL
Systolic BPmmHg
BP treatmentYes/No
DiabetesYes/No (binary, no duration)
SmokingCurrent smoker

Race Controversy

  • Separate equations for Black vs White/Other
  • Black individuals: Lower coefficients → lower calculated risk for same risk factors
  • 2023 ACC/AHA: Moving toward race-free equations (PREVENT)

Output

  • 10-year hard ASCVD risk (fatal/nonfatal MI, fatal/nonfatal stroke)
  • ACC/AHA 2018 Cholesterol Guideline: ≥7.5% 10-year risk → moderate/high-intensity statin

4. Comparison Table

FeatureQRISK3ASCVD Pooled Cohort
PopulationUK primary care (THIN/QResearch)US cohorts (ARIC, CARDIA, CHS, FHS)
Age range25–8440–79
EthnicityDetailed (16 categories)Binary (Black/White/Other)
DeprivationTownsend scoreNo
CKDeGFR + ACRNo
AutoimmuneRA, SLENo
MedicationsSteroids, antipsychoticsNo
Mental healthSMI, migraineNo
Diabetes detailT1/T2 + durationBinary only
OutcomeCVD (CHD+stroke+TIA)Hard ASCVD (MI+stroke)
Statin threshold≥10% (NICE)≥7.5% (ACC/AHA)
CalibrationUK populationUS population

5. Diabetes as Risk Enhancer — Beyond Calculators

Neither calculator fully captures:

  • Diabetes duration
  • HbA1c trajectory
  • Microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy)
  • Macrovascular complications (prior CVD)
  • CKD (in ASCVD eq)
  • Insulin use vs oral agents
  • Hypoglycaemia episodes

ACC/AHA 2018 "Risk Enhancers" (Diabetes-Specific)

If 10-year risk 5–7.5% (borderline) or 7.5–20% (intermediate), these favour statin:

  • Diabetes duration ≥10 years (T2DM) or ≥20 years (T1DM)
  • Albuminuria (ACR ≥30)
  • eGFR <60
  • Retinopathy
  • Neuropathy
  • ABI <0.9
  • Family history premature CVD
  • hsCRP ≥2 mg/L
  • Lp(a) ≥50 mg/dL
  • APOB ≥130 mg/dL

6. Practical Application in Diabetes

ScenarioCalculatorAction
UK primary careQRISK3 (mandated)If ≥10% → atorvastatin 20mg
US / internationalASCVD Pooled CohortIf ≥7.5% → moderate/high-intensity statin
T1DM >40y or >10y durationDon't rely on calculatorAutomatic high-intensity statin (NICE/ACC)
CKD G3+QRISK3 includes; ASCVD doesn'tQRISK3 preferred
Ethnic minority UKQRISK3 detailed ethnicityQRISK3 required
Patient communicationBoth give %Use "heart age", visual aids, shared decision-making

7. Limitations & Future

LimitationSolution
No diabetes duration in ASCVDUse risk enhancers; consider PREVENT (2023)
Race in ASCVDRace-free equations in development
HbA1c not in eitherEmerging models include HbA1c
Dynamic risk (treatment changes)Lifetime risk models; serial calculation
Young adults <40Not validated; use clinical judgement
Frailty/elderlyNot validated >79 (QRISK3) / >79 (ASCVD)

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
QRISK3 unique variablesDeprivation (Townsend), detailed ethnicity, CKD (eGFR+ACR), RA, SLE, steroids, antipsychotics, SMI, migraine, ED
QRISK3 diabetesT1 vs T2 separate; duration included
ASCVD Pooled CohortRace-specific equations (Black/White/Other); no CKD, no deprivation
Statin threshold QRISK3≥10% 10-year CVD risk (NICE CG181)
Statin threshold ASCVD≥7.5% 10-year hard ASCVD risk (ACC/AHA)
Diabetes as risk equivalentT1DM >40y or >10y duration; T2DM >40y → automatic high-intensity statin
Risk enhancersAlbuminuria, eGFR<60, retinopathy, neuropathy, ABI<0.9, family history, hsCRP≥2, Lp(a)≥50
HbA1c in calculatorsNot included in either
CommunicationUse heart age, visual aids, shared decision-making
Limitations<40y not validated; >79/84 not validated; frailty not captured

Variables (20+)
Diabetes in QRISK3
Output
3. ASCVD Pooled Cohort Equations (ACC/AHA 2013)
Variables
Race Controversy
Output
4. Comparison Table

▸ 21.7.1 Beta Cell Dysfunction And Failure

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe the timeline of beta-cell failure in T2DM
  • [ ] Explain mechanisms: glucolipotoxicity, amyloid, ER stress, dedifferentiation
  • [ ] Apply proinsulin/insulin ratio as marker of beta-cell stress
  • [ ] Contrast T2DM beta-cell failure vs T1DM autoimmune destruction

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionProgressive loss of beta-cell function and mass in T2DM
TimelinePresent at diagnosis (~50% function lost); continues ~4%/year decline
Key ConceptBeta-cell failure = inadequate insulin for degree of IR

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Stages of Beta-Cell Dysfunction (UKPDS Model)

StageBeta-Cell FunctionClinical Correlate
CompensationIncreased insulin secretion (hyperinsulinaemia)Normal glucose (insulin resistance compensated)
DecompensationDecreased first-phase insulin responseIGT / IFG
FailureInadequate insulin for IROvert T2DM (fasting + post-prandial hyperglycaemia)
ExhaustionSevere loss; insulin requiredInsulin-requiring T2DM

Key Pathogenic Mechanisms

MechanismDetails
GlucotoxicityChronic hyperglycaemia -> oxidative stress, ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction -> apoptosis
LipotoxicityFFA/ceramides -> down PDX-1, down MafA, up CHOP -> apoptosis; UPR activation
Amyloid (IAPP)Islet amyloid polypeptide deposits -> beta-cell toxicity; ~90% T2DM have islet amyloid
ER StressMisfolded proinsulin -> unfolded protein response -> CHOP-mediated apoptosis
DedifferentiationBeta-cells lose identity -> revert to progenitor-like or alpha-like cells (reversible?)
Oxidative StressMitochondrial ROS -> DNA damage, apoptosis

Beta-Cell Markers

MarkerSignificance
Proinsulin/Insulin ratioIncreased = beta-cell stress; decreased processing efficiency
C-peptideSurrogate for endogenous insulin secretion
First-phase insulin responseLost early in T2DM pathogenesis
HOMA-BBeta-cell function index (model-derived)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestInterpretation
C-peptideLow = beta-cell failure; preserved = insulin resistance
Proinsulin/InsulinIncreased ratio = beta-cell stress
OGTT with insulinFirst-phase loss; beta-cell glucose sensitivity
HOMA-BBeta-cell function index (model-derived)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoimmune, rapid loss, autoantibodies 2+, low C-peptide
MODYGenetic, preserved C-peptide, specific gene defects
Secondary DMPancreatic, endocrine, drug-induced mechanisms

7. Management Implications

StrategyEvidence
Early intensive controlUKPDS legacy effect; down beta-cell exhaustion rate
GLP-1 RAup Beta-cell survival (preclinical); down apoptosis
SGLT2iIndirect via weight loss, down glucotoxicity
Early insulin"Beta-cell rest" hypothesis; transient intensive insulin -> remission
TZDsPioglitazone: down lipotoxicity, down amyloid?

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Timeline~50% function lost at diagnosis; ~4%/year decline
MechanismsGlucolipotoxicity, IAPP amyloid, ER stress, dedifferentiation
AmyloidIAPP deposits in ~90% T2DM; toxic to beta-cells
Proinsulin/InsulinIncreased ratio = beta-cell stress marker
DedifferentiationBeta-cells lose identity -> progenitor/alpha-like (potentially reversible)
First-phase lossEarliest functional defect; precedes hyperglycaemia

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the natural history of beta-cell function in T2DM?Compensation (hyperinsulinaemia) -> decompensation (first-phase loss) -> failure (T2DM) -> exhaustion (insulin-requiring); ~4%/year decline
What is the role of islet amyloid (IAPP) in T2DM?Deposits in ~90% T2DM; toxic to beta-cells; contributes to apoptosis
What is glucolipotoxicity?Combined chronic hyperglycaemia + elevated FFA -> oxidative stress, ER stress, mitochondrial dysfunction -> beta-cell apoptosis
What is beta-cell dedifferentiation?Beta-cells lose identity markers (PDX-1, MafA) -> revert to progenitor-like or alpha-like cells; potentially reversible with early intervention

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Beta-cell failure = T1DM?NO - T2DM: functional decline + mass loss; T1DM: autoimmune destruction
Beta-cell mass vs function?Both decline; function declines first (dedifferentiation precedes apoptosis)

Mnemonic: BETA-CELL-FAIL

  • Beta-cell: ~50% lost at diagnosis
  • Exhaustion: final stage -> insulin required
  • Timeline: ~4%/year decline
  • Amyloid: IAPP deposits in ~90% T2DM
  • Compensation: hyperinsulinaemia early
  • ER stress: misfolded proinsulin -> CHOP apoptosis
  • Lipotoxicity: FFA/ceramides -> down PDX-1, up CHOP
  • Loss of first-phase: earliest defect
  • First-phase insulin response lost early
  • Amyloid: IAPP deposits ~90%
  • Insulin/proinsulin ratio: up = stress
  • LGLP-1 RA/SGLT2i: may slow decline
  • Dedifferentiation: loss of PDX-1/MafA -> progenitor/alpha-like

11. Mind Map

Beta-cell Dysfunction
Timeline
Compensation -> Decompensation -> Failure -> Exhaustion
~4%/year decline
Mechanisms
Glucolipotoxicity
IAPP amyloid
ER stress
Dedifferentiation
Markers
Proinsulin/Insulin ratio
C-peptide
First-phase insulin
HOMA-B
Treatment
Early intensive control
GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i
Early insulin (rest)

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionProgressive loss of beta-cell function/mass in T2DM
**Key Test"Proinsulin/Insulin ratio (up = stress); C-peptide (low = failure)
**Classification"Compensation -> Decompensation -> Failure -> Exhaustion
**Acute Mgmt"Early intensive insulin -> remission possible
**Chronic Mgmt"GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i, weight loss, early intensive control
**Key Score"Proinsulin/Insulin ratio up = stress; first-phase lost early
**Complications"Progressive hyperglycaemia; insulin requirement
**Prognosis"~4%/year decline; early intervention may preserve function

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Definition & Epidemiology
Classification & Staging
Diagnosis & Investigations
Management (Acute)
Management (Chronic)

▸ 21.7.10 Critical Limb Ischaemia

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, cli, critical-limb-ischaemia, ft-gang, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - CLI = rest pain >2w + tissue loss (ulcer/gangrene) + haemodynamic significance; Rutherford 4–6 / Fontaine III–IV; urgent revascularisation; WIfI classification; amputation risk; diabetes = PAD accelerator"

see_also: ["PAD screening (ABI)", "Diabetic foot disease/Amputation prevention", "Peripheral neuropathy", "Diabetes in CKD and dialysis"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Critical Limb Ischaemia (CLI)

[!info]
CLI = rest pain >2 weeks + tissue loss (ulcer/gangrene) + objective arterial disease; Rutherford 4–6 / Fontaine III–IV; amputation risk 25–40% at 1 year revascularisation; urgent vascular MDT; WIfI classification guides therapy; diabetes accelerates PAD and worsens outcomes.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define CLI (Rutherford/Fontaine/WIfI)
  • [ ] Diagnose CLI clinically and objectively
  • [ ] Apply WIfI classification for amputation risk stratification
  • [ ] Understand revascularisation principles (endovascular vs open)
  • [ ] Coordinate with diabetic foot MDT

2. Definition & Classification

Rutherford Classification (Clinical)

CategoryClinical Picture
0Asymptomatic
1Mild claudication
2Moderate claudication
3Severe claudication
4Ischaemic rest pain (typically nocturnal, relieved by dependency)
5Minor tissue loss (non-healing ulcer, focal gangrene)
6Major tissue loss (extensive gangrene, functional foot loss)

CLI = Rutherford 4, 5, 6

Fontaine Classification

StageClinical Picture
IAsymptomatic
IIaMild claudication (>200m)
IIbSevere claudication (<200m)
IIIRest pain
IVUlceration/gangrene

CLI = Fontaine III–IV

3. Diagnostic Criteria (ESC / SVS)

CLI = ALL THREE:

  1. Symptoms: Rest pain >2 weeks (typically nocturnal, dorsal foot, relieved by dependency) OR tissue loss (ulcer/gangrene)
  2. Haemodynamic evidence (ONE of):
  • ABI ≤0.40 (or <0.50 with diabetes/medial calcification)
  • Ankle pressure <50 mmHg (or <70 mmHg with diabetes)
  • Toe pressure <30 mmHg (or TBI <0.25)
  • TcPO2 <30 mmHg (or <40 mmHg with diabetes)
  1. Arterial disease on imaging (duplex/CTA/MRA) amenable to revascularisation

Diabetes adjustment: Use lower thresholds (ABI <0.50, toe pressure <40 mmHg) due to medial calcification.

4. WIfI Classification (SVS) — Amputation Risk Stratification

ComponentGrade 0Grade 1Grade 2Grade 3
WoundNoneSmall, shallow ulcer; no gangreneDeep ulcer, exposed bone; gangrene <2 toesExtensive gangrene; forefoot/heel loss
IschaemiaABI >0.80, TP >60ABI 0.6–0.8, TP 40–60ABI 0.4–0.6, TP 30–40ABI <0.4, TP <30, TcPO2 <30
Foot InfectionNoneLocal (≤2cm erythema)Deep (≥2cm, abscess, osteomyelitis)Sepsis, necrotising fasciitis

WIfI Clinical Stage (combines W+I+fI) → 1-year amputation risk & revascularisation benefit:

StageAmputation RiskRevascularisation Benefit
1 (Very Low)<5%Low
2 (Low)5–10%Moderate
3 (Moderate)10–40%High
4 (High)>40%High (but risk remains)

5. Management — Urgent Vascular MDT

Principles

  • Revascularisation = primary therapy (endovascular first for most)
  • Wound care = offloading, debridement, infection control (MDT)
  • Medical optimisation = statin, antiplatelet, BP, glucose, smoking cessation

Revascularisation Strategy (BASIL-2, BEST-CLI)

Lesion PatternPreferred Approach
TASC A/B (short, focal)Endovascular first (angioplasty ± stent)
TASC C/D (long, diffuse, tibial)Endovascular first (modern data); bypass if endo fails/anatomy unsuitable
Inflow disease (aortoiliac)Endovascular (iliac stenting)
Outflow disease (tibial/pedal)Endovascular (pedal loop technique)

Best outcomes: Multidisciplinary vascular MDT (vascular surgeon, interventional radiologist, diabetologist, podiatrist).

Medical Therapy

DrugDoseIndication
Aspirin75mg ODAll CLI (unless contraindicated)
Clopidogrel75mg ODIf aspirin intolerant; or DAPT post-endovascular
StatinAtorvastatin 80mgHigh-intensity — pleiotropic stabilisation
Rivaroxaban 2.5mg BD + AspirinVOYAGER PADPost-revascularisation (↓ ALI/amputation)
Cilostazol100mg BDSymptomatic claudication (not CLI)

6. Amputation — Indications & Levels

IndicationLevel
Non-reconstructable disease (no target vessel)Most distal viable level
Life-threatening sepsis (wet gangrene, necrotising)Guillotine (emergency) → definitive later
Failed revascularisation (persistent rest pain/tissue loss)Definitive level per perfusion
Non-ambulatory / non-reconstructableAKA (if popliteal disease) / BKA (if tibial disease)

BKA preferred over AKA if popliteal flow adequate — better prosthetic outcome, lower mortality.

7. Outcomes

MetricValue
1-year amputation-free survival~60–70% (with revascularisation)
1-year major amputation25–40% (without revascularisation)
1-year mortality20–25% (CV dominant)
5-year survival~40–50%
Diabetes multiplier2–3× amputation risk vs non-diabetes

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
CLI definitionRest pain >2w + tissue loss + haemodynamic evidence (ABI <0.4/0.5 in DM)
Rutherford CLI4 (rest pain), 5 (minor tissue loss), 6 (major tissue loss)
Fontaine CLIIII (rest pain), IV (ulcer/gangrene)
WIfIWound + Ischaemia + foot Infection → amputation risk (Stage 1–4)
Diabetes ABI threshold<0.50 (vs 0.40 non-diabetes) due to calcification
Toe pressure<30–40 mmHg diagnostic
RevascularisationEndovascular first (TASC A/B); modern data supports endo for C/D too
DAPT post-endoAspirin + clopidogrel (1–3 months)
Rivaroxaban 2.5mg BD + aspirinVOYAGER PAD: post-revascularisation CLI
BKA vs AKABKA preferred if popliteal flow adequate
1-year amputation CLI25–40% without revascularisation

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

▸ 21.7.12 Diabetes As Cardiovascular Risk Equivalent

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, diabetes-cv-equivalent, risk-equivalent, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Diabetes as 'coronary heart disease risk equivalent' — historical concept; current guidelines: risk continuum; T1DM >40y or >10y duration; T2DM >40y; treat as secondary prevention for statin/aspirin/BP"

see_also: ["Statin therapy in diabetes (intensity, targets)", "Aspirin primary prevention", "BP targets in diabetes (NICE, ADA, ESC)", "ASCVD risk calculators (QRISK3, ASCVD Pooled Cohort)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Diabetes as Cardiovascular Risk Equivalent

[!info]
Historical concept: "Diabetes = CHD risk equivalent" (Haffner 1998, NCEP ATP III) — 10-year CVD risk in diabetes ≈ prior MI. Current view: Risk continuum; not all diabetes same; use risk calculators + enhancers; but T1DM >40y/10y duration and T2DM >40y treated as very high risk.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain the origin and evolution of the "risk equivalent" concept
  • [ ] Apply current guideline approach (risk continuum vs binary equivalent)
  • [ ] Identify diabetes subgroups warranting secondary-prevention-level treatment
  • [ ] Differentiate T1DM vs T2DM risk profiles
  • [ ] Apply to statin, BP, aspirin decisions

2. Historical Concept

StudyFinding
Haffner et al. 1998 (NEJM)7-year MI risk in T2DM without prior MI = 20.2%; non-diabetes with prior MI = 18.8%
NCEP ATP III (2001)Formalised: Diabetes = CHD risk equivalent → treat LDL target <100 mg/dL (2.6 mmol/L)
UKPDST2DM CVD risk ≈ age-matched with prior CVD

Impact: Decades of guidelines treating all diabetes as secondary prevention for lipids.

3. Why the Concept Evolved

LimitationEvidence
Not all diabetes equalYoung T2DM, short duration, no complications = lower risk
Risk continuumDuration, HbA1c, complications, CKD modify risk enormously
T1DM vs T2DMT1DM longer duration, earlier onset, different risk trajectory
Risk calculatorsQRISK3/ASCVD Pooled Cohort include diabetes as variable, not automatic equivalent
Over-treatment riskLow-risk elderly/frail may not benefit from high-intensity statin

4. Current Guideline Approach

NICE (CG181 / NG28)

  • Does NOT use "risk equivalent" label
  • Uses QRISK3 (includes diabetes type + duration)
  • T1DM >40y OR >10y duration OR microvascular complications → atorvastatin 20mg (like primary prevention high risk)
  • Established CVD → atorvastatin 80mg (secondary prevention)

ADA/ACC (2018/2024)

  • Diabetes age 40–75 = high risk → high-intensity statin
  • Diabetes + ASCVD = very high risk → high-intensity + ezetimibe/PCSK9i if LDL not <1.8
  • Risk enhancers (albuminuria, eGFR<60, retinopathy, neuropathy, duration >10/20y) guide intensity

ESC/EAS (2019)

  • Diabetes + CVD / target organ damage / >20y duration = Very High Risk (LDL <1.4)
  • Diabetes age >40, no complications = High Risk (LDL <1.8)
  • Diabetes age <40, no complications = Moderate Risk (LDL <2.6)

5. Subgroups Treated as "Equivalent to Secondary Prevention"

SubgroupGuidelineRationale
T1DM, age >40NICE (20mg), ADA (high-intensity), ESC (very high)Long duration, cumulative exposure
T1DM, duration >10yNICE, ADA, ESCRisk ↑ exponentially with duration
T1DM, microvascular complicationsNICE, ESCRenal/retinal damage = systemic vascular disease
T2DM, age >40ADA (high-intensity), ESC (high)Age + diabetes = high 10-year risk
T2DM, established CVDAll (secondary prevention)True secondary prevention
T2DM, CKD G3+ / A2/A3All (CKD risk enhancer)Renal dysfunction = CVD risk multiplier

6. Risk Continuum — Practical Application

Clinical Scenario10-Year CVD RiskStatin IntensityBP TargetAspirin
T1DM, 25y, 5y duration, no complicationsLow (~3–5%)Moderate (NICE) / High (ADA)<130/80No
T1DM, 50y, 25y duration, no complicationsHigh (~15–20%)High (all)<130/80Consider (ADA)
T1DM, 45y, 20y duration, retinopathyVery High (~25%+)High + ezetimibe (ESC)<130/80Consider
T2DM, 55y, 5y duration, no complicationsModerate (~10–15%)Moderate/High<140/90 (NICE) / <130/80Consider if ≥10%
T2DM, 65y, 15y duration, CKD G3, prior MIVery High (>30%)High 80mg + ezetimibe<130/80Yes (secondary)

7. Implications for Drug Choices

Drug Class"Risk Equivalent" EraCurrent Risk-Stratified Approach
StatinHigh-intensity for ALL diabetesIntensity by risk tier (moderate for low-risk young; high for most; very high for ASCVD/CKD)
BP<130/80 for allNICE: <140/90 uncomplicated; <130/80 if CKD/albuminuria
AspirinPrimary prevention for allSelective (high CV risk + low bleed risk only)
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RANot availableNow preferred for T2DM + CVD/CKD/HF regardless of HbA1c

8. Communicating Risk to Patients

AvoidUse Instead
"You have the same risk as someone who had a heart attack""Your diabetes increases your heart risk. Your 10-year risk is X%. Here's what we can do to lower it."
"You need the strongest treatment because you have diabetes""Based on your age, diabetes duration, and [complications/none], guidelines recommend [moderate/high] statin. Let's discuss."
Binary "equivalent" languageContinuous risk with specific modifiers

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Haffner 1998T2DM no prior MI = 20.2% 7-yr MI risk ≈ non-DM with prior MI (18.8%)
Historical label"Diabetes = CHD risk equivalent" (NCEP ATP III)
Current viewRisk continuum — not all diabetes equal; use calculators + enhancers
NICE approachQRISK3 includes diabetes type + duration; no binary equivalent
T1DM high-risk criteriaAge >40 OR duration >10y OR microvascular complications
T2DM high-riskAge >40 (ADA high-intensity statin); QRISK3 ≥10% (NICE)
ESC very high riskDiabetes + CVD / target organ damage / >20y duration → LDL <1.4
Statin intensityMatch to risk tier, not diabetes label alone
AspirinNo longer primary prevention for all — selective only
SGLT2i/GLP-1Now foundational for T2DM + CVD/CKD/HF — independent of glucose

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD)|Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD)]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Statin therapy in diabetes (intensity, targets)]], [[Aspirin primary prevention]], [[BP targets in diabetes (NICE, ADA, ESC)]], [[ASCVD risk calculators (QRISK3, ASCVD Pooled Cohort)]]

Topic Overview

Diabetes as Cardiovascular Risk Equivalent

2. Historical Concept
3. Why the Concept Evolved
4. Current Guideline Approach
NICE (CG181 / NG28)
ADA/ACC (2018/2024)
ESC/EAS (2019)
5. Subgroups Treated as "Equivalent to Secondary Prevention"

▸ 21.7.14 Glp 1 Ra In Hf Fight Step Hfpef

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, glp-1-ra, hf, fight, step-hfpef, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - GLP-1 RA neutral for HFrEF (FIGHT); semaglutide benefits HFpEF with obesity (STEP-HFpEF); weight loss, symptom improvement, NT-proBNP ↓; not first-line for HF (SGLT2i preferred); liraglutide neutral (LIVE)"

see_also: ["HFrEF and HFpEF in diabetes", "SGLT2 inhibitors in HF (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR, DELIVER)", "DKD management (ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, finerenone, GLP-1 RA)", "GLP-1 receptor agonists"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

GLP-1 Receptor Agonists in Heart Failure (FIGHT, STEP-HFpEF)

[!info]
GLP-1 RA in HF: nuanced — HFrEF: neutral (FIGHT: liraglutide no benefit); HFpEF + obesity: benefit (STEP-HFpEF: semaglutide); weight loss drives improvement; not first-line for HF (SGLT2i preferred); safe in HF (no harm).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Summarise FIGHT trial (liraglutide in HFrEF)
  • [ ] Summarise STEP-HFpEF trial (semaglutide in HFpEF + obesity)
  • [ ] Summarise LIVE trial (liraglutide post-MI)
  • [ ] Apply GLP-1 RA positioning vs SGLT2i in HF
  • [ ] Identify patients who may benefit (HFpEF + obesity)

2. Key Trials

FIGHT Trial (2017) — Liraglutide in HFrEF

AspectDetail
Population300 patients, HFrEF (EF ≤40%), advanced (recent hospitalisation), ±T2DM (mostly non-diabetic)
InterventionLiraglutide 1.8mg daily vs placebo (180 days)
Primary OutcomeHierarchical clinical outcome (death, HF hosp, time to event)
ResultNeutral (HR 1.00, p=0.50); no benefit in any subgroup
SafetyMore serious AEs (GI); no increase in CV mortality

Conclusion: Liraglutide not beneficial in HFrEF — do not use for HF treatment.

STEP-HFpEF Trial (2023) — Semaglutide in HFpEF + Obesity

AspectDetail
Population529 patients, HFpEF (EF ≥45%), BMI ≥30, NYHA II–III, ±T2DM (mostly non-diabetic)
InterventionSemaglutide 2.4mg weekly (max dose) vs placebo (52 weeks)
Primary Outcomes1) Change in KCCQ-CSS (symptom score) 2) Change in 6-minute walk distance
ResultsKCCQ-CSS: +7.8 points (p<0.001); 6MWD: +21.5m (p<0.001); Weight: −13.3%; NT-proBNP: −42%
SafetyGI side effects (nausea); no increase in serious AEs

Conclusion: Semaglutide 2.4mg improves symptoms, exercise capacity, weight, NT-proBNP in HFpEF + obesity.

LIVE Trial (2023) — Liraglutide Post-MI

AspectDetail
PopulationPost-MI with EF ≤40% and/or diabetes, high-risk
ResultNeutral for CV death/HF hosp; weight loss, HbA1c improvement
ImplicationNo HF benefit post-MI

3. Mechanism & Physiology

MechanismEffect
Weight loss↓ Preload, ↓ afterload, ↓ cardiac workload (major in STEP-HFpEF)
Direct cardiac effects↑ Myocardial glucose uptake, ↓ lipotoxicity, ↓ oxidative stress (preclinical)
Haemodynamic↓ BP (2–4 mmHg), ↓ HR (2–3 bpm) — modest
Anti-inflammatory↓ CRP, IL-6, TNF-α
Renal↓ Albuminuria, stable eGFR

Key difference from SGLT2i: GLP-1 RA benefit largely weight-mediated; SGLT2i has direct cardiorenal mechanisms independent of glucose/weight.

4. Positioning in HF Guidelines (ESC 2023 / ACC/AHA)

HF TypeSGLT2iGLP-1 RA
HFrEFClass I, Level A (dapa/empa)Not recommended (FIGHT neutral)
HFmrEF (41–49%)Class I, Level AConsider if obese + HFpEF features
HFpEF (EF ≥50%)Class I, Level A (dapa/empa)Semaglutide 2.4mg if BMI ≥30 (STEP-HFpEF) — emerging

NICE: SGLT2i first-line for HF; GLP-1 RA for weight management in T2DM + obesity.

5. Practical Algorithm — GLP-1 RA in HF

Patient with HF + T2DM/Obesity
    │
    ├─► HFrEF (EF ≤40%)
    │     → **SGLT2i first-line** (dapa/empa)
    │     → GLP-1 RA **not for HF** (use for glucose/weight if needed)
    │
    ├─► HFpEF (EF ≥50%)
    │     → **SGLT2i first-line** (dapa/empa)
    │     → If **BMI ≥30** + symptomatic (NYHA II–III):
    │           **Add semaglutide 2.4mg** (STEP-HFpEF)
    │
    └─► T2DM + HF (any EF) — glucose-lowering choice
          1. **SGLT2i** (HF + renal + glucose)
          2. **GLP-1 RA** (add if HbA1c not controlled + obesity)
          3. Avoid DPP-4i (saxa/alo ↑ HF hosp), TZDs (contraindicated)

6. Adverse Effects in HF Context

Adverse EffectRelevance to HF
GI (nausea, vomiting)May cause volume depletion → worsen HF; start low, go slow
TachycardiaSemaglutide ↑ HR 2–3 bpm; monitor AF
HypoglycaemiaLow risk alone; ↑ with insulin/sulfonylurea
PancreatitisRare; avoid if history
Medullary thyroid cancerContraindicated if MEN2 / family history

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
FIGHT trialLiraglutide in HFrEF → neutral (no benefit)
STEP-HFpEFSemaglutide 2.4mg in HFpEF + BMI ≥30 → ↑ KCCQ, ↑ 6MWD, −13% weight, −42% NT-proBNP
LIVE trialLiraglutide post-MI → neutral
SGLT2i vs GLP-1 RASGLT2i = first-line for HF (all EF); GLP-1 RA = obesity-driven benefit in HFpEF
HFrEF + GLP-1 RANot recommended (FIGHT neutral)
HFpEF + obesitySemaglutide 2.4mg (STEP-HFpEF)
Weight lossPrimary mechanism in STEP-HFpEF (13% loss)
NT-proBNP−42% with semaglutide in STEP-HFpEF
PositioningSGLT2i first; GLP-1 RA add-on for glucose/weight/obesity-HFpEF
Semaglutide dose2.4mg weekly (max dose, not 1mg) for HFpEF

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

2. Key Trials

STEP-HFpEF Trial (2023) — Semaglutide in HFpEF + Obesity
LIVE Trial (2023) — Liraglutide Post-MI
3. Mechanism & Physiology

[!info]

AspectDetails
HFrEF (EF ≤40%)SGLT2i: DAPA-HF (dapagliflozin), EMPEROR-Reduced (empagliflozin) → ↓CV death/HF hosp
HFpEF (EF ≥50%)SGLT2i: EMPEROR-Preserved (empagliflozin), DELIVER (dapagliflozin) → ↓HF hosp · GLP-1 RA: STEP-HFpEF (semaglutide) → ↑symptoms/exercise
HFmrEF (41-49%)SGLT2i benefit across spectrum
Diabetic cardiomyopathyDiastolic dysfunction, fibrosis, microvascular disease, metabolic inflexibility

▸ 21.7.17 Hfref And Hfpef In Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, hfref, hfpef, heart-failure, diabetes, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Diabetes = independent HF risk factor (2–5×); HFpEF more common in T2DM; HFrEF also ↑; diabetic cardiomyopathy (metabolic, structural); SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA foundation; NT-proBNP interpretation in diabetes"

see_also: ["SGLT2 inhibitors in HF (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR, DELIVER)", "GLP-1 RA in HF (FIGHT, STEP-HFpEF)", "Cardiovascular risk in diabetes", "Diabetes in CKD and dialysis"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

HFrEF and HFpEF in Diabetes

[!info]
Diabetes = 2–5× HF risk; HFpEF more common in T2DM (obesity, HTN, metabolic); HFrEF also increased; diabetic cardiomyopathy = metabolic + structural remodelling; NT-proBNP lower in obesity/diabetes — adjust thresholds; SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA = disease-modifying.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define HF risk in diabetes (HFrEF vs HFpEF)
  • [ ] Explain diabetic cardiomyopathy pathophysiology
  • [ ] Diagnose HF in diabetes (adjusted NT-proBNP thresholds)
  • [ ] Apply guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) including SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
  • [ ] Manage HFpEF specifically in diabetes

2. Epidemiology

ParameterData
HF prevalence in T2DM15–25% (vs 1–2% non-diabetes)
Relative risk2–5× higher
HFpEF proportion50–60% of HF in T2DM (vs 40–50% non-diabetes)
HFrEF proportion40–50%
Mortality2–3× higher vs HF without diabetes
Hospitalisation3–4× higher HF admission rate

3. Diabetic Cardiomyopathy — Pathophysiology

MechanismFeatures
MetabolicImpaired glucose oxidation → ↑ fatty acid oxidation → ↓ cardiac efficiency; insulin resistance; lipotoxicity
StructuralLV hypertrophy (concentric > eccentric), fibrosis (interstitial > replacement), diastolic dysfunction
MicrovascularCoronary microvascular dysfunction (↓ flow reserve) without epicardial CAD
AutonomicCV autonomic neuropathy → resting tachycardia, exercise intolerance, silent ischaemia
InflammatorySystemic inflammation (IL-6, TNF-α, CRP) → myocardial inflammation

Diagnosis: HF symptoms + diabetes + no other cause (CAD, HTN, valvular) + evidence of LV dysfunction/structure.

4. Diagnosis in Diabetes — NT-proBNP Nuances

Standard NT-proBNP Cutoffs (ESC 2021)

SettingHFrEFHFpEF
Acute>300 pg/mL>300 pg/mL
Chronic>125 pg/mL>125 pg/mL

Diabetes/Obesity Adjustment

  • Obesity (BMI >35): NT-proBNP 30–50% lower for same HF severity
  • Diabetes: Independent NT-proBNP lowering effect
  • eGFR <60: NT-proBNP ↑ (renal clearance)

Practical approach: Use lower threshold in diabetes/obesity (e.g., >50–75 pg/mL chronic); combine with clinical assessment + echo.

Echo Parameters for HFpEF (HFA-PEFF / H₂FPEF Score)

ParameterThreshold
E/e'>14 (average) or >15 (septal)
LA volume index>34 mL/m²
TR velocity>2.8 m/s
RV dysfunctionTAPSE <17mm, S' <10 cm/s
Global longitudinal strain>-16% (impaired)

5. Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT) in Diabetes

HFrEF (LVEF ≤40%) — Four Pillars + SGLT2i

ClassAgentsDiabetes Consideration
ACEi/ARB/ARNISacubitril/valsartan (ARNI) preferredARNI > ACEi (PARADIGM-HF: ↓ HF hosp 21%, ↓ new diabetes)
Beta-blockerBisoprolol, carvedilol, nebivololCarvedilol may improve insulin sensitivity
MRASpironolactone, eplerenoneMonitor K+ (diabetes + CKD + MRA = hyperkalaemia risk)
SGLT2iDapagliflozin, EmpagliflozineGFR ≥20; Class I, Level A; ↓ CV death/HF hosp 25–30%
VericiguatIf symptomatic on GDMTVICTORIA trial — ↓ CV death/HF hosp

HFpEF (LVEF >40%) — Limited Evidence, SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA

ClassAgentsEvidence
SGLT2iDapagliflozin, EmpagliflozinDELIVER, EMPEROR-Preserved — ↓ CV death/HF hosp 18–21% regardless of EF
GLP-1 RASemaglutideSTEP-HFpEF, FIGHT — ↓ symptoms, weight, NT-proBNP (symptomatic HFpEF, BMI ≥30)
MRASpironolactoneTOPCAT: neutral overall; ↓ hosp in Americas subgroup
ARNISacubitril/valsartanPARAGON-HF: neutral (trend in EF <57%)
DiureticsLoop + thiazideSymptom relief only

6. Specific Diabetes Management in HF

IssueRecommendation
MetforminContinue if eGFR ≥30 (historical lactic acidosis concern disproven)
SGLT2iPreferred — HF + renal + glycaemic benefit; eGFR ≥20
GLP-1 RAConsider for HFpEF + obesity (semaglutide); no HF harm (FIGHT: neutral CV)
DPP-4iAvoid saxagliptin/alogliptin (SAVOR/EXAMINE: ↑ HF hosp)
TZDsContraindicated (fluid retention → HF exacerbation)
SulfonylureasNeutral HF; avoid if hypoglycaemia risk
InsulinNeutral HF; weight gain concern

7. HFpEF in Diabetes — Specific Management

PriorityAction
1. SGLT2iDapagliflozin/empagliflozin — first disease-modifying therapy for HFpEF
2. Weight lossSemaglutide (STEP-HFpEF: ↓ symptoms, 6-min walk, weight, NT-proBNP)
3. BP control<130/80; avoid excessive diuresis (preload dependence)
4. Rate controlBeta-blocker if AF; avoid excessive bradycardia
5. ComorbiditiesTreat anaemia, sleep apnoea, thyroid, iron deficiency
6. ExerciseCardiac rehab → improves VO₂ max, QoL

8. Monitoring in HF + Diabetes

ParameterFrequency
NT-proBNP3–6 monthly (trend more than absolute)
eGFR, K+1–2w post SGLT2i/MRA/ARNI change; then 3–6m
HbA1c6 monthly (target individualised; avoid hypoglycaemia)
WeightEach visit (fluid status + semaglutide effect)
Echo6–12 monthly if stable; sooner if decompensation

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Diabetes HF risk2–5× higher; HFpEF more common in T2DM
Diabetic cardiomyopathyMetabolic + structural + microvascular + autonomic
NT-proBNP in obesity/diabetesFalsely low — use lower thresholds (chronic >50–75 pg/mL)
HFrEF 4 pillars + SGLT2iARNI, BB, MRA, SGLT2i — all Class I
SGLT2i in HFeGFR ≥20; DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced, DELIVER, EMPEROR-Preserved
HFpEF treatmentSGLT2i (first disease-modifying); semaglutide if obese
DPP-4i to avoidSaxagliptin, alogliptin (↑ HF hosp)
TZDs in HFContraindicated (fluid retention)
Metformin in HFSafe if eGFR ≥30
Semaglutide HFpEFSTEP-HFpEF: ↓ symptoms, weight, NT-proBNP

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

▸ 21.7.19 Pad Screening Abi

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, abi, pad-screening, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - ABI = ankle-brachial index; screen diabetics ≥50y (or <50y with risk factors); diabetes calcifies vessels → ABI >1.3 = non-compressible (use TBI); <0.9 = PAD; <0.4/0.5 = CLI; annual in high-risk"

see_also: ["Critical limb ischaemia", "Diabetic foot disease/Foot risk classification", "Diabetic neuropathy/Distal symmetric polyneuropathy", "Diabetes in CKD and dialysis"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-2026

PAD Screening (ABI)

[!info]
ABI = Ankle-Brachial Index = highest ankle SBP / highest brachial SBP; screen diabetics ≥50y (or <50y with risk factors); diabetes calcifies vessels → ABI >1.3 = non-compressible (use TBI); <0.9 = PAD; <0.4 (0.5 in DM) = CLI.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Perform and interpret ABI correctly
  • [ ] Apply screening criteria for diabetes population
  • [ ] Identify non-compressible arteries and use TBI
  • [ ] Interpret ABI values for PAD and CLI diagnosis
  • [ ] Understand limitations and alternative tests

2. ABI Technique

Equipment

  • Doppler probe (8–10 MHz) + sphygmomanometer
  • Patient: Supine, rested 10 minutes, room warm

Procedure

  1. Brachial pressures: Measure both arms (right & left) — use higher as reference
  2. Ankle pressures: Measure dorsalis pedis (DP) and posterior tibial (PT) on each leg
  3. Calculation per leg:
  • ABI = Highest ankle SBP (DP or PT) / Highest brachial SBP
  1. Record: Right ABI, Left ABI

Interpretation (Standard)

ABI ValueInterpretation
>1.3 (or >1.4)Non-compressible arteries (medial calcification) — use TBI
1.0–1.3Normal
0.9–1.0Borderline — consider further testing if symptomatic
0.4–0.9PAD (mild 0.7–0.9; moderate 0.4–0.7)
<0.4 (or <0.5 in diabetes)Critical Limb Ischaemia (CLI)

3. Diabetes-Specific Adjustments

IssueImpactSolution
Medial arterial calcification (Mönckeberg's)Vessels non-compressible → falsely high ABI (>1.3)Use Toe-Brachial Index (TBI)
Threshold for CLIStandard <0.4; diabetes <0.50 (more sensitive)Use lower threshold
Sensitivity in diabetesABI <0.9 misses ~30% PAD (calcification)TBI + clinical assessment

TBI (Toe-Brachial Index)

  • Method: Photoplethysmography (PPG) cuff on great toe + brachial
  • Normal: >0.70
  • PAD: <0.70
  • CLI: <0.25 (or <0.30 in diabetes)
  • Advantage: Toe arteries do not calcify (no media)

4. Screening Recommendations (NICE / ESC / ADA)

PopulationScreening Frequency
All T2DM ≥50yBaseline + annual (or 2-yearly if normal)
T1DM ≥50y or ≥10y durationBaseline + annual
Diabetes <50y + risk factorsBaseline + risk-based
Risk factors: smoking, HTN, dyslipidaemia, CKD, prior CVD, foot ulcer
Prior PAD / revascularisationAnnual
Prior amputationAnnual (contralateral surveillance)

NICE CG147: Consider ABI in diabetes with leg symptoms or foot ulcer.

5. Alternative / Adjunctive Tests

TestIndicationAdvantage
TBIABI >1.3 (non-compressible); CLI confirmationNot affected by calcification
TcPO2CLI assessment; wound healing predictionMicrocirculation; <30 mmHg = poor healing
Duplex UltrasoundAnatomic localisation; planning revascularisationNon-invasive, real-time
CT AngiographyPre-procedural planning; complex anatomyHigh resolution; contrast nephropathy risk
MR AngiographyContrast allergy; CKD (gadolinium caution)No ionising radiation
Pulse Volume Recording (PVR)Qualitative waveform; ABI unmeasurableSimple, bedside

6. Management Based on ABI

ABI ResultAction
>1.3Repeat with TBI; assess clinically; consider CTA if symptomatic
1.0–1.3Reassure; annual rescreen; risk factor optimisation
0.9–1.0Borderline; repeat in 6–12 months; optimise risk factors
0.7–0.9Mild PAD — risk factor modification (statin, antiplatelet, BP, smoking, exercise)
0.4–0.7Moderate PAD — vascular referral; supervised exercise; consider imaging
<0.4 (<0.5 DM)CLI — urgent vascular MDT; WIfI classification; revascularisation

7. Diabetic Foot Integration

Foot Risk (IWGDF)ABI Role
Risk 0–1Annual screen
Risk 2 (LOPS + PAD)ABI confirms PAD; 3–6m podiatry MDT; vascular review
Risk 3 (prior ulcer)ABI/TBI annually; surveillance for CLI
Active ulcerABI/TBI at presentation; urgent vascular if CLI

8. Limitations of ABI

LimitationImpact
Calcification (ABI >1.3)Misses PAD in 30% diabetics — TBI mandatory
Operator dependencyRequires training; standardise protocol
Resting onlyMay miss functional limitation — exercise ABI if equivocal
Inflow vs outflowDoes not localise — duplex/CTA needed for intervention

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
ABI formulaHighest ankle SBP / Highest brachial SBP
Normal ABI1.0–1.3
PAD threshold<0.9
Diabetes CLI threshold<0.50 (vs 0.40 standard)
Non-compressibleABI >1.3 (or >1.4) = medial calcification → use TBI
TBI normal>0.70
TBI CLI<0.25–0.30
Screening age≥50y (or <50y with risk factors)
Diabetes calcification↓ ABI sensitivity — TBI essential
Exercise ABIIf resting borderline (0.9–1.0) + symptoms

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Critical limb ischaemia]], [[Diabetic foot disease/Foot risk classification]], [[Distal symmetric polyneuropathy (DSPN)]], [[Diabetes in CKD and dialysis]]

Topic Overview

PAD Screening (ABI
1. Learning Objectives
