▸ 21.8.29 Gfr Categories G1 G5

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, gfr-categories, ckd-epi, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - G1–G5 categories; CKD-EPI 2021 equation (race-free); chronicity >3 months; drug dosing adjustments by eGFR; nephrology referral <30"

see_also: ["Diabetic kidney disease staging (KDIGO)", "Albuminuria categories (A1/A2/A3)", "Diabetic nephropathy", "DKD management"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

GFR Categories (G1–G5)

[!info]
KDIGO GFR categories (G1–G5) based on CKD-EPI 2021 creatinine equation (race-free); requires chronicity >3 months; determines drug dosing, monitoring, referral.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Name and define all 5 GFR categories
  • [ ] Apply CKD-EPI equation and interpret eGFR
  • [ ] Adjust diabetes medications by eGFR
  • [ ] Identify nephrology referral thresholds

2. KDIGO GFR Categories

CategoryeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)DescriptionClinical Implications
G1≥90Normal / HighMonitor if risk factors (diabetes, HTN); drug dosing normal
G260–89Mildly decreasedMonitor annually; screen for albuminuria; drug dosing normal
G3a45–59Mild-moderate decreased6-monthly monitoring; adjust renally-cleared drugs; avoid NSAIDs
G3b30–44Moderate-severe decreased6-monthly; metformin max 1g BD (NICE) / contraindicated (some); nephrology referral
G415–29Severely decreased3–6 monthly; metformin contraindicated; SGLT2i OK (dapa/empa ≥20); RRT planning; mandatory nephrology
G5<15Kidney failureNephrology mandatory; RRT (dialysis/transplant) or conservative; adjust all drugs

3. CKD-EPI 2021 Equation (Race-Free)

Variables: Serum creatinine (µmol/L), age, sex

  • No race coefficient (removed 2021)
  • Preferred over MDRD (more accurate at higher GFR)
  • Cystatin C equation available (eGFRcys, eGFRcr-cys) — use if creatinine unreliable (amputees, muscle wasting, high meat diet)

Chronicity: eGFR <60 on ≥2 occasions ≥90 days apart = CKD.

4. Drug Dosing Adjustments by GFR Category (Key Diabetes Drugs)

DrugG1 (≥90)G2 (60–89)G3a (45–59)G3b (30–44)G4 (15–29)G5 (<15)
MetforminFullFullMax 1g BDContraindicated (NICE)ContraindicatedContraindicated
SulfonylureasFullGliclazide preferredAvoid glibenclamide/glimepirideAvoidAvoidAvoid
DPP-4iFullFullDose reduce (saxa/alo/vilda); Linagliptin no adjustDose reduceDose reduceDose reduce
SGLT2iFullFullDapa/Empa OK (≥20); Cana avoid <45Dapa/Empa OK (≥20)Dapa/Empa OK (≥20)Avoid
GLP-1 RAFullFullFullFullFullFull
FinerenoneFullFullFulleGFR ≥25eGFR ≥25Avoid
ACEi/ARBFullFullMonitor K+/Cr 1–2wMonitor K+/Cr 1–2wMonitor K+/Cr 1–2wSpecialist
InsulinStandard↓ 10–20%↓ 20–30%↓ 30–50%↓ 50%+↓ 50%+

5. Monitoring by GFR Category

CategoryUACR + eGFRBPHbA1cOther
G1–2Annual<130/803–6 monthlyLipids annual
G3a6-monthly<130/803–6 monthlyK+, bicarbonate, phosphate, Ca, PTH annual
G3b6-monthly<130/803–6 monthlyAs above + vitamin D, bone profile
G43–6 monthly<130/803–6 monthlyAs above + RRT planning, access
G5Monthly (dialysis)<130/803-monthlyFull dialysis bloods

6. Special Considerations

SituationeGFR Interpretation
Acute kidney injury (AKI)eGFR not valid — use KDIGO AKI criteria (Cr rise, UO)
Rapidly progressiveeGFR decline >5 mL/min/yr → urgent nephrology
Muscle wasting/amputeeCreatinine-based eGFR overestimates — use cystatin C
PregnancyeGFR ↑ physiologically — use pregnancy-specific ranges
Elderly/frailG3a may be "normal ageing" — assess trajectory, not single value

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
GFR categoriesG1 ≥90, G2 60–89, G3a 45–59, G3b 30–44, G4 15–29, G5 <15
CKD-EPI 2021Race-free; preferred over MDRD
ChronicityeGFR <60 on ≥2 occasions ≥90 days apart
Metformin cutoffG3a: max 1g BD; G3b (<45): contraindicated (NICE)
SGLT2i in CKDDapagliflozin/Empagliflozin ≥20; Canagliflozin ≥45
LinagliptinNo dose adjustment at any eGFR
FinerenoneeGFR ≥25 + A2/A3
ACEi/ARB monitoringK+/Cr at 1–2w after start/change, then 6m
Nephrology referraleGFR <30 (G4) or ACR >70 or rapid decline
Cystatin CUse if creatinine unreliable (low muscle mass, amputees)

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Topic Overview

GFR Categories (G1–G5
1. Learning Objectives
2. KDIGO GFR Categories
3. CKD-EPI 2021 Equation (Race-Free)
4. Drug Dosing Adjustments by GFR Category (Key Diabetes Drugs)
5. Monitoring by GFR Category
6. Special Considerations
7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

▸ 21.8.3 Amputation Prevention And Multidisciplinary Care

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, amputation-prevention, mdt, multidisciplinary, revascularisation, offloading, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - 85% amputations preventable; MDT reduces major amputation 50–80%; urgent vascular referral for critical limb ischaemia; TCC offloading; prosthesis and rehabilitation"

see_also: ["Diabetic foot disease/Foot risk classification", "Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) management", "Charcot neuroarthropathy", "Peripheral arterial disease/Critical limb ischaemia"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Amputation Prevention and Multidisciplinary Care

[!info]
85% of diabetes-related amputations are preventable — MDT care reduces major amputation rate 50–80%; key pillars: early recognition, offloading, infection control, revascularisation, MDT coordination.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State amputation prevention evidence (MDT impact)
  • [ ] Define criteria for urgent MDT referral
  • [ ] Apply "Toe and Flow" principles (wound + vascular)
  • [ ] Understand amputation levels and prosthesis
  • [ ] Coordinate rehabilitation and psychosocial support

2. Epidemiology & Impact

StatisticValue
Lifetime DFU risk19–34%
DFU preceding amputation85%
Annual amputation rate (diabetes)0.5–1.0% (vs 0.02% non-diabetes)
Major amputation (above ankle)20–30% of all amputations
5-year mortality post-major amputation50–70% (≈ aggressive cancer)
MDT reduces major amputation50–80% (multiple studies)
Cost: amputation vs MDTAmputation £15,000–25,000 + £5,000/yr; MDT saves £££

3. "Toe and Flow" — Two Pillars of Salvage

PillarActionUrgency
TOE (Wound)Offloading (TCC), debridement, infection control, wound careDays
FLOW (Vascular)Assessment → revascularisation (endovascular/bypass)Hours–Days

Neither alone suffices — revascularisation without offloading fails; offloading without flow fails.

4. Urgent MDT Referral Criteria (NICE NG19 / IWGDF)

Refer within 24–48 hours if ANY:

  • Active foot ulcer (any grade)
  • Spreading infection (erythema >2cm, lymphangitis, systemic signs)
  • Critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, tissue loss, ABI <0.5, TBI <0.25)
  • Active Charcot (hot, swollen, deformed foot)
  • Deep abscess / necrotising fasciitis (surgical emergency)
  • Gangrene (wet or dry)

MDT must include: Diabetologist + Podiatrist + Vascular Surgeon + Orthotist + Microbiologist/ID.

5. MDT Structure & Pathways

Core MDT Members

RoleResponsibility
DiabetologistMedical lead; glycaemic control; comorbidities; antibiotics
PodiatristWound assessment; debridement; offloading (TCC); footwear
Vascular SurgeonRevascularisation (endovascular/open); amputation surgery
OrthotistCustom footwear; insoles; TCC application; orthoses
Microbiologist/IDAntibiotic stewardship; culture interpretation
Orthopaedic SurgeonBony surgery; Charcot reconstruction; amputation
Tissue Viability NurseComplex wound dressings; NPWT
RadiologistImaging (Duplex, CTA, MRI, plain films)
Plastic SurgeonSoft tissue coverage; flaps
Rehab/ProsthetistPost-amputation rehabilitation

Pathway Tiers

TierPopulationFrequencyTeam
CommunityRisk 0–1Annual screenGP, Practice Nurse, Community Podiatry
Specialist Foot ProtectionRisk 23–6 monthlyPodiatry-led MDT (diabetologist access)
Multidisciplinary Foot ClinicRisk 3, active disease1–3 monthly / urgentFull MDT (all core members)

6. Amputation Levels & Indications

LevelIndicationFunctional Outcome
Toe (Digital)Localised gangrene/ulcer; good perfusionExcellent; minimal gait change
Ray (Toe + Metatarsal)Forefoot ulcer/gangrene extending to metatarsalGood; forefoot loading altered
Transmetatarsal (TMA)Forefoot loss; viable heel padGood with custom shoe; equinus risk
Lisfranc / ChopartMidfoot lossFair; requires AFO/orthosis
Below-Knee (BKA)Non-salvageable foot; adequate popliteal flowBest major amputation for prosthesis
Through-Knee (TKA)Knee joint preservedGood prosthetic fit; cosmesis
Above-Knee (AKA)Popliteal disease; proximal infectionHigher energy cost; harder rehab

Decision principle: Most distal level with viable tissue + adequate perfusion.

7. Pre-Amputation Optimisation

ActionTiming
Glycaemic optimisationHbA1c <64 (8.0%) if possible
NutritionProtein 1.5g/kg; vitamin C, zinc; correct malnutrition
Smoking cessationMandatory — 6 weeks pre-op ideal
Vascular imagingDuplex/CTA — define amputation level
Prosthetist reviewPre-op — discuss expectations, phantom pain
PsychosocialCounselling; peer support; body image
AntibioticsPerioperative per infection grade

8. Post-Amputation Rehabilitation

PhaseTimelineGoals
Acute0–2 weeksWound healing; pain control; oedema management (shrinker sock); mobility (wheelchair)
Pre-prosthetic2–6 weeksStump shaping; strengthening; balance; desensitisation; phantom pain management (gabapentin, mirror therapy)
Prosthetic training6–12 weeksCast/measuring; fitting; gait training; stairs; community reintegration
LifelongOngoingAnnual prosthesis review; skin care; contralateral foot protection (Risk 3); vocational support

9. Quality Indicators (NICE QS6 / National Diabetes Foot Audit)

IndicatorTarget
MDT referral within 24h (active disease)>90%
Major amputation rate<0.5/1000 diabetes population
Minor:Major amputation ratio>1:1 (ideally >2:1)
Revascularisation before major amputation>80%
TCC use for plantar ulcers>70% eligible
Foot risk documented100%

10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Preventable amputations85% (preceded by DFU)
MDT impact↓ Major amputation 50–80%
Urgent referralActive ulcer/infection/ischaemia/Charcot → MDT 24–48h
"Toe and Flow"Wound care + revascularisation — both needed
Critical limb ischaemiaRest pain + tissue loss + ABI <0.5 / TBI <0.25
Best major amputationBKA (below-knee) — best prosthetic outcome
TCC for plantar ulcersGold standard offloading; non-removable
5-year mortality post-BKA50–70% — counsel patient/family
Phantom painGabapentin, mirror therapy, desensitisation
Quality indicatorMinor:Major amputation ratio >1:1

11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetic foot disease|Diabetic foot disease]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) classification]], [[Foot risk classification (IWGDF/Scottish)]], [[DFU management]], [[Charcot neuroarthropathy]], [[Peripheral arterial disease/Critical limb ischaemia]]

Topic Overview

Amputation Prevention and Multidisciplinary Care
1. Learning Objectives
2. Epidemiology & Impact
3. "Toe and Flow" — Two Pillars of Salvage
4. Urgent MDT Referral Criteria (NICE NG19 / IWGDF)
5. MDT Structure & Pathways
Core MDT Members
Pathway Tiers
6. Amputation Levels & Indications
7. Pre-Amputation Optimisation
8. Post-Amputation Rehabilitation
9. Quality Indicators (NICE QS6 / National Diabetes Foot Audit)
10. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
11. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.8.3 Classic Symptomatic Presentation

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe classic symptomatic presentation of T1DM
  • [ ] Recognise DKA at diagnosis (25-30%)
  • [ ] Differentiate from T2DM, LADA, stress hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria in symptomatic patients

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PresentationAcute/subacute onset (days-weeks)
DKA at diagnosis25-30% of T1DM children; higher in <5 years
Peak age10-14 years (bimodal: 4-7, 10-14)
Seasonal variationWinter peak (viral triggers)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

SymptomFrequencyMechanism
Polyuria95%Osmotic diuresis (glucose > renal threshold)
Polydipsia95%Thirst from dehydration/hyperosmolality
Weight loss80%Catabolism (lipolysis/proteolysis) despite polyphagia
Fatigue/lethargy70%Insulin deficiency -> impaired glucose utilisation
Blurred vision30%Osmotic lens swelling
Abdominal pain40% (if DKA)Ketosis, gastric stasis
Kussmaul breathingDKA onlyRespiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis
Ketotic breathDKAAcetone excretion
Red Flags: Ketosis at presentation -> T1DM/LADA; rapid weight loss + osmotic symptoms in adult -> LADA; pancreatic cancer (new-onset DM >50y + weight loss).

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureT1DM ClassicT2DMLADA
OnsetAcute (days-weeks)Insidious (months-years)Subacute
AgePeak 10-14y>40y (usually)>30y
BMINormal/lowOverweight/obeseNormal/low
KetosisCommon (25-30% DKA)RareUncommon
Autoantibodies2+ (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)NegativeGAD65+ (often single)
C-peptideLow/absentNormal/highLow-normal

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationT1DMT2DMLADA
Autoantibodies2+ positiveNegativeGAD65+ (single often)
C-peptideLow/absentNormal/highLow-normal
HbA1c>48 usuallyVariableVariable
KetonesPositive if DKANegativeUsually negative

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DMInsidious, obese, no ketosis, negative autoantibodies
LADAAdult, slow progression, GAD65+, initial non-insulin response
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, resolves, no prior dysglycaemia
MODY<25y, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer>50y, weight loss, new-onset DM, no autoantibodies

7. Management

Acute (DKA at presentation)

StepAction
1ABCDE assessment; IV access; labs (glucose, ketones, VBG, U&E, FBC)
2DKA protocol: fluids, FRII 0.1U/kg/hr, K+ replacement, monitor
3Once stable: transition to SC insulin (basal-bolus)

Chronic (after DKA resolution)

ComponentDetail
InsulinBasal-bolus MDI or CSII (see Insulin therapy note)
EducationCarb counting, sick day rules, hypoglycaemia management
CGMStrongly recommended (NICE: all T1DM)
Follow-upMDT (diabetologist, educator, dietitian, psychologist)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Classic triadPolyuria, polydipsia, weight loss (despite polyphagia)
DKA at diagnosis25-30% children; <5y highest risk; medical emergency
Symptom durationDays to weeks before presentation
Age peak4-7y and 10-14y (bimodal)
vs T2DMAcute, lean, ketosis, autoantibodies 2+
vs LADAAcute vs subacute; child/adolescent vs adult

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the classic presentation of T1DM?Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss despite polyphagia, fatigue; duration days-weeks
What percentage present in DKA?25-30% of children; higher in <5 years
How do you differentiate T1DM from T2DM at presentation?Acute onset, lean, ketosis/DKA, autoantibodies 2+, low C-peptide
What is the peak age of onset?Bimodal: 4-7 years and 10-14 years
What are the osmotic symptoms?Polyuria (glucose > renal threshold), polydipsia (dehydration), polyphagia (catabolism)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Weight loss = T1DM only?NO - can occur in uncontrolled T2DM, pancreatic cancer, hyperthyroidism
DKA = T1DM only?NO - T2DM can present in DKA (esp. Afro-Caribbean, ketosis-prone T2DM)

Mnemonic: T1DM-CLASSIC

  • T1DM: acute onset days-weeks
  • 1 Osmotic: polyuria, polydipsia
  • DKA: 25-30% at diagnosis
  • Mass loss: weight loss despite polyphagia
  • Classic: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue
  • Leading to DKA if missed
  • Age peak: 4-7, 10-14 bimodal
  • Symptoms: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue
  • Seasonal: winter peak (viral)
  • Insulin required immediately
  • Child/adolescent peak

11. Mind Map

T1DM Classic Presentation
Symptoms
Polyuria
Polydipsia
Weight loss (polyphagia)
Fatigue
DKA at Dx
25-30% children
<5y highest
Medical emergency
Age
Bimodal: 4-7, 10-14
vs T2DM
Acute vs insidious
Lean vs obese
Ketosis vs none
vs LADA
Acute vs subacute
Child vs adult

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionAcute onset T1DM: osmotic symptoms + weight loss +/- DKA
**Key Test"Autoantibodies (2+); ketones; VBG if DKA suspected
**Classification"DKA at dx 25-30%; classic osmotic triad
**Acute Mgmt"DKA protocol if ketotic; then basal-bolus insulin
**Chronic Mgmt"Basal-bolus/CSII, carb counting, CGM, education
**Key Score"2+ autoantibodies; C-peptide low
**Complications"DKA (acute); micro/macrovascular (long-term)
**Prognosis"Lifelong insulin; DKA mortality <1% if treated

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt
Definition & Epidemiology
Classification & Staging
Diagnosis & Investigations
Management (Acute)
Management (Chronic)
Complications
Viva Questions
DDx Distinctions
Mnemonics/Algorithms

▸ 21.8.31 Mononeuropathies And Radiculoplexus Neuropathy

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, mononeuropathy, cranial, radiculoplexus, femoral, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Cranial nerve palsies (III > VI > IV); diabetic amyotrophy (radiculoplexus neuropathy) = severe pain + weakness + wasting; femoral neuropathy; median (carpal tunnel) common; mononeuropathy multiplex = vasculitis until proven otherwise"

see_also: ["Distal symmetric polyneuropathy (DSPN)", "Autonomic neuropathy", "Neuropathic pain management", "Neuropathy screening (10g monofilament, vibration, ANNS)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Mononeuropathies and Radiculoplexus Neuropathy

[!info]
Mononeuropathy = single nerve injury (compression/ischaemia); Cranial III palsy (pupil-sparing) classic; Diabetic amyotrophy (radiculoplexus) = severe pain → weakness/wasting; Mononeuropathy multiplex = vasculitis until proven otherwise; Carpal tunnel common (median at wrist).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify common diabetic mononeuropathies (cranial, limb)
  • [ ] Diagnose diabetic amyotrophy (radiculoplexus neuropathy)
  • [ ] Differentiate mononeuropathy multiplex from vasculitis
  • [ ] Manage compression neuropathies (carpal tunnel, ulnar)
  • [ ] Apply prognosis and recovery timelines

2. Cranial Nerve Palsies

NerveFrequencyPresentationKey Feature
III (Oculomotor)Most commonAcute diplopia, ptosis, eye down/outPupil-sparing (parasympathetic fibres peripheral → spared in ischaemia)
VI (Abducens)CommonHorizontal diplopia (worse on lateral gaze), esotropiaNo ptosis, pupil normal
IV (Trochlear)RareVertical diplopia (worse on downgaze/reading), head tiltSuperior oblique palsy
VII (Facial)UncommonBell's palsy (LMN face weakness); Rule out stroke (forehead spared in UMN)Hyperacusis, taste loss (ant 2/3)

Diabetic III palsy: Pupil-sparing = ischaemic microvascular; Pupil-involving = compressive (aneurysm, tumour) → urgent imaging.

Recovery: 8–12 weeks (most); if not → re-image.

3. Limb Mononeuropathies (Compression / Ischaemia)

NerveSitePresentationProvocative Test
Median (Carpal Tunnel)Wrist (carpal tunnel)Nocturnal paraesthesia (thumb/index/middle), thenar wasting, weak abductionPhalen's (wrist flexion 60s), Tinel's (wrist)
UlnarElbow (cubital tunnel)Medial hand paraesthesia, Froment's sign (adductor pollicis weak), thenar wasting (late)Tinel's at elbow; elbow flexion test
RadialSpiral groove (humerus)Wrist drop; sensory loss dorsal hand (snuffbox)Tinel's spiral groove
Common PeronealFibular neckFoot drop; sensory loss lateral leg/dorsum footTinel's fibular neck
FemoralInguinal ligament / pelvisQuadriceps wasting, knee buckling, sensory loss anteromedial thighHip extension weakness; diabetic amyotrophy overlap
Lateral Cutaneous Nerve of ThighInguinal ligament (meralgia paresthetica)Burning/numbness anterolateral thighTinel's at ASIS

Diabetes ↑ risk for all compression neuropathies (nerve susceptibility + oedema).

4. Diabetic Amyotrophy (Radiculoplexus Neuropathy / Bruns-Garland)

FeatureDetails
Other namesProximal diabetic neuropathy, lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy
DemographicsOlder T2DM (mean 60y); often recent diagnosis or poor control; weight loss
OnsetSevere, asymmetric pain (hip, thigh, buttock) → weakness + wasting (quadriceps, glutei)
ProgressionPain peaks at 4–12 weeks → weakness plateau → slow recovery (6–24 months)
SignsQuadriceps wasting, knee jerk reduced/absent, hip flexion weak, sensory loss anteromedial thigh
CSFRaised protein (often >1g/L), normal cells (albuminocytological dissociation)
MRISTIR/T2 hyperintensity in lumbosacral plexus; nerve root enhancement
NCS/EMGActive denervation (fibrillations) in L2–L4 myotomes; reduced recruitment

Pathophysiology: Microvasculitis / immune-mediated (not pure ischaemia) — inflammatory infiltrate in nerve biopsy.

5. Diabetic Amyotrophy Management

StepIntervention
1. Pain controlPregabalin/gabapentin/duloxetine; opioids short-term if severe
2. Immunotherapy (if early/severe)IVIG 2g/kg over 5 days; IV methylprednisolone 1g × 3–5 days → oral taper; PLEX (refractory)
3. PhysiotherapyQuadriceps strengthening, gait training, falls prevention
4. Glycaemic controlOptimise HbA1c; no specific evidence for immunotherapy benefit, but standard
5. PrognosisIncomplete recovery common (residual weakness/wasting); 50% walk unaided at 2y

6. Mononeuropathy Multiplex

FeatureSignificance
Definition≥2 non-contiguous nerves affected sequentially or simultaneously
In diabetesThink systemic vasculitis (PAN, ANCA, cryoglobulinaemia, rheumatoid)
WorkupESR, CRP, ANCA, ANA, cryoglobulins, complement, hepatitis B/C, HIV, SPEP, urine Bence Jones
Nerve biopsyVasculitic changes (necrotising inflammation, vessel wall destruction)
TreatmentImmunosuppression (steroids + cyclophosphamide/rituximab/azathioprine) per vasculitis protocol

Key: Isolated mononeuropathies in diabetes = common; multiplex = vasculitis until proven otherwise.

7. Recovery Timelines

Neuropathy TypeTypical Recovery
CN III / VI / IV palsy8–12 weeks (pupil-sparing)
Facial nerve (Bell's)6–12 weeks (85% full recovery)
Carpal tunnel (mild)Weeks-months (splint/injection); surgery if persistent
Ulnar at elbowMonths (conservative); surgery if wasting
Peroneal / Femoral3–12 months (often incomplete)
Diabetic amyotrophy6–24 months (often residual deficit)

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
CN III palsy in diabetesPupil-sparing = microvascular ischaemia; pupil-involving = compressive (aneurysm)
CN VI palsyHorizontal diplopia, esotropia, no ptosis
Carpal tunnelNocturnal paraesthesia thumb/index/middle; Phalen's +ve, Tinel's wrist
Diabetic amyotrophySevere hip/thigh pain → quadriceps wasting/weakness; older T2DM; CSF protein ↑
Amyotrophy CSFAlbuminocytological dissociation (protein >1g/L)
Amyotrophy treatmentPain control + IVIG/steroids/PLEX (early); physio
Mononeuropathy multiplexVasculitis until proven otherwise — full vasculitis screen
Recovery CN III8–12 weeks; if persistent → re-image
Femoral neuropathyQuadriceps wasting, knee buckling; overlaps with amyotrophy
Meralgia parestheticaLateral cutaneous nerve thigh; burning anterolateral thigh

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

▸ 21.8.34 Neuropathy Screening 10G Monofilament Vibration Anns

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, neuropathy-screening, monofilament, vibration, anns, mnsi, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - NICE 9 care processes: annual 10g monofilament (LOPS if -ve at ≥1 site), vibration (128Hz), ankle reflexes; ANNS/MNSI questionnaires; risk stratification (IWGDF 0–3); refer if high risk"

see_also: ["Distal symmetric polyneuropathy (DSPN)", "Autonomic neuropathy", "Mononeuropathies and radiculoplexus neuropathy", "Neuropathic pain management", "Foot risk classification (IWGDF/Scottish)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Neuropathy Screening (10g Monofilament, Vibration, ANNS)

[!info]
NICE 9 Care Process #8: Annual foot examination with 10g monofilament, vibration perception, ankle reflexes; ANNS/MNSI questionnaires; LOPS = monofilament -ve at ≥1 site; risk stratification per IWGDF.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Perform annual neuropathy screen per NICE
  • [ ] Apply 10g monofilament technique (3 sites/foot)
  • [ ] Assess vibration perception (128Hz tuning fork)
  • [ ] Use ANNS and MNSI questionnaires
  • [ ] Stratify risk (IWGDF 0–3) and refer appropriately

2. Annual Screen Components (NICE 9 Care Process)

TestMethodAbnormal Threshold
10g MonofilamentApply to 3 sites per foot (plantar hallux, 1st & 5th metatarsal heads); buckle, hold 1s, releaseNegative at ≥1 of 6 sites = LOPS (Loss of Protective Sensation)
Vibration Perception128Hz tuning fork on hallux IP joint and medial malleolus; compare to examinerReduced vs examiner or duration <10s
Ankle ReflexesTap Achilles tendon with knee flexedAbsent

LOPS = Loss of Protective Sensation — defines IWGDF Risk 2+.

3. Monofilament Technique (Semmes-Weinstein 10g)

Procedure

  1. Site selection: Plantar hallux, 1st metatarsal head, 5th metatarsal head (3 sites/foot)
  2. Application: Touch skin perpendicularly → buckle → hold 1 second → release
  3. Patient response: "Yes" when felt; eyes closed
  4. Repeat each site once (do not repeat if felt)
  5. Record: Positive (felt) / Negative (not felt) per site

Interpretation

ResultMeaning
All 6 sites positiveNormal protective sensation
≥1 site negativeLOPS = IWGDF Risk 2 (if pulses present) or Risk 3 (if absent pulses/prior ulcer)

Key: Do not test over callus/ulcer; avoid callused areas.

4. Vibration Perception (128Hz Tuning Fork)

Procedure

  1. Strike fork on rubber surface
  2. Place stem on hallux IP joint (distal) → move to medial malleolus if not felt
  3. Ask patient to say "now" when vibration stops
  4. Examiner feels fork simultaneously — compare duration

Interpretation

FindingSignificance
Felt at IP joint, duration similar to examinerNormal
Felt at IP but duration ↓Early impairment
Not felt at IP, felt at malleolusModerate impairment
Not felt at malleolusSevere impairment

Quantitative: Biothesiometer / Neurothesiometer — voltage threshold >25V = abnormal.

5. Questionnaires

ANNS (Annual Neuropathy Symptom Score) — 4 Questions

QuestionScore if Yes
1. Do you have pain in legs/feet?1
2. Do you have numbness in legs/feet?1
3. Do you have tingling in legs/feet?1
4. Do symptoms worsen at night?1
Total ≥1Positive screen

Advantages: Quick, patient-reported, identifies painful DSPN.

MNSI (Michigan Neuropathy Screening Instrument) — 15-item Questionnaire + Exam

ComponentAbnormal Threshold
Questionnaire (15 yes/no)≥7/15
Exam (inspection, monofilament, vibration, reflexes)≥2.5/10

More comprehensive than ANNS; used in research and some clinics.

6. Risk Stratification (IWGDF) — Links to Foot Risk

Neuropathy FindingVascular StatusIWGDF RiskAction
No LOPS, normal vibrationPulses presentRisk 0Annual screen
LOPS only (monofilament -ve)Pulses presentRisk 1Annual + podiatry nail care
LOPS + PAD (absent pulses)Absent pulsesRisk 23–6m podiatry MDT; footwear
LOPS + deformityPulses presentRisk 23–6m podiatry MDT; orthotics
Prior ulcer/amputationAnyRisk 31–3m specialist MDT
Active ulcer/infection/CharcotAnyActive DiseaseUrgent MDT 24–48h

7. Electrodiagnostics (NCS) — Not Routine

IndicationPurpose
Atypical features (asymmetric, motor-predominant, rapid progression)Rule out CIDP, radiculopathy, vasculitis
Diagnostic uncertaintyConfirm axonal vs demyelinating
Research / trialsObjective endpoint
MedicolegalDocumentation

Typical DSPN NCS: Axonal sensorimotor — ↓ SNAP/CMAP amplitudes, normal/ mildly slowed velocities.

8. Screening Intervals

PopulationFrequency
All diabetes ≥12yAnnual (NICE)
Risk 2 (LOPS + PAD/deformity)3–6 months (podiatry)
Risk 3 (prior ulcer/amputation)1–3 months (specialist MDT)
Pregnancy (pre-existing DM)Each trimester (foot risk increases)
Post-foot surgeryPer surgical protocol

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Monofilament sites3 per foot: hallux, 1st & 5th met heads
LOPS definitionMonofilament -ve at ≥1 of 6 sites
Vibration test128Hz fork on hallux IP joint → malleolus
Ankle reflexAbsent = neuropathy sign
ANNS4 questions; ≥1 yes = positive
MNSIQuestionnaire ≥7/15; Exam ≥2.5/10
IWGDF Risk 0No LOPS, pulses present
IWGDF Risk 1LOPS only, pulses present
IWGDF Risk 2LOPS + (PAD or deformity)
IWGDF Risk 3Prior ulcer/amputation, active Charcot, critical ischaemia
NCSNot routine; atypical features only

Interpretation

▸ 21.8.36 Non Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy Npdr

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, npdr, background-retinopathy, pre-proliferative, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - NPDR = R1 (mild) + R2 (moderate/severe); 4-2-1 rule for severe NPDR; risk of progression to PDR; optimise HbA1c/BP/lipids; no specific eye treatment unless CSME"

see_also: ["Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR)", "Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)", "Retinopathy screening and grading (ETDRS)", "Retinopathy treatment"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Non-Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy (NPDR)

[!info]
NPDR = R1 (mild/background) + R2 (pre-proliferative); microvascular leakage → haemorrhages, exudates, ischaemia; 4-2-1 rule defines severe NPDR (R2); progression to PDR driven by ischaemia/VEGF; systemic optimisation is mainstay.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify NPDR severity (R1 vs R2)
  • [ ] Apply 4-2-1 rule for severe NPDR
  • [ ] Explain pathophysiology (hyperglycaemia → microvascular damage)
  • [ ] Manage systemically (HbA1c, BP, lipids)
  • [ ] Understand progression risk to PDR/DMO

2. Classification (ETDRS / ENDESP)

GradeNameLesionsProgression Risk to PDR (5y)
R1Mild / Background NPDR≥1 Microaneurysm ± dot/blot haemorrhages ± hard exudates <1 DD from foveaLow (~5%)
R2Moderate/Severe / Pre-proliferative NPDRVenous beading, IRMA, multiple haemorrhages (>4 quadrants), cotton wool spotsModerate-High (~15–25%)

3. 4-2-1 Rule (Severe NPDR / R2 Criteria)

Any ONE of the following in ≥1 quadrant = Severe NPDR (ETDRS Level 53):

  • 4 quadrants of haemorrhages/microaneurysms
  • 2 quadrants of venous beading
  • 1 quadrant of IRMA

Very Severe NPDR (ETDRS Level 61): 2+ of the above — highest risk of PDR.

4. Pathophysiology

ProcessMechanism
Pericyte lossEarly hyperglycaemia → pericyte apoptosis → capillary wall weakening → microaneurysms
Endothelial damage↑ VEGF, PKC, AGEs, oxidative stress → vascular leakage → haemorrhages, exudates
Capillary non-perfusionMicrothrombi, endothelial loss → ischaemia → cotton wool spots (axonal stasis), venous beading, IRMA (shunt vessels)
VEGF upregulationIschaemia → HIF-1α → VEGF → vascular leakage (DMO) + neovascularisation (PDR)

5. Clinical Features

FindingSignificance
MicroaneurysmsEarliest lesion; saccular capillary outpouching
Dot/blot haemorrhagesIntraretinal; deep retinal layers
Flame haemorrhagesSuperficial (nerve fibre layer) — more hypertensive
Hard exudatesLipid leakage; circinate rings if focal
Soft exudates (CWS)Ischaemic axonal stasis; pre-proliferative sign
Venous beadingVenous wall irregularity; ischaemia marker
IRMAIntraretinal shunts (arterio-venous); pre-proliferative
Venous loops/reduplicationAdvanced venous remodelling

6. Risk Factors for Progression

ModifiableNon-Modifiable
HbA1c (each 1% ↑ = 30–40% ↑ progression)Duration of diabetes
Hypertension (SBP >140)Puberty (accelerates)
Dyslipidaemia (↑ TG, ↓ HDL)Pregnancy (↑ progression 2–3×)
Anaemia (Hb <11)Genetics (familial clustering)
SmokingEthnicity (South Asian, Black higher)
CKD (eGFR <60)

DCCT/EDIC: Intensive control (HbA1c ~7% vs 9%) → 76% ↓ onset, 54% ↓ progression; legacy effect persists 30y.

7. Management

Systemic Optimisation (Mainstay)

TargetEvidence
HbA1c <53 mmol/mol (7.0%)DCCT/UKPDS: ↓ onset & progression
BP <130/80UKPDS: tight BP ↓ progression 34%
Lipids — Statins ± FenofibrateACCORD-Eye: fenofibrate + statin ↓ progression 40% (especially mild NPDR)
SGLT2i / GLP-1 RAEmerging retinal benefit (post-hoc)

Ophthalmology

  • No laser/anti-VEGF for NPDR alone (unless CSME/DMO)
  • Pan-retinal photocoagulation (PRP) — not indicated for NPDR (ETDRS: PRP increases DMO risk without benefit in NPDR)
  • Monitor: R1 annual; R2 6–12 monthly (ophthalmology)

8. Diabetic Macular Oedema (DMO) in NPDR

  • Can occur at any NPDR stage (even R1)
  • CSME → refer for anti-VEGF (first-line) ± laser
  • Screen: OCT at each visit if exudates near fovea

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
R1 (Background)Microaneurysms ± dot/blot haemorrhages
R2 (Pre-proliferative)Venous beading, IRMA, CWS, multiple haemorrhages
4-2-1 rule4 quadrants haemorrhages OR 2 quadrants venous beading OR 1 quadrant IRMA = severe NPDR
Progression R1→PDR~5% at 5y
Progression R2→PDR~15–25% at 5y
DCCT/EDIC legacyEarly intensive control → 30y benefit
FenofibrateACCORD-Eye: ↓ progression 40% (mild NPDR)
PRP in NPDRNot indicated (increases DMO risk)
Pregnancy↑ NPDR progression 2–3× — screen each trimester
Referral R2Ophthalmology 6–12 weeks

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Topic Overview

Non-Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy (NPDR
1. Learning Objectives
2. Classification (ETDRS / ENDESP)
3. 4-2-1 Rule (Severe NPDR / R2 Criteria)
4. Pathophysiology
5. Clinical Features
6. Risk Factors for Progression
7. Management
Systemic Optimisation (Mainstay)
Ophthalmology
8. Diabetic Macular Oedema (DMO) in NPDR

▸ 21.8.37 Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy Pdr

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, pdr, proliferative-retinopathy, neo-vascularisation, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - PDR = new vessels (NVD/NVE) + vitreous/preretinal haemorrhage; high-risk characteristics (HRCs): NVD >1/3 disc area, NVD + haemorrhage, NVE >1/2 disc area + haemorrhage; PRP = gold standard; anti-VEGF alternative; vitrectomy for non-clearing VH"

see_also: ["Non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR)", "Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)", "Retinopathy screening and grading (ETDRS)", "Retinopathy treatment (laser, anti-VEGF, vitrectomy)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy (PDR)

[!info]
PDR = neovascularisation (NVD at disc, NVE elsewhere) driven by retinal ischaemia/VEGF; high-risk characteristics (HRCs) define urgent PRP need; PRP = gold standard; anti-VEGF alternative; vitrectomy for tractional RD/non-clearing vitreous haemorrhage.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Identify PDR features: NVD, NVE, vitreous/pre-retinal haemorrhage
  • [ ] Apply ETDRS High-Risk Characteristics (HRCs)
  • [ ] Compare PRP vs anti-VEGF vs vitrectomy
  • [ ] Manage complications (tractional RD, rubeosis, neovascular glaucoma)
  • [ ] Apply systemic optimisation

2. Classification (ETDRS / ENDESP = R3)

FeatureDescription
NVD (New Vessels at Disc)On/within 1 DD of optic disc; most ominous
NVE (New Vessels Elsewhere)Beyond 1 DD from disc; along vascular arcades
Vitreous haemorrhage (VH)Blood in vitreous cavity → sudden vision loss
Pre-retinal haemorrhageBetween retina and posterior hyaloid
Fibrovascular proliferationEpiretinal membrane + vessels → traction
Tractional retinal detachment (TRD)Fibrovascular contraction → retinal elevation

3. High-Risk Characteristics (HRCs) — ETDRS Definition

Any ONE = High-Risk PDR (requires urgent PRP):

HRCDefinition
1. Large NVDNVD >1/3 disc area (≈ >standard photograph disc area)
2. NVD + HaemorrhageAny NVD (any size) + vitreous or pre-retinal haemorrhage
3. Large NVE + HaemorrhageNVE >1/2 disc area + vitreous/pre-retinal haemorrhage

No HRC = Early PDR (still needs treatment but less urgent).

4. Pathophysiology

StepMechanism
1. IschaemiaCapillary non-perfusion (NPDR progression) → retinal hypoxia
2. HIF-1α stabilisationHypoxia → HIF-1α ↑ → VEGF transcription
3. VEGF upregulationVEGF diffuses → binds VEGFR2 on endothelial cells
4. NeovascularisationNew vessels grow from retinal vessels (not choroidal) → fragile, leaky
5. ComplicationsBleeding → VH; Contraction → TRD; Rubeosis → neovascular glaucoma

5. Clinical Presentation

ScenarioSymptoms
Asymptomatic PDRNo symptoms; detected on screening (R3)
Vitreous haemorrhageSudden painless vision loss ("floaters", "cobwebs", "curtain")
Tractional RDProgressive visual field loss, metamorphopsia
Neovascular glaucomaPain, red eye, high IOP, rubeosis iridis (NVI) — ophthalmic emergency

6. Management

A. Pan-Retinal Photocoagulation (PRP) — Gold Standard

AspectDetail
IndicationHRC PDR (urgent); Early PDR (within 4–6 months)
MechanismAblate peripheral retina → ↓ VEGF production → regression of new vessels
Protocol (ETDRS)1200–1600 burns (500µm, 0.1s, 100–200mW); 2–3 sessions
Effectiveness50% ↓ severe vision loss (ETDRS); regression of NVD/NVE
Side effectsPeripheral field loss (driving), night vision loss, DMO worsening (15–30%), choroidal detachment (rare)
Follow-up4–6 weekly until regression; then 3–6 monthly

B. Anti-VEGF (Alternative / Adjunct)

AgentRegimenRole
Ranibizumab/AfliberceptMonthly ×3 → PRN / T&ENon-inferior to PRP for regression (Protocol S, CLARITY); less field loss
Bevacizumab (off-label)1.25mg intravitrealCost-effective; similar efficacy
IndicationsEarly PDR, DMO + PDR, media opacity (prevents PRP), patient refusal PRP
DisadvantageRequires frequent injections; rebound if stopped; cost; no peripheral ablation

C. Vitrectomy

IndicationTiming
Non-clearing vitreous haemorrhage>3 months (or <3m if severe + no view for PRP)
Tractional retinal detachmentMacula-threatening → urgent; macula-off → within 1w
Combined TRD + rhegmatogenousUrgent
Ghost cell glaucomaUrgent
Procedure23/25G pars plana; delamination ± endolaser ± gas/silicone oil

7. Complications

ComplicationManagement
Vitreous haemorrhageObserve 3m; PRP if view; vitrectomy if non-clearing
Tractional RDVitrectomy + membrane peeling ± endolaser ± tamponade
Neovascular glaucoma (NVG)Emergency: IOP control (topical + systemic) + PRP (or cryo) + anti-VEGF; drainage device if refractory
Rubeosis iridis (NVI)New vessels on iris → NVG precursor → urgent PRP
PRP-induced DMOAnti-VEGF ± focal laser; usually transient

8. Systemic Optimisation (Essential Adjunct)

TargetRationale
HbA1c <53 (7.0%)DCCT: intensive control ↓ PDR progression 50%
BP <130/80UKPDS: tight BP ↓ progression
LipidsFenofibrate + statin ↓ progression (ACCORD-Eye)
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RACVOTs: ↓ DR progression (post-hoc)
Anaemia correctionHb <11 → ↑ VEGF; correct if possible

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
PDR definitionNew vessels (NVD/NVE) ± vitreous/pre-retinal haemorrhage
NVD vs NVENVD = at disc (worse); NVE = elsewhere
HRC 1NVD >1/3 disc area
HRC 2Any NVD + haemorrhage
HRC 3NVE >1/2 disc area + haemorrhage
PRPGold standard; 1200–1600 burns; 50% ↓ severe vision loss
Anti-VEGF for PDRAlternative; less field loss; needs frequent injections; rebound risk
Vitrectomy indicationsNon-clearing VH >3m; tractional RD (macula-on urgent); NVG
NVGPain + high IOP + rubeosis → emergency PRP + IOP control
PRP complicationsPeripheral field loss, night blindness, DMO worsening
Systemic controlHbA1c, BP, lipids — essential adjunct

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Proliferative Diabetic Retinopathy (PDR

3. High-Risk Characteristics (HRCs) — ETDRS Definition

5. Clinical Presentation
6. Management
A. Pan-Retinal Photocoagulation (PRP) — Gold Standard
B. Anti-VEGF (Alternative / Adjunct)
C. Vitrectomy

▸ 21.8.38 Retinopathy Screening And Grading Etdrs

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, retinopathy-screening, etdrs, grading, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - English National Screening Programme; R0–R3, M0–M1 grading; digital photography; referral thresholds; screening intervals (annual/2-yearly); sensitivity/specificity"

see_also: ["Non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR)", "Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR)", "Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)", "Retinopathy treatment (laser, anti-VEGF, vitrectomy)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Retinopathy Screening and Grading (ETDRS)

[!info]
English National Diabetic Eye Screening Programme (ENDESP) — digital mydriatic photography; ETDRS-based grading R0–R3 (retinopathy) + M0–M1 (maculopathy); annual if R1–R2, 2-yearly if R0 ×2; urgent referral R3/M1.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe screening programme methodology (mydriatic digital photography)
  • [ ] Apply ETDRS grading: R0–R3, M0–M1
  • [ ] Determine screening interval and referral criteria
  • [ ] Interpret screening results for clinical management
  • [ ] Understand quality standards (sensitivity >95%, specificity >80%)

2. Screening Programme (ENDESP / UK)

ElementSpecification
ModalityMydriatic digital retinal photography (2-field: macula + disc; or 3-field)
MydriasisTropicamide 1% (phenylephrine 2.5% if needed)
FrequencyAnnual (default); 2-yearly if R0 at two consecutive screens
Age range12 years and older (all diabetes types)
Quality standardsSensitivity >95% for referable retinopathy; Specificity >80%
Fail-safeRecall if ungradable (cataract, small pupil) — re-screen 4–6 weeks

3. ETDRS Grading — Retinopathy (R)

GradeDescriptionLesionsReferral
R0No retinopathyNoneRoutine (annual / 2y if R0×2)
R1Background NPDR (Mild)Microaneurysms ± dot/blot haemorrhages ± hard exudates <1 DD from foveaAnnual
R2Pre-proliferative NPDR (Moderate/severe)Venous beading, IRMA (intraretinal microvascular abnormalities), multiple haemorrhages (>4 quadrants), CWS (cotton wool spots)Ophthalmology 6–12 weeks
R3Proliferative DR (PDR)New vessels (NVD at disc, NVE elsewhere), vitreous/pre-retinal haemorrhage, fibrovascular proliferationUrgent ophthalmology 2–4 weeks

Key: Any R grade + M1 = referable maculopathy (see below).

4. ETDRS Grading — Maculopathy (M)

GradeDescriptionOCT / Clinical FindingsReferral
M0No maculopathyNo exudate/retinal thickening within 1 DD of foveaPer R grade
M1Referable maculopathyExudate within 1 DD of fovea OR retinal thickening (circinate exudates, CSME on OCT)Ophthalmology 4–8 weeks

CSME (Clinically Significant Macular Oedema) — ETDRS definition:

  • Retinal thickening ≤500µm from foveal centre
  • Hard exudates ≤500µm with adjacent thickening
  • Zone of thickening ≥1 DD involving centre

OCT: Central subfield thickness >300µm (men) / >310µm (women) / >250µm (central 1mm) = DMO.

5. Screening Interval Algorithm

Screen Result → Next Screen
R0, M0  → Annual (if first R0) → **2-yearly if R0 x 2**
R1, M0  → Annual
R1, M1  → Refer ophthalmology (4-8w); annual screen continues
R2, M0  → Refer ophthalmology (6-12w); annual screen continues
R2, M1  → Refer ophthalmology (4-8w); annual screen continues
R3, M0  → **Urgent refer** (2-4w); annual screen continues
R3, M1  → **Urgent refer** (2-4w + 4-8w); annual screen continues
Ungradable → Re-screen 4-6 weeks (cataract → refer for surgery)

6. Referral Criteria Summary

Screen ResultReferral TimeframeSpecialty
R3 (any M)2–4 weeksUrgent ophthalmology
R2 (any M)6–12 weeksOphthalmology
Any R + M14–8 weeksOphthalmology (macula clinic)
Ungradable (media opacity)4–6 weeks (re-screen)Cataract surgery if indicated
R0/R1, M0No referralContinue screening

7. Special Populations

PopulationScreening Modification
Pregnancy (pre-existing diabetes)Trimester 1, Trimester 2, Trimester 3 (3 screens); postpartum if R2/R3/M1
GDMScreen if pre-existing retinopathy or develops T2DM
Children <12yNot routinely screened; start at 12y or puberty
End-stage renal diseaseHigh risk — ensure annual screen compliance
Learning disabilitiesReasonable adjustments (longer appointments, familiariser)

8. Quality Assurance & Metrics

MetricTarget
Uptake>80% of eligible population
Sensitivity (referable DR)>95%
Specificity>80%
Time to result<3 weeks
Time to referral appointmentPer timeframe above
Ungradable rate<5%

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Screening modalityMydriatic digital photography (2-field minimum)
Grading systemETDRS — R0–R3 + M0–M1
R1Microaneurysms ± dot/blot haemorrhages
R2Venous beading, IRMA, multiple haemorrhages, CWS
R3New vessels (NVD/NVE), vitreous haemorrhage
M1Exudate/thickening within 1 DD of fovea
Screening intervalAnnual; 2-yearly if R0 × 2
Pregnancy3 screens (each trimester)
R3 referralUrgent 2–4 weeks
R2 referral6–12 weeks
M1 referral4–8 weeks
UngradableRe-screen 4–6 weeks; cataract → surgery

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR)]], [[Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR)]], [[Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)]], [[Retinopathy treatment (laser, anti-VEGF, vitrectomy)]]

Retinopathy Screening and Grading (ETDRS

▸ 21.8.4 Autonomic Neuropathy Cardiovascular Gi Genitourinary

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, autonomic-neuropathy, cardiovascular, gi, gu, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Cardiovascular: orthostatic hypotension, resting tachycardia, silent MI; GI: gastroparesis, diarrhoea, constipation; GU: bladder dysfunction, ED; sudomotor: anhidrosis, gustatory sweating; screening: HRV, Valsalva, 30:15 ratio, Ewing battery"

see_also: ["Distal symmetric polyneuropathy (DSPN)", "Mononeuropathies and radiculoplexus neuropathy", "Neuropathic pain management", "Neuropathy screening (10g monofilament, vibration, ANNS)"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Autonomic Neuropathy (Cardiovascular, GI, Genitourinary)

[!info]
Autonomic neuropathy = common, underdiagnosed; CV: orthostatic hypotension, silent MI, exercise intolerance; GI: gastroparesis (nausea, vomiting, erratic glucose); GU: neurogenic bladder, ED; sudomotor: anhidrosis, gustatory sweating; mortality ↑ 2–3× (CV autonomic).

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise cardiovascular, GI, and GU autonomic neuropathy
  • [ ] Apply screening tests (HRV, Valsalva, 30:15 ratio, orthostatic BP)
  • [ ] Manage gastroparesis (diet, prokinetics, gastric stimulation)
  • [ ] Treat orthostatic hypotension (non-drug + fludrocortisone/midodrine)
  • [ ] Manage erectile dysfunction (PDE5i, vacuum, prosthesis)

2. Cardiovascular Autonomic Neuropathy (CAN)

Clinical Features

ManifestationFeatures
Orthostatic hypotensionSBP drop ≥20 / DBP ≥10 within 3min standing; syncope, falls, "coat-hanger" pain
Resting tachycardiaHR >100 bpm (denervation supersensitivity)
Fixed heart rateLoss of respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA)
Exercise intoleranceInadequate HR response (chronotropic incompetence)
Silent myocardial ischaemiaAngina absent in 25–50% of diabetic MI;-screen with ECG/stress echo
Post-prandial hypotensionSBP drop >20mmHg after meals (splanchnic pooling)

Screening Tests (Ewing Battery)

TestNormalAbnormal (CAN)
Heart Rate Variability (HRV) — deep breathingE:I ratio >1.15<1.10 (severe), 1.10–1.15 (moderate)
30:15 Ratio (standing)>1.04<1.00
Valsalva Ratio>1.21<1.11
Orthostatic BPSBP drop <20, DBP <10≥20/10 drop
BP Response to Sustained HandgripDBP rise >16mmHg<10mmHg

CAN Staging: Early (1 abnormal), Definite (≥2), Severe (≥3 + orthostatic hypotension).

3. Gastrointestinal Autonomic Neuropathy

Gastroparesis

FeatureDetails
SymptomsNausea, vomiting (undigested food), early satiety, bloating, erratic glucose, weight loss
DiagnosisGastric emptying scintigraphy (gold standard): 4h retention >10% (solids) / 4h >60% (liquids); C13 breath test alternative
ExcludeMechanical obstruction (endoscopy/CT), medications (opioids, GLP-1 RA, anticholinergics)

Other GI Manifestations

ConditionSymptoms
Diabetic diarrhoeaNocturnal, watery, incontinence; bacterial overgrowth common
ConstipationSlow transit; overflow diarrhoea possible
Oesophageal dysmotilityDysphagia, reflux (impaired LES)

Gastroparesis Management

StepIntervention
1. DietarySmall frequent meals (6/day); low fat, low fibre; liquid/blended; avoid carbonated
2. Glycaemic optimisationHyperglycaemia delays emptying; avoid hypoglycaemia (unpredictable absorption)
3. ProkineticsMetoclopramide 10mg TDS (max 5 days — tardive dyskinesia risk); Domperidone 10mg TDS (QT caution); Erythromycin 250mg TDS (motilin agonist — tachyphylaxis); Prucalopride 1–2mg OD (5-HT4 agonist)
4. AntiemeticsOndansetron, cyclizine, prochlorperazine
5. RefractoryGastric electrical stimulation (GES) — Enterra Therapy; Jejunostomy feeding; TPN (last resort)

4. Genitourinary Autonomic Neuropathy

Neurogenic Bladder

StageFeatures
EarlyImpaired sensation → increased capacity, infrequent voiding
LateDetrusor underactivity → retention, overflow incontinence, recurrent UTI

Diagnosis: Post-void residual (PVR) >100mL (ultrasound); urodynamics if complex.

Management: Timed voiding, double voiding, intermittent self-catheterisation (ISC), bethanechol (limited), sacral neuromodulation.

Erectile Dysfunction (ED)

  • Prevalence: 50–75% of diabetic men (3× non-diabetic); earlier onset, more severe
  • Aetiology: Neurogenic + vasculogenic + endothelial (NO deficiency) + hormonal (low T)
  • Screening: SHIM/IIEF-5 questionnaire annually
  • Management:
  1. PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil) — 1st line; check nitrate contraindication
  2. Vacuum erection device
  3. Intracavernosal injections (alprostadil)
  4. Penile prosthesis (refractory)

Female Sexual Dysfunction

  • Arousal, lubrication, orgasm difficulty; under-recognised
  • Management: Lubricants, topical oestrogen, referral psychosexual

5. Sudomotor Dysfunction

ManifestationFeatures
AnhidrosisDry skin (distal → proximal); fissures, ulcer risk
Gustatory sweatingFacial/neck sweating with eating (spicy/hot foods) — Frey's syndrome variant
Compensatory hyperhidrosisTruncal sweating (proximal to anhidrotic area)

6. Systemic Management Principles

DomainAction
Glycaemic controlDCCT: intensive → 50% ↓ CAN incidence; target HbA1c <53 (7.0%)
Risk factor modificationBP <130/80, statin, smoking cessation, weight management
Fall preventionReview meds (antihypertensives, sedatives); orthostatic training; hip protectors
ExerciseSupervised cardiac rehab; avoid Valsalva; monitor HR/BP

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
CAN mortality2–3× higher (sudden death, silent MI)
Orthostatic hypotensionSBP drop ≥20 / DBP ≥10 in 3min standing
Silent MI25–50% diabetic MI painless; screen with ECG/stress test
Ewing batteryHRV (deep breathing), 30:15 ratio, Valsalva, orthostatic BP, handgrip
Gastroparesis diagnosisGastric emptying scintigraphy (4h retention >10% solids)
Gastroparesis dietSmall frequent, low fat/fibre, liquid/blended
ProkineticsMetoclopramide (max 5d), domperidone, erythromycin, prucalopride
ED prevalence50–75% diabetic men; 3× non-diabetic
ED 1st linePDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil)
SudomotorAnhidrosis (dry feet), gustatory sweating (face/neck with eating)

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

Autonomic Neuropathy (Cardiovascular, GI, Genitourinary

Clinical Features
Screening Tests (Ewing Battery)
3. Gastrointestinal Autonomic Neuropathy
Gastroparesis
Other GI Manifestations
Gastroparesis Management
4. Genitourinary Autonomic Neuropathy

Erectile Dysfunction (ED)
Female Sexual Dysfunction
5. Sudomotor Dysfunction
6. Systemic Management Principles

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.8.4 Lada Latent Autoimmune Diabetes In Adults

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Define LADA and distinguish from T1DM and T2DM
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria (age, autoantibodies, progression)
  • [ ] Manage initial non-insulin phase and insulin transition
  • [ ] Recognise LADA vs T2DM vs classic T1DM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionLatent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults: adult-onset autoimmune diabetes with slow progression
Age at onset>30 years (peak 40-60)
Prevalence2-12% of adult-onset diabetes; ~50% of non-obese T2DM
AutoantibodiesGAD65+ (90%); IA-2, ZnT8, IAA less common
ProgressionSlow: insulin required at ~3-7 years (vs months in classic T1DM)
GeneticsHLA-DR3/DR4 (like T1DM); TCF7L2 (like T2DM) - hybrid

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureLADAClassic T1DMT2DM
Age>30y (peak 40-60)Peak 10-14y>40y
BMINormal/lowNormal/lowOverweight/obese
OnsetSubacute (months-years)Acute (days-weeks)Insidious (years)
AutoantibodiesGAD65+ (90%); often single2+ (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)Negative
C-peptideLow-normal (preserved initially)Low/absentNormal/high
Initial responseNon-insulin agents work (1-5y)Insulin required immediatelyNon-insulin agents work
DKA at dxRareCommon (25-30%)Rare

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

SymptomFrequencyNotes
Osmotic symptomsCommonPolyuria, polydipsia, weight loss
Ketosis/DKARare at diagnosisUnlike classic T1DM
MisdiagnosisCommon as T2DMDue to age, BMI, initial oral agent response

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Diagnostic Criteria (Immunology of Diabetes Society)

CriterionRequirement
Age>=30 years at diagnosis
Autoantibodies>=1 islet autoantibody (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)
Insulin independenceNot insulin-requiring for >=6 months after diagnosis

Subtypes

TypeAutoantibodiesProgression
LADA 1Low titre GAD65 onlySlow; T2DM-like
LADA 2High titre GAD65 + other AbsFaster; T1DM-like

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestLADAT1DMT2DM
GAD65Positive (90%)Positive (70-80%)Negative
IA-2/ZnT8Occasionally positiveOften positiveNegative
C-peptideLow-normal (preserved initially)Low/absentNormal/high
Autoantibodies1+ (GAD65 most common)2+0
HLADR3/DR4 (like T1DM)DR3/DR4DR3/DR4 (variable)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Classic T1DM<30y, acute, DKA common, 2+ Abs, C-peptide low
T2DMObese, insidious, no Abs, C-peptide high, insulin resistance
MODY<25y, autosomal dominant FH, non-obese, negative Abs, preserved C-peptide
Type 3cPancreatic disease (pancreatitis, cancer), exocrine insufficiency

7. Management

Initial Phase (non-insulin)

AgentSuitabilityNotes
MetforminOften usedMay delay insulin need; weight neutral
DPP-4iCan be usedLow hypo risk
GLP-1 RAIncreasingly usedWeight loss benefit; preserves beta-cell?
SGLT2iUsedCV/renal benefit; euglycaemic DKA risk
SulfonylureasAvoid if possibleAccelerates beta-cell exhaustion
TZDsAvoidFluid retention, fracture risk

Insulin Transition

TriggerAction
HbA1c >58 on oral agentsStart basal insulin
C-peptide low/undetectableInsulin required
Symptomatic hyperglycaemiaBasal-bolus insulin
PregnancyInsulin immediately

Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
HbA1c3-monthly
C-peptideAnnually (tracks progression)
AutoantibodiesNot needed to repeat

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DefinitionAdult-onset (>30y) autoimmune diabetes; slow progression
Key featureGAD65+ (90%); single Ab often; initial non-insulin response
DiagnosisAge >=30, 1+ Ab, insulin-independent >=6 months
vs T1DMAdult, slow, no DKA, single Ab, preserved C-peptide
vs T2DMNon-obese, GAD65+, C-peptide low-normal, fails oral agents
ManagementMetformin/DPP-4i/GLP-1 RA initially; avoid SU; transition to insulin when C-peptide low or HbA1c uncontrolled

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is LADA?Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults: autoimmune diabetes onset >30y with slow progression
What are the diagnostic criteria for LADA?Age >=30, 1+ islet autoantibody, insulin-independent for >=6 months after diagnosis
How does LADA differ from T1DM?Adult onset, slower progression, no DKA at dx, single Ab (GAD65), preserved C-peptide initially
How does LADA differ from T2DM?Non-obese, GAD65+, C-peptide low-normal, fails oral agents sooner, autoimmune
What is the initial management of LADA?Metformin/DPP-4i/GLP-1 RA; avoid sulfonylureas (accelerate beta-cell failure)
When do you start insulin in LADA?HbA1c >58 on orals, low C-peptide, symptomatic, or pregnancy

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
LADA = T1DM?Subtype of T1DM but distinct clinical entity (adult, slow)
LADA = T2DM?Autoimmune (GAD65+); not insulin resistance primary
All GAD65+ = LADA?GAD65+ also in classic T1DM; need clinical context

Mnemonic: LADA-ADULT

  • LADA: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults
  • Age >=30 at diagnosis
  • Diabetes: autoimmune (GAD65+ 90%)
  • Adult onset: >30y, peak 40-60
  • Autoantibody: GAD65+ (single often); =6 months insulin-free
  • Distinguish from T2DM: non-obese, GAD65+, C-peptide low-normal
  • Ups insulin: when C-peptide low or HbA1c uncontrolled
  • Low C-peptide: progression marker
  • T2DM misdiagnosis common initially

11. Mind Map

LADA
Definition
Adult-onset autoimmune
>30 years
Slow progression
Diagnosis
Age >=30
1+ Ab (GAD65 90%)
Insulin-free >=6mo
Features
GAD65+ (90%)
Single Ab often
Non-obese
C-peptide low-normal
vs T1DM
Adult vs child
Slow vs acute
Single Ab vs 2+
vs T2DM
Autoimmune vs metabolic
Non-obese vs obese
C-peptide low-normal
Management
Initial: Met/DPP-4i/GLP-1
Avoid SU
Insulin when C-peptide low

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionAdult-onset (>30y) autoimmune diabetes; 1+ Ab; insulin-free >=6mo
**Key Test"GAD65 (90%); C-peptide (low-normal); autoantibodies
**Classification"LADA 1 (low titre GAD65) vs LADA 2 (high titre + other Abs)
**Acute Mgmt"Rare DKA; usually stable
**Chronic Mgmt"Met/DPP-4i/GLP-1 initially; avoid SU; insulin when C-peptide low
**Key Score"Age >=30 + 1+ Ab + insulin-free >=6mo
**Complications"Same as T1DM once insulin-requiring
PrognosisInsulin required at ~3-7 years; then same as T1DM

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

SectionScore /5Last Attempt

Management (Acute)

▸ 21.8.42 Scottish

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, foot-risk, iwgdf, scottish, classification, screening, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - IWGDF risk categories 0–3 (replaces Scottish 1–3); annual screening with 10g monofilament, vibration, pulses; determines referral frequency; prevents amputation"

see_also: ["Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) classification", "Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) management", "Charcot neuroarthropathy", "Peripheral neuropathy screening"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Foot Risk Classification (IWGDF / Scottish)

[!info]
IWGDF 2019/2023 = 4 categories (Risk 0–3); Scottish = 3 categories (Low/Moderate/High); both based on neuropathy, ischaemia, deformity, prior ulcer/amputation; determines screening frequency and referral pathway.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify foot risk using IWGDF (0–3) and Scottish systems
  • [ ] Perform annual foot screen (monofilament, vibration, pulses)
  • [ ] Apply screening frequency per risk category
  • [ ] Understand referral pathways to MDT

2. IWGDF Risk Classification (2019/2023) — International Standard

RiskCriteriaClinical FeaturesScreening FrequencyReferral
0 (Very Low)No LOPS, No PAD, No deformityNormal sensation, palpable pulses, no deformity, no prior ulcerAnnualSelf-care education; routine podiatry not needed
1 (Low)LOPS onlyNeuropathy (monofilament -ve) but palpable pulses, no deformityAnnualPodiatry for nail/skin care; self-care education
2 (Moderate)LOPS + PAD OR LOPS + DeformityNeuropathy plus absent pulses OR foot deformity (claw toes, Charcot, prominent met heads)3–6 monthsPodiatry MDT; footwear assessment; preventive care
3 (High)Prior ulcer/amputation OR Active Charcot OR Critical ischaemiaHistory of DFU/amputation, active Charcot, or critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, tissue loss)1–3 monthsSpecialist MDT (vascular, podiatry, orthotics, diabetology); urgent if active problem

LOPS = Loss of Protective Sensation (10g monofilament negative at ≥1 site)

PAD = Peripheral Arterial Disease (absent DP/PT pulses OR ABI <0.9)

3. Scottish Risk Classification (Historical / Still Used UK)

RiskIWGDF EquivalentCriteriaReview
Low0–1No risk factors (normal sensation, pulses)Annual
Moderate2Neuropathy OR absent pulses OR deformity3–6 months
High3Previous ulcer/amputation OR active Charcot OR critical ischaemia1–3 months
Active DiseaseN/AActive ulcer, spreading infection, critical ischaemia, active CharcotUrgent MDT (24–48h)

Note: Scottish system merges IWGDF 0+1 into "Low"; IWGDF 1 is "Low" but with neuropathy.

4. Annual Foot Screen — Essential Components

ComponentMethodAbnormal Threshold
Protective Sensation10g Semmes-Weinstein monofilament (apply to 6 sites: hallux, 1st/3rd/5th met heads, dorsum, heel)Negative at ≥1 site = LOPS
Vibration Perception128Hz tuning fork on hallux IP joint / medial malleolusReduced vs examiner / <10s
PulsesPalpate dorsalis pedis (DP) and posterior tibial (PT)Absent either = PAD
Capillary RefillDorsum of foot>3 seconds
DeformityVisual inspectionClaw toes, hallux valgus, Charcot, prominent met heads
Skin/NailsInspectionCallus, fissures, mycosis, thickened nails
FootwearAssess fit, wear patternIll-fitting, pressure marks
HistoryAskPrevious ulcer/amputation, recent trauma

Minimum: Monofilament + pulses + inspection (NICE 9 care processes).

5. Risk-Based Management Pathway

RiskAction
0Annual screen; education (daily inspection, footwear, moisturise); no routine podiatry
1Annual screen; podiatry for nail/skin care if unable self-care; education
23–6 monthly podiatry MDT; footwear assessment (orthotics, insoles); callus debridement; vascular review if pulses absent
31–3 monthly specialist MDT; custom footwear/orthotics; vascular review; consider prophylactic surgery; emergency pathway if active

Active foot disease (ulcer, infection, ischaemia, Charcot) → Urgent MDT referral within 24–48 hours (NICE NG19).

6. Key Preventive Measures by Risk

MeasureRisk 0–1Risk 2Risk 3
Daily self-inspection✓✓✓ (carer)
Moisturise (not between toes)✓✓✓
Appropriate footwear✓Prescribed/orthoticCustom-moulded
Professional nail careIf needed✓✓
Callus debridement—✓ (podiatry)✓ (podiatry)
Vascular assessment—If pulses absentMandatory (ABI, TcPO2, duplex)
Temperature monitoring—ConsiderInfrared thermometry (early Charcot)

7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
IWGDF Risk 0No LOPS, no PAD, no deformity → annual screen
IWGDF Risk 1LOPS only → annual + podiatry for nails
IWGDF Risk 2LOPS + (PAD OR deformity) → 3–6m podiatry MDT
IWGDF Risk 3Prior ulcer/amputation, active Charcot, critical ischaemia → 1–3m specialist MDT
Scottish Low= IWGDF 0+1 (no risk factors)
Scottish Moderate= IWGDF 2 (one risk factor)
Scottish High= IWGDF 3
Monofilament sites6 sites: hallux, 1st/3rd/5th met heads, dorsum, heel
LOPS definitionMonofilament -ve at ≥1 site
Active diseaseUrgent MDT 24–48h (ulcer, infection, ischaemia, active Charcot)

8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetic foot disease|Diabetic foot disease]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]
See also: [[Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) classification]], [[Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) management]], [[Charcot neuroarthropathy]], [[Amputation prevention and multidisciplinary care]]

Topic Overview

Foot Risk Classification (IWGDF / Scottish
1. Learning Objectives
2. IWGDF Risk Classification (2019/2023) — International Standard
3. Scottish Risk Classification (Historical / Still Used UK)
4. Annual Foot Screen — Essential Components
5. Risk-Based Management Pathway
6. Key Preventive Measures by Risk
7. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)
8. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)
▸ 21.8.5 Insulin Pump Therapy Csii

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Indications for CSII/pump therapy in T1DM
  • [ ] Pump components: basal rates, bolus calculator, ICR/ISF
  • [ ] Automated insulin delivery (AID) systems: hybrid closed-loop
  • [ ] Manage pump failure, site issues, DKA risk

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
CSIIContinuous subcutaneous insulin infusion - rapid analogue only
Usage~30-40% T1DM in UK; increasing with AID
Indications (NICE)HbA1c>58 on MDI, severe hypo, Dawn phenomenon, pregnancy, lifestyle

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Pump Types

TypeExamplesFeatures
TetheredMedtronic 780G, Tandem t:slim X2Tubing, large reservoir, colour screen
PatchOmnipod 5, Accu-Chek SoloTubeless, disposable pod, PDA/controller
Closed-loop (AID)Algorithm adjusts basal + microbolusHybrid: meal announcement; Full: no announcement

Commercial Hybrid Closed-Loop Systems

SystemAlgorithmCGMPump
Medtronic 780GSmartGuardGuardian 4780G
Tandem Control-IQControl-IQDexcom G6/G7t:slim X2
Omnipod 5SmartAdjustDexcom G6/G7Omnipod 5
CamAPS FXCamAPSDexcom G6/G7 / Libre 2/3Dana-i / YpsoPump
Loop (DIY)LoopDexcom / LibreMedtronic / Omnipod / RileyLink

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Pump Setup

ParameterTypical Setup
Basal rates24h profile from MDI basal (e.g., 0.5-1.5 U/h); Dawn: +20-30% 3-5am
ICR (Insulin:Carb Ratio)500/TDD = g carb per 1U (e.g., TDD 50 → 10g/U)
ISF (Insulin Sensitivity Factor)100/TDD = mmol/L drop per 1U (e.g., TDD 50 → 2 mmol/L/U)
Target BG5.5-6.5 mmol/L (individualised)
Active insulin time3-5 hours (prevents stacking)

Bolus Types

TypeUse
NormalStandard meal
Extended/SquareHigh fat/protein meals (pizza, pasta) - over 2-4h
Dual/ComboMixed meals - immediate + extended

Automated Insulin Delivery (AID)

FeatureHybrid Closed-LoopFull Closed-Loop (experimental)
Meal announcementRequiredNot required
Basal adjustmentAlgorithm ± microbolusAlgorithm ± microbolus
Correction bolusAuto (some systems)Auto
Examples780G, Control-IQ, Omnipod 5, CamAPS FXResearch only

Troubleshooting

IssueAction
Occlusion alarmChange set + reservoir + insulin; check for DKA if >4h
High BG unexplainedCheck site (lipohypertrophy), insulin expiry, pump settings; give correction via pen
DKA riskPump failure = rapid DKA (no basal depot); check ketones if BG>14
LipohypertrophyRotate sites; avoid palpable nodules

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
IndicationsHbA1c>58 on MDI, severe hypo, Dawn phenomenon, pregnancy, lifestyle
Basal rates24h profile; Dawn phenomenon: increase 3-5am
ICR/ISFICR=500/TDD; ISF=100/TDD
AID systems780G, Control-IQ, Omnipod 5, CamAPS FX; hybrid = meal announce
DKA riskNo basal depot; check ketones if BG>14 or unwell
Site rotationPrevent lipohypertrophy; avoid nodules

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the indications for insulin pump therapy in T1DM?HbA1c>58mmol/mol on MDI, severe hypoglycaemia, Dawn phenomenon, pregnancy, lifestyle flexibility
How do you calculate ICR and ISF?ICR = 500/TDD (g carb per 1U); ISF = 100/TDD (mmol/L drop per 1U)
What is the difference between hybrid and full closed-loop?Hybrid requires meal announcement; full does not (research only)
What is the DKA risk with pump therapy?No basal depot → rapid DKA if occlusion/disconnection; check ketones if BG>14 or unwell
How do you manage an occlusion alarm?Change set + reservoir + insulin; give correction via pen if BG high; check ketones

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Pump = no hypos?NO - hypos still occur; AID reduces but not eliminates
Basal = background?YES - basal = background = long-acting covering hepatic glucose production
Pre-mixed = basal-bolus?NO - pre-mixed is fixed ratio (30/70); less flexible; for regular meals only

Mnemonic: PUMP-CSII

  • Pump: CSII - rapid insulin only
  • Ultra: AID systems (780G, Control-IQ, Omnipod 5, CamAPS)
  • Meal: Hybrid = announce; Full = no announce
  • Parameters: Basal rates, ICR=500/TDD, ISF=100/TDD
  • CSII: indications HbA1c>58, severe hypo, Dawn, pregnancy
  • Site rotation: prevent lipohypertrophy
  • Insulin: rapid analogue only (no basal depot)
  • Interruption: DKA risk if occlusion/disconnect >4h; check ketones |

11. Mind Map

Insulin Pump Therapy
Regimens
Basal-bolus (MDI)
Basal only
Pre-mixed BD
CSII (Pump)
Analogues
Rapid: Lispro, Aspart, Glulisine
Ultra-rapid: Fiasp, Lyumjev
Long: Glargine U-100, Detemir
Ultra-long: Glargine U-300, Degludec
Concentrated: U-200, U-300, U-500
Calculations
TDD: 0.3-0.7 U/kg
Basal:Bolus 40:60
ICR = 500/TDD
ISF = 100/TDD
T1DM
MDI or CSII
Carb counting
T2DM
Basal 1st
Stop SU
Concentrated if >200U
CSII
Programmable basal
Bolus calculator
SAP / HCL

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionCSII: continuous rapid insulin; basal + bolus via pump
**Key Test"SMBG/CGM (pre-meal, bed, 3am, drive); HbA1c 3-6mo; ketones if unwell
**Classification"Rapid (lispro/aspart), ultra-rapid (Fiasp), long (glargine), ultra-long (degludec/U-300), concentrated (U-200/300/500)
**Acute Mgmt"DKA: FRII 0.1U/kg/hr; Hypo: carbs/glucagon/IV dextrose
**Chronic Mgmt"T1DM: MDI/CSII; T2DM: basal → basal-bolus; ICR/ISF; carb counting
**Key Score"ICR=500/TDD; ISF=100/TDD; TDD=0.3-0.7 U/kg
**Complications"Hypoglycaemia, weight gain, lipohypertrophy, allergy
**Prognosis"T1DM: lifelong; T2DM: progressive; CSII/HCL improves control/hypo

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days
