Tags

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #fcps #mrcp #full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.8.6 Insulin Therapy
[!info]
Insulin therapy: essential for all T1DM; progressive in T2DM — Basal-bolus (MDI) standard: basal 40–50% TDD (glargine/degludec) + bolus 50–60% TDD (rapid analogues); CSII/pump if HbA1c>58 on MDI, severe hypo, Dawn phenomenon; Carb counting (ICR/ISF); Analogues: rapid (lispro/aspart/glulisine), long (glargine U-100/U-300, degludec), ultra-rapid (Fiasp/Lyumjev); Concentrated: U-200 degludec, U-300 glargine, U-500 regular.

1. Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Construct basal-bolus regimen (basal 40–50%, bolus 50–60% TDD)
  • [ ] Select insulin analogues by onset/peak/duration profile
  • [ ] Calculate ICR (insulin-to-carb ratio) and ISF (insulin sensitivity factor)
  • [ ] Apply CSII/pump indications and settings (basal rates, bolus wizard)
  • [ ] Manage concentrated insulins (U-200/300/500) and transition in T2DM

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionExogenous insulin replacement for absolute (T1DM) or relative (T2DM) insulin deficiency
T1DM100% require insulin from diagnosis; basal-bolus (MDI) or CSII
T2DM~30% eventually require insulin; progressive β-cell failure; start when HbA1c>58 on dual/triple oral
TDD (Total Daily Dose)Estimate: 0.3–0.5 U/kg/day (higher in obesity, puberty, pregnancy, steroids)
Basal : Bolus SplitBasal 40–50% TDD, Bolus 50–60% TDD (pre-meal + correction)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A — therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Insulin Analogues — Pharmacokinetic Profiles

TypeAgentOnsetPeakDurationKey Features
Rapid-actingLispro (Humalog), Aspart (Novorapid), Glulisine (Apidra)10–15 min1–2 h3–5 hStandard pre-meal; inject 0–15 min before eating
Ultra-rapidFiasp (faster aspart), Lyumjev (faster lispro)5–10 min30–60 min3–5 hNiacinamide/vit B3 + citrate → faster absorption; inject at meal start or ≤20 min after
Long-actingGlargine U-100 (Lantus), Detemir (Levemir)1–2 hFlat (minimal)24 hGlargine U-100: once daily; Detemir: once/twice daily
Ultra-longGlargine U-300 (Toujeo), Degludec (Tresiba)1–2 hFlat>42 h (Degludec 42h, U-300 ~36h)Once daily; flexible timing (±3h degludec); less hypoglycaemia (nocturnal)
IntermediateNPH (Humulin I, Insulatard)1–2 h4–8 h12–18 hCheap; used in resource-limited; ↑nocturnal hypo
Pre-mixedBiphasic 30/70 (30% rapid, 70% intermediate)As rapid componentDual12–18 hFixed ratio; twice daily; regular meals required
ConcentratedDegludec U-200, Glargine U-300, Regular U-500Per base insulinPer basePer baseFor insulin resistance >200U/day; ↓volume, ↓injection pain

Insulin Regimens

RegimenIndicationStructure
Basal-bolus (MDI)T1DM standard; T2DM on multiple oralsBasal (glargine/degludec) 1–2x daily + Rapid (lispro/aspart) pre-meal ×3–4
Basal onlyT2DM initiationGlargine/degludec 10U or 0.1–0.2 U/kg OD; titrate to FPG 4–7 mmol/L
Basal + 1 bolusT2DM partial controlAdd largest meal bolus
Pre-mixed BDT2DM, regular meals, limited monitoring0.3–0.5 U/kg BD (2/3 AM, 1/3 PM); fixed ratio
CSII (Pump)T1DM: HbA1c>58 on MDI, severe hypo, Dawn phenomenon, pregnancy, lifestyleRapid only; programmable basal rates; bolus calculator; suspend on low (SAP)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleKey Details
HbA1cEfficacy monitoring3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
SMBG / CGMDose adjustmentPre-meal, bedtime, 3am (basal check), pre-driving, suspected hypo
C-peptideResidual β-cell functionLow = T1DM/insulin-requiring; preserved = T2DM/MODY
Ketones (blood)DKA risk / insulin adequacy>0.6 mmol/L on insulin = inadequate; SGLT2i: euglycaemic DKA risk
WeightInsulin-associated gainMonitor; SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA mitigate

6. Differential Diagnosis

ScenarioDistinguishing Features
Insulin resistance vs deficiencyHigh TDD (>1U/kg), obesity, acanthosis, cysts → resistance; add SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/Metformin
Dawn phenomenon3am glucose normal, rising 3–8am; ↑basal rate 3–5am (pump) or switch to ultra-long basal
Somogyi effect3am hypoglycaemia → rebound hyperglycaemia; ↓basal dose
LipohypertrophyPalpable nodules at injection sites; erratic absorption; rotate sites
Insulin allergyLocal (erythema, pruritus) vs systemic (anaphylaxis); human insulin/analogues rare; desensitisation

7. Management

Basal-Bolus (MDI) Construction

StepCalculation
1. TDD estimate0.3–0.5 U/kg/day (T1DM: 0.5–0.7; T2DM: 0.3–0.5; higher if obese/pubertal/steroids)
2. Basal dose40–50% TDD (e.g., 50U TDD → 20–25U basal)
3. Bolus dose50–60% TDD ÷ 3–4 meals (e.g., 30U bolus → 7–10U per meal)
4. ICR (Insulin:Carb Ratio)500 ÷ TDD = grams carb per 1U (e.g., 500/50 = 10g/U)
5. ISF (Insulin Sensitivity Factor)100 ÷ TDD = mmol/L drop per 1U (e.g., 100/50 = 2 mmol/L/U)
6. Pre-meal bolus(Carbs ÷ ICR) + (Current BG − Target) ÷ ISF

Carbohydrate Counting

ConceptDetail
ICR1 unit rapid insulin per X grams carbohydrate (500/TDD rule)
ISF1 unit rapid insulin drops BG by Y mmol/L (100/TDD rule)
Target pre-meal4–7 mmol/L (individualised)
Correction dose(Current BG − Target) ÷ ISF
Bolus timingRapid: 10–15 min before meal; Ultra-rapid: at meal start

CSII (Pump) Settings

ParameterTypical Starting Point
Basal rates24h profile from MDI basal (e.g., 0.5–1.5 U/h); Dawn phenomenon: ↑3–5am
Bolus calculatorICR, ISF, target BG, active insulin time (3–5h)
Suspend on low (SAP)Threshold 3.9–3.5 mmol/L; suspend 30–120 min
Auto-mode (HCL)Algorithm adjusts basal ± micro-bolus; meal announcement needed

Concentrated Insulins in T2DM

InsulinConcentrationUse Case
Degludec U-200200 U/mLHigh dose (>100U/day); ↓volume, less injection pain
Glargine U-300300 U/mLHigh dose; flatter profile, ↓nocturnal hypo
Regular U-500500 U/mLSevere resistance (>200U/day); extended duration (18–24h); U-500 syringe/pen only

Transition to Insulin in T2DM

TriggerApproach
HbA1c >58 on dual/tripleBasal insulin 10U or 0.1–0.2 U/kg OD; continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; stop SU
Symptomatic hyperglycaemiaBasal-bolus from outset; TDD 0.3–0.5 U/kg
PregnancyBasal-bolus; tight targets (FPG <5.3, 1h <7.8, 2h <6.4); stop SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/SU/TZD
CKD G4–5Basal insulin ↓dose (25–50%); avoid SGLT2i if eGFR<20; monitor hypoglycaemia
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Insulin Indicated
↓
Type?
↓
Basal-bolus MDI OR CSII
Trigger
↓
Basal 40-50% (glargine/degludec)
Bolus 50-60% (rapid/ultra-rapid)
ICR = 500/TDD, ISF = 100/TDD
Basal insulin 0.1-0.2U/kg OD
Basal-bolus 0.3-0.5U/kg
Basal-bolus + tight targets
Basal ↓dose 25-50%
↓
Titrate to FPG 4-7
↓
Target met?
↓
Add bolus → basal-bolus
Continue + monitor 3-monthly

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
TDD estimate0.3–0.5 U/kg/day (T1DM 0.5–0.7; T2DM 0.3–0.5)
Basal:Bolus splitBasal 40–50% TDD; Bolus 50–60% TDD
ICR500 ÷ TDD = g carb per 1U (e.g., TDD 50 → 10g/U)
ISF100 ÷ TDD = mmol/L drop per 1U (e.g., TDD 50 → 2 mmol/L/U)
Rapid analoguesLispro, Aspart, Glulisine — 10–15min onset, 1–2h peak, 3–5h duration
Ultra-rapidFiasp, Lyumjev — 5–10min onset; inject at meal start
Long-actingGlargine U-100, Detemir — 24h, once daily
Ultra-longGlargine U-300, Degludec — >42h, flexible timing, ↓nocturnal hypo
ConcentratedDegludec U-200, Glargine U-300, Regular U-500 — for >200U/day
CSII indicationsHbA1c>58 on MDI, severe hypo, Dawn phenomenon, pregnancy, lifestyle
T2DM insulin startBasal 0.1–0.2 U/kg; continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; stop SU
Pump (CSII)Rapid only; programmable basal; bolus calculator; SAP/HCL

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
How do you calculate TDD for a new T1DM patient?0.5–0.7 U/kg/day; split basal 40–50%, bolus 50–60%
What is the 500 rule and 100 rule?500 ÷ TDD = ICR (g carb per 1U); 100 ÷ TDD = ISF (mmol/L drop per 1U)
Difference between glargine U-100 and U-300?U-300: 300U/mL, flatter, >36h, ↓nocturnal hypo, ↓injection volume; U-100: 100U/mL, 24h
What are the indications for CSII (pump) in T1DM?HbA1c >58mmol/mol (7.5%) on MDI, severe hypoglycaemia, Dawn phenomenon, pregnancy, erratic lifestyle
How do you manage insulin in T2DM starting basal insulin?Start 10U or 0.1–0.2 U/kg OD; titrate 2–4U q3–4d to FPG 4–7 mmol/L; continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; stop SU
What are concentrated insulins and when used?Degludec U-200, Glargine U-300, Regular U-500; for insulin resistance >200U/day; ↓volume, ↓pain, ↓nocturnal hypo
Difference between Fiasp/Lyumjev and standard rapid analogues?Ultra-rapid: onset 5–10min (vs 10–15min); inject at meal start (vs 10–15min before); niacinamide/citrate or treprostinil accelerates absorption
How do you adjust for Dawn phenomenon?CSII: ↑basal rate 3–5am; MDI: switch to ultra-long basal (degludec/U-300) or split basal (glargine U-100 50% AM/50% PM)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Basal = same as background?YES — basal = background = long-acting insulin covering hepatic glucose production
All rapid analogues interchangeable?Similar profiles; but ultra-rapid (Fiasp/Lyumjev) ≠ standard — different timing
Pre-mixed = basal-bolus?NO — pre-mixed is fixed ratio (30/70); less flexible; for regular meals only
Insulin dose in CKD?Reduce 25–50%; ↓clearance; monitor hypoglycaemia; SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA preferred if eGFR allows

Mnemonic: INSULIN-BASICS

  • Insulin TDD: 0.3-0.5 U/kg (T2DM), 0.5-0.7 (T1DM)
  • NPB: Basal 40-50%, Bolus 50-60%
  • Split: Basal (glargine/degludec) + Bolus (rapid/ultra-rapid)
  • Ultra-rapid: Fiasp/Lyumjev (5-10min onset, at meal)
  • Long: Glargine U-100/Detemir (24h); Ultra-long: U-300/Degludec (>42h)
  • ICR = 500/TDD; ISF = 100/TDD
  • No SU when insulin started in T2DM
  • Basal only 1st in T2DM → add bolus if needed
  • Auto-mode (HCL): algorithm adjusts basal
  • SAP: suspend on low (threshold 3.9)
  • ICSII indications: HbA1c>58, severe hypo, Dawn, pregnancy
  • Concentrated: U-200/U-300/U-500 for >200U/day
  • Stop SU when insulin started

11. Mind Map

Insulin Therapy
Regimens
Basal-bolus (MDI)
Basal only
Pre-mixed BD
CSII (Pump)
Analogues
Rapid: Lispro, Aspart, Glulisine
Ultra-rapid: Fiasp, Lyumjev
Long: Glargine U-100, Detemir
Ultra-long: Glargine U-300, Degludec
Concentrated: U-200, U-300, U-500
Calculations
TDD: 0.3-0.7 U/kg
Basal:Bolus 40:60
ICR = 500/TDD
ISF = 100/TDD
T1DM
MDI or CSII
Carb counting
T2DM
Basal 1st
Stop SU
Concentrated if >200U
CSII
Programmable basal
Bolus calculator
SAP / HCL

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionExogenous insulin for absolute (T1DM) or relative (T2DM) deficiency
**Key Test"SMBG/CGM (pre-meal, bed, 3am, drive); HbA1c 3-6mo; ketones if unwell
**Classification"Rapid (lispro/aspart), ultra-rapid (Fiasp), long (glargine), ultra-long (degludec/U-300), concentrated (U-200/300/500)
**Acute Mgmt"DKA: FRII 0.1U/kg/hr; Hypo: carbs/glucagon/IV dextrose
**Chronic Mgmt"T1DM: MDI/CSII; T2DM: basal → basal-bolus; ICR/ISF; carb counting
**Key Score"ICR=500/TDD; ISF=100/TDD; TDD=0.3-0.7 U/kg
**Complications"Hypoglycaemia, weight gain, lipohypertrophy, allergy
**Prognosis"T1DM: lifelong; T2DM: progressive; CSII/HCL improves control/hypo

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

▸ 21.8.8 Dfu Management Offloading Debridement Infection Revascularisation

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, dfu-management, offloading, debridement, infection, revascularisation, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - TIME principles (Tissue, Infection, Moisture, Edge); offloading = cornerstone (TCC gold standard); debridement; infection classification (IWGDF/PEDIS); vascular assessment; MDT approach"

see_also: ["Diabetic foot ulcer (DFU) classification", "Foot risk classification (IWGDF/Scottish)", "Charcot neuroarthropathy", "Amputation prevention and multidisciplinary care"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Diabetic Foot Ulcer (DFU) Management

[!info]
DFU management = MDT + TIME + OFFLOADING — offloading is cornerstone (Total Contact Cast gold standard); debridement essential; infection per IWGDF/PEDIS; vascular assessment mandatory; coordinate diabetology, podiatry, vascular, orthotics, microbiology.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply TIME framework to wound bed preparation
  • [ ] Select appropriate offloading modality (TCC, removable walker, etc.)
  • [ ] Classify and treat infection per IWGDF/PEDIS
  • [ ] Assess vascular status and refer for revascularisation
  • [ ] Coordinate MDT care

2. TIME Framework — Wound Bed Preparation

LetterPrincipleAction
TTissue (non-viable)Debridement (sharp, enzymatic, autolytic, larval) — remove necrosis, callus, slough
IInfection / InflammationAssess (PEDIS); treat with topical/systemic antibiotics per severity
MMoisture balanceMoist wound healing — appropriate dressings (foam, hydrocolloid, alginate, hydrogel)
EEdge (non-advancing)Stimulate edges — consider advanced therapies (NPWT, biologics) if stalled >4w

3. Offloading — Cornerstone of DFU Healing

ModalityDescriptionIndicationHealing RatePros/Cons
Total Contact Cast (TCC)Fibreglass/plaster cast, moulded to foot, changed weeklyGold standard for plantar forefoot/midfoot ulcers (UT 1A–2C)Highest (~80–90% at 12w)Non-removable (ensures adherence); skilled application; contraindicated if infection/ischaemia
Instant TCC (iTCC)Removable walker wrapped with cohesive bandageAlternative to TCCSimilar to TCCEasier application
Removable Cast Walker (RCW)Aircast, DH Offloading Walker, etc.If TCC contraindicated; patient preferenceLower (adherence ~30%)Removable → poor adherence
Offloading Shoes/InsolesCustom orthotics, healing sandalsPrevention; low-risk ulcersLimited for active ulcersAdherence-dependent
Crutches/WheelchairNon-weight-bearingAdjunct; severe ulcersPoor adherenceNot practical long-term

Key: Non-removable offloading (TCC/iTCC) > removable — adherence is critical.

4. Debridement

TypeMethodIndicationFrequency
Sharp (surgical)Scalpel, curette — by trained clinicianNecrotic tissue, callus, slough, biofilmWeekly at dressing change (TCC)
AutolyticHydrogel, hydrocolloidNon-urgent; non-infectedDaily
EnzymaticCollagenase, papain-ureaWhen sharp not availableDaily
Larval (maggot)Lucilia sericata larvaeNecrotic, sloughy; not infected48–72h cycles

Callus removal essential — increases plantar pressure 30–50%; debride at every visit.

5. Infection Classification & Management (IWGDF / PEDIS)

PEDIS Infection Grades

GradeClinical CriteriaTreatment
1 (No infection)No purulence, no inflammationNo antibiotics; local wound care
2 (Mild)≥2 signs inflammation (erythema 0.5–2cm, warmth, induration) Limited to skin/subcutNarrow-spectrum oral (flucloxacillin 500mg QDS 1–2w)
3 (Moderate)Erythema >2cm OR deep tissue involvement (abscess, osteomyelitis, septic arthritis) OR systemic signs (fever, raised WCC/CRP) BUT haemodynamically stableBroad-spectrum oral/IV (co-amoxiclav, clindamycin + ciprofloxacin) 2–4w; bone = 6w
4 (Severe)Systemic sepsis (hypotension, tachycardia, confusion, lactate >2, organ dysfunction)IV broad-spectrum (piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem) + urgent surgical debridement + ICU

Osteomyelitis Diagnosis

  • Probe-to-bone +ve (specificity >85%)
  • ESR >70, CRP elevated
  • MRI (gold standard imaging)
  • Bone biopsy (culture + histology) — definitive

Antibiotic duration: Soft tissue 1–2w; Bone 6w (2w IV → 4w oral step-down).

6. Vascular Assessment & Revascularisation

TestThreshold for Action
ABI (Ankle-Brachial Index)<0.9 = PAD; <0.5 = severe; >1.3 = non-compressible (medial calcification) — use TBI
Toe-Brachial Index (TBI)<0.7 = PAD; <0.25 = critical limb ischaemia
TcPO2 (Transcutaneous O2)<30 mmHg = poor healing; <20 = critical
Duplex US / CTA / MRAAnatomic mapping for revascularisation

Revascularisation Indications (IWGDF):

  • Rest pain, tissue loss (ulcer/gangrene) + haemodynamic significance
  • Endovascular first (angioplasty, stenting) for TASC A/B
  • Bypass surgery for TASC C/D or failed endovascular
  • Multidisciplinary vascular MDT decision

7. MDT Composition & Pathways

Core MDT (NICE NG19 / IWGDF):

  • Diabetologist (medical lead)
  • Podiatrist (wound care, offloading, debridement)
  • Vascular Surgeon (revascularisation)
  • Orthotist (footwear, insoles, TCC)
  • Microbiologist (antibiotic stewardship)
  • Infectious Disease (complex infection)
  • Orthopaedic Surgeon (amputation, Charcot)
  • Tissue Viability Nurse
  • Radiologist (imaging)
  • Plastic Surgeon (reconstruction)

Pathways:

  • Low risk (Risk 1): Community podiatry
  • Moderate risk (Risk 2): MDT clinic 3–6 monthly
  • High risk (Risk 3): Specialist MDT 1–3 monthly
  • Active ulcer/infection/ischaemia: Urgent MDT within 24–48h

8. Adjunctive / Advanced Therapies (If Stalled >4 Weeks)

TherapyEvidenceUse
NPWT (Negative Pressure)RCT support for post-surgical / large woundsAfter debridement; bridge to healing
Hyperbaric Oxygen (HBOT)Cochrane: ↓ major amputation in selectedRefractory Wagner 3–4; adjunct
Growth Factors (PDGF, becaplermin)Modest benefit; expensiveRefractory clean ulcers
Skin Substitutes (dermal matrices)Case series; RCT limitedLarge defects post-debridement
Stem Cells / PRPExperimentalResearch settings

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Cornerstone of DFU healingOffloading — TCC gold standard (non-removable > removable)
TIMETissue (debridement), Infection, Moisture, Edge
Debridement frequencyWeekly sharp debridement at TCC change
PEDIS Grade 2 infectionMild: erythema 0.5–2cm → narrow oral abx (flucloxacillin)
PEDIS Grade 3 infectionModerate: >2cm or deep → broad-spectrum + imaging for bone
PEDIS Grade 4 infectionSevere: sepsis → IV broad + urgent surgical debridement
OsteomyelitisProbe-to-bone +ve; ESR >70; MRI; bone biopsy definitive
Antibiotic duration bone6 weeks (2 IV + 4 oral)
ABI <0.9PAD; <0.5 severe; >1.3 calcified (use TBI)
TcPO2 <30 mmHgPoor healing likely
MDT referralActive ulcer = urgent 24–48h
IWGDF Risk 31–3m specialist MDT

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Parent: [[../Diabetic foot disease|Diabetic foot disease]]
Hierarchy: [[../../Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]
Template: [[../../../Templates/Diabetes Topic Template|Diabetes Topic Template]]

Osteomyelitis Diagnosis

▸ 21.9 Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus 26
▸ 21.9.1 Asymptomatic Screening Ada Nice Uspstf

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply ADA, NICE, and USPSTF screening guidelines for T2DM
  • [ ] Identify high-risk populations requiring earlier/frequent screening
  • [ ] Choose appropriate screening test (HbA1c, FPG, OGTT)
  • [ ] Interpret screening results and apply confirmatory testing rules

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PurposeDetect asymptomatic T2DM and prediabetes for early intervention
Undiagnosed T2DM~1M in UK; ~8.5M in US; screening reduces complications
Cost-effectivenessScreening high-risk adults cost-effective; universal screening debated

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A - asymptomatic screening)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Screening Guidelines Comparison

GuidelinePopulationTestIntervalConfirmatory
ADA 2024Age >=35 OR BMI >=25 (23 Asian) + >=1 risk factorHbA1c, FPG, or OGTTEvery 3 years if normalRepeat abnormal test
NICE NG28Risk assessment (Leicester, QDiabetes, etc.) -> if high riskHbA1c (preferred) or FPGPer risk scoreRepeat HbA1c if 42-47
USPSTF 2021Age 35-70 with BMI >=25HbA1c, FPG, or OGTTEvery 3 years if normalRepeat abnormal test
WHOAge >40 or high-riskFPG preferredPer programmeRepeat on separate day

Risk Factors (ADA)

CategoryFactors
DemographicAge >=35; first-degree relative T2DM; high-risk ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean, Black African, Chinese)
AnthropometricBMI >=25 (>=23 Asian); waist >94cm M / >80cm F
MetabolicHypertension; dyslipidaemia (TG>1.7, HDL<1.0 M/<1.3 F); PCOS; previous GDM; CVD
LifestylePhysical inactivity; smoking

Screening in Special Populations

PopulationRecommendation
Children/AdolescentsBMI >=85th percentile + 2 risk factors (ADA: age >=10 or puberty)
PregnancyUniversal (IADPSG) or risk-based (NICE); see GDM note
HIVAnnual FPG/HbA1c on ART (high risk with PI, age>40, BMI>25)
AntipsychoticsBaseline + 12 weeks + annually (metabolic monitoring)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestAdvantageDisadvantage
HbA1cNo fasting; reflects 3mo; standardisedFalsely low in anaemia/haemoglobinopathy/CKD/pregnancy
FPGSimple, cheapRequires fasting; single timepoint
OGTTGold standard pathophysiologyInconvenient; poor reproducibility; rarely used for routine screening

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A - screening context)

7. Management After Screening

ResultAction
Normal (FPG <6.1, HbA1c <39)Routine screening per interval
Prediabetes (IFG 6.1-6.9, IGT 7.8-11.0, HbA1c 39-47)Intensive lifestyle (DPP); metformin if high risk (BMI>=35, age<60, GDM hx, rising HbA1c)
Diabetes (FPG>=7.0, HbA1c>=48, 2h-OGTT>=11.1)Confirm on separate day (unless symptomatic + random>=11.1); initiate treatment
Discordant resultsRepeat abnormal test; if still discordant -> 3rd test

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
ADAAge >=35 OR BMI>=25+1 RF; HbA1c/FPG/OGTT; repeat 3y if normal
NICERisk score -> if high risk: HbA1c (preferred) or FPG
USPSTFAge 35-70, BMI>=25; HbA1c/FPG/OGTT; repeat 3y
Confirmatory ruleAsymptomatic: 2 abnormal tests (same test different day, or 2 different tests); Symptomatic + random>=11.1 = single test
ChildrenBMI>=85th %ile + 2 RF; age>=10 or puberty
HIVAnnual on ART

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What are the ADA screening criteria for T2DM?Age >=35 OR BMI>=25 (23 Asian) + >=1 risk factor (FH, high-risk ethnicity, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, PCOS, GDM, CVD, inactivity)
How does NICE screening differ from ADA?NICE: validated risk score first -> if high risk then HbA1c/FPG; no universal age cut-off
When do you need a confirmatory test?Asymptomatic patient: two abnormal results needed (same test different day, or FPG + HbA1c). Symptomatic + random glucose>=11.1 = single test sufficient.
What are the risk factors for T2DM screening?Age>=35, BMI>=25, FH T2DM, high-risk ethnicity (South Asian, African-Caribbean), hypertension, dyslipidaemia, PCOS, previous GDM, CVD
Screening in children?BMI>=85th percentile + 2 risk factors; age>=10 or puberty onset

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Screening = diagnosis?NO - screening identifies candidates for confirmatory testing
HbA1c always preferred?NO - falsely low in anaemia/CKD/pregnancy/haemoglobinopathy; use FPG in these

Mnemonic: SCREEN-T2DM

  • Screening: ADA age>=35 or BMI>=25+1 RF
  • Confirm: asymptomatic = 2 tests; symptomatic = 1 test
  • Repeat interval: 3 years if normal
  • Ethnicity: South Asian, African-Caribbean, Chinese = high risk
  • Early: age 35 (ADA) / risk score (NICE)
  • NICE: risk score first
  • Tests: HbA1c, FPG, OGTT (all acceptable)
  • 2 abnormal = diagnosis (asymptomatic)
  • Discordant: repeat abnormal test
  • Metabolic syndrome components = risk factors
  • Undiagnosed: ~1M UK, ~8.5M US

11. Mind Map

T2DM Asymptomatic Screening
ADA
Age>=35 or BMI>=25+1 RF
HbA1c/FPG/OGTT
3-yearly if normal
NICE
Risk score first
If high -> HbA1c/FPG
USPSTF
35-70, BMI>=25
3-yearly if normal
Confirmatory
Asymptomatic: 2 tests
Symptomatic: 1 test
Special
Children: BMI 85th% + 2 RF
HIV: annual on ART
Antipsychotics: baseline, 12wk, annual

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionDetect asymptomatic T2DM/prediabetes in high-risk adults
**Key Test"HbA1c (preferred), FPG, OGTT (all acceptable)
**Classification"Normal <6.1/<39; Prediabetes 6.1-6.9/39-47; DM >=7.0/>=48
**Acute Mgmt"N/A
**Chronic Mgmt"Normal -> 3y repeat; Prediabetes -> lifestyle; DM -> confirm -> treat
**Key Score"ADA age>=35/BMI>=25+1RF; NICE risk score; confirmatory rule
**Complications"Early detection -> reduced complications
**Prognosis"Early intervention prevents/delays T2DM in prediabetes

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

SectionScore /5Last Attempt

▸ 21.9.1 Gck Mody Mody 2

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, gck-mody, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3)", "HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

GCK-MODY (MODY 2)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise GCK-MODY clinical features
  • [ ] Understand why no treatment is needed
  • [ ] Avoid unnecessary treatment
  • [ ] Recognise in pregnancy (GDM overlap)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GeneGCK (glucokinase)
Chromosome7p15.3
Prevalence2nd most common MODY (30-35% of MODY)
MechanismGlucokinase = pancreatic glucose sensor; heterozygous loss -> ↑glucose set-point

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
OnsetNeonatal/childhood; often asymptomatic
HyperglycaemiaMild fasting (5.5-8.0 mmol/L); stable lifelong
HbA1c39-58 mmol/mol (5.7-7.5%); stable
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved
OGTMild impairment (2h ~8-10 mmol/L)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureGCK-MODYHNF1A-MODYT1DMT2DM
TreatmentNoneSulfonylureaInsulinMetformin ± others
HyperglycaemiaMild fasting (5.5-8.0)ProgressiveSevereVariable
ComplicationsVirtually noneMicrovascularHighHigh
PregnancyTreat only if maternalTreat with SUInsulinMetformin/insulin

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Fasting glucose5.5-8.0 mmol/L (stable)
HbA1c39-58 mmol/mol (5.7-7.5%)
2h OGTTMild elevation
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved
Genetic testingGCK heterozygous mutation

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoantibodies +, severe hyperglycaemia
T2DMObese, progressive, insulin resistance
HNF1A-MODYProgressive, low CRP, SU responsive
Gestational DMResolves postpartum; GCK may persist

7. Management

No Treatment Needed

PrincipleDetail
No glucose-lowering therapyStable mild hyperglycaemia; no microvascular complications
Annual monitoringHbA1c, fasting glucose
PregnancyTreat only if maternal GCK mutation (fetal risk of macrosomia)
Insulin/SUContraindicated - causes hypoglycaemia

Pregnancy Management

Fetal GenotypeManagement
Maternal GCK mutationInsulin if fetal GCK mutation NOT inherited (fetus can't compensate)
Fetus inherits GCK mutationNo treatment (fetus has same set-point)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
No treatmentStable mild hyperglycaemia; no complications
Fasting glucose5.5-8.0 mmol/L (stable lifelong)
HbA1c39-58 mmol/mol (5.7-7.5%)
ComplicationsVirtually none (no micro/macrovascular)
PregnancyTreat ONLY if mother has GCK mutation & fetus does NOT

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the treatment for GCK-MODY?No treatment needed - stable mild hyperglycaemia, no complications
What is the fasting glucose range in GCK-MODY?5.5-8.0 mmol/L (stable lifelong)
Why no treatment for GCK-MODY?Glucokinase mutation -> ↑glucose set-point; stable mild hyperglycaemia; no complications
When do you treat GCK-MODY in pregnancy?Only if mother has GCK mutation and fetus does NOT inherit it (fetal macrosomia risk)
What are the complications of GCK-MODY?Virtually none - no microvascular, no macrovascular

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All MODY need treatment?NO - GCK-MODY needs no treatment
GCK-MODY = GDM?NO - GCK is genetic, lifelong; GDM resolves postpartum
All MODY need insulin?NO - GCK needs none; HNF1A needs SU; HNF1B needs insulin

Mnemonic: GCK-NO-TREATMENT

  • GCK: glucokinase (glucose sensor)
  • Chromosome: 7p15
  • Kinase: heterozygous loss -> ↑set-point
  • No treatment needed
  • Overwhelmingly stable
  • Treatment: none
  • Rarely any complications
  • Excellent prognosis
  • Autosomal dominant
  • Treats: none
  • Mild fasting 5.5-8.0
  • Excellent prognosis
  • No microvascular complications
  • Treatment: NONE

Topic Overview

GCK-MODY (MODY 2
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
No Treatment Needed
Pregnancy Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics

Neonatal diabetes

[!info]
Topic-Group hub for Neonatal diabetes — covers 3 disease-level topic(s) per Davidson Ch25.
Related: [[Diabetes MOC]] · [[Davidson Chapter 25 - Diabetes Hierarchy|Diabetes Hierarchy]]

Disease-Level Topics

  • [[KCNJ11/ABCC8]]
  • [[Transient vs permanent]]
  • [[Monogenic and Atypical Diabetes/Neonatal diabetes/Sulfonylurea responsiveness.md|Sulfonylurea responsiveness]]

High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)

AspectDetails
DefinitionDiabetes diagnosed <6 months of age
GeneticsKCNJ11 (Kir6.2) ~40% · ABCC8 (SUR1) ~10% · 6q24 imprinting (transient) · INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A, FOXP3, EIF2AK3 (Wolcott-Rallison)
Transient (TNDM)Remission by 18 months, relapse in adolescence/adulthood · 6q24 methylation defect (70%) · KCNJ11/ABCC8 activating mutations
Permanent (PNDM)Lifelong · KCNJ11/ABCC8 (most common) · INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A, EIF2AK3
Sulfonylurea ResponseKCNJ11/ABCC8: 90% switch successfully (tolbutamide/gliclazide) · Dose 0.5-1.0mg/kg/day · Neurological features may persist (DEND syndrome)

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #topic-group-hub #fcps #mrcp

Topic Overview

Neonatal diabetes
Disease-Level Topics
High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)
Quick Links
Tags
▸ 21.9.10 Sulfonylureas

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify sulfonylureas by generation and duration
  • [ ] Apply renal/hepatic dosing adjustments
  • [ ] Recognise hypoglycaemia risk factors and management
  • [ ] Apply CAROLINA trial implications (CV neutrality but ↑hypo vs DPP-4i)
  • [ ] Choose preferred agent in CKD/elderly (gliclazide MR)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassSulfonylureas (insulin secretagogues)
MechanismBind SUR1 subunit of KATP channel → channel closure → membrane depolarisation → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin exocytosis (glucose-independent at high doses)
Generations1st gen: tolbutamide, chlorpropamide (long duration, more side effects); 2nd gen: glibenclamide (glyburide), glipizide, gliclazide, glimepiride (more potent, shorter duration); 3rd gen: glimepiride
Agents & DosesGliclazide MR: 30-120mg OD (max 120mg); Gliclazide: 40-320mg BD; Glimepiride: 1-6mg OD; Glipizide: 2.5-20mg BD; Glibenclamide: 2.5-15mg BD (AVOID in elderly/CKD)
HbA1c Reduction1.0-1.5% (11-16 mmol/mol) monotherapy
Weight Change+2-4kg (insulin anabolic effect)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A — drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Agent Comparison

AgentGenerationDurationDose RangeRenal AdjustmentKey Features
Gliclazide MR2nd24h30-120mg ODSafe in CKD (no active metabolites)Preferred in elderly/CKD; antioxidant; ↓platelet aggregation
Gliclazide2nd12-24h40-320mg BDCaution CKDStandard
Glimepiride3rd24h1-6mg ODUse caution (active metabolites)Once daily; ↑hypo risk in elderly
Glipizide2nd12-24h2.5-20mg BDSafe in CKD (inactive metabolites)Short-acting option
Glibenclamide2nd24h+2.5-15mg BDAVOID in CKD/elderlyProlonged active metabolites → severe/prolonged hypo

CAROLINA Trial (Glimepiride vs Linagliptin)

OutcomeGlimepirideLinagliptinHR (95% CI)
MACE (CV death/MI/stroke)12.4%12.0%1.03 (0.90-1.18) — non-inferior
Severe hypoglycaemia2.3%0.6%3.8x higher
Weight gain+1.2kg–1.0kg—
ConclusionCV neutral vs DPP-4iBut significantly more hypoglycaemia

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
Glucose (SMBG/CGM)Hypoglycaemia detectionEducate on symptoms; CGM if high risk
Renal function (eGFR)Dose adjustmentBefore initiation, then 6-12 monthly
WeightWeight gain trackingEvery visit

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycaemiaCan occur hours after dose; prolonged (esp glibenclamide, glimepiride); elderly, CKD, missed meals, alcohol, β-blockers mask symptoms
Factitious hypoglycaemiaSurreptitious SU use; ↑insulin + ↓C-peptide (exogenous insulin) — but SU → ↑insulin + ↑C-peptide; screen urine for SU metabolites
InsulinomaFasting hypo; ↑insulin + ↑C-peptide + ↑proinsulin; CT/MRI pancreas
Post-bariatric hypoglycaemiaLate dumping (1-3h post-meal); nesidioblastosis; GLP-1 mediated

7. Management

Initiation & Dosing

StepAction
1. Select agentGliclazide MR 1st choice (CKD safe, once daily, ↓hypo); Glipizide if short-acting needed; AVOID glibenclamide
2. Start lowGliclazide MR 30mg OD; Glimepiride 1mg OD; Glipizide 2.5mg BD
3. TitrateIncrease every 2-4 weeks to max dose or till HbA1c target
4. CombineAdd to metformin; avoid triple SU + insulin + DPP-4i (hypo risk)

Hypoglycaemia Prevention

Risk FactorMitigation
Elderly (>75)Use gliclazide MR; start 30mg; target HbA1c 58-64; avoid glimepiride/glibenclamide
CKD (eGFR<45)Gliclazide MR or glipizide only; dose reduce; monitor glucose
Erratic mealsEducate on regular meals; consider DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA instead
AlcoholWarn on fasting alcohol + SU = severe hypo
β-blockersMask autonomic symptoms; educate on neuroglycopenic signs

Switching/Deintensification

ScenarioAction
Recurrent hypoglycaemia↓Dose or stop SU; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Starting insulinSTOP SU (↓hypo risk); continue metformin ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Frailty/elderlyDeintensify: stop SU, relax HbA1c target, simplify regimens
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Sulfonylurea Indicated
↓
Agent Selection
↓
Gliclazide MR 30mg OD
Glipizide 2.5mg BD
Glimepiride 1mg OD - caution elderly/CKD
Glibenclamide - prolonged hypo
↓
Titrate q2-4wk to max
↓
Recurrent hypo?
Starting insulin?
↓
↓Dose or STOP; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Continue + monitor HbA1c 6-monthly
STOP SU; continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismBind SUR1 → close KATP channel → depolarisation → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin secretion (glucose-independent at high doses)
Preferred agentGliclazide MR: once daily, CKD safe, ↓hypo vs glimepiride, antioxidant
AVOIDGlibenclamide (prolonged active metabolites → severe/prolonged hypo in elderly/CKD)
Hypoglycaemia riskElderly, CKD, erratic meals, alcohol, β-blockers, high dose, glimepiride/glibenclamide
Weight gain+2-4kg (insulin anabolic effect)
CAROLINA trialGlimepiride ≈ linagliptin for MACE (non-inferior) but 3.8x more severe hypo; weight gain vs loss
Renal dosingGliclazide MR/glipizide safe; glimepiride caution; glibenclamide CONTRAINDICATED
With insulinSTOP SU when insulin started (↓hypo risk); continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
DeintensificationStop SU if recurrent hypo, elderly/frail; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of sulfonylureas?Bind SUR1 subunit of KATP channel on pancreatic β-cells → channel closure → membrane depolarisation → voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channel opening → Ca²⁺ influx → insulin exocytosis
Which sulfonylurea is preferred in CKD/elderly?Gliclazide MR — no active metabolites, once daily, ↓hypoglycaemia risk vs glimepiride, antioxidant properties
Why is glibenclamide avoided in elderly/CKD?Prolonged active metabolites excreted renally → accumulation → severe/prolonged hypoglycaemia; higher CV mortality in some studies
What did the CAROLINA trial show?Glimepiride non-inferior to linagliptin for MACE (HR 1.03) but severe hypoglycaemia 3.8x higher (2.3% vs 0.6%); weight gain +1.2kg vs -1.0kg
How do you manage sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycaemia?Acute: 15-20g fast carbs; chronic: ↓dose or stop SU; switch to DPP-4i/SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; educate on meal regularity, alcohol avoidance
What happens when you start insulin on a sulfonylurea?STOP the sulfonylurea — combination ↑hypoglycaemia risk; continue metformin ± SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
Difference between gliclazide MR and standard gliclazide?MR: modified release, once daily (30-120mg), smoother profile, ↓hypo; standard: BD dosing (40-320mg BD)
Can sulfonylureas be used in T1DM?NO — require functional β-cells; ineffective in absolute insulin deficiency

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Sulfonylureas cause weight loss?NO — cause weight gain 2-4kg (insulin anabolic)
All sulfonylureas same hypo risk?NO — gliclazide MR < glipizide < glimepiride < glibenclamide (highest)
Sulfonylurea + DPP-4i combo?Possible but ↑hypo risk vs DPP-4i alone; prefer SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA as add-on
Sulfonylurea + GLP-1 RA?Possible but ↑hypo risk; GLP-1 RA preferred over SU as 2nd line after metformin

Mnemonic: SU-GLIC-MR

  • Secretagogue: close KATP (SUR1) → insulin release
  • Up weight: +2-4kg
  • Gliclazide MR: 1st choice (CKD safe, once daily, ↓hypo)
  • Low dose start: Glic MR 30mg, Glime 1mg
  • Insulin combo: STOP SU when insulin started
  • CAROLINA: Glimepiride = Lina MACE but 3.8x severe hypo
  • MR preferred: once daily, smoother profile
  • Renal: Glic MR/Glipizide safe; Glime caution; Glibenclamide AVOID

11. Mind Map

Sulfonylureas
Mechanism
SUR1 binding
KATP closure
Ca2+ influx
Insulin secretion
Agents
Gliclazide MR (1st choice)
Gliclazide
Glimepiride
Glipizide
Glibenclamide (AVOID)
Effects
HbA1c ↓1-1.5%
Weight ↑2-4kg
Hypoglycaemia risk
CAROLINA
Glimepiride vs Linagliptin
MACE non-inferior
Hypo 3.8x higher
Renal
Glic MR safe
Glipizide safe
Glimepiride caution
Glibenclamide CONTRAINDICATED
With Insulin
STOP SU
Continue Met ± SGLT2i/GLP-1

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionSulfonylureas: gliclazide MR, glimepiride, glipizide, glibenclamide — KATP channel blockers
**Key Test"SMBG for hypoglycaemia; HbA1c 6-monthly; eGFR before initiation
**Classification"1st gen: tolbutamide; 2nd gen: gliclazide, glipizide, glibenclamide; 3rd gen: glimepiride
**Acute Mgmt"Hypoglycaemia: 15-20g carbs; severe: glucagon/IV dextrose
**Chronic Mgmt"Glic MR 30mg OD → titrate; stop if hypo; STOP when insulin started
**Key Score"CAROLINA: MACE non-inferior but 3.8x severe hypo vs DPP-4i
**Complications"Hypoglycaemia (elderly, CKD, erratic meals), weight gain
**Prognosis"Effective glucose lowering; hypoglycaemia limits use in high-risk

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

▸ 21.9.11 Easd 2023 Consensus Algorithm

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply ADA/EASD 2023+ consensus algorithm for T2DM management
  • [ ] Select 1st/2nd/3rd line agents based on comorbidities (ASCVD, HF, CKD, obesity)
  • [ ] Integrate CVOT evidence (SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA) into treatment decisions
  • [ ] Navigate NICE NG28 pathway for UK practice
  • [ ] Intensify therapy appropriately (dual -> triple -> insulin)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GuidelineADA/EASD Consensus Report 2023 (Diabetes Care, Diabetologia) -- updated annually
PhilosophyPerson-centred care -- individualised targets, shared decision-making, comorbidity-driven drug selection
Key ShiftComorbidity-first -- ASCVD/HF/CKD/obesity determine 1st add-on after metformin, NOT glucose-lowering potency alone

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A -- treatment algorithm applies after diagnosis confirmed)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

ADA/EASD 2023 Algorithm Steps

StepActionDetails
1. FoundationLifestyle + Metformin (if tolerated)Metformin 1st line unless contraindicated (eGFR<30, intolerance)
2. Comorbidity AssessmentASCVD? HF? CKD? Obesity?Drives drug choice -- see Indication Matrix
3. Add-on TherapyBased on comorbidity profileSee Indication Matrix below
4. IntensificationDual -> Triple -> InsulinAdd agents with complementary mechanisms
5. DeintensificationIf hypoglycaemia, side effects, frailtySimplify, stop SU/insulin, higher targets

Indication Matrix (Comorbidity-First)

ComorbidityPreferred 1st Add-onAlternativeEvidence
Established ASCVDGLP-1 RA (proven MACE[down]) OR SGLT2i (proven MACE[down])If both contraindicated: DPP-4i (neutral)LEADER, SUSTAIN-6, REWIND, EMPA-REG, CANVAS, DECLARE
Heart Failure (HFrEF/HFpEF)SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin)GLP-1 RA (less evidence)DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced/Preserved, DELIVER
CKD (eGFR >=20)SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozin)GLP-1 RA (semaglutide FLOW, liraglutide LEADER renal)CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY, FLOW
Obesity (BMI >=30/27.5 Asian)GLP-1 RA (semaglutide 2.4mg, liraglutide 3mg) OR TirzepatideSGLT2i (modest weight loss)STEP, SURMOUNT, SURPASS
No High-Risk ComorbiditiesLow hypo risk: SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, DPP-4i, TZDCost/access driven: SU, basal insulinIndividualise

CVOT Evidence Summary

Drug ClassKey TrialsMACE BenefitHF BenefitRenal Benefit
SGLT2iEMPA-REG, CANVAS, DECLARE, VERTIS-CV[check] (empagliflozin, canagliflozin)[check][check] (all)[check][check] (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY)
GLP-1 RALEADER, SUSTAIN-6, REWIND, HARMONY, PIONEER-6, FLOW[check][check] (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide, albiglutide)Neutral (FIGHT) / [up]symptoms HFpEF (STEP-HFpEF)[check] (semaglutide FLOW, liraglutide LEADER renal)
DPP-4iTECOS, SAVOR, EXAMINE, CARMELINA, CAROLINA[x] (saxagliptin: [up]HF hosp)Saxagliptin [up]HF hospNeutral
TZDPROactive (pioglitazone)[down]Stroke, neutral MACE[up]HF riskNeutral
SUCAROLINA (linagliptin vs glimepiride)Neutral/possible harm[up]HF riskNeutral

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

(N/A -- algorithm applies after diagnosis)

6. Differential Diagnosis

(N/A)

7. Management

Stepwise Algorithm (ADA/EASD 2023)

⬇ Clinical Pathway
T2DM Diagnosed
↓
Lifestyle + Metformin
↓
Comorbidities?
↓
GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i with MACE benefit
SGLT2i preferred
SGLT2i preferred
GLP-1 RA or Tirzepatide
SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/DPP-4i/TZD + cost/access
↓
Target not met?
↓
Add 3rd agent: complementary mechanism
Maintain + monitor
↓
Target not met?
↓
Basal insulin OR GLP-1 RA if not used
↓
Intensify to basal-bolus if needed

Dual Therapy Options (after Metformin)

If on Metformin +AddRationale
SGLT2iGLP-1 RAComplementary: SGLT2i (renal/HF) + GLP-1 (weight/CV)
GLP-1 RASGLT2iSame as above
DPP-4iSGLT2i or GLP-1 RAUpgrade to CV/renal benefit agent
SUSGLT2i or GLP-1 RA[down]Hypo risk, CV benefit
TZDSGLT2iMitigate fluid retention

Triple Therapy

CombinationUse Case
Met + SGLT2i + GLP-1 RAIdeal for ASCVD/HF/CKD/Obesity -- maximal CV/renal/weight benefit
Met + SGLT2i + DPP-4iIf GLP-1 RA not tolerated/contraindicated
Met + GLP-1 RA + Basal insulinIf HbA1c >58 on dual; convert to basal-bolus if needed

Insulin Initiation

TriggerApproach
HbA1c >58 (7.5%) on dual/tripleBasal insulin 0.1-0.2 U/kg or 10U OD; continue Met [plus/minus] SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; stop SU
Symptomatic hyperglycaemiaBasal-bolus from outset
Pregnancy/pre-conceptionInsulin preferred; stop SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/TZD/SU
CKD G4-5Basal insulin ([down]dose 25-50%); avoid SGLT2i if eGFR<20

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Step 1Lifestyle + Metformin (unless contraindicated)
Step 2Assess comorbidities -- ASCVD/HF/CKD/Obesity drive choice
ASCVDGLP-1 RA (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide) OR SGLT2i (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin)
HFSGLT2i 1st line (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) -- all HF types
CKDSGLT2i 1st line (dapa [ge]25, empa [ge]20, cana [ge]30) -- continue to dialysis
ObesityGLP-1 RA (semaglutide 2.4mg, liraglutide 3mg) OR Tirzepatide 15mg
No comorbiditySGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/DPP-4i/TZD -- consider cost, access, patient preference
Triple therapyMet + SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA = optimal CV/renal/weight combo
Insulin startBasal 0.1-0.2 U/kg; continue Met [plus/minus] SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA; stop SU
NICE NG28Met -> dual (add SGLT2i if ASCVD/HF/CKD) -> triple -> insulin; GLP-1 RA if BMI[ge]35

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the 1st line therapy for T2DM per ADA/EASD 2023?Lifestyle + Metformin (if tolerated, eGFR[ge]30)
How do comorbidities change 2nd line choice?ASCVD -> GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i; HF -> SGLT2i; CKD -> SGLT2i; Obesity -> GLP-1 RA or Tirzepatide
Which SGLT2i have proven CV benefit?Empagliflozin (EMPA-REG), Canagliflozin (CANVAS), Dapagliflozin (DECLARE), Ertugliflozin (VERTIS-CV)
Which GLP-1 RA have proven CV benefit?Liraglutide (LEADER), Semaglutide SC (SUSTAIN-6), Dulaglutide (REWIND), Albiglutide (HARMONY), Oral semaglutide (PIONEER-6)
What is the preferred agent for HF in diabetes?SGLT2i -- dapagliflozin (DAPA-HF, DELIVER), empagliflozin (EMPEROR-Reduced/Preserved) -- benefit across HFrEF/HFpEF
What is the preferred agent for CKD in diabetes?SGLT2i -- canagliflozin (CREDENCE), dapagliflozin (DAPA-CKD), empagliflozin (EMPA-KIDNEY) -- eGFR thresholds: initiate [ge]20/30, continue to dialysis
When do you start insulin in T2DM?HbA1c >58mmol/mol (7.5%) on dual/triple therapy, symptomatic hyperglycaemia, pregnancy, CKD G4-5
What is the difference between ADA/EASD and NICE NG28?NICE: Met -> Dual (add SGLT2i if ASCVD/HF/CKD) -> Triple -> Insulin; GLP-1 RA only if BMI[ge]35. ADA/EASD: more flexible, comorbidity-first at every step.

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Metformin always 1st line?Yes unless contraindicated (eGFR<30, intolerance). In CKD G3b, use with caution (half dose).
SGLT2i vs GLP-1 RA for ASCVD?BOTH have MACE benefit; choice depends on HF/CKD/Obesity comorbidities, patient preference, cost
Tirzepatide position?Dual GIP/GLP-1; superior weight/HbA1c vs semaglutide; use for obesity + T2DM; CVOT ongoing (SURPASS-CVOT)
SGLT2i in eGFR<20?YES -- continue to dialysis once initiated; dapa/empa continue <20

Mnemonic: ADA-EASD-3C

  • Assess comorbidities (ASCVD, HF, CKD, Obesity)
  • Decide 2nd line by comorbidity
  • Add GLP-1 RA/SGLT2i for CV/renal/weight
  • Evidence-based (CVOTs)
  • Avoid SU/TZD if high-risk comorbidities
  • Stepwise: Met -> Dual -> Triple -> Insulin
  • Deintensify if hypo/frail
  • 3rd agent: complementary mechanism
  • Combination Met+SGLT2i+GLP-1 RA = optimal
  • Cost/access considered
  • Continue Met + SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA with insulin"""

notes.append(("Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus/Treatment algorithms", "ADA/EASD 2023+ consensus algorithm", "Type 2 Diabetes", ada_algo_content))

Write the note

for parent, topic, section, content in notes:

safe_name = topic.lower().replace(' ', '-').replace('(', '').replace(')', '').replace('/', '-').replace('&', 'and').replace(',', '').replace('+', 'plus')

path = f"/mnt/tb/Medicine/Diabetes/{parent}/{safe_name}.md"

result = write_file(path=path, content=make_full_note(parent, topic, section, content))

print(f"Created: {topic} ({result.get('bytes_written', 'error')} bytes)")

print("Algorithm note complete")

▸ 21.9.11 Other Mody Subtypes

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3)", "GCK-MODY (MODY 2)", "HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Other MODY subtypes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise less common MODY subtypes (HNF1B, PDX1, NEUROD1, KCNJ11, BLK, APPL1)
  • [ ] Identify distinguishing clinical features
  • [ ] Apply appropriate management per subtype

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Collective prevalence~5-10% of MODY
Key subtypesHNF1B (MODY5), PDX1 (MODY4), NEUROD1 (MODY6), KCNJ11 (MODY13), BLK (MODY11), APPL1 (MODY14)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)

FeatureDetail
GeneHNF1B (17cen-q21.3)
Key featuresRenal cysts, renal dysplasia, GU malformations, hyperuricaemia
DiabetesVariable; often insulin-requiring
Extra-pancreaticLiver dysfunction, genital tract malformations, hypomagnesaemia
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptideVariable

NEUROD1-MODY (MODY6)

FeatureDetail
GeneNEUROD1 (2q32)
FeaturesPancreatic/brain development; neurological features possible
DiabetesYoung onset; insulin often required
NeurologicalLearning difficulties, cerebellar hypoplasia

PDX1-MODY (MODY4)

FeatureDetail
GenePDX1 (13q12.1)
FeaturesPancreatic duodenal homeobox 1; pancreatic agenesis if homozygous
DiabetesVariable; may need insulin

KCNJ11-MODY (MODY13)

FeatureDetail
GeneKCNJ11 (11p15.1) - Kir6.2 subunit
FeaturesOverlap with neonatal diabetes
SulfonylureaResponsive

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestIndication
Genetic panelAll MODY genes if probability >6%
Renal ultrasoundHNF1B-MODY (renal cysts)
MagnesiumHNF1B-MODY (hypomagnesaemia)
Liver functionHNF1B-MODY (liver dysfunction)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HNF1A-MODYLow CRP, excellent SU response
GCK-MODYMild fasting hyperglycaemia, no treatment
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide
T2DMObese, older, insulin resistance

7. Management

HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)

IssueManagement
DiabetesOften insulin-requiring; SU variable
RenalNephrology follow-up; monitor eGFR, magnesium
GUUrology review; prenatal counselling
MagnesiumSupplement if hypomagnesaemia

NEUROD1/PDX1

AspectManagement
DiabetesOften insulin-requiring
NeurologicalPaediatric neurology follow-up (NEUROD1)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
HNF1B-MODYRenal cysts, GU malformations, hypomagnesaemia; insulin often needed
NEUROD1Neurological features; insulin often needed
KCNJ11Sulfonylurea responsive
Rare MODYPDX1, NEUROD1, BLK, APPL1 - insulin often needed

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What distinguishes HNF1B-MODY?Renal cysts, GU malformations, hypomagnesaemia; insulin often needed
What is the key feature of NEUROD1-MODY?Neurological involvement (learning difficulties, cerebellar hypoplasia)
How do you manage HNF1B-MODY diabetes?Often insulin-requiring; monitor renal function, magnesium

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All MODY = sulfonylurea responsive?NO - HNF1B/PDX1/NEUROD1 often insulin-requiring
HNF1B = renal only?No - GU malformations, liver, magnesium, pancreas

Mnemonic: OTHER-MODY-SUBTYPES

  • Other MODY: 5-10%
  • Types: HNF1B(5), PDX1(4), NEUROD1(6), KCNJ11(13), BLK(11), APPL1(14)
  • HNF1B: Renal cysts, GU, hypoMg, insulin
  • ENUROD1: Neuro + diabetes, insulin
  • Rare: PDX1, BLK, APPL1
  • MODY5 = HNF1B = renal/GU
  • Overlap: KCNJ11 = neonatal DM spectrum
  • Diabetes: insulin often needed
  • Subtypes: each distinct
  • Unique features per gene
  • Beyond HNF1A/GCK/HNF4A
  • You need genetic panel
  • Test if probability >6%
  • You refer: nephrology, urology, genetics
  • Prenatal: if familial
  • Exome if panel negative
  • Sequencing: confirm VUS**

Topic Overview

Other MODY subtypes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)
NEUROD1-MODY (MODY6)
PDX1-MODY (MODY4)
KCNJ11-MODY (MODY13)
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
HNF1B-MODY (MODY5)
NEUROD1/PDX1
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.9.12 Cardiovascular Outcome Trial Cvot Guided Therapy
[!info]
FDA 2008 mandate: All new T2DM drugs must demonstrate CV safety; CVOTs transformed T2DM management from glucose-centric to comorbidity-centric; SGLT2i = CV/renal benefit (EMPA-REG, CANVAS, CREDENCE, DAPA-HF, DELIVER); GLP-1 RA = CV benefit (LEADER, SUSTAIN-6, REWIND, FLOW); DPP-4i = neutral (SAVOR/EXAMINE: ↑ HF hosp); agent selection by comorbidity.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Summarise key CVOT results by drug class
  • [ ] Apply CVOT evidence to agent selection
  • [ ] Identify drugs with CV benefit vs neutral vs harm
  • [ ] Apply per-comorbidity (ASCVD, HF, CKD, obesity)
  • [ ] Understand regulatory impact (FDA 2008 mandate)

2. SGLT2 Inhibitor CVOTs — CV & Renal Benefit

TrialDrugPopulationPrimary OutcomeKey Result
EMPA-REG OUTCOME (2015)EmpagliflozinT2DM + ASCVD3P-MACE↓ 14% (CV death 38%, HF hosp 35%)
CANVAS Program (2017)CanagliflozinT2DM + ASCVD/high risk3P-MACE↓ 14% (HF hosp 33%); ↑ amputation
DECLARE-TIMI 58 (2019)DapagliflozinT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACENeutral MACE; ↓ 17% CV death/HF hosp; renal benefit
VERTIS-CV (2020)ErtugliflozinT2DM + ASCVD3P-MACENeutral MACE; ↓ HF hosp
CREDENCE (2019)CanagliflozinT2DM + DKD (eGFR 30–90, ACR 300–5000)Renal composite↓ 30% renal composite; ↓ CV death/HF hosp
DAPA-CKD (2020)DapagliflozinCKD (eGFR 25–75, ACR 200–5000) ± T2DMRenal composite↓ 39% renal composite; ↓ CV death
EMPA-KIDNEY (2022)EmpagliflozinCKD (eGFR 20–45 or 45–90 + ACR ≥200) ± T2DMRenal composite↓ 28% renal composite
DAPA-HF (2019)DapagliflozinHFrEF (EF ≤40%) ± T2DMCV death/HF hosp↓ 26% (non-diabetic benefit)
EMPEROR-Reduced (2020)EmpagliflozinHFrEF (EF ≤40%) ± T2DMCV death/HF hosp↓ 25%
EMPEROR-Preserved (2021)EmpagliflozinHFpEF (EF >40%) ± T2DMCV death/HF hosp↓ 21%
DELIVER (2022)DapagliflozinHFmrEF/pEF (EF >40%) ± T2DMCV death/HF hosp↓ 18%

Consistent message: SGLT2i = CV death ↓, HF hosp ↓, renal progression ↓ across spectrum.

3. GLP-1 Receptor Agonist CVOTs — CV Benefit

TrialDrugPopulationPrimary OutcomeKey Result
ELIXA (2015)LixisenatideT2DM + recent ACS3P-MACENeutral (no harm, no benefit)
LEADER (2016)LiraglutideT2DM + ASCVD/high risk3P-MACE↓ 13% (CV death 22%, MI/Stroke neutral)
SUSTAIN-6 (2016)Semaglutide SCT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACE↓ 26% (non-fatal stroke 39%)
EXSCEL (2017)Exenatide QWT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACENeutral (trend CV death ↓)
HARMONY (2018)AlbiglutideT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACE↓ 22% (MACE)
REWIND (2019)DulaglutideT2DM + ASCVD/risk (primary prev 31%)3P-MACE↓ 12% (broad population incl. primary prev)
PIONEER-6 (2019)Oral SemaglutideT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACENeutral (trend benefit)
AMPLITUDE-O (2021)EfpeglenatideT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACE↓ 27%
FLOW (2024)SemaglutideT2DM + CKD (eGFR ≥50 + ACR ≥30 or eGFR <50)Renal composite↓ 24% renal composite; ↓ CV death
STEP-HFpEF (2023)Semaglutide 2.4mgHFpEF + obesity (BMI ≥30) ± T2DMKCCQ-CSS + 6MWD↑ KCCQ-CSS 7.8pts; ↑ 6MWD 21.5m
FIGHT (2017)LiraglutideHFrEF (EF ≤40%) ± T2DMHierarchical clinicalNeutral (no harm)
LIVE (2023)LiraglutidePost-MI + EF ≤40%/DMCV death/HF hospNeutral

Key: GLP-1 RA = CV benefit (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide, albiglutide, efpeglenatide); weight loss major driver; HFpEF + obesity → semaglutide 2.4mg.

4. DPP-4 Inhibitor CVOTs — Neutral / HF Risk

TrialDrugPopulationPrimary OutcomeKey Result
SAVOR-TIMI 53 (2013)SaxagliptinT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACENeutral MACE; ↑ 27% HF hosp
EXAMINE (2013)AlogliptinT2DM + recent ACS3P-MACENeutral MACE; ↑ HF hosp (trend)
TECOS (2015)SitagliptinT2DM + ASCVD3P-MACENeutral (no HF signal)
CARMELINA (2018)LinagliptinT2DM + ASCVD/CKD3P-MACE + renalNeutral (no HF signal; renal safe)
CAROLINA (2019)Linagliptin vs GlimepirideT2DM + ASCVD/risk3P-MACENon-inferior to SU; ↓ hypoglycaemia, ↓ weight gain

Key: DPP-4i = CV neutral; avoid saxagliptin/alogliptin in HF; sitagliptin/linagliptin safe.

5. Other Agents

Agent ClassKey CVOTResult
SulfonylureasUKPDS (legacy), CAROLINA (vs linagliptin)Neutral CV; ↑ hypoglycaemia, weight gain
ThiazolidinedionesPROactive (pioglitazone), RECORD (rosiglitazone)Pioglitazone: ↓ MACE; ↑ HF hosp; Rosiglitazone: ↑ MI/CV death (withdrawn)
InsulinORIGIN (glargine)Neutral CV
MetforminUKPDS (legacy)↓ MI, mortality (subgroup)
Bile acid sequestrants—Neutral

6. Comorbidity-Based Selection (CVOT Evidence)

ComorbidityPreferred (CV Benefit)Avoid
ASCVDSGLT2i (empagliflozin, canagliflozin) + GLP-1 RA (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide)Saxagliptin/alogliptin
HFrEFSGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin)TZDs (contraindicated), saxagliptin, alogliptin
HFpEFSGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin); Semaglutide 2.4mg if obeseTZDs
CKDSGLT2i (dapa/empa ≥20), GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, dulaglutide, liraglutide), FinerenoneMetformin <30, SU, TZD
ObesityGLP-1 RA (semaglutide, tirzepatide)TZD, SU, insulin (weight gain)
High hypoglycaemia riskSGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, DPP-4i (linagliptin), TZDSU, insulin, meglitinides

7. Algorithm Integration (ADA 2024 / KDIGO 2024)

T2DM + Metformin (if eGFR ≥30)
    │
    ├─► ASCVD / High CV Risk → **SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA**
    ├─► HF (HFrEF/HFpEF) → **SGLT2i** (first); add GLP-1 RA if obese HFpEF
    ├─► CKD (eGFR <60 or ACR ≥30) → **SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA + Finerenone**
    ├─► Obesity (BMI ≥30) → **GLP-1 RA** (semaglutide/tirzepatide)
    └─► No comorbidities → Individualised (cost, weight, hypo risk, preference)

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
FDA 2008 mandateAll new T2DM drugs require CVOT
EMPA-REGEmpagliflozin: ↓ CV death 38%, HF hosp 35%
CANVASCanagliflozin: ↓ MACE 14%; ↑ amputation
DECLAREDapagliflozin: neutral MACE; ↓ HF hosp; renal benefit
CREDENCE/DAPA-CKD/EMPA-KIDNEYSGLT2i = renal benefit (CKD ± DM)
DAPA-HF/EMPEROR/DELIVERSGLT2i = HFrEF + HFpEF benefit (non-diabetic too)
LEADER/SUSTAIN-6/REWINDGLP-1 RA: CV benefit (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide)
FLOWSemaglutide: renal benefit in CKD
STEP-HFpEFSemaglutide 2.4mg: HFpEF + obesity benefit
SAVOR/EXAMINESaxagliptin/Alogliptin: ↑ HF hosp — avoid in HF
SAVOR/EXAMINESitagliptin/Linagliptin: CV neutral, safe in HF
PROactivePioglitazone: ↓ MACE; ↑ HF hosp
RosiglitazoneWithdrawn (↑ MI/CV death)
Agent selectionBy comorbidity (ASCVD→SGLT2i+GLP-1; HF→SGLT2i; CKD→SGLT2i+GLP-1+Finerenone; Obesity→GLP-1)

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

▸ 21.9.12 Sulfonylurea Responsiveness

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, sulfonylurea-neonatal, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["KCNJ11 and ABCC8 neonatal diabetes", "Transient vs permanent neonatal diabetes", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Sulfonylurea responsiveness

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain mechanism of sulfonylurea responsiveness in KCNJ11/ABCC8 neonatal diabetes
  • [ ] Apply switch protocol from insulin to sulfonylurea
  • [ ] Monitor for dose adjustments and adverse effects

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
MechanismKATP channel mutation prevents closure -> sulfonylurea binds SUR1 -> closes channel -> insulin secretion
Responsiveness~90% of KCNJ11/ABCC8 PNDM respond to sulfonylureas
DoseHigher than MODY (0.5-3 mg/kg/day)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
MechanismKATP channel mutation prevents closure -> sulfonylurea binds SUR1 -> closes channel -> insulin secretion
Responsiveness~90% of KCNJ11/ABCC8 PNDM respond to sulfonylureas
DoseHigher than MODY (0.5-3 mg/kg/day)

3. Classification / Staging / Grading

Sulfonylurea Response by Gene

GeneResponsivenessTypical Dose
KCNJ1190-95%0.5-3 mg/kg/day
ABCC885-90%0.5-3 mg/kg/day
INS0%Insulin only
GCK0%No treatment needed

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Switch Protocol

StepAction
1Confirm KCNJ11/ABCC8 mutation
2Start gliclazide MR 0.5 mg/kg/day (max 30mg if <10kg)
3Reduce insulin by 50% day 1; stop insulin by day 3-5 if glucose controlled
4Titrate SU weekly by 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day to max 3 mg/kg/day
5Monitor glucose 4-6x/day for first 2 weeks

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Switch Protocol

StepAction
1Confirm KCNJ11/ABCC8 mutation
2Start gliclazide MR 0.5 mg/kg/day (max 30mg if <10kg)
3Reduce insulin by 50% day 1; stop insulin by day 3-5 if glucose controlled
4Titrate SU weekly by 0.25-0.5 mg/kg/day to max 3 mg/kg/day
5Monitor glucose 4-6x/day for first 2 weeks

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestPurpose
Genetic confirmationKCNJ11/ABCC8 activating mutation
Glucose monitoring4-6x/day during switch
HbA1c3-monthly after stable

6. Differential Diagnosis

GeneSU Response
KCNJ1195%
ABCC890%
INS0%
GCK0%

7. Management

Dose Titration

WeekDose (mg/kg/day)Monitoring
10.54-6x/day glucose; reduce insulin 50%
21.0Stop insulin if glucose <10 mmol/L
41.5-2.0Monitor for hypoglycaemia
8Up to 3.0Max dose; monitor neuro development

Long-term Follow-up

ParameterFrequency
HbA1c3-monthly
Growth6-monthly (height, weight)
NeurologicalAnnual (DEND/iDEND)
Dose adjustmentAs needed with growth

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Response rateKCNJ11 95%, ABCC8 90%
MechanismSU binds SUR1 -> closes KATP -> insulin release
DoseHigher than MODY (0.5-3 mg/kg/day)
SwitchGliclazide MR 0.5 -> 3 mg/kg/day over weeks
DEND/iDENDHigher doses needed; neuro monitoring

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the sulfonylurea response rate in KCNJ11 PNDM?95%
What is the mechanism of sulfonylurea in KCNJ11 diabetes?SU binds SUR1 -> closes KATP channel -> insulin secretion
What dose of sulfonylurea for neonatal diabetes?0.5-3 mg/kg/day gliclazide MR (higher than MODY)
What is the switch protocol?Start 0.5 mg/kg/day gliclazide MR; reduce insulin 50% day 1; stop by day 3-5

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Same dose as HNF1A-MODY?NO - neonatal DM needs higher doses (0.5-3 mg/kg vs 30-120mg fixed)
All neonatal DM = SU responsive?NO - only KCNJ11/ABCC8; INS/GCK = no
Sulfonylurea = cure?Life-long treatment; dose increases with age

Mnemonic: SU-NEONATAL-RESPONSE

  • Sulfonylurea: closes KATP -> insulin
  • Unique: KCNJ11 95%, ABCC8 90%
  • Response: 90% overall
  • Excellent: switch from insulin
  • Switch protocol: 0.5 -> 3 mg/kg over weeks
  • Protocol: reduce insulin 50% day 1
  • Once daily: gliclazide MR
  • Neonatal: higher dose than MODY
  • Sulfonylurea: bind SUR1 -> close KATP
  • Efficacy: 90-95%
  • Rate increase: weekly
  • Patient: monitor 4-6x/day glucose
  • Over time: dose increases with growth
  • Sustain: life-long treatment
  • Expert: neurological monitoring for DEND/iDEND
  • Alternative: insulin if SU fails
  • Titration: weekly increments
  • Infant: tolbutamide if <10kg
  • Variable: DEND needs higher dose
  • End: life-long; no cure**

Topic Overview

Sulfonylurea responsiveness

2. Definition & Epidemiology
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Classification / Staging / Grading
Sulfonylurea Response by Gene

Switch Protocol
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Switch Protocol
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Dose Titration
Long-term Follow-up
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.9.13 Nice Ng28 Pathway
[!info]
NICE NG28 (2022) = UK guideline for T2DM management; metformin first-line; comorbidity-driven second-line (SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA if CVD/HF/CKD/obesity); SU not first-line; basal insulin if HbA1c >58 on dual therapy; pre-mixed only if basal-bolus not feasible; individualised HbA1c; structured education at diagnosis.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Apply NICE NG28 stepwise treatment algorithm
  • [ ] Identify first-line and comorbidity-driven second-line options
  • [ ] State individualised HbA1c targets
  • [ ] Apply insulin initiation criteria
  • [ ] Implement structured education and monitoring

2. Stepwise Pharmacological Algorithm

Step 1: First-Line — Metformin

DetailRecommendation
DrugMetformin (standard release)
Titration500mg OD → 500mg BD → 1g BD over 4–8 weeks
Extended releaseIf GI intolerance
ContraindicationeGFR <30 (stop); 30–45 (max 1g BD)
If contraindicated/intolerantConsider SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA or DPP-4i or Pioglitazone

Step 2: Second-Line — Comorbidity-Driven (Add to Metformin)

Clinical ScenarioPreferred Add-OnAlternative
Established CVD (MI, stroke, PAD)SGLT2i (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin) + GLP-1 RAIf SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA not suitable: DPP-4i (linagliptin)
Heart Failure (HFrEF or HFpEF)SGLT2i (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin) firstGLP-1 RA if obese HFpEF (semaglutide 2.4mg)
CKD (eGFR <60 or ACR ≥30)SGLT2i (dapa/empa ≥20) + GLP-1 RAFinerenone if ACR ≥30, eGFR ≥25
Obesity (BMI ≥30; ≥27.5 South Asian)GLP-1 RA (semaglutide/tirzepatide)SGLT2i
High hypoglycaemia risk (elderly, renal, frail)SGLT2i / GLP-1 RA / DPP-4i (linagliptin) / PioglitazoneAvoid SU, insulin
No specific comorbiditySGLT2i or GLP-1 RA (preferred)DPP-4i (linagliptin) or Pioglitazone
Cost primary concernDPP-4i (generic sitagliptin) / PioglitazoneSulfonylurea (gliclazide)

Key: Sulfonylureas NOT first-line add-on — only if other options unsuitable.

Step 3: Third-Line — Dual/Triple Therapy

  • Metformin + SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA (ideal for CVD/HF/CKD/obesity)
  • Metformin + SGLT2i + DPP-4i (if GLP-1 RA not tolerated)
  • Metformin + GLP-1 RA + DPP-4i (if SGLT2i contraindicated)
  • Metformin + Pioglitazone + DPP-4i (if others unavailable)

Step 4: Insulin Initiation

TriggerCriteria
Basal insulinHbA1c >58 mmol/mol (7.5%) on dual therapy (metformin + add-on) AND symptomatic hyperglycaemia OR HbA1c >75 (9%)
Urgent insulinHbA1c >86 (10%) OR symptomatic (osmotic) OR ketosis
Basal-bolusIf basal optimised (fasting 4–7) but HbA1c >58 + post-prandial >8.5
Pre-mixedOnly if basal-bolus not feasible (regular meals, no carb counting)

3. Individualised HbA1c Targets

PopulationTargetRationale
Most adults<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)If achievable without problematic hypoglycaemia
High hypoglycaemia risk / frailty / limited life expectancy<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)Balance benefit vs harm
Long duration / advanced complications<64 mmol/mol (8.0%)Avoid intensive control risks (ACCORD)
Pregnancy (pre-existing)<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)Pre-conception and throughout
Children/Young people<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)If achievable safely

Relax targets if: hypoglycaemia unawareness, recurrent severe hypo, advanced frailty, limited life expectancy.

4. Structured Education (NICE Quality Standard)

RequirementDetail
Offer at diagnosisT1DM: DAFNE; T2DM: DESMOND/X-PERT
TimingWithin 12 months of diagnosis
Annual reviewAssess need for refresher at each annual review
Cultural adaptationAvailable in appropriate languages
Digital optionsCan supplement (DAFNE Online, Oviva, Liva) if accessible

5. Monitoring & Annual Review (9 Care Processes)

Care ProcessTarget / Action
HbA1c3–6 monthly until stable; then 6-monthly
Blood Pressure<140/90 (<130/80 if CKD/albuminuria)
LipidsNon-HDL <2.6 (primary) / <2.2 (secondary); statin per QRISK3
Urine ACRAnnual (6-monthly if A2/A3)
eGFRAnnual (6-monthly if <60)
BMIAnnual; weight management if ≥25
SmokingAnnual; cessation support
Foot examinationAnnual (neurovascular); risk stratification (IWGDF 0–3)
Retinal screeningAnnual (2-yearly if R0 ×2); refer per ETDRS grading

6. Drug-Specific NICE Positioning

Drug ClassNICE PositionKey Criteria
MetforminFirst-lineUniversal
SGLT2iPreferred 2nd-lineIf CVD/HF/CKD/obesity
GLP-1 RAPreferred 2nd-lineIf CVD/HF/CKD/obesity
DPP-4iAlternativeIf SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA not suitable; linagliptin preferred (no renal adjust)
PioglitazoneAlternativeIf others unsuitable; avoid HF/fracture/bladder cancer
SulfonylureaNot first/second-lineOnly if others unsuitable; gliclazide preferred
MeglitinidesSpecialistIrregular meals, renal impairment
Alpha-glucosidaseSpecialistPost-prandial dominant, post-bariatric dumping
Basal insulinStep 4HbA1c >58 on dual therapy
Basal-bolusStep 5Basal optimised + HbA1c >58
Pre-mixedOnly if basal-bolus not feasibleRegular meals, no carb counting

7. Special Populations

PopulationKey Modifications
Renal impairment (eGFR <30)Stop metformin; SGLT2i (dapa/empa ≥20), GLP-1 RA, DPP-4i (linagliptin)
Heart failureSGLT2i first; avoid TZD, saxagliptin/alogliptin
Elderly/FrailRelaxed HbA1c (<58–64); avoid SU/insulin if possible; SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA/DPP-4i
PregnancyInsulin (detemir/glargine U100, aspart/lispro); stop oral agents (except metformin if ADA)
South Asian ethnicityBMI thresholds −2.5; earlier intervention

8. QOF Indicators (2024/25)

CodeIndicatorThreshold
DM003HbA1c ≤58 mmol/mol (7.5%)17 pts
DM004HbA1c ≤48 mmol/mol (6.5%)10 pts
DM005BP ≤140/9011 pts
DM006Non-HDL <2.6 or >40% reduction6 pts
DM007Statin if CVD/QRISK3 ≥10%5 pts
DM008UACR recorded3 pts
DM009eGFR recorded3 pts
DM010Foot exam (neurovascular)3 pts
DM011Retinal screening3 pts
DM012Smoking status + cessation offer3 pts

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
NICE NG28 first-lineMetformin (universal)
Second-line driverComorbidities (CVD/HF/CKD/obesity) → SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA
SulfonylureaNot first/second-line — only if others unsuitable
SGLT2i preferred ifCVD, HF, CKD, obesity
GLP-1 RA preferred ifCVD, HFpEF+obesity, CKD, obesity
DPP-4iAlternative; linagliptin (no renal adjust)
PioglitazoneAlternative; avoid HF, fracture, bladder cancer
Insulin criteriaHbA1c >58 on dual therapy
Pre-mixed insulinOnly if basal-bolus not feasible
HbA1c target most<48 mmol/mol (6.5%)
HbA1c target frail<58 mmol/mol (7.5%)
Structured educationAt diagnosis (DAFNE T1DM, DESMOND/X-PERT T2DM)
9 Care processesAnnual review mandatory

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

▸ 21.9.13 Transient Vs Permanent Neonatal Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, neonatal-dm-transient, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["KCNJ11 and ABCC8 neonatal diabetes", "Sulfonylurea responsiveness", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Transient vs permanent neonatal diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Differentiate transient (TNDM) vs permanent (PNDM) neonatal diabetes
  • [ ] Recognise 6q24 imprinting defect in TNDM
  • [ ] Apply management differences
  • [ ] Counsel on relapse risk

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureTransient (TNDM)Permanent (PNDM)
DefinitionRemission by 18 monthsLifelong
Proportion~50% of neonatal DM~50% of neonatal DM
Genetics6q24 imprinting (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
RemissionMedian 12 weeks (range 2w-18mo)Never
RelapseAdolescence/early adulthood (50%)N/A

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTNDMPNDM
Age at dx<6 months<6 months
RemissionBy 18 monthsNever
RelapseAdolescence/young adulthoodNever
Genetics6q24 (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
Birth weightLow (IUGR)Low (IUGR)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTNDMPNDM
Age at dx<6 months<6 months
RemissionBy 18 monthsNever
RelapseAdolescence/young adulthoodNever
Genetics6q24 (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
Birth weightLow (IUGR)Low (IUGR)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

TNDM Genetic Subtypes

SubtypeGene/RegionMechanismProportion
TNDM16q24 (PLAGL1/HYMAI)Imprinting defect (paternal UPD/duplication)70%
TNDM26q24 (ZFP57)ZFP57 mutation -> imprinting lossRare
TNDM3KCNJ11/ABCC8Activating mutations (milder)10-20%

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureTNDMPNDM
Age at dx<6 months<6 months
RemissionBy 18 monthsNever
RelapseAdolescence/young adulthoodNever
Genetics6q24 (70%); ZFP57, HYMAIKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A
Birth weightLow (IUGR)Low (IUGR)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestTNDMPNDM
Genetic testing6q24 methylation analysisKCNJ11, ABCC8, INS, GCK, PDX1, PTF1A, EIF2AK3
Methylation assayHypomethylation at 6q24Normal
C-peptideLow at dx; recovers at remissionPersistently low

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
PNDMNever remits; KCNJ11/ABCC8/INS
SyndromicWolcott-Rallison (EIF2AK3), IPEX (FOXP3), Wolfram

7. Management

TNDM

PhaseManagement
AcuteInsulin (usually low dose; ~0.3 U/kg/day)
RemissionStop insulin; monitor glucose weekly x 3mo
SurveillanceAnnual OGTT/HbA1c from age 10; relapse risk 50%

PNDM

GeneManagement
KCNJ11/ABCC8Sulfonylurea 90% switch
INS/GCK/PDX1/PTF1AInsulin lifelong
EIF2AK3 (Wolcott-Rallison)Insulin; multi-system management

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
TNDM6q24 imprinting (70%); remission by 18mo; relapse 50% in adulthood
PNDMKCNJ11/ABCC8 (90% SU switch); INS/GCK/PDX1 = insulin
6q24Paternal UPD/duplication; HYMAI/PLAGL1 overexpression
RelapseTNDM relapse ~50% in adolescence/young adulthood
Birth weightLow in both (IUGR)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the main genetic cause of TNDM?6q24 imprinting defect (70%) - paternal UPD or duplication at 6q24
What is the remission age for TNDM?Median 12 weeks; by 18 months
What is the relapse rate for TNDM?~50% relapse in adolescence/young adulthood
How does TNDM differ from PNDM?TNDM remits by 18mo (6q24); PNDM lifelong (KCNJ11/ABCC8/INS)
What is the 6q24 mechanism?Paternal UPD or duplication -> HYMAI/PLAGL1 overexpression -> ↓insulin

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All TNDM = 6q24?70%; KCNJ11/ABCC8 also cause TNDM
Relapse = new diabetes?Same genetic defect; beta-cell failure recurs
TNDM = always transient?50% relapse later; not "cured"

Mnemonic: TNDM-TRANSIENT

  • TNDM: 6q24 imprinting (70%)
  • Neonatal diabetes: <6 months
  • Defect: paternal UPD/duplication at 6q24
  • Methylation: hypomethylation at 6q24
  • Transient: remission by 18 months
  • Remission: median 12 weeks
  • Adolescence relapse: 50%
  • Neonatal: <6 months
  • Syndromic: ZFP57 (TNDM2)
  • Interval: remission by 18mo
  • Expect relapse: 50% in adulthood
  • Next: PNDM if no remission
  • Test: 6q24 methylation assay
  • Temporary: not cured, just remission

Topic Overview

Transient vs permanent neonatal diabetes

PNDM

▸ 21.9.14 Wolfram Syndrome

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, wolfram, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Mitochondrial diabetes", "MODY", "Lipodystrophy syndromes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Wolfram syndrome

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise Wolfram syndrome (DIDMOAD)
  • [ ] Identify WFS1 gene and inheritance
  • [ ] Apply multidisciplinary management
  • [ ] Counsel on prognosis and genetic counselling

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
AcronymDIDMOAD: Diabetes Insipidus, Diabetes Mellitus, Optic Atrophy, Deafness
GeneWFS1 (wolframin) - 4p16.1; also WFS2 (CISD2)
InheritanceAutosomal recessive
Prevalence1:500,000 - 1:1,000,000

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
Diabetes MellitusOnset 4-10y; insulin-requiring; non-autoimmune
Diabetes InsipidusCentral (pituitary); polyuria/polydipsia; onset ~14y
Optic AtrophyProgressive; onset ~11y; colour vision loss; legal blindness by adulthood
DeafnessSensorineural; high-frequency; onset ~13y
NeurologicalAtaxia, peripheral neuropathy, autonomic dysfunction, psychiatric
UrinaryNeurogenic bladder; recurrent UTIs
EndocrineHypogonadism, growth hormone deficiency, hypothyroidism

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

WFS1 vs WFS2

GeneFeatures
WFS1 (Wolframin)Classic DIDMOAD; ER calcium handling
WFS2 (CISD2)Similar; also peptic ulcer disease, bleeding diathesis

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Progression Timeline

AgeOnset
4-10yDiabetes Mellitus
~11yOptic Atrophy
~13yDeafness
~14yDiabetes Insipidus
AdolescenceNeurological decline

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingWFS1 mutation (biallelic); WFS2 (CISD2)
OphthalmologyOptic atrophy (temporal pallor)
AudiometrySensorineural deafness
Water deprivation testCentral DI
MRI brainCerebellar/brainstem atrophy (late)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingWFS1 mutation (biallelic); WFS2 (CISD2)
OphthalmologyOptic atrophy (temporal pallor)
AudiometrySensorineural deafness
Water deprivation testCentral DI
MRI brainCerebellar/brainstem atrophy (late)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
MIDD (m.3243A>G)Maternal inheritance; diabetes + deafness; no DI/OA
MODYAutosomal dominant; no DI/OA/deafness
Isolated DINo DM/OA/deafness
Alström syndromeALMS1; obesity, cardiomyopathy, hepatic steatosis

7. Management

SystemManagement
Diabetes MellitusInsulin (basal-bolus); tight control slows complications
Diabetes InsipidusDesmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal; monitor electrolytes
Optic AtrophyLow vision aids; regular ophthalmology
DeafnessHearing aids; cochlear implant if severe
NeurologicalPhysiotherapy; orthotics for ataxia
HypogonadismHormone replacement

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
MIDD (m.3243A>G)Maternal inheritance; no DI/OA
MODYAutosomal dominant; no DI/OA/deafness
Alström syndromeALMS1; obesity, cardiomyopathy, hepatic steatosis, no DI
Isolated DI + DMNo optic atrophy/deafness

7. Management

SystemManagement
Diabetes MellitusInsulin (basal-bolus); tight control slows complications
Diabetes InsipidusDesmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal; monitor electrolytes
Optic AtrophyLow vision aids; regular ophthalmology
DeafnessHearing aids; cochlear implant if severe
NeurologicalPhysiotherapy; orthotics for ataxia
HypogonadismHormone replacement

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
DIDMOADDiabetes Insipidus, Diabetes Mellitus, Optic Atrophy, Deafness
GeneWFS1 (autosomal recessive); WFS2/CISD2
ProgressionDM (4-10y) -> OA (11y) -> Deafness (13y) -> DI (14y) -> Neuro
Life expectancyReduced (median ~30y); respiratory failure, neurogenic bladder
InheritanceAutosomal recessive (WFS1, CISD2)

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What does DIDMOAD stand for?Diabetes Insipidus, Diabetes Mellitus, Optic Atrophy, Deafness
What is the inheritance of Wolfram syndrome?Autosomal recessive (WFS1/WFS2)
What is the typical progression of Wolfram syndrome?DM -> Optic Atrophy -> Deafness -> DI -> Neurological decline
How do you manage diabetes insipidus in Wolfram?Desmopressin (DDAVP) oral/nasal; monitor sodium
What is the inheritance pattern?Autosomal recessive (WFS1/CISD2)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Wolfram = MIDD?NO - Wolfram = AR, DIDMOAD; MIDD = maternal, no DI/OA
All DI + DM = Wolfram?NO - need optic atrophy + deafness
WFS1 = WFS2?WFS1 = classic; WFS2 = also peptic ulcer, bleeding

Mnemonic: WOLFRAM-DIDMOAD

  • Wolfram: WFS1 gene (4p16)
  • Optic atrophy: ~11y; progressive blindness
  • Life expectancy: ~30y (median)
  • Features: DIDMOAD
  • Recessive: autosomal recessive
  • Autosomal recessive
  • Mellitus: DM 4-10y
  • Insipidus: central DI ~14y
  • Deafness: sensorineural ~13y
  • Optic atrophy: ~11y
  • Median survival: ~30y
  • Diabetes: DM + DI
  • Insulin: required for DM
  • Desmopressin: for DI
  • Mortality: respiratory failure, neurogenic bladder
  • Ophthalmology: annual optic nerve assessment
  • Ataxia: cerebellar atrophy late
  • Diabetes: both mellitus and insipidus**

Topic Overview

Wolfram syndrome

2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
WFS1 vs WFS2
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Progression Timeline
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
