10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.9.2 Hnf1A Mody Mody 3

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, hnf1a-mody, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["GCK-MODY (MODY 2)", "HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical features of HNF1A-MODY
  • [ ] Explain sulfonylurea responsiveness mechanism
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing criteria
  • [ ] Differentiate from T1DM, T2DM, other MODY types

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise clinical features of HNF1A-MODY
  • [ ] Explain sulfonylurea responsiveness mechanism
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing criteria
  • [ ] Differentiate from T1DM, T2DM, other MODY types

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GeneHNF1A (hepatocyte nuclear factor 1-alpha)
Chromosome12q24.31
PrevalenceMost common MODY (50-65% of MODY)
InheritanceAutosomal dominant; high penetrance (>90% by 25y)

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GeneHNF1A (hepatocyte nuclear factor 1-alpha)
Chromosome12q24.31
PrevalenceMost common MODY (50-65% of MODY)
InheritanceAutosomal dominant; high penetrance (>90% by 25y)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
OnsetAdolescence/early adulthood (10-30y)
ProgressionProgressive beta-cell dysfunction
GlycaemiaFasting 7-10 mmol/L; post-prandial marked hyperglycaemia
Renal thresholdLow (glycosuria at lower glucose)
CRPLow (<1 mg/L) despite hyperglycaemia
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
OnsetAdolescence/early adulthood (10-30y)
ProgressionProgressive beta-cell dysfunction
GlycaemiaFasting 7-10 mmol/L; post-prandial marked hyperglycaemia
Renal thresholdLow (glycosuria at lower glucose)
CRPLow (<1 mg/L) despite hyperglycaemia
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureHNF1A-MODYGCK-MODYT1DMT2DM
GeneHNF1AGCKMultipleMultiple
Onset10-30yNeonatal/childhood<25y>40y
BMINormalNormalNormal/lowObese
AutoantibodiesNegativeNegativePositive (2+)Negative
C-peptidePreservedPreservedLow/absentHigh
SU responseExcellentNoneNoVariable
CRPLowNormalNormal/↑Normal/↑
Renal thresholdLowNormalNormalNormal

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingHNF1A mutation (heterozygous)
AutoantibodiesNegative (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)
C-peptidePreserved
CRPLow (<1 mg/L) - hallmark
Renal thresholdLow (glycosuria at glucose <10 mmol/L)
HbA1c58-75 mmol/mol typical

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingHNF1A mutation (heterozygous)
AutoantibodiesNegative (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)
C-peptidePreserved
CRPLow (<1 mg/L) - hallmark
Renal thresholdLow (glycosuria at glucose <10 mmol/L)
HbA1c58-75 mmol/mol typical

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide, DKA
T2DMObese, insulin resistance, negative Abs
GCK-MODYMild fasting hyperglycaemia, no treatment needed
HNF4A-MODYMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, SU responsive
Type 3cExocrine insufficiency, pancreatic pathology

7. Management

Sulfonylurea Therapy (1st Line)

AspectDetail
DrugGliclazide MR (preferred)
Starting dose30-60mg OD
TitrationUp to 120mg OD (max)
ResponseExcellent (>90% achieve target); often allows insulin discontinuation
MechanismHNF1A regulates KATP channel expression; SU closes residual channels

Monitoring

ParameterFrequency
HbA1c6-monthly
Renal functionAnnual (monitor for diabetic nephropathy)
LipidsAnnual
Renal thresholdMonitor for glycosuria at normal glucose

Genetic Counselling

AspectDetail
InheritanceAutosomal dominant (50% offspring risk)
PrenatalAvailable if mutation known
Family screeningOffer testing to 1st degree relatives

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Most common MODYHNF1A-MODY = 50-65% of all MODY
Key featuresYoung, non-obese, negative Abs, low CRP, low renal threshold
SulfonylureaExcellent response; 1st line; often replaces insulin
CRPLow (<1 mg/L) - diagnostic clue vs T2DM
GeneticsHNF1A (12q24); autosomal dominant; >90% penetrance by 25y
Low renal thresholdGlycosuria at glucose <10 mmol/L

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the most common type of MODY?HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3) - 50-65% of MODY
What is the hallmark of HNF1A-MODY?Low CRP (<1 mg/L) despite hyperglycaemia; excellent sulfonylurea response
Why are sulfonylureas effective in HNF1A-MODY?HNF1A regulates KATP channel expression; SU closes residual functional channels
How does HNF1A-MODY differ from T1DM?Negative autoantibodies, preserved C-peptide, low CRP, SU responsive
How does HNF1A-MODY differ from T2DM?Non-obese, young onset, negative Abs, low CRP, low renal threshold, SU responsive
What is the renal threshold in HNF1A-MODY?Low - glycosuria at glucose <10 mmol/L

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HNF1A-MODY = T1DM?NO - negative Abs, preserved C-peptide, SU responsive
All MODY = SU responsive?NO - only HNF1A/4A; GCK = no treatment; HNF1B = often insulin
HNF1A = T2DM?NO - young, non-obese, negative Abs, low CRP, SU responsive
All MODY = insulin later?NO - HNF1A stays on SU for decades; GCK = never needs treatment

Mnemonic: HNF1A-MODY-SU

  • HNF1A: most common MODY (50-65%)
  • NFR1A: hepatocyte nuclear factor 1-alpha
  • First choice: Sulfonylurea (excellent response)
  • 1st line: gliclazide MR
  • Autosomal dominant (12q24)
  • Most common MODY (50-65%)
  • Onset: 10-30y
  • Diagnosis: negative Abs, preserved C-peptide
  • Young: 10-30y, non-obese
  • Sulfonylurea: excellent response (>90%)
  • Unusual: low CRP (<1 mg/L) - hallmark
  • Renal threshold: low (glycosuria <10 mmol/L)
  • Excellent SU response: >90% achieve target
  • Stop insulin: often possible with SU
  • Unique: low renal threshold (glycosuria <10 mmol/L)
  • Low CRP: <1 mg/L (diastolic)
  • Family: autosomal dominant, 3 generations
  • Age: 10-30y typical**

Topic Overview

HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3

1. Learning Objectives

2. Definition & Epidemiology

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Sulfonylurea Therapy (1st Line)
Monitoring
Genetic Counselling

▸ 21.9.2 Symptomatic Presentation

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Describe classic symptomatic presentation of T2DM
  • [ ] Differentiate from T1DM, LADA, stress hyperglycaemia
  • [ ] Recognise hyperglycaemic emergencies (DKA, HHS) at presentation
  • [ ] Apply diagnostic criteria in symptomatic patients

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
PresentationInsidious onset (months-years); often asymptomatic at diagnosis
Asymptomatic at Dx~50% detected on screening/incidental finding
SymptomaticOsmotic symptoms, weight loss, infections, fatigue
Age at diagnosis>40 years (increasing in younger due to obesity)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

SymptomFrequencyMechanism
PolyuriaCommon if marked hyperglycaemiaOsmotic diuresis
PolydipsiaCommon if marked hyperglycaemiaDehydration from osmotic diuresis
Weight lossVariableCatabolism if significant insulin deficiency
Fatigue/lethargyCommonImpaired glucose utilisation
Blurred vision20-30%Osmotic lens swelling
Recurrent infectionsCommonCandidiasis, UTIs, skin infections
Acanthosis nigricans30-50%Insulin resistance marker
Tingling/numbnessIf neuropathy presentPeripheral neuropathy at diagnosis
Red Flags: Ketosis at presentation -> T1DM/LADA/ketosis-prone T2DM; rapid weight loss + osmotic symptoms in adult -> LADA; new-onset DM >50y + weight loss -> pancreatic cancer.

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureT2DM ClassicT1DMLADA
OnsetInsidious (months-years)Acute (days-weeks)Subacute
Age>40y (usually)Peak 10-14y>30y
BMIOverweight/obeseNormal/lowNormal/low
KetosisRare (unless ketosis-prone)Common (25-30% DKA)Uncommon
AutoantibodiesNegative2+ (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, IAA)GAD65+ (often single)
C-peptideNormal/highLow/absentLow-normal

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationT2DMT1DMLADA
AutoantibodiesNegative2+ positiveGAD65+ (often single)
C-peptideNormal/highLow/absentLow-normal
HbA1cVariable>48 usuallyVariable
KetonesNegative (usually)Positive if DKAUsually negative

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T1DMAcute, lean, DKA common, 2+ autoantibodies
LADAAdult, slow, GAD65+, initial oral response
Stress hyperglycaemiaAcute illness, resolves, no prior dysglycaemia
MODY<25y, non-obese, strong FH, negative autoantibodies
Pancreatic cancer>50y, weight loss, new-onset DM, no autoantibodies

7. Management

Acute (if DKA/HHS)

EmergencyProtocol
DKADKA protocol: fluids, FRII 0.1U/kg/hr, K+, monitor
HHSHHS protocol: fluids, lower insulin 0.05U/kg/hr, osmolality monitoring

Chronic (after stabilisation)

ComponentDetail
LifestyleMNT, 150min/week exercise, weight loss 5-10%
Metformin1st line unless contraindicated (eGFR<30)
Add-onPer ADA/EASD algorithm (SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA for ASCVD/HF/CKD/obesity)
EducationSelf-monitoring, sick day rules, hypoglycaemia awareness
CGMConsider if on insulin, hypoglycaemia unawareness

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
OnsetInsidious (months-years); 50% asymptomatic at diagnosis
Classic symptomsPolyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, recurrent infections
Weight lossOccurs if significant insulin deficiency/catabolism
Acanthosis nigricans30-50%; insulin resistance marker
Asymptomatic diagnosis~50% on screening/incidental
vs T1DMInsidious, obese, no ketosis, negative autoantibodies
vs LADAObese, negative autoantibodies, insulin resistance primary

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the typical presentation of T2DM?Insidious onset; often asymptomatic; if symptomatic: polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, recurrent infections
What percentage are asymptomatic at diagnosis?~50% detected on screening/incidental finding
How does T2DM differ from T1DM at presentation?Insidious vs acute; obese vs lean; no ketosis vs DKA common; negative vs 2+ autoantibodies
What is acanthosis nigricans and its significance?Velvety hyperpigmentation (axilla, neck); marker of insulin resistance
Can T2DM present in DKA?Rare; ketosis-prone T2DM (Afro-Caribbean); usually HHS if emergency

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
T2DM always asymptomatic?NO - 50% present with symptoms if hyperglycaemia marked
Weight loss = T1DM?NO - can occur in uncontrolled T2DM with significant insulin deficiency

Mnemonic: T2DM-INSIDIOUS

  • T2DM: insidious onset months-years
  • 2 presentations: asymptomatic (50%) or symptomatic
  • Dysglycaemia: slow progression
  • Metabolic: insulin resistance + beta-cell failure
  • Insidious: no acute crisis usually
  • Diagnosis: often screening/incidental
  • Infections: recurrent (candidiasis, UTI)
  • Obesity: overweight/obese usual
  • Unrecognized: 50% asymptomatic
  • Screening: ADA age>=35 + 1 RF

11. Mind Map

T2DM Symptomatic Presentation
Asymptomatic
50% at diagnosis
Screening/incidental
Symptoms
Polyuria, polydipsia
Fatigue
Recurrent infections
Weight loss (if severe)
Blurred vision
Signs
Acanthosis nigricans (30-50%)
Peripheral neuropathy
vs T1DM
Insidious vs acute
Obese vs lean
Negative Abs vs 2+
vs LADA
Obese vs normal BMI
Negative Abs vs GAD65+
IR vs autoimmunity

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionInsidious onset T2DM; 50% asymptomatic; osmotic symptoms if hyperglycaemic
**Key Test"HbA1c/FPG (screening); autoantibodies (negative)
**Classification"Insidious onset; obese; negative autoantibodies
**Acute Mgmt"HHS if severe; then lifestyle + metformin
**Chronic Mgmt"ADA/EASD algorithm: Met -> SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA per comorbidities
**Key Score"ADA screening age>=35/BMI>=25+1RF
**Complications"Early micro/macrovascular if undiagnosed
**Prognosis"Progressive; early intervention preserves beta-cell function

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days
90 days

14. Self-Test Scorecard

SectionScore /5Last Attempt

Management (Acute)

Tags

#medicine #diabetes #davidson #fcps #mrcp #full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.9.3 Glp 1 Receptor Agonists Tirzepatide

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Explain tirzepatide mechanism: dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonism
  • [ ] State SURPASS/SURMOUNT trial efficacy: superior HbA1c/weight loss vs semaglutide
  • [ ] Position in ADA/EASD algorithm: obesity + T2DM, after metformin
  • [ ] Manage GI side effects (similar to GLP-1 RA)
  • [ ] Recognise contraindications: MTC, MEN2, pancreatitis history, pregnancy

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassDual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist
MechanismDual agonism: GIP-R + GLP-1R -> [up]insulin, [down]glucagon, [up]satiety, [down]gastric emptying; GIP adds: [up]insulin secretion (synergistic with GLP-1), [up]adipocyte lipid storage, [down]food intake (central)
AgentTirzepatide (Mounjaro) -- only dual agonist approved currently
Doses2.5 -> 5 -> 7.5 -> 10 -> 12.5 -> 15 mg SC weekly
HbA1c Reduction1.8-2.5% (20-28 mmol/mol) -- superior to semaglutide 1mg
Weight Loss8-12kg (T2DM); 15-20% body weight (obesity, 15mg) -- superior to semaglutide
PharmacokineticsSC weekly; half-life ~5 days; no renal/hepatic dose adjustment

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A -- drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

SURPASS Trials (T2DM)

TrialComparatorHbA1c ReductionWeight LossKey Finding
SURPASS-1Placebo-1.8% (5mg) to -2.1% (15mg)-7 to -10kgMonotherapy efficacy
SURPASS-2Semaglutide 1mgTirzepatide superior: -2.0% vs -1.9% (NS); weight -11.3kg vs -6.7kgSuperior weight lossTirzepatide beats semaglutide
SURPASS-3Insulin degludec-1.5% to -1.9% vs -1.3%-10 to -13kg vs +1.5kgSuperior glycaemic/weight
SURPASS-4Insulin glargine (high CV risk)-2.3% (15mg) vs -1.9%-10.5 to -12.9kg vs +1.6kgCV safety, weight loss
SURPASS-5Add-on to SGLT2i-1.6% to -2.1%-8 to -10kgEffective combo

SURMOUNT Trials (Obesity)

TrialPopulationWeight Loss (15mg)Key Finding
SURMOUNT-1Obesity (no T2DM)-20.9% vs -3.1% placebo~20% weight loss
SURMOUNT-2T2DM + obesity-15.7% vs -3.3% placeboEffective in T2DM
SURMOUNT-3/4Post lifestyle / maintenanceSustained loss with continued therapyMaintenance with continued therapy

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
WeightObesity benefitEvery visit; target [ge]5% loss
Lipase/amylasePancreatitis suspicionIf severe abdominal pain; not routine
Calcitonin / thyroid USMTC screening (MEN2)Contraindicated if personal/family MTC or MEN2
Renal functionNo dose adjustment neededMonitor if CKD

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
PancreatitisSevere epigastric pain radiating to back, [up]lipase >3xULN; STOP tirzepatide; avoid if history
Medullary thyroid cancer (MTC)Contraindicated if personal/family MTC or MEN2; rodent C-cell tumours (human relevance uncertain)
RetinopathyRapid HbA1c drop risk; baseline retinal exam if proliferative DR
GI intoleranceNausea 20-30%; [up]with rapid titration; manage per protocol

7. Management

Initiation & Titration

StepDoseDuration
12.5mg weekly4 weeks
25mg weekly4 weeks
37.5mg weekly4 weeks
410mg weekly4 weeks
512.5mg weekly4 weeks
615mg weeklyMaintenance
Titration key: Mandatory slow titration to minimise GI side effects; if intolerant, stay at tolerated dose.

GI Side Effect Management

StrategyDetail
Slow titrationMandatory -- 4 weeks per step
Take with foodNot required but may help
Anti-emeticsOndansetron/metoclopramide PRN ([down]gastric emptying caution)
Dose reductionIf intolerant at max dose -- stay at tolerated dose
Split dosingNot applicable (weekly only)

Special Situations

SituationManagement
Renal impairmentNo dose adjustment (all stages)
Hepatic impairmentNo dose adjustment (Child-Pugh A/B); avoid C
PregnancyContraindicated (Category C); stop [ge]2mo pre-conception
Pancreatitis historyContraindicated
MTC/MEN2 family historyContraindicated
With insulin[down]Insulin dose 20-30% to avoid hypo; monitor
Sick-day rulesHold if unwell, unable to eat, vomiting
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Tirzepatide Candidate
↓
Contraindications?
↓
Contraindicated
Contraindicated
Start 2.5mg weekly
↓
Titrate q4wk: 2.5->5->7.5->10->12.5->15mg
↓
GI intolerant?
↓
Slow titration / anti-emetics / stay at tolerated dose
Continue to max tolerated dose
↓
Monitor HbA1c/weight 3-monthly

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismDual GIP/GLP-1 RA -> [up]insulin, [down]glucagon, [down]gastric emptying, [up]satiety; GIP adds: [up]insulin synergy, [up]adipocyte lipid storage, [down]food intake (central)
AgentsTirzepatide (Mounjaro) -- only dual agonist approved
Doses2.5 -> 5 -> 7.5 -> 10 -> 12.5 -> 15 mg SC weekly (titrate q4wk)
HbA1c Reduction1.8-2.5% (20-28 mmol/mol) -- superior to semaglutide 1mg
Weight LossT2DM: 8-12kg; Obesity: 15-20% body weight (SURMOUNT) -- superior to semaglutide
CVOTSURPASS-CVOT ongoing; SURPASS-4: CV safety in high risk
GI SENausea 20-30%; slow titration mandatory; anti-emetics PRN
PancreatitisRare; STOP if suspected; avoid if history
MTC/MEN2Contraindicated (rodent C-cell tumours)
PregnancyContraindicated; stop [ge]2mo pre-conception
Renal/HepaticNo dose adjustment

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of tirzepatide?Dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist -> [up]insulin, [down]glucagon, [down]gastric emptying, [up]satiety; GIP adds: [up]insulin synergy, [up]adipocyte lipid storage, [down]food intake (central)
How does tirzepatide compare to semaglutide?SURPASS-2: Superior HbA1c reduction (-2.0% vs -1.9%) and superior weight loss (-11.3kg vs -6.7kg) vs semaglutide 1mg
What is the weight loss with tirzepatide 15mg?T2DM: 8-12kg; Obesity (SURMOUNT-1): ~20% body weight loss (vs ~15% semaglutide 2.4mg)
What is the titration schedule for tirzepatide?2.5mg weekly x4wk -> 5mg x4wk -> 7.5mg x4wk -> 10mg x4wk -> 12.5mg x4wk -> 15mg weekly
What are the contraindications for tirzepatide?Personal/family history of MTC or MEN2; pancreatitis history; pregnancy (stop [ge]2mo pre-conception)
How do you manage GI side effects?Slow titration (mandatory 4wk per step); anti-emetics PRN; if persistent -> stay at tolerated dose
Is tirzepatide safe in renal impairment?No dose adjustment needed (all stages including dialysis)
What is the CVOT status for tirzepatide?SURPASS-CVOT ongoing; SURPASS-4: CV safety in high-risk patients

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Tirzepatide = GLP-1 RA?NO -- dual GIP/GLP-1 RA; GIP adds insulin synergy, adipocyte effects, central satiety
Tirzepatide vs semaglutide efficacy?Tirzepatide 15mg superior to semaglutide 1mg for both HbA1c and weight (SURPASS-2)
Tirzepatide in T1DM?Not approved; no data
Retinopathy risk like semaglutide?Limited data; monitor if proliferative DR; rapid HbA1c drop risk factor

Mnemonic: TIRZE-TIDE

  • Tirzepatide: Dual GIP/GLP-1 RA (only one)
  • Insulin synergy: GIP adds [up]insulin secretion
  • Reduces weight: T2DM -10kg, Obesity 20% (SURMOUNT)
  • Zero renal dose adjust (all stages)
  • E-fficacy: SURPASS-2 beats semaglutide 1mg (HbA1c/weight)
  • Titration: 2.5->5->7.5->10->12.5->15mg q4wk
  • Intolerant GI: slow titration, anti-emetics, stay at tolerated
  • Dual agonist: GIP + GLP-1 (not just GLP-1)
  • Exclude: MTC/MEN2, pancreatitis, pregnancy

11. Mind Map

Tirzepatide
Mechanism
Dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist
GIP: insulin synergy, adipocyte lipid storage
GLP-1: insulin, glucagon, gastric emptying, satiety
Doses
2.5 -> 5 -> 7.5 -> 10 -> 12.5 -> 15 mg weekly
Titrate q4wk
Trials
SURPASS-1: monotherapy
SURPASS-2: beat semaglutide
SURPASS-3/4: vs insulin
SURPASS-5: + SGLT2i
SURMOUNT: obesity 20% weight loss
CVOT
SURPASS-CVOT ongoing
SURPASS-4: CV safety high risk
Adverse
GI nausea 20-30%
Pancreatitis (rare)
MTC/MEN2 contraindicated
Contraindications
MTC/MEN2
Pancreatitis history
Pregnancy
Renal
No dose adjust (all stages)

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionDual GIP/GLP-1 RA: tirzepatide (Mounjaro) -- only dual agonist
**Key Test"HbA1c, weight, lipase if abdominal pain, calcitonin if MTC risk
**Classification"Dual GIP/GLP-1 RA; weekly SC; doses 2.5-15mg
**Acute Mgmt"Pancreatitis: STOP, supportive; Hypoglycaemia (w/ insulin): [down]insulin
**Chronic Mgmt"Titrate q4wk 2.5->15mg; GI SE: slow titration, anti-emetics; sick-day: hold if unwell
**Key Score"SURPASS-2: beats semaglutide 1mg (HbA1c/weight); SURMOUNT: 20% weight loss
**Complications"GI (20-30%), pancreatitis (rare), MTC (contraindicated)
**Prognosis"Superior HbA1c/weight vs semaglutide; CVOT ongoing; renal safe

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes
24 hours
7 days
15 days
30 days

▸ 21.9.3 Hnf4A Mody Mody 1

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, hnf4a-mody, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["HNF1A-MODY (MODY 3)", "GCK-MODY (MODY 2)", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1)

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise HNF4A-MODY clinical features (macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia)
  • [ ] Explain sulfonylurea responsiveness
  • [ ] Differentiate from HNF1A-MODY and other MODY types

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
GeneHNF4A (hepatocyte nuclear factor 4-alpha)
Chromosome20q13.1
Prevalence~5-10% of MODY
InheritanceAutosomal dominant

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
OnsetChildhood/adolescence
Birth weightMacrosomia (>4kg)
NeonatalTransient neonatal hypoglycaemia (due to hyperinsulinaemia)
ProgressionProgressive beta-cell dysfunction (similar to HNF1A)
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

FeatureHNF4A-MODYHNF1A-MODYGCK-MODY
BirthMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemiaNormalNormal
SU responseExcellentExcellentNone
LipidsLow HDL, high ApoAIILow HDLNormal
AutoantibodiesNegativeNegativeNegative

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingHNF4A mutation
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptidePreserved
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
HNF1A-MODYNo macrosomia/neonatal hypoglycaemia; low CRP; low renal threshold
T1DMAutoantibodies +, low C-peptide
T2DMObese, older, insulin resistance
Beckwith-WiedemannMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, macrosomia, hemihypertrophy

7. Management

AspectDetail
SulfonylureaExcellent response (gliclazide MR 1st line)
MonitoringHbA1c 6-monthly; lipids annual
Genetic counsellingAutosomal dominant; 50% offspring risk

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Key featuresMacrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, SU responsive
LipidsLow HDL, raised ApoAII
SulfonylureaExcellent response (like HNF1A)
GeneticsHNF4A (20q13); autosomal dominant

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What distinguishes HNF4A-MODY from HNF1A-MODY?Macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycaemia, low HDL, raised ApoAII
What is the sulfonylurea response in HNF4A-MODY?Excellent (similar to HNF1A)
What is the inheritance pattern?Autosomal dominant (HNF4A, 20q13)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HNF4A = HNF1A?Both SU responsive but HNF4A has macrosomia/neonatal hypoglycaemia
All MODY need insulin?NO - HNF1A/4A = SU; GCK = none; HNF1B = insulin

Mnemonic: HNF4A-MODY-MACRO

  • HNF4A: hepatocyte nuclear factor 4-alpha
  • Neonatal hypoglycaemia (transient)
  • Fetal macrosomia (>4kg)
  • 4 (MODY 1)
  • Autosomal dominant (20q13)
  • Macrosomia + neonatal hypoglycaemia
  • Organic: low HDL, raised ApoAII
  • Diagnosis: macrosomia + neonatal hypo + SU responsive
  • You: SU excellent response
  • Macrosomia = key differentiator from HNF1A
  • Organic lipids: low HDL
  • DNA: HNF4A gene (20q13)

Topic Overview

HNF4A-MODY (MODY 1
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.9.4 Glp 1 Receptor Agonists

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify GLP-1 RAs by dosing frequency and structure
  • [ ] State CVOT evidence for each agent (MACE benefit)
  • [ ] Apply indication matrix: ASCVD, obesity, CKD, HF
  • [ ] Manage GI side effects (titration, dosing timing)
  • [ ] Recognise contraindications: MTC, MEN2, pancreatitis history, pregnancy

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassGlucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists
MechanismGLP-1R agonism -> [up]glucose-dependent insulin secretion, [down]glucagon secretion, [down]gastric emptying, [up]satiety (hypothalamic), [up][beta]-cell proliferation (preclinical)
Agents & DosesSemaglutide SC: 0.25->0.5->1.0 mg weekly (Ozempic); Semaglutide oral: 3->7->14 mg daily (Rybelsus); Liraglutide: 0.6->1.2->1.8 mg daily (Victoza); Dulaglutide: 0.75/1.5 mg weekly (Trulicity); Exenatide XR: 2mg weekly (Bydureon); Lixisenatide: 10->20 mcg daily (Lyxumia)
HbA1c ReductionSemaglutide 1.5-2.0%; Liraglutide 1.0-1.5%; Dulaglutide 1.0-1.5%
Weight LossSemaglutide 5-10kg; Liraglutide 3-5kg; Dulaglutide 3-4kg
PharmacokineticsSC: weekly (semaglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide XR) or daily (liraglutide, lixisenatide); Oral: daily, fasting + 30min before food/water

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A -- drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

GLP-1 RA Classification

AgentFrequencyStructureHalf-lifeCVOT (MACE)Weight LossKey Features
Semaglutide SCWeeklyHuman GLP-1 analogue (94% homology) + fatty acid~7 daysSUSTAIN-6 [check]5-10kgHighest efficacy; retinopathy[up] transient
Semaglutide oralDailySame + SNAC absorption enhancer~7 daysPIONEER-6 [check]4-6kgFirst oral GLP-1; fasting administration
LiraglutideDailyHuman GLP-1 analogue (97% homology) + fatty acid~13hLEADER [check]3-5kgDaily injection; 3mg (Saxenda) for obesity
DulaglutideWeeklyFc-GLP-1 fusion protein~5 daysREWIND [check]3-4kg50% no prior ASCVD; renal benefit
Exenatide XRWeeklyExendin-4 (Gila monster) + microspheres~17 daysEXSCEL (neutral)2-3kgNodules at injection site
LixisenatideDailyExendin-4 analogue~3hELIXA (neutral)2-3kgPost-prandial focus; short half-life

Indications by Comorbidity

IndicationPreferred Agent(s)Evidence
ASCVDSemaglutide SC, Liraglutide, DulaglutideSUSTAIN-6, LEADER, REWIND, HARMONY, PIONEER-6
Obesity (BMI[ge]30/27.5)Semaglutide 2.4mg (Wegovy), Liraglutide 3mg (Saxenda)STEP, SCALE
CKD (eGFR[ge]15)Semaglutide (FLOW), Dulaglutide, LiraglutideFLOW, REWIND renal, LEADER renal
HFNeutral/limited (FIGHT: liraglutide neutral in HFrEF; STEP-HFpEF: semaglutide improves symptoms)Not 1st line for HF
No high-risk comorbidityAny (consider weight loss need, dosing preference, cost)--

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
WeightObesity benefitEvery visit; target [ge]5% loss
Lipase/amylasePancreatitis suspicionIf severe abdominal pain; not routine
Retinal examSemaglutide retinopathy riskBaseline before semaglutide if proliferative DR; monitor
Calcitonin / thyroid USMTC screening (MEN2)Contraindicated if personal/family MTC or MEN2

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
PancreatitisSevere epigastric pain radiating to back, [up]lipase >3xULN; STOP GLP-1 RA; avoid if history
Retinopathy worsening (semaglutide)Transient [up]DR progression (SUSTAIN-6: 3.0% vs 1.8%); rapid HbA1c drop >1.5% in 3mo risk factor; baseline retinal exam if proliferative DR
Medullary thyroid cancer (MTC)Contraindicated if personal/family MTC or MEN2; rodent C-cell tumours (human relevance uncertain)
HypoglycaemiaRare as monotherapy (glucose-dependent); [up]with insulin/SU -- reduce those doses
GI intoleranceNausea 20-30%, vomiting, diarrhoea; [up]with rapid titration; manage per protocol

7. Management

Initiation & Titration

AgentStarting DoseTitration ScheduleMax Dose
Semaglutide SC0.25mg weekly x4wk0.5mg x4wk -> 1.0mg1.0mg (T2DM); 2.4mg (obesity)
Semaglutide oral3mg daily x30d7mg x30d -> 14mg14mg
Liraglutide0.6mg daily x1wk1.2mg x1wk -> 1.8mg1.8mg (T2DM); 3.0mg (obesity)
Dulaglutide0.75mg weekly-> 1.5mg if needed1.5mg
Exenatide XR2mg weekly--2mg
Lixisenatide10mcg daily x14d-> 20mcg20mcg
Titration key: Semaglutide/liraglutide must titrate to minimise GI side effects; dulaglutide less titration needed.

GI Side Effect Management

StrategyDetail
Slow titrationMandatory for semaglutide/liraglutide
Take with foodNot required but may help
Anti-emeticsOndansetron/metoclopramide PRN ([down]gastric emptying caution)
Dose reductionIf intolerant at max dose -- stay at tolerated dose
Switch agentDulaglutide/Exenatide XR may be better tolerated

Special Situations

SituationManagement
Renal impairmentNo dose adjustment (all); semaglutide oral: no data eGFR<30
Hepatic impairmentNo dose adjustment (Child-Pugh A/B); avoid C
PregnancyContraindicated (Category C); stop [ge]2mo pre-conception
Pancreatitis historyContraindicated
MTC/MEN2 family historyContraindicated
With insulin[down]Insulin dose 20-30% to avoid hypo; monitor
Sick-day rulesHold if unwell, unable to eat, vomiting
⬇ Clinical Pathway
GLP-1 RA Candidate
↓
Contraindications?
↓
Contraindicated
Contraindicated
Select agent by comorbidity
↓
ASCVD/Obesity?
↓
Semaglutide SC or Liraglutide
Consider dulaglutide, patient preference
↓
Titration schedule mandatory
↓
GI intolerant?
↓
Slow titration / anti-emetics / switch
Continue + monitor HbA1c/weight 3-monthly

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismGLP-1R agonism -> [up]glucose-dependent insulin, [down]glucagon, [down]gastric emptying, [up]satiety
CVOT EvidenceSemaglutide SC (SUSTAIN-6), Liraglutide (LEADER), Dulaglutide (REWIND), Oral semaglutide (PIONEER-6), Albiglutide (HARMONY) -- ALL [check] MACE[down]
Weight lossSemaglutide 5-10kg (2.4mg -> 15kg); Liraglutide 3-5kg; Dulaglutide 3-4kg
IndicationsASCVD -> Semaglutide/Liraglutide/Dulaglutide; Obesity -> Semaglutide 2.4mg/Liraglutide 3mg; CKD -> Semaglutide (FLOW), Dulaglutide, Liraglutide
Gi side effectsNausea 20-30%; slow titration mandatory; anti-emetics PRN
PancreatitisRare (~0.1-0.2%); STOP if suspected; avoid if history
Retinopathy (semaglutide)Transient worsening (SUSTAIN-6: 3.0% vs 1.8%); rapid HbA1c drop >1.5%/3mo risk; baseline retinal exam if proliferative DR
MTC contraindicationPersonal/family MTC or MEN2; rodent C-cell tumours
PregnancyContraindicated; stop [ge]2mo pre-conception
HypoglycaemiaRare monotherapy; [down]insulin/SU dose when combining

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of GLP-1 receptor agonists?GLP-1R agonism -> [up]glucose-dependent insulin secretion, [down]glucagon secretion, [down]gastric emptying, [up]satiety (hypothalamic), [up][beta]-cell proliferation (preclinical)
Which GLP-1 RAs have proven CV benefit?Semaglutide SC (SUSTAIN-6), Liraglutide (LEADER), Dulaglutide (REWIND), Oral semaglutide (PIONEER-6), Albiglutide (HARMONY) -- all show MACE reduction
What is the weight loss with semaglutide?T2DM dose 1mg: 5-10kg; Obesity dose 2.4mg (Wegovy): ~15kg
How do you manage GI side effects of GLP-1 RAs?Slow titration (mandatory for semaglutide/liraglutide); anti-emetics PRN; take with food; if persistent -> reduce dose or switch to dulaglutide/exenatide XR
What is the retinopathy risk with semaglutide?SUSTAIN-6: 3.0% vs 1.8% placebo; transient worsening; risk with rapid HbA1c drop >1.5%/3mo; baseline retinal exam if proliferative DR; monitor
Contraindications for GLP-1 RAs?Personal/family MTC or MEN2; pancreatitis history; pregnancy (stop [ge]2mo pre-conception)
How does semaglutide oral differ from SC?Daily dosing, fasting + 30min before food/water (SNAC absorption enhancer); lower weight loss (4-6kg vs 5-10kg); same CV benefit (PIONEER-6)
GLP-1 RA in heart failure?FIGHT: liraglutide neutral in HFrEF; STEP-HFpEF: semaglutide improves symptoms/QoL in HFpEF; not 1st line for HF (SGLT2i preferred)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
GLP-1 RA cause hypoglycaemia?NO -- glucose-dependent; only with insulin/SU -> reduce those
All GLP-1 RA same CV benefit?Most have MACE[down], but exenatide XR (EXSCEL) and lixisenatide (ELIXA) neutral; semaglutide/liraglutide/dulaglutide preferred for ASCVD
Semaglutide oral vs SC efficacySC > oral for HbA1c/weight; oral = convenience
Retinopathy = permanent damage?Transient; stabilises after initial period

Mnemonic: GLP1-SEMA-LIRA-DULA

  • GLP-1R agonism: [up]insulin, [down]glucagon, [down]gastric emptying, [up]satiety
  • Loss weight: Sema 5-10kg, Lira 3-5kg, Dula 3-4kg
  • Pancreatitis risk: rare, STOP if suspected, avoid if history
  • 1 (once weekly options: Sema, Dula, Exenatide XR)
  • SUSTAIN-6: Sema CV[check]
  • ELITE: Lira LEADER CV[check]
  • MACE: Dula REWIND CV[check]
  • Albiglutide HARMONY CV[check]
  • Retinopathy: Sema transient worsening (rapid HbA1c drop)
  • EMTC/MEN2: contraindicated
  • Pregnancy: contraindicated (stop 2mo pre)
  • Insulin combo: [down]insulin dose 20-30%
  • Dulaglutide: weekly Fc-fusion, less GI
  • Albiglutide: discontinued but CV[check]**

11. Mind Map

GLP-1 Receptor Agonists
Mechanism
GLP-1R agonism
Glucose-dependent insulin
[down]Glucagon, [down]gastric emptying
[up]Satiety
Agents
Semaglutide SC 1mg/wk
Semaglutide oral 14mg/d
Liraglutide 1.8mg/d
Dulaglutide 1.5mg/wk
Exenatide XR 2mg/wk
Lixisenatide 20mcg/d
CVOT Evidence
SUSTAIN-6 (Sema) [check]
LEADER (Lira) [check]
REWIND (Dula) [check]
PIONEER-6 (Oral Sema) [check]
EXSCEL (Exenatide) neutral
ELIXA (Lixi) neutral
Indications
ASCVD
Obesity (Sema 2.4mg, Lira 3mg)
CKD (FLOW, REWIND renal)
Side Effects
GI: nausea 20-30%
Pancreatitis (rare)
Retinopathy (Sema transient)
Contraindications
MTC/MEN2
Pancreatitis history
Pregnancy

12. One-Page Revision Card

DomainKey Points
DefinitionGLP-1 RAs: semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide, lixisenatide
Key TestHbA1c, weight, lipase if abdominal pain, retinal exam (semaglutide)
ClassificationWeekly: sema SC, dula, exenatide XR; Daily: lira, lixi, sema oral
**Acute Mgmt"Pancreatitis: STOP, supportive; Hypoglycaemia (w/ insulin): [down]insulin
**Chronic Mgmt"Titration mandatory; monitor weight/HbA1c; sick-day: hold if unwell
**Key Score"ASCVD/Obesity/CKD -> GLP-1 RA 1st/2nd line; CVOT: sema, lira, dula [check]
**Complications"GI (20-30%), pancreatitis (rare), retinopathy (sema), MTC (contraindicated)
**Prognosis"MACE[down] (sema, lira, dula), weight[down][down], CKD benefit (sema, lira, dula)

13. Spaced Repetition Trackers

Review IntervalDate CompletedConfidence (1-5)Notes

▸ 21.9.4 Kcnj11 And Abcc8 Neonatal Diabetes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, neonatal-dm, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Transient vs permanent neonatal diabetes", "Sulfonylurea responsiveness", "MODY genetic testing indications"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

KCNJ11 and ABCC8 neonatal diabetes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Recognise neonatal diabetes (<6 months) due to KCNJ11/ABCC8
  • [ ] Explain sulfonylurea responsiveness mechanism
  • [ ] Apply genetic testing and management
  • [ ] Recognise DEND/iDEND syndromes

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionDiabetes diagnosed <6 months age
GeneticsActivating mutations in KCNJ11 (Kir6.2) or ABCC8 (SUR1)
Incidence~1:100,000-500,000 live births; 40-50% of neonatal DM
MechanismMutated KATP channel fails to close -> ↓insulin secretion

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

FeatureDetail
Age<6 months (often <1 month)
PresentationHyperglycaemia, dehydration, ketoacidosis
Birth weightOften low (reduced fetal insulin -> ↓growth)
NeurologicalDEND (developmental delay, epilepsy, neonatal diabetes)
GrowthFailure to thrive

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Neurological Spectrum

SyndromeFeaturesMutation Severity
Permanent Neonatal Diabetes (PNDM)Diabetes onlyMild
iDENDDiabetes + developmental delayModerate
DENDDevelopmental delay, epilepsy, neonatal diabetesSevere

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestFinding
Genetic testingKCNJ11 or ABCC8 activating mutation
AutoantibodiesNegative
C-peptideLow/undetectable
CT/MRI brainNormal or subtle changes (DEND)
EEGEpileptiform activity (DEND/iDEND)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
6q24 TNDMRemission by 18mo; imprinting defect
INS mutationNo SU response; insulin only
GCK-MODYMild fasting hyperglycaemia; no treatment
Transient neonatal DM6q24 imprinting; remission by 18mo

7. Management

Sulfonylurea Switch (90% Success)

AspectDetail
DrugGliclazide MR (tolbutamide if <10kg)
Starting dose0.5-1.0 mg/kg/day
TitrationIncrease weekly to max 2-3 mg/kg/day
MonitoringPre-prandial glucose; HbA1c 3-monthly; neuro assessment
Insulin taperGradual over 2-4 weeks as SU established

DEND/iDEND Specific

AspectManagement
EpilepsyAnticonvulsants (sodium channel blockers may help)
DevelopmentalEarly intervention; physiotherapy, speech therapy

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
GenesKCNJ11 (Kir6.2), ABCC8 (SUR1) - KATP channel subunits
PNDMDiabetes <6mo; no remission
TNDM6q24 imprinting; remission by 18mo, relapse later
Sulfonylurea90% switch successfully (KCNJ11/ABCC8)
DEND/iDENDDevelopmental delay, epilepsy; higher SU doses needed
MechanismMutated KATP stays open -> ↓insulin; SU closes channel

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What genes cause neonatal diabetes?KCNJ11 (Kir6.2), ABCC8 (SUR1) - KATP channel subunits
What is the sulfonylurea response rate?~90%
What is DEND syndrome?Developmental delay, Epilepsy, Neonatal Diabetes
How do you switch from insulin to sulfonylurea?Gradual taper over 2-4 weeks; SU gliclazide 0.5-1mg/kg/day

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
All neonatal DM = KCNJ11/ABCC8?NO - 6q24 TNDM, INS, GCK, PDX1 also
All KCNJ11 = PNDM?Some TNDM also have KCNJ11 mutations
Sulfonylurea = cure?No - life-long treatment; dose may need increase with age

Mnemonic: NEONATAL-KCNJ11

  • Neonatal DM: <6 months
  • Early onset: <1 month common
  • One gene: KCNJ11 (Kir6.2) or ABCC8 (SUR1)
  • Neonatal: <6 months
  • ACTivating KATP mutation
  • Treatment: sulfonylurea 90%
  • A: ABCC8 (SUR1) or KCNJ11 (Kir6.2)
  • L: Low birth weight
  • KATP channel: stays open -> no insulin
  • Close mutations: severe = DEND
  • Neurological: DEND / iDEND
  • Juvenile: remission? no (PNDM)
  • 1 (KCNJ11) + 1 (ABCC8) = 2 genes
  • 1 in 100,000-500,000

Topic Overview

KCNJ11 and ABCC8 neonatal diabetes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Neurological Spectrum
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Sulfonylurea Switch (90% Success)
DEND/iDEND Specific
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.9.5 Bariatric Surgery Indications
[!info]
NICE NG191: BMI ≥35 + T2DM OR BMI ≥30 + recent T2DM (<10y); ADA: BMI ≥30 + T2DM; RYGB/Sleeve/OAGB = main procedures; 75–85% remission; multidisciplinary assessment mandatory; lifelong follow-up.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State NICE and ADA criteria for bariatric surgery in T2DM
  • [ ] Compare procedures (RYGB, Sleeve, OAGB, BPD/DS)
  • [ ] Conduct preoperative assessment
  • [ ] Understand remission mechanisms and outcomes
  • [ ] Apply postoperative follow-up schedule

2. Indications for Surgery

NICE NG191 (2023) — UK Standard

CriterionThreshold
BMI + T2DMBMI ≥35 kg/m² + T2DM OR BMI ≥30 kg/m² + recent T2DM (<10 years)
Ethnicity adjustmentSouth Asian/Chinese/Other Asian: BMI thresholds −2.5 kg/m² (i.e., ≥32.5 / ≥27.5)
Failed non-surgicalStructured weight management (Tier 3) ≥12 months
Age18–65 (consider >65 individually)
CommitmentLifelong follow-up, nutritional supplementation

ADA 2024 / ASMBS — International

CriterionThreshold
BMI + T2DMBMI ≥30 kg/m² + T2DM (no duration limit)
BMI + Obesity complicationsBMI ≥35 (any); BMI 30–34.9 + comorbidity
Asian adjustmentBMI ≥27.5 + T2DM; BMI ≥25 + obesity complications

3. Surgical Procedures — Comparison

ProcedureTypeRemission (1y)Remission (5y)MechanismKey Risks
RYGB (Roux-en-Y)Mixed (restrictive + malabsorptive)80–85%50–60%Small pouch + biliopancreatic limb bypass → GLP-1 ↑, nutrient sensing, restrictionMarginal ulcer, internal hernia, dumping, micronutrient deficiency
Sleeve Gastrectomy (SG)Restrictive + hormonal70–80%40–50%Ghrelin ↓ (fundus removed), GLP-1 ↑, restrictionLeak, stricture, reflux, weight regain
OAGB (One-Anastomosis / Mini-Gastric Bypass)Mixed80–85%50–60%Single anastomosis; simpler than RYGBBile reflux, marginal ulcer, micronutrient deficiency
BPD/DS (Biliopancreatic Diversion / Duodenal Switch)Predominantly malabsorptive85–95%60–70%Extensive bypass; greatest weight lossSevere malabsorption, protein deficiency, frequent diarrhoea
Adjustable Gastric BandRestrictive only40–50%20–30%Adjustable restrictionBand erosion, slippage, port infection, low remission — not recommended for T2DM

4. Preoperative Assessment (Multidisciplinary)

DomainAssessment
MedicalHbA1c, lipids, renal, liver, thyroid, iron, B12, folate, vitamin D, coagulation
CardiacECG, echo if indicated (HF, valve disease)
RespiratorySleep study (OSA screening); CPAP if severe
GIEndoscopy (H. pylori, Barrett's, hiatus hernia)
PsychologicalEating disorder screen (BED, night eating); depression/anxiety; capacity for consent
NutritionalDiet history, protein intake, micronutrient baseline
AnaestheticAirway, Mallampati, VTE risk (Caprini score)
ConsentProcedure-specific risks, lifelong supplementation, follow-up commitment

Team: Surgeon, physician/endocrinologist, dietitian, psychologist, anaesthetist, specialist nurse.

5. Preoperative Optimisation

TargetIntervention
HbA1c<64 mmol/mol (8.0%) if possible (reduces infection risk)
Weight loss5–10% pre-op (liver shrinkage diet: 800kcal 2 weeks)
SmokingStop ≥6 weeks (wound healing, anastomotic leak)
MedicationsStop SGLT2i (euglycaemic DKA risk); adjust insulin; stop anticoagulants per protocol
NutritionalHigh protein (1.5g/kg); multivitamin; vitamin D if deficient
VTE prophylaxisLMWH preoperative + extended post-discharge (28 days)

6. Postoperative Diabetes Management — Immediate

TimeframeGlucose Management
Day 0–1IV insulin infusion (sliding scale) OR SC basal + correction; NPO → clear fluids
Day 1–2Transition to SC insulin; reduce total dose 50–70% (incretin effect, caloric restriction)
Week 1–2Stop most glucose meds (except metformin if eGFR ok); monitor capillary glucose 4–6x/day
Month 1Off all glucose meds if remission (HbA1c <48); continue monitoring
Month 3Assess remission (HbA1c <48 off meds); nutritional review

Key: Rapid remission often within days (incretin effect, caloric restriction, weight loss).

7. Long-Term Outcomes

OutcomeRYGBSleeveOAGB
T2DM Remission (1y)80–85%70–80%80–85%
Remission (5y)50–60%40–50%50–60%
Weight loss (1y)30–35% TBWL25–30% TBWL30–35% TBWL
Weight regain (5y)15–20%20–25%15–20%
Microvascular benefit↓ Retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathySimilarSimilar
Macrovascular benefit↓ MACE, mortalitySimilarSimilar
Mortality (30-day)0.1–0.3%0.1–0.2%0.2–0.3%

8. Lifelong Follow-Up Requirements

TimelineAssessments
6 weeksWound, diet progression, glucose, nutritional (B12, iron, folate, D, Ca)
3 monthsFull bloods, HbA1c, weight, diet adherence, supplementation
6 monthsFull nutritional panel, DEXA (baseline), medication review
Annual (lifelong)Full bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, iron, B12, folate, D, Ca, PTH, Cu, Zn, Se), HbA1c, weight, DEXA q2y, endoscopy if symptoms
Supplementation (lifelong)Multivitamin + B12 (1mg IM 3-monthly or oral 1mg daily) + Iron + Calcium/Vit D + Folate + Thiamine

9. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
NICE BMI threshold≥35 + T2DM OR ≥30 + recent T2DM (<10y)
Ethnicity adjustmentSouth Asian: −2.5 BMI (threshold ≥32.5 / ≥27.5)
ADA thresholdBMI ≥30 + T2DM
Best remissionRYGB/OAGB 80–85%; Sleeve 70–80%
Remission durabilityRYGB 50–60% at 5y; Sleeve 40–50%
MechanismRYGB/OAGB: incretin (GLP-1) + restriction + malabsorption; Sleeve: ghrelin ↓ + GLP-1 ↑
Pre-op liver shrink800kcal 2 weeks pre-op
Smoking cessation≥6 weeks pre-op
SGLT2i pre-opSTOP (euglycaemic DKA risk)
Lifelong supplementsB12, Iron, Ca/VitD, Folate, Thiamine, Multivitamin
Follow-upAnnual lifelong nutritional monitoring mandatory

10. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

NICE NG191 (2023) — UK Standard

▸ 21.9.5 Lipodystrophy Syndromes

tags: [medicine, diabetes, davidson, lipodystrophy, fcps, mrcp]

davidson_part: Part 3: Clinical Medicine

davidson_chapter: Chapter 25: Endocrinology and Diabetes

status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

priority: HIGH

exam_relevance: "FCPS/MRCP High Yield - Core diabetes topic"

see_also: ["Wolfram syndrome", "Mitochondrial diabetes", "Secondary Diabetes"]

created: 2026-06-13

modified: 2026-06-13

Lipodystrophy syndromes

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify lipodystrophy syndromes (generalised vs partial)
  • [ ] Recognise genetic vs acquired forms
  • [ ] Apply management: metreleptin, insulin, metabolic control
  • [ ] Recognise associated complications

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
DefinitionSelective loss of adipose tissue -> metabolic complications
ClassificationGeneralised (CGL) vs Partial (FPLD); Genetic vs Acquired
PrevalenceRare: CGL 1:10M; FPLD 1:1M
Metabolic consequenceSevere insulin resistance, diabetes, hypertriglyceridaemia, hepatic steatosis

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

Congenital Generalised Lipodystrophy (CGL)

TypeGeneFeatures
CGL1AGPAT2Severe; early onset; acanthosis nigricans; hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
CGL2BSCL2Severe; intellectual disability; cardiomyopathy risk
CGL3CAV1Mild; vitamin D resistance
CGL4PTRF (CAVIN1)Myopathy; pyloric stenosis

Familial Partial Lipodystrophy (FPLD)

TypeGeneFeatures
FPLD1UnknownWomen; gluteofemoral fat loss
FPLD2 (Dunnigan)LMNAPuberty onset; limb/trunk fat loss; face/neck fat gain; high CVD risk
FPLD3PPARGEarly onset; severe insulin resistance
FPLD4PLIN1Adult onset; mild
FPLD5CIDECChildhood onset; severe

Acquired Lipodystrophy

TypeFeatures
Generalised (Lawrence)Autoimmune; post-infectious; panniculitis
Partial (Barraquer-Simons)Centripetal fat loss; complement C3 nephritic factor; MPGN
Drug-inducedProtease inhibitors (HIV); insulin injections (local)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Generalised vs Partial

FeatureGeneralised (CGL, Lawrence)Partial (FPLD, Barraquer-Simons)
Fat lossNear-totalPartial (limbs, trunk)
Metabolic severityExtremeVariable
OnsetInfancy/childhoodPuberty/adulthood
GeneticCGL1-4 (AGPAT2, BSCL2, CAV1, PTRF)FPLD1-6 (LMNA, PPARG, etc.)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

TestPurpose
Genetic testingAGPAT2, BSCL2, LMNA, PPARG, others
Body compositionDEXA, MRI (adipose tissue quantification)
MetabolicGlucose, HbA1c, lipids (TG >> cholesterol), insulin
LeptinLow (hallmark)
LiverUltrasound/MRI (steatosis); ALT/AST
HeartEcho (cardiomyopathy in CGL)
KidneyProteinuria (CGL2)

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
T2DM with obesityIncreased fat mass (vs loss)
Cushing syndromeCentral obesity + fat redistribution
Anorexia nervosaVoluntary weight loss; psychopathology
AgeingGradual fat redistribution

7. Management

Metabolic Management

TargetStrategy
DiabetesHigh-dose insulin (often >2 U/kg/day); U-500 insulin; CSII
HypertriglyceridaemiaFibrates; omega-3; avoid alcohol; strict glucose control
Leptin replacementMetreleptin (FDA approved for GL; off-label for PL)
Hepatic steatosisWeight loss (if partial); pioglitazone? (caution)

Metreleptin Therapy

AspectDetail
IndicationFDA approved for GL; off-label for PL with severe metabolic complications
Dose0.06 mg/kg/day SC (adjust by response)
Effects↓HbA1c 2-3%; ↓TG 50-60%; ↓hepatic fat; ↑satiety
MonitoringLeptin levels; HbA1c, TG, LFT; TSH (thyroid autoimmunity risk)
Side effectsAnti-leptin antibodies (neutralising); hypoglycaemia; T-cell lymphoma (theoretical)

Complication Management

ComplicationManagement
DiabetesHigh-dose insulin; CSII; metreleptin
HypertriglyceridaemiaFibrates + omega-3; avoid alcohol/estrogen
CardiomyopathyEcho surveillance; standard HF therapy
AutoimmunityScreen (CGL2); treat if present

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
LeptinLOW (hallmark); metreleptin replacement
CGLAGPAT2, BSCL2; severe IR; infancy onset
FPLD2LMNA; puberty onset; limb fat loss; high CVD
MetreleptinLeptin replacement; ↓HbA1c, ↓TG, ↓hepatic fat
ComplicationsSevere IR, diabetes, TG >20, hepatic steatosis, pancreatitis

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the hallmark of lipodystrophy?Low leptin; severe insulin resistance; hypertriglyceridaemia
How do you classify lipodystrophy?Generalised (CGL1-4) vs Partial (FPLD1-6); Genetic vs Acquired
What is metreleptin?Recombinant leptin; FDA approved for GL; ↓HbA1c, ↓TG, ↓hepatic fat
What is FPLD2 (Dunnigan)?LMNA mutation; puberty onset; limb fat loss; face/neck fat gain; high CVD
How do you manage diabetes in lipodystrophy?High-dose insulin (often >2 U/kg); CSII; metreleptin; U-500 insulin

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Lipodystrophy = anabolic?NO - CATABOLIC state; fat loss -> metabolic chaos
All lipodystrophy = genetic?NO - acquired forms exist (Lawrence, Barraquer-Simons)
Leptin = weight loss drug?In lipodystrophy = REPLACEMENT therapy for deficiency

Mnemonic: LIPO-DYSTROPHY-SYNDROMES

  • Lipodystrophy: fat loss -> metabolic chaos
  • Insulin resistance: severe
  • Partial (FPLD) vs Generalised (CGL)
  • Obesity: NO - fat LOSS
  • Diabetes: severe IR; high-dose insulin
  • Yleptin: LOW (hallmark); metreleptin replacement
  • Syndromes: CGL1-4, FPLD1-6, Acquired
  • Triglycerides: very high (>20 mmol/L)
  • Risk: pancreatitis, hepatic steatosis, CVD
  • Organomegaly: hepatomegaly (fatty liver)
  • Partial: FPLD2 (LMNA) - Dunnigan
  • Hypertriglyceridaemia: >20 mmol/L
  • Yleptin replacement: metreleptin (FDA for GL)
  • Extreme IR: >2 U/kg/day insulin
  • Syndromes: CGL1-4, FPLD1-6, Acquired
  • You monitor: leptin, TG, HbA1c, LFT
  • Nephropathy: CGL2 -> proteinuria
  • Dunnigan: LMNA, FPLD2
  • Receptor: none (leptin resistance not the issue)
  • Organ: liver (steatosis), heart (cardiomyopathy)
  • Metreleptin: ↓HbA1c 2-3%, ↓TG 50-60%
  • Echo: cardiomyopathy (CGL)
  • Severe: CGL1 (AGPAT2) - infantile**

Topic Overview

Lipodystrophy syndromes
1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
Congenital Generalised Lipodystrophy (CGL)
Familial Partial Lipodystrophy (FPLD)
Acquired Lipodystrophy
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Generalised vs Partial
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Metabolic Management
Metreleptin Therapy
Complication Management
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
▸ 21.9.6 Post Surgery Diabetes Management
[!info]
Post-bariatric glucose management: IV insulin → SC 50–70% dose reduction within days; rapid remission (incretin effect); dumping syndrome (early/late); nutritional deficiencies (B12, iron, Ca, D, thiamine); lifelong supplementation + monitoring; contraception 12–18m; pregnancy planning.

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Manage glucose in immediate postoperative period (Days 0–30)
  • [ ] Recognise and treat dumping syndrome (early vs late)
  • [ ] Prevent and monitor nutritional deficiencies
  • [ ] Apply long-term follow-up schedule
  • [ ] Advise on contraception and pregnancy planning

2. Immediate Postoperative Glucose Management (Days 0–30)

Day 0–1 (ICU/HDU)

SituationManagement
NPO / IV fluidsIV insulin infusion (sliding scale) target 6–10 mmol/L
Regular insulin drip0.05–0.1u/kg/h; adjust q1–2h
Existing basal insulinSTOP — IV insulin replaces

Day 1–2 (Ward, Starting Oral Intake)

StepAction
1. Stop IV insulinWhen tolerating oral fluids/protein shakes
2. Start SC insulin50–70% of pre-op total daily dose (incretin effect + caloric restriction)
3. RegimenBasal (glargine/detemir/degludec) + minimal/rapid correction only
4. Stop oral glucose medsSulfonylurea, SGLT2i, DPP-4i, GLP-1 RA, TZD → all STOP
5. MetforminContinue if eGFR ≥30 (adjust for weight loss)

Day 3–7

Glucose PatternAction
Normoglycaemia (4–7)Stop ALL glucose meds (assess remission)
Mild hyperglycaemia (7–10)Low-dose basal only (e.g., 5–10u); recheck daily
Hypoglycaemia (<4)Reduce/stop insulin; carb snack; reassess dose

Key: Rapid remission common — 50–70% off all meds by discharge.

3. Dumping Syndrome — Early vs Late

FeatureEarly Dumping (15–30min post-meal)Late Dumping (1–3h post-meal)
MechanismRapid fluid shift → hypovolaemiaReactive hypoglycaemia (hyperinsulinaemia)
SymptomsNausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, cramps, flushing, tachycardia, dizziness, syncopeSweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger, confusion, neuroglycopenia
TriggerHyperosmolar foods (sugar, simple carbs), large volumesHigh glycaemic carbs → rapid absorption → insulin surge
DiagnosisClinical (Symptom score: Sigstad >7)CGM/glucose <3.5 at 1–3h post-meal
ManagementDiet: small frequent meals, low sugar, low GI, fluid separate from food (30min), protein/fat with carbs, lie down post-mealDiet: complex carbs, protein/fat each meal, avoid simple sugars; Acarbose 50mg TDS (delays absorption); Octreotide (refractory)

4. Nutritional Deficiencies — Lifelong Monitoring

NutrientDeficiency RiskMonitoringSupplementation
Vitamin B12High (ileal bypass, ↓ intrinsic factor + acid)Annual (B12, MMA, homocysteine)IM 1mg 3-monthly OR oral 1mg daily
IronHigh (duodenal bypass, ↓ acid, menstrual loss)6-monthly (ferritin, Tsat, Hb)Oral 100–200mg elemental ± IV if severe
Calcium / Vit DHigh (bypass, ↓ absorption, bone loss)Annual (Ca, PO4, ALP, PTH, 25-OH-D, DEXA q2y)Ca 1200–1500mg + Vit D 2000–4000 IU daily
Thiamine (B1)Critical (Wernicke's risk — rapid weight loss, vomiting)Annual (clinical; erythrocyte transketolase if indicated)Prophylactic 100mg daily post-op; IV 500mg TDS if suspected Wernicke's
FolateModerateAnnual1mg daily (in multivitamin)
Zinc / Copper / SeleniumModerate (malabsorptive procedures)AnnualIn bariatric multivitamin
Vitamin A / KLow (DS/BPD)If DS/BPDAs per protocol

5. Long-Term Follow-Up Schedule

TimepointAssessments
6 weeksWound, diet advancement, glucose, B12, iron, folate, D, Ca, PTH, thiamine
3 monthsFull bloods, HbA1c, weight, diet adherence, medication review, supplementation check
6 monthsFull nutritional panel, DEXA (baseline), weight trajectory
Annual (lifelong)CBC, U&E, LFT, iron studies, B12, folate, Vit D, Ca, PTH, Mg, Zn, Cu, Se, HbA1c, weight, DEXA q2y, endoscopy if symptoms

Supplementation (Lifelong, all procedures):

  • Bariatric multivitamin (2/day)
  • B12: IM 1mg 3-monthly OR oral 1mg daily
  • Iron: 100–200mg elemental daily (adjust by ferritin)
  • Calcium citrate 1200–1500mg + Vit D3 2000–4000 IU daily
  • Thiamine 100mg daily

6. Specific Post-Surgical Issues

Hypoglycaemia (Late Dumping / Reactive)

  • CGM essential for detection
  • Diet: complex carbs, protein/fat each meal, avoid simple sugars
  • Acarbose 50–100mg TDS with meals
  • Diazoxide / Octreotide (refractory, specialist)

Bone Health

  • DEXA at baseline, 2y, then q2–3y
  • High fracture risk (↓ weight-bearing, ↓ Ca/VitD, ↑ bone turnover)
  • Bisphosphonate if T-score <−2.5 or fracture

Anaemia

  • Multifactorial: Iron, B12, folate, copper, chronic inflammation
  • Treat specific deficiency; IV iron if oral fails/intolerant

Nephrolithiasis

  • ↑ Oxalate absorption (fat malabsorption) → calcium oxalate stones
  • High fluid intake (2–3L/day); citrate (potassium citrate); low oxalate diet

7. Contraception & Pregnancy

RecommendationDetail
ContraceptionEffective required 12–18 months post-op (rapid fertility return)
PreferredLARC (IUD, implant); avoid oral (malabsorption risk)
Pregnancy planningWait 12–18 months (weight stabilisation, nutrition optimisation)
Pre-conceptionNutritional optimisation (B12, iron, folate 5mg, Ca, D); HbA1c <48; stop teratogens
During pregnancySpecialist obstetric + bariatric team; increased nutritional monitoring; adjusted supplementation

8. Exam Pearls (FCPS/MRCP)

TopicKey Point
Post-op insulin reduction50–70% dose reduction (incretin effect)
Rapid remissionOften within days off all meds
Dumping syndromeEarly (osmotic, 15–30min) vs Late (reactive hypo, 1–3h)
Early dumping managementSmall meals, fluid separate, lie down post-meal, low sugar
Late dumping managementComplex carbs, protein/fat, acarbose, octreotide
B12 deficiencyIM 1mg 3-monthly lifelong (ileal bypass)
Iron deficiency6-monthly ferritin; oral/IV replacement
ThiamineProphylactic 100mg daily; Wernicke's risk with vomiting
DEXABaseline, 2y, q2–3y (high fracture risk)
ContraceptionLARC; avoid oral (malabsorption); wait 12–18m for pregnancy

9. Local Navigation (for Dashboard UI)

Bone Health
Anaemia
Nephrolithiasis

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.9.7 Dpp 4 Inhibitors

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] Classify DPP-4 inhibitors and their dosing
  • [ ] Apply renal dose adjustments (linagliptin unique = no adjustment)
  • [ ] Recognise saxagliptin HF hospitalisation risk
  • [ ] Position in ADA/EASD algorithm (2nd/3rd line, no CV benefit)
  • [ ] Monitor for bullous pemphigoid, pancreatitis, arthralgia

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassDipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors
MechanismInhibit DPP-4 enzyme → ↓degradation of endogenous GLP-1 & GIP → ↑glucose-dependent insulin secretion, ↓glucagon secretion
Agents & DosesLinagliptin 5mg OD (no renal adjust); Sitagliptin 100mg OD (50mg if eGFR 30-45, 25mg if <30); Vildagliptin 50mg BD (50mg OD if eGFR<30); Alogliptin 25mg OD (12.5mg if eGFR 30-45, 6.25mg if <30); Saxagliptin 5mg OD (2.5mg if eGFR≤50)
HbA1c Reduction0.5-0.8% (5-9 mmol/mol)
Weight ChangeNeutral (0 to –0.5kg)
Hypoglycaemia RiskVery low (glucose-dependent action)

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A — drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Agent Comparison

AgentStandard DoseRenal AdjustmentKey Features
Linagliptin5mg ODNO adjustment (biliary excretion)CKD safe; CARMELINA (renal neutral); CAROLINA (vs glimepiride)
Sitagliptin100mg ODeGFR 30-45: 50mg; <30: 25mgTECOS (CV neutral); widely used
Vildagliptin50mg BDeGFR<30: 50mg ODRequires LFT monitoring (hepatotoxicity rare)
Alogliptin25mg ODeGFR 30-45: 12.5mg; <30: 6.25mgEXAMINE (post-ACS); CV neutral
Saxagliptin5mg ODeGFR≤50: 2.5mgSAVOR-TIMI: ↑HF hospitalisation (HR 1.27); avoid in HF

CVOT Evidence

TrialAgentPopulationMACEHF Hospitalisation
TECOSSitagliptinT2DM + ASCVDNeutralNeutral
SAVOR-TIMISaxagliptinT2DM + ASCVD/riskNeutral↑27% (HR 1.27)
EXAMINEAlogliptinPost-ACS (15-90d)NeutralNeutral
CARMELINALinagliptinT2DM + ASCVD/CKDNeutralNeutral
CAROLINALinagliptinT2DM + CV riskNon-inferior to glimepirideNeutral

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly
Renal function (eGFR)Dose adjustment (except linagliptin)Before initiation, then 6-12 monthly
LFT (vildagliptin)Hepatotoxicity monitoringPre-treatment, then per local protocol
Heart failure symptomsSaxagliptin riskScreen if on saxagliptin

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Bullous pemphigoidTense blisters on erythematous base; ↑with DPP-4i (sitagliptin, linagliptin); STOP drug, refer dermatology
PancreatitisSevere epigastric pain radiating to back, ↑lipase >3×ULN; rare but reported; STOP if suspected
ArthralgiaJoint pain; onset weeks-months after start; STOP drug, resolves in weeks
HF decompensation (saxagliptin)New/worsening dyspnoea, oedema, weight gain; avoid saxagliptin in HF

7. Management

Initiation & Positioning

StepAction
1. Select agentLinagliptin 5mg OD (no renal adjust, CKD safe); Sitagliptin if eGFR≥30; Avoid saxagliptin if HF
2. Check renal functionAdjust dose per table (except linagliptin)
3. Add-on therapyAfter metformin; no CV/renal benefit — use if SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA not tolerated/contraindicated
4. CombineWith metformin, SGLT2i, insulin, TZD; avoid with GLP-1 RA (same pathway)

Adverse Effect Management

Adverse EffectManagement
Bullous pemphigoidSTOP DPP-4i; topical/systemic steroids; dermatology referral
ArthralgiaSTOP DPP-4i; switch agent class; resolves in weeks
PancreatitisSTOP DPP-4i; supportive care; avoid rechallenge
HF symptoms (saxagliptin)STOP saxagliptin; switch to linagliptin/sitagliptin/alogliptin/vildagliptin; treat HF per guidelines

Special Situations

SituationManagement
CKDLinagliptin preferred (no adjust); others dose reduce
HFAvoid saxagliptin; linagliptin/sitagliptin/alogliptin/vildagliptin OK
With GLP-1 RADo not combine — same pathway (↑GLP-1)
With insulinSafe; ↓insulin dose if hypoglycaemia
PregnancyNot recommended (Category B but limited data); insulin preferred
⬇ Clinical Pathway
DPP-4i Candidate
↓
Select Agent
↓
Linagliptin 5mg OD
Sitagliptin 100mg OD
AVOID saxagliptin
Linagliptin or dose-reduced others
↓
Add to metformin
↓
Adverse effects?
↓
STOP, dermatology
STOP, switch class
STOP, avoid rechallenge
Continue + HbA1c 6-monthly

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
MechanismInhibit DPP-4 → ↑endogenous GLP-1/GIP → ↑insulin, ↓glucagon (glucose-dependent)
WeightNeutral (0 to –0.5kg)
HypoglycaemiaVery low (glucose-dependent)
Renal dosingLinagliptin: NO adjustment (biliary excretion); others: dose reduce per eGFR
Key agentLinagliptin 5mg OD — 1st choice for CKD, no adjust, CV neutral (CARMELINA, CAROLINA)
Saxagliptin↑HF hospitalisation (SAVOR: HR 1.27); avoid in HF
CVOTAll CV neutral (TECOS, SAVOR, EXAMINE, CARMELINA, CAROLINA)
Bullous pemphigoidClass effect; tense blisters; STOP drug, dermatology
ArthralgiaClass effect; STOP drug
PancreatitisRare; STOP if suspected
Position in algorithm2nd/3rd line if SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA not tolerated/contraindicated; no CV/renal benefit

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of DPP-4 inhibitors?Inhibit DPP-4 enzyme → ↓degradation of endogenous GLP-1 & GIP → ↑glucose-dependent insulin secretion, ↓glucagon secretion
Which DPP-4 inhibitor requires NO renal dose adjustment?Linagliptin — biliary excretion; all others require eGFR-based dose reduction
What is the HF risk with saxagliptin?SAVOR-TIMI 53: ↑27% HF hospitalisation (HR 1.27; 3.5% vs 2.8%); avoid in HF
Do DPP-4 inhibitors have CV benefit?No — all CVOTs neutral: TECOS (sitagliptin), SAVOR (saxagliptin), EXAMINE (alogliptin), CARMELINA (linagliptin), CAROLINA (linagliptin vs glimepiride)
What is bullous pemphigoid and which DPP-4i cause it?Tense blisters on erythematous base; class effect (sitagliptin, linagliptin mainly); STOP drug, dermatology referral
Can you combine DPP-4i with GLP-1 RA?No — same pathway (both ↑GLP-1 action); not recommended
How does linagliptin differ from sitagliptin?Linagliptin: no renal adjust (biliary excretion); sitagliptin: renal adjust needed (50/25mg)

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
DPP-4i cause weight gain?NO — weight neutral
DPP-4i + GLP-1 RA?Do not combine — same pathway
All DPP-4i same renal dosing?NO — linagliptin unique: no adjustment
Saxagliptin = other DPP-4i?NO — saxagliptin ↑HF hosp; avoid in HF

Mnemonic: DPP4-LINA-SITA-SAXA

  • DPP-4 inhibition → ↑endogenous GLP-1/GIP
  • Pancreatitis: rare, STOP if suspected
  • 4 (DPP-4) enzyme inhibited
  • Linagliptin: NO renal adjust (biliary excretion) — 1st choice CKD
  • Incretin effect: glucose-dependent → low hypo
  • Neutral CV: all trials (TECOS, SAVOR, EXAMINE, CARMELINA, CAROLINA)
  • Arthralgia: STOP drug
  • Sitagliptin: renal adjust (50/25mg)
  • Avoid saxagliptin in HF (SAVOR: ↑HF hosp)
  • XA: saxagliptin ↑HF hosp
  • Alogliptin: EXAMINE (post-ACS)
▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 21.9.7 Maturity Onset Diabetes Of The Young Mody

Topic Overview

Maturity-onset diabetes of the young (MODY
Disease-Level Topics
High-Yield Exam Points (FCPS/MRCP)
Quick Links
Tags
▸ 21.9.8 Metformin

1. Learning Objectives

By the end of this note you should be able to:

  • [ ] State metformin mechanism, pharmacokinetics, and dosing
  • [ ] Apply renal dosing guidelines (eGFR-based)
  • [ ] Recognise contraindications and precautions (lactic acidosis risk factors)
  • [ ] Manage B12 deficiency monitoring
  • [ ] Counsel on GI side effect mitigation

2. Definition & Epidemiology

FeatureDetail
Drug ClassBiguanide (only available agent)
Mechanism[down]Hepatic gluconeogenesis ([up]AMPK -> [down]mTOR -> [down]gluconeogenic genes); [up]peripheral insulin sensitivity ([up]GLUT4); [down]intestinal glucose absorption; [up]GLP-1 secretion
PharmacokineticsOral absorption 50-60%; no protein binding; renal excretion unchanged (t[half] 4-9h); not metabolised
DosingImmediate-release (IR): 500mg BD/TDS with meals -> max 3g/day; Extended-release (XR): 500mg OD with evening meal -> max 2g/day
HbA1c ReductionMonotherapy: 1.0-1.5% (11-16 mmol/mol); Add-on: 0.5-1.0%

3. Clinical Features / Presentation

(N/A -- drug therapy)

4. Classification / Staging / Grading

Renal Dosing (eGFR-based) -- CRITICAL FOR EXAMS

eGFR (mL/min/1.73m2)CKD StageMetformin DoseAction
>=45G1-G3aFull dose (IR up to 3g, XR up to 2g)Standard
30-44G3bHalf max dose (IR 1g/day, XR 1g/day)Use with caution; monitor 3-6 monthly
<30G4-G5CONTRAINDICATEDStop; switch to alternative
UK (MHRA) / ADA / KDIGO alignment: eGFR <30 = contraindicated; 30-45 = dose reduction; [ge]45 = full dose.
In hospital: Hold if eGFR <45, contrast procedures, surgery, hypoxia, sepsis.

Contraindications

AbsoluteRelative / Precaution
eGFR <30 mL/mineGFR 30-45 (dose reduce)
Acute metabolic acidosis (DKA, lactic acidosis)Age >80 (caution, assess renal function)
Severe hepatic impairmentConstrast media (hold 48h post-IV contrast if eGFR<60)
HypersensitivityAlcohol excess, malnutrition, hypoxia (sepsis, MI, HF)
Pregnancy (Category B but insulin preferred for GDM/pre-existing)Surgery (hold periop)

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
eGFR (CKD-EPI)Renal dosingBefore initiation, then 3-6 monthly if <60; annually if >60
Vitamin B12Deficiency screeningBaseline, then annually (metformin [down]B12 absorption 10-30%)
LactateLactic acidosis suspicionONLY if clinically suspected (rare) -- not routine
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly

5. Diagnosis & Investigations

InvestigationRoleMonitoring
eGFR (CKD-EPI)Renal dosingBefore initiation, then 3-6 monthly if <60; annually if >60
Vitamin B12Deficiency screeningBaseline, then annually (metformin [down]B12 absorption 10-30%)
LactateLactic acidosis suspicionONLY if clinically suspected (rare) -- not routine
HbA1cEfficacy3-monthly till target, then 6-monthly

6. Differential Diagnosis

ConditionDistinguishing Features
Metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA)Metabolic acidosis (pH<7.3, lactate >5 mmol/L) + metformin accumulation (renal failure, hypoxia, sepsis); mortality 30-50%; STOP metformin, supportive care, CRRT
GI intoleranceDiarrhoea, nausea, abdominal pain (20-30%); [down]with XR, titration, taking with meals
B12 deficiencyMacrocytic anaemia, peripheral neuropathy; screen annually, replace if <200 pg/mL

7. Management

Initiation & Titration

StepDoseTitration
1500mg OD (IR with breakfast) or 500mg XR with evening meal--
2500mg BD (IR) or 1000mg XRAfter 1 week if tolerated
3500mg TDS (IR 1500mg) or 1500mg XRAfter 2 weeks
4Max 2550-3000mg (IR) / 2000-2500mg (XR)As tolerated
XR preferred for GI tolerability; take with evening meal.

GI Side Effect Mitigation

StrategyDetail
XR formulation[down]GI adverse effects 30-50% vs IR
Gradual titration500mg weekly increments
With mealsIR: with food; XR: with evening meal
Switch to XRIf IR intolerant
ProbioticsSome evidence for [down]diarrhoea

Special Situations

SituationManagement
Contrast proceduresHold metformin at time of procedure; restart 48h post if eGFR [ge]45; if eGFR 30-44, assess risk/benefit
SurgeryHold on day of surgery; restart when eating/drinking and renal function stable
Hospital admissionReview indication; hold if acute illness, hypoxia, sepsis, contrast planned
PregnancySafe (Category B) but insulin preferred for GDM/pre-existing DM; can continue if already on for PCOS
CKD G3b (eGFR 30-44)Half max dose; monitor eGFR 3-6 monthly; stop if <30
CKD G4-5 (eGFR <30)STOP (contraindicated)

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Points
Mechanism[down]Hepatic gluconeogenesis (AMPK), [up]insulin sensitivity, [up]GLP-1, [down]gut glucose absorption
DosingIR: 500mg BD/TDS meals -> max 3g; XR: 500mg OD evening meal -> max 2g; titrate weekly
Renal dosingeGFR [ge]45: full; 30-44: half max; <30: STOP (contraindicated)
ContraindicationseGFR<30, acute acidosis, severe hepatic, contrast/sepsis/surgery (hold), alcohol excess
B12 deficiency10-30% on long-term; screen annually; replace if <200 pg/mL
Lactic acidosisRare (3-10/100,000 pt-yrs); mortality 30-50%; risk: renal failure, hypoxia, sepsis, contrast, alcohol, liver disease
GI intolerance20-30%; XR + titration + meals [down]risk; switch to XR if intolerant
WeightNeutral or modest loss (1-3kg)
CV benefitUKPDS: [down]MI (39%), [down]all-cause mortality (36%) in overweight T2DM; neutral in modern CVOTs

9. Viva Questions

QuestionExpected Answer
What is the mechanism of action of metformin?Activates AMPK -> [down]hepatic gluconeogenesis, [up]peripheral insulin sensitivity ([up]GLUT4), [up]GLP-1 secretion, [down]intestinal glucose absorption
What are the renal dosing guidelines for metformin?eGFR [ge]45: full dose; eGFR 30-44: half maximum dose; eGFR <30: CONTRAINDICATED -- stop metformin
When do you hold metformin in hospital?Acute illness, sepsis, hypoxia, contrast procedures (hold 48h post-IV contrast if eGFR<60), surgery (hold periop), eGFR<30
What is metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA)?Metabolic acidosis (pH<7.3, lactate>5) + metformin accumulation; risk factors: renal failure, hypoxia, sepsis, contrast, alcohol, liver disease; mortality 30-50%
How do you manage GI side effects of metformin?Use XR formulation; titrate slowly (500mg weekly); take with meals; switch from IR to XR; consider probiotics
What is the effect of metformin on vitamin B12?[down]B12 absorption (10-30% prevalence on long-term); screen annually; replace if <200 pg/mL (oral 1mg OD or IM 1mg monthly)
Is metformin safe in pregnancy?Category B; safe but insulin preferred for GDM/pre-existing DM; can continue if on for PCOS
What is the UKPDS metformin legacy effect?Overweight T2DM: metformin [down]MI (39%) and all-cause mortality (36%) vs conventional; persisted 10y post-trial

10. Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Metformin causes hypoglycaemia?NO -- minimal risk as monotherapy; only with insulin/SU
Metformin contraindicated in all CKD?NO -- only eGFR<30; 30-44 = half dose; [ge]45 = full dose
Metformin before contrast?Hold at time of IV contrast; restart 48h post if eGFR[ge]45; if eGFR 30-44, individualise
XR vs IR efficacy?Equivalent HbA1c reduction; XR better GI tolerability

Mnemonic: METFORMIN-RX

  • Mechanism: AMPK -> [down]HGP, [up]insulin sensitivity
  • Entered: not metabolised, renal excretion
  • Titrate: 500mg weekly to max 2-3g
  • Full dose if eGFR [ge]45
  • Over 30-44 -> half dose
  • Renal <30 -> STOP (contraindicated)
  • Monitor B12 annually
  • Intolerance GI: use XR, with meals
  • No hypoglycaemia as monotherapy
  • Lactic acidosis: rare, pH<7.3 + lactate>5 + renal failure/hypoxia
  • Contrast/surgery/sepsis: HOLD
  • Pregnancy: safe but insulin preferred
  • Renal <30 = contraindicated
  • XR preferred for GI tolerability

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Definition & Epidemiology
3. Clinical Features / Presentation
4. Classification / Staging / Grading
Renal Dosing (eGFR-based) -- CRITICAL FOR EXAMS
Contraindications
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
5. Diagnosis & Investigations
6. Differential Diagnosis
7. Management
Initiation & Titration
GI Side Effect Mitigation
Special Situations
8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
9. Viva Questions
10. Confusions & Mnemonics
